Our segmentation pipeline achieves median Dice scores of 0.96 (liver) and 0.93 (spleen), driving a CRLM segmentation Dice score of 0.78 and a detection F1-score of 0.79. Accurate segmentation enables our radiomics pipeline to achieve a survival prediction C-index of 0.69. Our results show the potential of integrating segmentation algorithms with radiomics-based survival analysis to deliver accurate and automated CRLM outcome prediction.

AI.tldr: An automated AI framework combines anatomy-aware segmentation and radiomics to predict postoperative survival in CRLM after hepatectomy using pre- and post-contrast MRI; achieved robust organ/tumor segmentation and a C-index of 0.69 for survival.
AI.motivation: CRLM patients undergoing hepatectomy have highly heterogeneous outcomes; accurate, automated pre/postoperative survival prediction is needed to avoid non-beneficial surgery and enable personalized therapy.
AI.method: An anatomy-aware segmentation pipeline for liver, CRLMs, and spleen using partially-annotated data with promptable foundation models to generate pseudo-labels via SAMONAI (3D prompt-propagation for segmentation); a radiomics pipeline extracts per-tumor features from pre- and post-contrast segmentations; survival prediction via SurvAMINN, an autoencoder-based multiple-instance network handling right-censored time-to-event data.
AI.result: Segmentation achieved median Dice of 0.96 (liver) and 0.93 (spleen); CRLM Dice 0.78 and F1 0.79 for detection; survival C-index of 0.69; framework compared against Cox-based methods and biomarkers.
AI.conclusion: Integrating automated segmentation with radiomics enables automated CRLM survival prediction and has potential to guide surgical decisions and personalized management; the approach demonstrates feasibility and competitive performance in a retrospective multicenter-like dataset using 3D MRI.

title: Adaptive RAN Slicing Control via Reward-Free Self-Finetuning Agents
summary: The integration of Generative AI models into AI-native network systems offers a transformative path toward achieving autonomous and adaptive control. However, the application of such models to continuous control tasks is impeded by intrinsic architectural limitations, including finite context windows, the lack of explicit reward signals, and the degradation of the long context. This paper posits that the key to unlocking robust continuous control is enabling agents to internalize experience by distilling it into their parameters, rather than relying on prompt-based memory. To this end, we propose a novel self-finetuning framework that enables agentic systems to learn continuously through direct interaction with the environment, bypassing the need for handcrafted rewards. Our framework implements a bi-perspective reflection mechanism that generates autonomous linguistic feedback to construct preference datasets from interaction history. A subsequent preference-based fine-tuning process distills long-horizon experiences into the model's parameters. We evaluate our approach on a dynamic Radio Access Network (RAN) slicing task, a challenging multi-objective control problem that requires the resolution of acute trade-offs between spectrum efficiency, service quality, and reconfiguration stability under volatile network conditions. Experimental results show that our framework outperforms standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) baselines and existing Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents in sample efficiency, stability, and multi-metric optimization. These findings demonstrate the potential of self-improving generative agents for continuous control tasks, paving the way for future AI-native network infrastructure.

AI.tldr: A self-finetuning framework enables generative agents to learn continuous control by distilling long-horizon experiences into model parameters via autonomous linguistic feedback and preference-based fine-tuning, evaluated on dynamic RAN slicing with improved sample efficiency, stability, and multi-objective performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome key architectural limits of generative models in continuous control (limited context, no explicit reward signals, long-context degradation) by internalizing experience into parameters rather than relying on prompt-based memory.
AI.method: A bi-perspective reflection mechanism generates autonomous linguistic feedback to create preference datasets from interaction history, followed by preference-based fine-tuning to distill long-horizon experiences into model parameters, enabling self-interaction with the environment without handcrafted rewards.
AI.result: In a dynamic Radio Access Network slicing task with multiple objectives and volatile conditions, the approach outperforms standard RL baselines and LLM-based agents in sample efficiency, stability, and multi-metric optimization.
AI.conclusion: Self-improving generative agents via self-finetuning and preference-based distillation show strong potential for continuous control tasks and AI-native network infrastructures.

title: Autonomous Search for Sparsely Distributed Visual Phenomena through Environmental Context Modeling
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) are increasingly used to survey coral reefs, yet efficiently locating specific coral species of interest remains difficult: target species are often sparsely distributed across the reef, and an AUV with limited battery life cannot afford to search everywhere. When detections of the target itself are too sparse to provide directional guidance, the robot benefits from an additional signal to decide where to look next. We propose using the visual environmental context -- the habitat features that tend to co-occur with a target species -- as that signal. Because context features are spatially denser and often vary more smoothly than target detections, we hypothesize that a reward function targeted at broader environmental context will enable adaptive planners to make better decisions on where to go next, even in regions where no target has yet been observed. Starting from a single labeled image, our method uses patch-level DINOv2 embeddings to perform one-shot detections of both the target species and its surrounding context online. We validate our approach using real imagery collected by an AUV at two reef sites in St. John, U.S. Virgin Islands, simulating the robot's motion offline. Our results demonstrate that one-shot detection combined with adaptive context modeling enables efficient autonomous surveying, sampling up to 75$\%$ of the target in roughly half the time required by exhaustive coverage when the target is sparsely distributed, and outperforming search strategies that only use target detections.

AI.tldr: Proposes using environmental context as a reward signal for adaptive planning in AUV surveys to locate sparsely distributed coral species, via one-shot detection with patch-level DINOv2 embeddings; validated offline on reef imagery, achieving ~75% target detection in about half the time of exhaustive search, outperforming target-only strategies.
AI.motivation: Target species are sparsely distributed and AUVs have limited battery life. Environmental context features co-occur with targets and provide a denser, smoother signal to guide search when direct detections are sparse.
AI.method: From a single labeled image, perform one-shot detection of target and surrounding context using patch-level DINOv2 embeddings. Build an adaptive planner whose reward focuses on contextual signals rather than direct detections. Evaluate offline on real AUV imagery from two reef sites (St. John, U.S. Virgin Islands) by simulating motion; compare context-based planning to target-detection-only baselines.
AI.result: Context-informed adaptive planning substantially improves survey efficiency: can sample up to 75% of the target in roughly half the time required by exhaustive coverage when the target is sparsely distributed; outperforms strategies that rely solely on target detections.
AI.conclusion: Context features provide a valuable, denser signal for navigation under uncertainty. One-shot context detection with dense environmental cues enables more efficient autonomous reef surveys and can generalize to similar scenarios with sparse targets, though real-time deployment and domain variation deserve further study.

title: Cluster-Aware Attention-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning for Pickup and Delivery Problems
summary: The Pickup and Delivery Problem (PDP) is a fundamental and challenging variant of the Vehicle Routing Problem, characterized by tightly coupled pickup--delivery pairs, precedence constraints, and spatial layouts that often exhibit clustering. Existing deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approaches either model all nodes on a flat graph, relying on implicit learning to enforce constraints, or achieve strong performance through inference-time collaborative search at the cost of substantial latency. In this paper, we propose \emph{CAADRL} (Cluster-Aware Attention-based Deep Reinforcement Learning), a DRL framework that explicitly exploits the multi-scale structure of PDP instances via cluster-aware encoding and hierarchical decoding. The encoder builds on a Transformer and combines global self-attention with intra-cluster attention over depot, pickup, and delivery nodes, producing embeddings that are both globally informative and locally role-aware. Based on these embeddings, we introduce a Dynamic Dual-Decoder with a learnable gate that balances intra-cluster routing and inter-cluster transitions at each step. The policy is trained end-to-end with a POMO-style policy gradient scheme using multiple symmetric rollouts per instance. Experiments on synthetic clustered and uniform PDP benchmarks show that CAADRL matches or improves upon strong state-of-the-art baselines on clustered instances and remains highly competitive on uniform instances, particularly as problem size increases. Crucially, our method achieves these results with substantially lower inference time than neural collaborative-search baselines, suggesting that explicitly modeling cluster structure provides an effective and efficient inductive bias for neural PDP solvers.

AI.tldr: Introduces CAADRL, a cluster-aware transformer-based DRL for PDP with a dynamic dual-decoder, achieving strong performance on clustered PDP while maintaining low inference time compared to neural collaborative-search baselines.
AI.motivation: PDP's tight pickup-delivery coupling, precedence constraints, and clustered spatial layouts pose challenges for DRL. Prior methods either flatten the problem and rely on implicit constraint learning or use expensive inference-time search. There is a need to exploit multi-scale, cluster structure for better performance and efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes CAADRL: a cluster-aware encoding built on Transformer with global self-attention and intra-cluster attention over depot/pickup/delivery. Introduces a Dynamic Dual-Decoder with a learnable gate to balance intra-cluster routing and inter-cluster transitions at each step. Training uses end-to-end policy gradient (POMO-style) with multiple symmetric rollouts per instance.
AI.result: On synthetic clustered and uniform PDP benchmarks, CAADRL matches or surpasses strong state-of-the-art baselines on clustered instances and remains competitive on uniform instances, with notably lower inference time than neural collaborative-search baselines, especially as problem size grows.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling cluster structure via cluster-aware encoding provides an effective inductive bias for neural PDP solvers, achieving competitive accuracy with substantially reduced inference latency.

title: Robotic Ultrasound Makes CBCT Alive
summary: Intraoperative Cone Beam Computed Tomography (CBCT) provides a reliable 3D anatomical context essential for interventional planning. However, its static nature fails to provide continuous monitoring of soft-tissue deformations induced by respiration, probe pressure, and surgical manipulation, leading to navigation discrepancies. We propose a deformation-aware CBCT updating framework that leverages robotic ultrasound as a dynamic proxy to infer tissue motion and update static CBCT slices in real time. Starting from calibration-initialized alignment with linear correlation of linear combination (LC2)-based rigid refinement, our method establishes accurate multimodal correspondence. To capture intraoperative dynamics, we introduce the ultrasound correlation UNet (USCorUNet), a lightweight network trained with optical flow-guided supervision to learn deformation-aware correlation representations, enabling accurate, real-time dense deformation field estimation from ultrasound streams. The inferred deformation is spatially regularized and transferred to the CBCT reference to produce deformation-consistent visualizations without repeated radiation exposure. We validate the proposed approach through deformation estimation and ultrasound-guided CBCT updating experiments. Results demonstrate real-time end-to-end CBCT slice updating and physically plausible deformation estimation, enabling dynamic refinement of static CBCT guidance during robotic ultrasound-assisted interventions. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/anonymous-codebase/us-cbct-demo.

AI.tldr: Deformation-aware CBCT updating using robotic ultrasound to infer tissue motion and refresh static CBCT slices in real time, via a lightweight USCorUNet for dense deformation field estimation and LC2-based rigid refinement, enabling motion-consistent guidance with reduced radiation.
AI.motivation: Intraoperative CBCT provides static 3D context but cannot capture soft-tissue deformations due to respiration, probe pressure, and manipulation, causing navigation discrepancies; real-time deformation-aware updating is needed for accurate guidance and reduced radiation exposure.
AI.method: Initialize with calibration-based LC2 rigid refinement to establish multimodal alignment. Develop USCorUNet, a lightweight network trained with optical flow-guided supervision to learn deformation-aware correlation representations from ultrasound streams, producing real-time dense deformation fields. Regularize the deformation and transfer it to the CBCT reference to generate deformation-consistent visualizations. Validate via deformation estimation and ultrasound-guided CBCT updating experiments, achieving end-to-end real-time CBCT slice updates.
AI.result: Demonstrated real-time end-to-end CBCT slice updating and physically plausible deformation estimation; enables dynamic refinement of static CBCT guidance during robotic ultrasound-assisted interventions.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables deformation-aware, real-time updating of CBCT guidance using ultrasound, reducing radiation exposure while maintaining accurate, deformation-consistent visualization for robotic ultrasound-guided interventions.

title: CUAAudit: Meta-Evaluation of Vision-Language Models as Auditors of Autonomous Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) are emerging as a new paradigm in human-computer interaction, enabling autonomous execution of tasks in desktop environment by perceiving high-level natural-language instructions. As such agents become increasingly capable and are deployed across diverse desktop environments, evaluating their behavior in a scalable and reliable manner becomes a critical challenge. Existing evaluation pipelines rely on static benchmarks, rule-based success checks, or manual inspection, which are brittle, costly, and poorly aligned with real-world usage. In this work, we study Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as autonomous auditors for assessing CUA task completion directly from observable interactions and conduct a large-scale meta-evaluation of five VLMs that judge task success given a natural-language instruction and the final environment state. Our evaluation spans three widely used CUA benchmarks across macOS, Windows, and Linux environments and analyzes auditor behavior along three complementary dimensions: accuracy, calibration of confidence estimates, and inter-model agreement. We find that while state-of-the-art VLMs achieve strong accuracy and calibration, all auditors exhibit notable performance degradation in more complex or heterogeneous environments, and even high-performing models show significant disagreement in their judgments. These results expose fundamental limitations of current model-based auditing approaches and highlight the need to explicitly account for evaluator reliability, uncertainty, and variance when deploying autonomous CUAs in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can act as autonomous auditors for Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) by judging task success from final state and observations, but their effectiveness is tempered by environment complexity and model disagreement; reliability and uncertainty must be addressed for real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, reliable evaluation of CUAs by moving beyond brittle static benchmarks and manual checks, leveraging VLMs to audit task completion from observable interactions.
AI.method: Large-scale meta-evaluation of five VLMs as auditors, given an instruction and final environment state, across three CUA benchmarks spanning macOS, Windows, and Linux; evaluation axes include accuracy, calibration of confidence estimates, and inter-model agreement.
AI.result: VLMs achieve strong accuracy and calibration in some settings but exhibit performance degradation in more complex or heterogeneous environments; substantial inter-model disagreement indicates limited reliability for auditing CUAs in the wild.
AI.conclusion: Current model-based auditing approaches have fundamental limitations; auditing CUAs requires explicit handling of evaluator reliability, uncertainty, and variance, and should be complemented with strategies that account for cross-model disagreement and environment complexity.

title: Octopus-inspired Distributed Control for Soft Robotic Arms: A Graph Neural Network-Based Attention Policy with Environmental Interaction
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables, submitted for IROS 2026
summary: This paper proposes SoftGM, an octopus-inspired distributed control architecture for segmented soft robotic arms that learn to reach targets in contact-rich environments using online obstacle discovery without relying on global obstacle geometry. SoftGM formulates each arm section as a cooperative agent and represents the arm-environment interaction as a graph. SoftGM uses a two-stage graph attention message passing scheme following a Centralised Training Decentralised Execution (CTDE) paradigm with a centralised critic and decentralised actor. We evaluate SoftGM in a Cosserat-rod simulator (PyElastica) across three tasks that increase the complexity of the environment: obstacle-free, structured obstacles, and a wall-with-hole scenario. Compared with six widely used MARL baselines (IDDPG, IPPO, ISAC, MADDPG, MAPPO, MASAC) under identical information content and training conditions, SoftGM matches strong CTDE methods in simpler settings and achieves the best performance in the wall-with-hole task. Robustness tests with observation noise, single-section actuation failure, and transient disturbances show that SoftGM preserves success while keeping control effort bounded, indicating resilient coordination driven by selective contact-relevant information routing.

AI.tldr: SoftGM is a distributed, graph-based CTDE MARL approach for segmented soft robotic arms that learns to reach targets in contact-rich environments; it outperforms baselines in complex obstacle scenarios and shows robust, bounded control under perturbations.
AI.motivation: Address control of segmented soft robots in contact-rich environments with partial knowledge of obstacle geometry by leveraging a graph representation of arm–environment interactions and distributed agents coordinated through centralized training.
AI.method: Each arm section is modeled as a cooperative MARL agent; the arm–environment interaction is represented as a graph; a two-stage graph attention message passing scheme is used within a Centralised Training Decentralised Execution (CTDE) framework (centralized critic, decentralized actors); evaluated in a Cosserat-rod (PyElastica) simulator across obstacle-free, structured obstacles, and wall-with-hole tasks, against six MARL baselines.
AI.result: SoftGM matches strong CTDE methods in simple settings and achieves the best performance in the wall-with-hole task; robustness tests show maintained success under observation noise, single-section actuation failure, and transient disturbances with bounded control effort, indicating resilient, contact-aware coordination.
AI.conclusion: A graph-based CTDE MARL approach enables resilient, scalable coordination for segmented soft arms in contact-rich environments, effectively routing contact-relevant information and maintaining performance under perturbations; promising for robust manipulation when full obstacle geometry is unavailable.

title: OilSAM2: Memory-Augmented SAM2 for Scalable SAR Oil Spill Detection
summary: Segmenting oil spills from Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery remains challenging due to severe appearance variability, scale heterogeneity, and the absence of temporal continuity in real world monitoring scenarios. While foundation models such as Segment Anything (SAM) enable prompt driven segmentation, existing SAM based approaches operate on single images and cannot effectively reuse information across scenes. Memory augmented variants (e.g., SAM2) further assume temporal coherence, making them prone to semantic drift when applied to unordered SAR image collections. We propose OilSAM2, a memory augmented segmentation framework tailored for unordered SAR oil spill monitoring. OilSAM2 introduces a hierarchical feature aware multi scale memory bank that explicitly models texture, structure, and semantic level representations, enabling robust cross image information reuse. To mitigate memory drift, we further propose a structure semantic consistent memory update strategy that selectively refreshes memory based on semantic discrepancy and structural variation.Experiments on two public SAR oil spill datasets demonstrate that OilSAM2 achieves state of the art segmentation performance, delivering stable and accurate results under noisy SAR monitoring scenarios. The source code is available at https://github.com/Chenshuaiyu1120/OILSAM2.

AI.tldr: OilSAM2 is a memory-augmented, cross-image segmentation framework for unordered SAR oil spill monitoring that uses a hierarchical multi-scale memory bank and structure-semantic consistent memory updates to enable robust cross-image reuse, achieving state-of-the-art results on two public SAR datasets.
AI.motivation: Oil spills in SAR images are highly variable in appearance, scale, and lack temporal continuity across scenes, making segmentation difficult. Existing SAM-based methods are single-image; memory-augmented approaches assume temporal coherence and deteriorate with unordered collections.
AI.method: OilSAM2 introduces a hierarchical feature-aware multi-scale memory bank that explicitly models texture, structure, and semantic representations. It also proposes a structure-semantic consistent memory update strategy that refreshes memory selectively based on semantic discrepancy and structural variation to mitigate memory drift.
AI.result: Evaluated on two public SAR oil spill datasets, OilSAM2 achieves state-of-the-art segmentation performance and provides stable, accurate results under noisy monitoring conditions.
AI.conclusion: OilSAM2 enables robust cross-image information reuse for unordered SAR oil spill monitoring, with a design that reduces semantic drift and improves segmentation reliability; source code is publicly available.

title: Does LLM Alignment Really Need Diversity? An Empirical Study of Adapting RLVR Methods for Moral Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has achieved remarkable success in logical reasoning tasks, yet whether large language model (LLM) alignment requires fundamentally different approaches remains unclear. Given the apparent tolerance for multiple valid responses in moral reasoning, a natural hypothesis is that alignment tasks inherently require diversity-seeking distribution-matching algorithms rather than reward-maximizing policy-based methods. We conduct the first comprehensive empirical study comparing both paradigms on MoReBench. To enable stable RLVR training, we build a rubric-grounded reward pipeline by training a Qwen3-1.7B judge model. Contrary to our hypothesis, we find that distribution-matching approaches do not demonstrate significant advantages over reward-maximizing methods as expected on alignment tasks. Through semantic visualization mapping high-reward responses to semantic space, we demonstrate that moral reasoning exhibits more concentrated high-reward distributions than mathematical reasoning, where diverse solution strategies yield similarly high rewards. This counter-intuitive finding explains why mode-seeking optimization proves equally or more effective for alignment tasks. Our results suggest that alignment tasks do not inherently require diversity-preserving algorithms, and standard reward-maximizing RLVR methods can effectively transfer to moral reasoning without explicit diversity mechanisms.

AI.tldr: Empirically compares distribution-matching vs reward-maximizing RLVR for LLM alignment; finds no clear advantage for diversity-preserving methods, with moral reasoning showing concentrated high-reward regions and mode-seeking doing well.
AI.motivation: Tests the assumption that alignment tasks, especially moral reasoning, require diversity-preserving, distribution-matching algorithms rather than standard reward-maximizing methods.
AI.method: Perform an empirical study on MoReBench comparing RLVR with reward-based optimization to distribution-matching approaches; build a rubric-grounded reward pipeline via a Qwen3-1.7B judge; use semantic visualization to map high-reward responses in semantic space.
AI.result: Distribution-matching methods do not outperform reward-maximizing methods; moral reasoning has a more concentrated high-reward distribution than mathematical reasoning, where diverse strategies yield similar high rewards; mode-seeking optimization is effective.
AI.conclusion: Alignment tasks may not inherently require diversity-preserving algorithms; standard reward-maximizing RLVR can effectively transfer to moral reasoning without explicit diversity mechanisms.

title: Perceptive Hierarchical-Task MPC for Sequential Mobile Manipulation in Unstructured Semi-Static Environments
summary: As compared to typical mobile manipulation tasks, sequential mobile manipulation poses a unique challenge -- as the robot operates over extended periods, successful task completion is not solely dependent on consistent motion generation but also on the robot's awareness and adaptivity to changes in the operating environment. While existing motion planners can generate whole-body trajectories to complete sequential tasks, they typically assume that the environment remains static and rely on precomputed maps. This assumption often breaks down during long-term operations, where semi-static changes such as object removal, introduction, or shifts are common. In this work, we propose a novel perceptive hierarchical-task model predictive control (HTMPC) framework for efficient sequential mobile manipulation in unstructured, changing environments. To tackle the challenge, we leverage a Bayesian inference framework to explicitly model object-level changes and thereby maintain a temporally accurate representation of the 3D environment; this up-to-date representation is embedded in a lexicographic optimization framework to enable efficient execution of sequential tasks. We validate our perceptive HTMPC approach through both simulated and real-robot experiments. In contrast to baseline methods, our approach systematically accounts for moved and phantom obstacles, successfully completing sequential tasks with higher efficiency and reactivity, without relying on prior maps or external infrastructure.

AI.tldr: A perceptive HTMPC framework with Bayesian scene updates for robust sequential mobile manipulation in changing environments, validated in simulation and on real robots; it handles moved/phantom obstacles without relying on prior maps, improving efficiency and reactivity.
AI.motivation: Sequential mobile manipulation over long horizons suffers when the environment is semi-static or dynamic; static maps degrade performance as objects are moved or removed.
AI.method: Proposes perceptive hierarchical-task model predictive control (HTMPC). Uses Bayesian inference to model object-level changes and maintain an up-to-date 3D environment; integrates this into a lexicographic optimization for efficient task execution.
AI.result: Through simulated and real-robot experiments, the method outperforms baselines in handling moved and phantom obstacles, achieving higher efficiency and reactivity without precomputed maps.
AI.conclusion: In dynamic, unstructured environments, perceptive HTMPC with Bayesian scene awareness enables robust, efficient sequential manipulation without reliance on static maps.

title: HTMuon: Improving Muon via Heavy-Tailed Spectral Correction
summary: Muon has recently shown promising results in LLM training. In this work, we study how to further improve Muon. We argue that Muon's orthogonalized update rule suppresses the emergence of heavy-tailed weight spectra and over-emphasizes the training along noise-dominated directions. Motivated by the Heavy-Tailed Self-Regularization (HT-SR) theory, we propose HTMuon. HTMuon preserves Muon's ability to capture parameter interdependencies while producing heavier-tailed updates and inducing heavier-tailed weight spectra. Experiments on LLM pretraining and image classification show that HTMuon consistently improves performance over state-of-the-art baselines and can also serve as a plug-in on top of existing Muon variants. For example, on LLaMA pretraining on the C4 dataset, HTMuon reduces perplexity by up to $0.98$ compared to Muon. We further theoretically show that HTMuon corresponds to steepest descent under the Schatten-$q$ norm constraint and provide convergence analysis in smooth non-convex settings. The implementation of HTMuon is available at https://github.com/TDCSZ327/HTmuon.

AI.tldr: HTMuon extends Muon with Heavy-Tailed Self-Regularization ideas to produce heavier-tailed updates and weight spectra, improving LLM pretraining and image classification while preserving parameter interdependencies; includes theoretical convergence under Schatten-q norms and a plug-in design; code available.
AI.motivation: Muon's orthogonalized update dampens heavy-tailed weight spectra and over-emphasizes noise directions. HT-SR theory suggests beneficial heavy-tailed regularization. HTMuon aims to maintain parameter interdependencies while promoting heavier-tailed updates and spectra to improve optimization and generalization.
AI.method: Propose HTMuon as a modification of Muon that yields heavier-tailed updates and weight spectra guided by Schatten-q norm constraints; establish theoretical equivalence to steepest descent under Schatten-q constraint; provide convergence analysis in smooth non-convex settings; validate experimentally on LLM pretraining (LLaMA on C4) and image classification; implement as a plug-in and release code at the cited repository.
AI.result: HTMuon consistently improves performance over state-of-the-art baselines across tasks; in LLaMA pretraining on C4, perplexity reduced by up to 0.98 compared with Muon; shows improvements in image classification and serves as a plug-in for Muon variants.
AI.conclusion: HTMuon preserves Muon's ability to capture parameter interdependencies while enabling heavier-tailed updates and weight spectra; supported by theoretical convergence guarantees under Schatten-q norms; practical improvements demonstrated; code available for adoption.

title: Why Does It Look There? Structured Explanations for Image Classification
summary: Deep learning models achieve remarkable predictive performance, yet their black-box nature limits transparency and trustworthiness. Although numerous explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) methods have been proposed, they primarily provide saliency maps or concepts (i.e., unstructured interpretability). Existing approaches often rely on auxiliary models (\eg, GPT, CLIP) to describe model behavior, thereby compromising faithfulness to the original models. We propose Interpretability to Explainability (I2X), a framework that builds structured explanations directly from unstructured interpretability by quantifying progress at selected checkpoints during training using prototypes extracted from post-hoc XAI methods (e.g., GradCAM). I2X answers the question of "why does it look there" by providing a structured view of both intra- and inter-class decision making during training. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR10 demonstrate effectiveness of I2X to reveal prototype-based inference process of various image classification models. Moreover, we demonstrate that I2X can be used to improve predictions across different model architectures and datasets: we can identify uncertain prototypes recognized by I2X and then use targeted perturbation of samples that allows fine-tuning to ultimately improve accuracy. Thus, I2X not only faithfully explains model behavior but also provides a practical approach to guide optimization toward desired targets.

AI.tldr: I2X converts unstructured explanations from post-hoc XAI into structured, prototype-driven explanations during training, enabling faithfulness, intra-/inter-class insights, and actionable improvements across MNIST/CIFAR-10.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of transparency and dependency on auxiliary models in XAI by creating faithful, structured explanations that reflect actual training dynamics and can guide optimization.
AI.method: Quantify progress at selected training checkpoints by extracting prototypes from post-hoc XAI methods (e.g., Grad-CAM) and organizing them into a structured view of intra- and inter-class decision making; identify uncertain prototypes and use targeted perturbations for fine-tuning to improve performance.
AI.result: I2X reveals prototype-based inference processes and can improve predictions across different model architectures and datasets, demonstrated on MNIST and CIFAR-10; explanations are faithful and actionable.
AI.conclusion: I2X provides faithful, actionable explanations that guide optimization while preserving fidelity to the original model, with potential applicability to other architectures and datasets.

title: Trajectory-Informed Memory Generation for Self-Improving Agent Systems
summary: LLM-powered agents face a persistent challenge: learning from their execution experiences to improve future performance. While agents can successfully complete many tasks, they often repeat inefficient patterns, fail to recover from similar errors, and miss opportunities to apply successful strategies from past executions. We present a novel framework for automatically extracting actionable learnings from agent execution trajectories and utilizing them to improve future performance through contextual memory retrieval. Our approach comprises four components: (1) a Trajectory Intelligence Extractor that performs semantic analysis of agent reasoning patterns, (2) a Decision Attribution Analyzer that identifies which decisions and reasoning steps led to failures, recoveries, or inefficiencies, (3) a Contextual Learning Generator that produces three types of guidance -- strategy tips from successful patterns, recovery tips from failure handling, and optimization tips from inefficient but successful executions, and (4) an Adaptive Memory Retrieval System that injects relevant learnings into agent prompts based on multi-dimensional similarity. Unlike existing memory systems that store generic conversational facts, our framework understands execution patterns, extracts structured learnings with provenance, and retrieves guidance tailored to specific task contexts. Evaluation on the AppWorld benchmark demonstrates consistent improvements, with up to 14.3 percentage point gains in scenario goal completion on held-out tasks and particularly strong benefits on complex tasks (28.5~pp scenario goal improvement, a 149\% relative increase).

AI.tldr: A four-component framework that extracts actionable learnings from agent execution trajectories and uses adaptive memory retrieval to improve future performance, achieving significant gains on AppWorld benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLM-powered agents often repeat inefficient patterns and struggle to transfer learnings from past executions. There is a need for structured, provenance-backed extraction of execution insights and context-aware retrieval to guide future decisions.
AI.method: Four components: (1) Trajectory Intelligence Extractor for semantic analysis of reasoning patterns; (2) Decision Attribution Analyzer to attribute outcomes to decisions/steps; (3) Contextual Learning Generator producing strategy tips, recovery tips, and optimization tips; (4) Adaptive Memory Retrieval System injecting relevant learnings into prompts via multi-dimensional similarity. The approach emphasizes execution-pattern understanding and provenance over generic memory.
AI.result: Evaluation on the AppWorld benchmark shows up to 14.3 percentage-point gains in scenario goal completion on held-out tasks; particularly strong on complex tasks with 28.5 pp improvement and a 149% relative increase.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that structured extraction of execution learnings and context-aware memory retrieval can meaningfully improve the performance of LLM-powered agents, especially for complex tasks.

title: Hierarchical Task Model Predictive Control for Sequential Mobile Manipulation Tasks
comment: 8 pages, Published in IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters ( Volume: 9, Issue: 2, February 2024)
summary: Mobile manipulators are envisioned to serve more complex roles in people's everyday lives. With recent breakthroughs in large language models, task planners have become better at translating human verbal instructions into a sequence of tasks. However, there is still a need for a decision-making algorithm that can seamlessly interface with the high-level task planner to carry out the sequence of tasks efficiently. In this work, building on the idea of nonlinear lexicographic optimization, we propose a novel Hierarchical-Task Model Predictive Control framework that is able to complete sequential tasks with improved performance and reactivity by effectively leveraging the robot's redundancy. Compared to the state-of-the-art task-prioritized inverse kinematic control method, our approach has improved hierarchical trajectory tracking performance by 42% on average when facing task changes, robot singularity and reference variations. Compared to a typical single-task architecture, our proposed hierarchical task control architecture enables the robot to traverse a shorter path in task space and achieves an execution time 2.3 times faster when executing a sequence of delivery tasks. We demonstrated the results with real-world experiments on a 9 degrees of freedom mobile manipulator.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Task Model Predictive Control (HT-MPC) for mobile manipulators using nonlinear lexicographic optimization to exploit redundancy and better interface high-level planners with low-level execution.
AI.motivation: Bridge high-level task planning with low-level control to improve efficiency and reactivity in sequential tasks for mobile manipulators.
AI.method: Proposes a hierarchical MPC framework based on nonlinear lexicographic optimization to leverage robot redundancy, enabling improved hierarchical trajectory tracking under task changes, singularities, and reference variations.
AI.result: Outperforms task-prioritized inverse kinematic control by 42% on average in challenging conditions; achieves 2.3x faster execution for a sequence of delivery tasks; validated on a 9-DOF mobile manipulator in real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical Task Model Predictive Control enhances performance and reactivity for sequential tasks in mobile manipulation by exploiting redundancy through a hierarchical MPC framework, demonstrating practicality in real-world experiments.

title: Improving Search Agent with One Line of Code
summary: Tool-based Agentic Reinforcement Learning (TARL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for training search agents to interact with external tools for a multi-turn information-seeking process autonomously. However, we identify a critical training instability that leads to catastrophic model collapse: Importance Sampling Distribution Drift(ISDD). In Group Relative Policy Optimization(GRPO), a widely adopted TARL algorithm, ISDD manifests as a precipitous decline in the importance sampling ratios, which nullifies gradient updates and triggers irreversible training failure. To address this, we propose \textbf{S}earch \textbf{A}gent \textbf{P}olicy \textbf{O}ptimization (\textbf{SAPO}), which stabilizes training via a conditional token-level KL constraint. Unlike hard clipping, which ignores distributional divergence, SAPO selectively penalizes the KL divergence between the current and old policies. Crucially, this penalty is applied only to positive tokens with low probabilities where the policy has shifted excessively, thereby preventing distribution drift while preserving gradient flow. Remarkably, SAPO requires only one-line code modification to standard GRPO, ensuring immediate deployability. Extensive experiments across seven QA benchmarks demonstrate that SAPO achieves \textbf{+10.6\% absolute improvement} (+31.5\% relative) over Search-R1, yielding consistent gains across varying model scales (1.5B, 14B) and families (Qwen, LLaMA).

AI.tldr: SAPO stabilizes TARL training by a conditional token-level KL penalty to mitigate ISDD in GRPO, enabling stable learning and +10.6% absolute gains over Search-R1 across 7 QA benchmarks and model families/sizes.
AI.motivation: Investigation of training instability due to Importance Sampling Distribution Drift (ISDD) in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for tool-based agentic RL. ISDD causes a sharp drop in importance sampling ratios, halting gradient updates and causing training collapse.
AI.method: Introduce Search Agent Policy Optimization (SAPO), a lightweight, token-level KL constraint that penalizes divergence between the current and old policies only for positive tokens with low probabilities (where the policy has shifted excessively). This selective penalty prevents distribution drift while preserving gradient flow. Implementation requires only a one-line code modification to standard GRPO.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on seven QA benchmarks show SAPO yields +10.6% absolute improvement (+31.5% relative) over Search-R1, with consistent gains across model scales (1.5B and 14B) and families (Qwen, LLaMA).
AI.conclusion: SAPO stabilizes training in TARL by preventing ISDD-driven drift with a selective KL penalty, delivering robust performance gains and easy deployability via a minimal code change.

title: One Adapter for All: Towards Unified Representation in Step-Imbalanced Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Class-incremental learning (CIL) aims to acquire new classes over time while retaining prior knowledge, yet most setups and methods assume balanced task streams. In practice, the number of classes per task often varies significantly. We refer to this as step imbalance, where large tasks that contain more classes dominate learning and small tasks inject unstable updates. Existing CIL methods assume balanced tasks and therefore treat all tasks uniformly, producing imbalanced updates that degrade overall learning performance. To address this challenge, we propose One-A, a unified and imbalance-aware framework that incrementally merges task updates into a single adapter, maintaining constant inference cost. One-A performs asymmetric subspace alignment to preserve dominant subspaces learned from large tasks while constraining low-information updates within them. An information-adaptive weighting balances the contribution between base and new adapters, and a directional gating mechanism selectively fuses updates along each singular direction, maintaining stability in head directions and plasticity in tail ones. Across multiple benchmarks and step-imbalanced streams, One-A achieves competitive accuracy with significantly low inference overhead, showing that a single, asymmetrically fused adapter can remain both adaptive to dynamic task sizes and efficient at deployment.

AI.tldr: A single, imbalance-aware adapter (One-A) for class-incremental learning under step imbalance, using asymmetric subspace alignment, information-adaptive weighting, and directional gating to maintain stability and efficiency, achieving competitive accuracy with low inference cost.
AI.motivation: Step imbalance in class-incremental learning: tasks vary in the number of classes; large tasks dominate learning while small tasks inject unstable updates. This challenges standard CIL methods that assume balanced task streams.
AI.method: One-A incrementally merges all task updates into a single adapter. It uses asymmetric subspace alignment to preserve dominant subspaces from large tasks while restricting low-information updates. An information-adaptive weighting balances contributions between the base and new adapters, and a directional gating mechanism fuses updates along each singular direction to preserve head stability and tail plasticity while keeping a constant inference cost.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks with step-imbalanced streams, One-A achieves competitive accuracy and requires significantly lower inference overhead, demonstrating that a single asymmetrically fused adapter can remain adaptive to varying task sizes and deployment-efficient.
AI.conclusion: A concise, asymmetrically fused single-adapter approach can effectively handle step-imbalanced CIL, balancing stability and plasticity while maintaining deployment efficiency.

title: FAME: Formal Abstract Minimal Explanation for Neural Networks
summary: We propose FAME (Formal Abstract Minimal Explanations), a new class of abductive explanations grounded in abstract interpretation. FAME is the first method to scale to large neural networks while reducing explanation size. Our main contribution is the design of dedicated perturbation domains that eliminate the need for traversal order. FAME progressively shrinks these domains and leverages LiRPA-based bounds to discard irrelevant features, ultimately converging to a formal abstract minimal explanation. To assess explanation quality, we introduce a procedure that measures the worst-case distance between an abstract minimal explanation and a true minimal explanation. This procedure combines adversarial attacks with an optional VERIX+ refinement step. We benchmark FAME against VERIX+ and demonstrate consistent gains in both explanation size and runtime on medium- to large-scale neural networks.

AI.tldr: FAME provides scalable, formal abstract minimal explanations for large neural networks by using dedicated perturbation domains and LiRPA bounds to shrink explanations, surpassing VERIX+ in size and speed.
AI.motivation: Need for scalable, formally grounded, minimal explanations for large networks; existing methods struggle with scalability and produce large explanations.
AI.method: Design perturbation domains that remove traversal order; progressively shrink these domains; leverage LiRPA-based bounds to discard irrelevant features; converge to a formal abstract minimal explanation; assess quality via a procedure that measures worst-case distance to a true minimal explanation using adversarial attacks with optional VERIX+ refinement.
AI.result: Consistent gains in explanation size and runtime on medium- to large-scale networks when benchmarked against VERIX+.
AI.conclusion: FAME enables scalable formal minimal explanations with reduced size and faster runtime while preserving formal guarantees.

title: Degeneracy-Resilient Teach and Repeat for Geometrically Challenging Environments Using FMCW Lidar
summary: Teach and Repeat (T&R) topometric navigation enables robots to autonomously repeat previously traversed paths without relying on GPS, making it well suited for operations in GPS-denied environments such as underground mines and lunar navigation. State-of-the-art T&R systems typically rely on iterative closest point (ICP)-based estimation; however, in geometrically degenerate environments with sparsely structured terrain, ICP often becomes ill-conditioned, resulting in degraded localization and unreliable navigation performance. To address this challenge, we present a degeneracy-resilient Frequency-Modulated Continuous-Wave (FMCW) lidar T&R navigation system consisting of Doppler velocity-based odometry and degeneracy-aware scan-to-map localization. Leveraging FMCW lidar, which provides per-point radial velocity measurements via the Doppler effect, we extend a geometry-independent, correspondence-free motion estimation to include principled pose uncertainty estimation that remains stable in degenerate environments. We further propose a degeneracy-aware localization method that incorporates per-point curvature for improved data association, and unifies translational and rotational scales to enable consistent degeneracy detection. Closed-loop field experiments across three environments with varying structural richness demonstrate that the proposed system reliably completes autonomous navigation, including in a challenging flat airport test field where a conventional ICP-based system fails.

AI.tldr: A degeneracy-resilient Teach-and-Repeat (T&R) navigation system using FMCW lidar with Doppler-based odometry and degeneracy-aware scan-to-map localization to robustly localize and navigate in GPS-denied, geometrically degenerate environments, validated in diverse field tests including flat areas where ICP-based methods fail.
AI.motivation: ICP-based estimation degrades in geometrically degenerative environments with sparsely structured terrain. There is a need for a robust, geometry-independent localization and motion estimation method that can handle degeneracy and provide reliable uncertainty estimates.
AI.method: Use FMCW lidar to obtain per-point radial velocity (Doppler) measurements and develop a geometry-independent, correspondence-free motion estimation with principled pose uncertainty. Introduce degeneracy-aware localization leveraging per-point curvature for improved data association and unify translational/rotational scales to enable robust degeneracy detection. Validate through closed-loop field experiments across environments with varying structural richness, including a flat test field where ICP fails.
AI.result: The system reliably completes autonomous navigation across tested environments, maintaining localization and navigation performance even in degenerate scenarios that defeat ICP-based approaches; demonstrates resiliency in a flat field that challenges conventional methods.
AI.conclusion: Degeneracy-aware FMCW lidar T&R navigation provides robust autonomous operation in GPS-denied, geometrically degenerate environments by exploiting Doppler-based odometry, per-point curvature data association, and unified pose scaling to detect and handle degeneracy, as evidenced by successful field trials.

title: Dissecting Chronos: Sparse Autoencoders Reveal Causal Feature Hierarchies in Time Series Foundation Models
comment: Accepted as a poster in ICLR 2026 Workshop on Time Series in the Age of Large Models (TSALM)
summary: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains, yet their internal representations remain opaque. We present the first application of sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to a TSFM, training TopK SAEs on activations of Chronos-T5-Large (710M parameters) across six layers. Through 392 single-feature ablation experiments, we establish that every ablated feature produces a positive CRPS degradation, confirming causal relevance. Our analysis reveals a depth-dependent hierarchy: early encoder layers encode low-level frequency features, the mid-encoder concentrates causally critical change-detection features, and the final encoder compresses a rich but less causally important taxonomy of temporal concepts. The most critical features reside in the mid-encoder (max single-feature Delta CRPS = 38.61), not in the semantically richest final encoder layer, where progressive ablation paradoxically improves forecast quality. These findings demonstrate that mechanistic interpretability transfers effectively to TSFMs and that Chronos-T5 relies on abrupt-dynamics detection rather than periodic pattern recognition.

AI.tldr: SAEs applied to Chronos-T5-Large activations reveal a depth-wise feature hierarchy in TSFMs: early layers encode low-level frequency features, mid-layers capture change-detection features with strong causal relevance, and final layers are less causally important; ablation degrades performance across features, highlighting interpretability benefits.
AI.motivation: Address the opacity of TsFMs by using sparse autoencoders to interpret layer-wise representations and assess causal relevance of single features via ablations in a large TSFM.
AI.method: Train TopK sparse autoencoders on activations from Chronos-T5-Large (710M parameters) across six layers. Perform 392 single-feature ablations and evaluate via CRPS degradation to assess causal relevance of each feature.
AI.result: Each ablated feature causes positive CRPS degradation, indicating causal relevance. Early encoder encodes low-frequency features; mid-encoder concentrates crucial change-detection features; final encoder compresses rich but less causally important temporal concepts. The maximum single-feature Delta CRPS observed in the mid-encoder is 38.61. Ablation in the final encoder paradoxically improves forecast quality, suggesting diminishing causal utility there.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic interpretability transfers to TSFMs, with Chronos-T5 relying on abrupt-dynamics detection rather than periodic pattern recognition; interpretability reveals a depth-dependent hierarchy of representations.

title: Joint Imaging-ROI Representation Learning via Cross-View Contrastive Alignment for Brain Disorder Classification
summary: Brain imaging classification is commonly approached from two perspectives: modeling the full image volume to capture global anatomical context, or constructing ROI-based graphs to encode localized and topological interactions. Although both representations have demonstrated independent efficacy, their relative contributions and potential complementarity remain insufficiently understood. Existing fusion approaches are typically task-specific and do not enable controlled evaluation of each representation under consistent training settings. To address this gap, we propose a unified cross-view contrastive framework for joint imaging-ROI representation learning. Our method learns subject-level global (imaging) and local (ROI-graph) embeddings and aligns them in a shared latent space using a bidirectional contrastive objective, encouraging representations from the same subject to converge while separating those from different subjects. This alignment produces comparable embeddings suitable for downstream fusion and enables systematic evaluation of imaging-only, ROI-only, and joint configurations within a unified training protocol. Extensive experiments on the ADHD-200 and ABIDE datasets demonstrate that joint learning consistently improves classification performance over either branch alone across multiple backbone choices. Moreover, interpretability analyses reveal that imaging-based and ROI-based branches emphasize distinct yet complementary discriminative patterns, explaining the observed performance gains. These findings provide principled evidence that explicitly integrating global volumetric and ROI-level representations is a promising direction for neuroimaging-based brain disorder classification. The source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/imaging-roi-contrastive-152C/.

AI.tldr: A unified cross-view contrastive learning framework aligns subject-level global imaging and local ROI-graph representations in a shared latent space, enabling controlled evaluation of imaging-only, ROI-only, and joint configurations; joint learning yields consistent classification gains on ADHD-200 and ABIDE across backbones, with complementary interpretability findings.
AI.motivation: To disentangle and compare the contributions of full-volume imaging and ROI-based graphs in brain disorder classification, and to provide a unified training protocol that enables fair, controlled evaluation of each representation and their combination.
AI.method: Introduce a cross-view contrastive framework that learns two subject-level embeddings: a global imaging embedding and a local ROI-graph embedding. A bidirectional contrastive objective aligns representations from the same subject while pushing apart different subjects. The model supports imaging-only, ROI-only, and joint configurations within a unified training pipeline and is evaluated on ADHD-200 and ABIDE using multiple backbone choices.
AI.result: Joint imaging-ROI learning consistently improves classification performance over either branch alone across backbone choices. Interpretability analyses show imaging and ROI branches capture distinct, complementary discriminative patterns, explaining performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly integrating global volumetric and ROI-level representations is a principled and effective direction for neuroimaging-based brain disorder classification. The unified framework enables principled fusion and controlled evaluation of representations, with open-source code available.

title: Emulating Clinician Cognition via Self-Evolving Deep Clinical Research
summary: Clinical diagnosis is a complex cognitive process, grounded in dynamic cue acquisition and continuous expertise accumulation. Yet most current artificial intelligence (AI) systems are misaligned with this reality, treating diagnosis as single-pass retrospective prediction while lacking auditable mechanisms for governed improvement. We developed DxEvolve, a self-evolving diagnostic agent that bridges these gaps through an interactive deep clinical research workflow. The framework autonomously requisitions examinations and continually externalizes clinical experience from increasing encounter exposure as diagnostic cognition primitives. On the MIMIC-CDM benchmark, DxEvolve improved diagnostic accuracy by 11.2% on average over backbone models and reached 90.4% on a reader-study subset, comparable to the clinician reference (88.8%). DxEvolve improved accuracy on an independent external cohort by 10.2% (categories covered by the source cohort) and 17.1% (uncovered categories) compared to the competitive method. By transforming experience into a governable learning asset, DxEvolve supports an accountable pathway for the continual evolution of clinical AI.

AI.tldr: DxEvolve is a self-evolving diagnostic agent that autonomously requisitions tests and externalizes clinical experience into governance-ready learning primitives, achieving notable diagnostic gains and parity with clinicians on a subset, with strong generalization to an external cohort.
AI.motivation: Address the misalignment between current AI diagnostic systems (single-pass, non-auditable) and the dynamic, experience-driven nature of clinical diagnosis, by enabling continual, auditable learning through an interactive clinical research workflow.
AI.method: An interactive deep clinical research workflow where DxEvolve autonomously requests examinations and externalizes encounter-derived experience as diagnostic cognition primitives, enabling continuous, governable learning and iterative improvement.
AI.result: On MIMIC-CDM, average diagnostic accuracy improved by 11.2% over backbone models; 90.4% accuracy on a reader-study subset vs 88.8% clinician reference. External cohort showed 10.2% improvement for covered categories and 17.1% for uncovered categories compared to a competitive method.
AI.conclusion: DxEvolve demonstrates a governance-ready pathway for continual clinical AI evolution, aligning AI learning with clinical practice through auditable, experience-driven updates.

title: From Prior to Pro: Efficient Skill Mastery via Distribution Contractive RL Finetuning
summary: We introduce Distribution Contractive Reinforcement Learning (DICE-RL), a framework that uses reinforcement learning (RL) as a "distribution contraction" operator to refine pretrained generative robot policies. DICE-RL turns a pretrained behavior prior into a high-performing "pro" policy by amplifying high-success behaviors from online feedback. We pretrain a diffusion- or flow-based policy for broad behavioral coverage, then finetune it with a stable, sample-efficient residual off-policy RL framework that combines selective behavior regularization with value-guided action selection. Extensive experiments and analyses show that DICE-RL reliably improves performance with strong stability and sample efficiency. It enables mastery of complex long-horizon manipulation skills directly from high-dimensional pixel inputs, both in simulation and on a real robot. Project website: https://zhanyisun.github.io/dice.rl.2026/.

AI.tldr: DICE-RL uses reinforcement learning as a distribution contraction operator to refine pretrained diffusion/flow-based robot policies into high-performing, data-efficient controllers trained from pixel inputs, applicable in simulation and real hardware.
AI.motivation: Pretrained generative policies offer broad behavioral coverage but often underfit task-specific objectives; a stable, sample-efficient fine-tuning mechanism is needed to leverage online feedback and enable long-horizon manipulation.
AI.method: Pretrain a diffusion- or flow-based policy to achieve broad behavioral coverage. Finetune with a stable, residual off-policy RL framework that combines selective behavior regularization with value-guided action selection, treating RL as a distribution contraction that amplifies high-success behaviors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show improved performance with strong stability and sample efficiency. DICE-RL enables mastering complex long-horizon manipulation from high-dimensional pixel inputs in both simulation and real-robot settings.
AI.conclusion: DICE-RL provides a practical, general framework for refining pretrained generative policies with RL, achieving data-efficient, stable improvement and real-world applicability in challenging robotic tasks.

title: Marginals Before Conditionals
summary: We construct a minimal task that isolates conditional learning in neural networks: a surjective map with K-fold ambiguity, resolved by a selector token z, so H(A | B) = log K while H(A | B, z) = 0. The model learns the marginal P(A | B) first, producing a plateau at exactly log K, before acquiring the full conditional in a sharp, collective transition. The plateau has a clean decomposition: height = log K (set by ambiguity), duration = f(D) (set by dataset size D, not K). Gradient noise stabilizes the marginal solution: higher learning rates monotonically slow the transition (3.6* across a 7* η range at fixed throughput), and batch-size reduction delays escape, consistent with an entropic force opposing departure from the low-gradient marginal. Internally, a selector-routing head assembles during the plateau, leading the loss transition by ~50% of the waiting time. This is the Type 2 directional asymmetry of Papadopoulos et al. [2024], measured dynamically: we track the excess risk from log K to zero and characterize what stabilizes it, what triggers its collapse, and how long it takes.

AI.tldr: A minimal benchmark isolates conditional learning in neural nets: a surjective map with K-fold ambiguity resolved by a selector token, yielding an information-theoretic plateau (H(A|B)=log K) and a subsequent sharp transition to H(A|B,z)=0. The plateau height is fixed by K, while its duration scales with dataset size D. Gradient noise and learning rate control the transition timing; batch-size reductions delay escape; an internal selector-routing head forms during the plateau, driving the loss transition earlier. The work measures a Type 2 directional asymmetry (Papadopoulos et al. 2024) dynamically via excess risk from log K to 0 and analyzes stabilizers, triggers, and timescales.
AI.motivation: To understand how neural networks separate marginal and conditional information representations, quantify the dynamics of conditional learning, and connect information-theoretic measures with optimization dynamics and internal model machinery.
AI.method: Construct a minimal surjective mapping with K-fold ambiguity in A given B, resolved by a selector token z. Compute H(A|B) and H(A|B,z) to verify log K and 0 respectively. Track learning trajectories: plateau in marginal P(A|B) at log K, followed by a collective transition to the full conditional P(A|B,z). Vary dataset size D, learning rate η, and batch size; measure plateau height, duration, transition timing, and the emergence of an internal selector-routing head (and its timing relative to the transition). Dynamically quantify the Type 2 directional asymmetry by monitoring excess risk from log K to 0.
AI.result: Plateau height equals log K; plateau duration scales with dataset size D; transition timing slows with higher η and faster with lower η; gradient noise stabilizes the marginal solution; reducing batch size delays the escape from marginal. An internal selector-routing head forms during the plateau and leads the loss transition about halfway through the waiting time. The authors operationalize a dynamic measure of the Type 2 directional asymmetry by tracking excess risk from log K to zero and characterizing stabilizers, triggers, and timescales.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a two-stage learning dynamic where a marginal, entropy-dominated representation at H(A|B)=log K is learned first and stabilized by gradient noise and data-scale effects, followed by a sharp, collective transition to the true conditional representation H(A|B,z)=0. The phenomenon is governed by dataset size and optimization dynamics rather than ambiguity K alone, with an internal mechanism (selector-routing head) driving the transition. This supports an information-theoretic view of learning and demonstrates a robust, measurable Type 2 directional asymmetry in dynamic learning.

title: A Robust Deep Learning Framework for Bangla License Plate Recognition Using YOLO and Vision-Language OCR
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI and Data Analytics (ICAD 2026). Final version will appear in IEEE Xplore
summary: An Automatic License Plate Recognition (ALPR) system constitutes a crucial element in an intelligent traffic management system. However, the detection of Bangla license plates remains challenging because of the complicated character scheme and uneven layouts. This paper presents a robust Bangla License Plate Recognition system that integrates a deep learning-based object detection model for license plate localization with Optical Character Recognition for text extraction. Multiple object detection architectures, including U-Net and several YOLO (You Only Look Once) variants, are compared for license plate localization. This study proposes a novel two-stage adaptive training strategy built upon the YOLOv8 architecture to improve localization performance. The proposed approach outperforms the established models, achieving an accuracy of 97.83% and an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 91.3%. The text recognition problem is phrased as a sequence generation problem with a VisionEncoderDecoder architecture, with a combination of encoder-decoders evaluated. It was demonstrated that the ViT + BanglaBERT model gives better results at the character level, with a Character Error Rate of 0.1323 and Word Error Rate of 0.1068. The proposed system also shows a consistent performance when tested on an external dataset that has been curated for this study purpose. The dataset offers completely different environment and lighting conditions compared to the training sample, indicating the robustness of the proposed framework. Overall, our proposed system provides a robust and reliable solution for Bangla license plate recognition and performs effectively across diverse real-world scenarios, including variations in lighting, noise, and plate styles. These strengths make it well suited for deployment in intelligent transportation applications such as automated law enforcement and access control.

AI.tldr: A robust Bangla ALPR system combining YOLOv8-based localization with VisionEncoderDecoder OCR, achieving high localization accuracy and robust Bangla text recognition, validated on external datasets.
AI.motivation: Bangla license plates have complex scripts and uneven layouts, requiring a reliable end-to-end ALPR solution for traffic management and enforcement.
AI.method: Compare U-Net and various YOLO variants for plate localization; introduce a two-stage adaptive training strategy on YOLOv8; formulate text recognition as sequence generation using VisionEncoderDecoder architectures; evaluate encoder-decoder pairs, with ViT+BanglaBERT yielding best character-level results.
AI.result: Localization accuracy 97.83% and IoU 91.3% for plate localization; text recognition: Character Error Rate 0.1323, Word Error Rate 0.1068; system demonstrates robustness on an external dataset with differing environment and lighting.
AI.conclusion: A robust Bangla ALPR pipeline combining advanced object detection with BI-based OCR, performant across diverse real-world conditions, suitable for automated law enforcement and access-control deployments.

title: Nurture-First Agent Development: Building Domain-Expert AI Agents Through Conversational Knowledge Crystallization
summary: The emergence of large language model (LLM)-based agent frameworks has shifted the primary challenge in building domain-expert AI agents from raw capability to effective encoding of domain expertise. Two dominant paradigms -- code-first development, which embeds expertise in deterministic pipelines, and prompt-first development, which captures expertise in static system prompts -- both treat agent construction as a discrete engineering phase preceding deployment. We argue that this sequential assumption creates a fundamental mismatch with the nature of domain expertise, which is substantially tacit, deeply personal, and continuously evolving. We propose Nurture-First Development (NFD), a paradigm in which agents are initialized with minimal scaffolding and progressively grown through structured conversational interaction with domain practitioners. The central mechanism is the Knowledge Crystallization Cycle, whereby fragmented knowledge embedded in operational dialogue is periodically consolidated into structured, reusable knowledge assets. We formalize NFD through: (1) a Three-Layer Cognitive Architecture organizing agent knowledge by volatility and personalization degree; (2) the Knowledge Crystallization Cycle with formal definitions of crystallization operations and efficiency metrics; and (3) an operational framework comprising a Dual-Workspace Pattern and Spiral Development Model. We illustrate the paradigm through a detailed case study on building a financial research agent for U.S. equity analysis and discuss the conditions, limitations, and broader implications of NFD for human-agent co-evolution.

AI.tldr: A new paradigm, Nurture-First Development (NFD), enables continual growth of domain-expert AI agents through structured dialogue with practitioners, leveraging a Knowledge Crystallization Cycle and a Three-Layer Cognitive Architecture, plus Dual-Workspace and Spiral Development; demonstrated via a financial research agent case study.
AI.motivation: To address the mismatch between static, pre-engineered pipelines (code-first, prompt-first) and the tacit, evolving nature of domain expertise, by enabling ongoing co-evolution between humans and agents.
AI.method: Proposes Nurture-First Development (NFD) framework comprising: (1) Three-Layer Cognitive Architecture organizing knowledge by volatility and personalization; (2) Knowledge Crystallization Cycle with crystallization operations and efficiency metrics; (3) operational framework with Dual-Workspace Pattern and Spiral Development Model; illustrated with a detailed case study on a U.S. equity financial research agent.
AI.result: A conceptual framework is formalized and illustrated; introduces definitions and metrics, plus a case study. The abstract does not report empirical results.
AI.conclusion: NFD fosters human-agent co-evolution by converting tacit, evolving domain knowledge from dialogue into structured assets, while outlining conditions, limitations, and broader implications for deploying such co-evolving agents.

title: Design of a Robot-Assisted Chemical Dialysis System
comment: Accepted at ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction (HRI'26), Late Breaking Reports 5 pages, 2 figures
summary: Scientists perform diverse manual procedures that are tedious and laborious. Such procedures are considered a bottleneck for modern experimental science, as they consume time and increase burdens in fields including material science and medicine. We employ a user-centered approach to designing a robot-assisted system for dialysis, a common multi-day purification method used in polymer and protein synthesis. Through two usability studies, we obtain participant feedback and revise design requirements to develop the final system that satisfies scientists' needs and has the potential for applications in other experimental workflows. We anticipate that integration of this system into real synthesis procedures in a chemical wet lab will decrease workload on scientists during long experimental procedures and provide an effective approach to designing more systems that have the potential to accelerate scientific discovery and liberate scientists from tedious labor.

AI.tldr: A user-centered, robot-assisted dialysis system for lab workflows (polymer/protein synthesis) is developed and evaluated through two usability studies, aiming to reduce scientist workload and accelerate discovery; the system shows promise for broader experimental applications.
AI.motivation: Manual, multi-day dialysis procedures are tedious bottlenecks in modern experimental science, especially in polymer and protein synthesis, hindering throughput and increasing labor.
AI.method: A user-centered design process to create a robot-assisted dialysis system, followed by two usability studies to gather participant feedback, iteratively revise design requirements, and develop a final system.
AI.result: The final robot-assisted dialysis system meets scientists' needs, demonstrates usable design through two studies, and has the potential to be integrated into real synthesis workflows, reducing workload and enabling broader automation.
AI.conclusion: Integrating robot-assisted dialysis can alleviate long manual labor in wet labs and serve as a model for designing automation to accelerate scientific discovery and liberate researchers from tedious tasks.

title: Stochastic Port-Hamiltonian Neural Networks: Universal Approximation with Passivity Guarantees
summary: Stochastic port-Hamiltonian systems represent open dynamical systems with dissipation, inputs, and stochastic forcing in an energy based form. We introduce stochastic port-Hamiltonian neural networks, SPH-NNs, which parameterize the Hamiltonian with a feedforward network and enforce skew symmetry of the interconnection matrix and positive semidefiniteness of the dissipation matrix. For Itô dynamics we establish a weak passivity inequality in expectation under an explicit generator condition, stated for a stopped process on a compact set. We also prove a universal approximation result showing that, on any compact set and finite horizon, SPH-NNs approximate the coefficients of a target stochastic port-Hamiltonian system with $C^2$ accuracy of the Hamiltonian and yield coupled solutions that remain close in mean square up to the exit time. Experiments on noisy mass spring, Duffing, and Van der Pol oscillators show improved long horizon rollouts and reduced energy error relative to a multilayer perceptron baseline.

AI.tldr: SPH-NNs parameterize the Hamiltonian with a neural network and enforce structural constraints (skew interconnection, PSD dissipation) to model stochastic port-Hamiltonian systems; prove weak passivity under a generator condition for Ito dynamics; establish universal approximation for the Hamiltonian and closeness of trajectories on compact sets with finite horizon; demonstrate improved long-horizon rollouts and lower energy error vs an MLP on noisy oscillator benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Bridge energy-based stochastic modeling and neural networks by enforcing physical structure and providing approximation guarantees for SPH-NNs in stochastic port-Hamiltonian systems.
AI.method: Parameterize Hamiltonian via feedforward neural network; enforce skew-symmetric interconnection and PSD dissipation in the system's interconnection-damping structure; derive weak passivity inequality in expectation for Ito dynamics with a stopped process on a compact set; prove universal approximation: on any compact set and finite horizon, SPH-NNs approximate the target system's Hamiltonian with C^2 accuracy and keep coupled solutions close in mean square up to exit time; validate via experiments on noisy mass-spring, Duffing, and Van der Pol oscillators.
AI.result: Demonstrates a weak passivity bound in expectation under a generator condition; proves universal approximation accuracy (C^2) for the Hamiltonian and mean-square closeness of trajectories up to exit time; empirical results show improved long-horizon rollouts and reduced energy error compared with a multilayer perceptron baseline across several oscillators.
AI.conclusion: SPH-NNs provide physically grounded stochastic modeling with theoretical guarantees and practical gains, highlighting the viability of energy-based neural networks for stochastic control and dynamics with structure-preserving properties.

title: From Imitation to Intuition: Intrinsic Reasoning for Open-Instance Video Classification
summary: Conventional video classification models, acting as effective imitators, excel in scenarios with homogeneous data distributions. However, real-world applications often present an open-instance challenge, where intra-class variations are vast and complex, beyond existing benchmarks. While traditional video encoder models struggle to fit these diverse distributions, vision-language models (VLMs) offer superior generalization but have not fully leveraged their reasoning capabilities (intuition) for such tasks.
