In this paper, we bridge this gap with an intrinsic reasoning framework that evolves open-instance video classification from imitation to intuition. Our approach, namely DeepIntuit, begins with a cold-start supervised alignment to initialize reasoning capability, followed by refinement using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enhance reasoning coherence through reinforcement learning. Crucially, to translate this reasoning into accurate classification, DeepIntuit then introduces an intuitive calibration stage. In this stage, a classifier is trained on this intrinsic reasoning traces generated by the refined VLM, ensuring stable knowledge transfer without distribution mismatch. Extensive experiments demonstrate that for open-instance video classification, DeepIntuit benefits significantly from transcending simple feature imitation and evolving toward intrinsic reasoning. Our project is available at https://bwgzk-keke.github.io/DeepIntuit/.

AI.tldr: Proposes DeepIntuit to enable open-instance video classification by shifting from imitation to intrinsic reasoning in vision-language models, using a cold-start supervised alignment, Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for reasoning refinement, and an intuitive calibration stage to translate intrinsic traces into stable classification.
AI.motivation: Open-instance video classification involves large intra-class variation and distribution drift not well captured by traditional imitation-based encoders. Vision-language models offer better generalization but underutilize their reasoning capabilities for this task. The work aims to harness intrinsic reasoning to improve open-set/open-instance video classification.
AI.method: 1) Cold-start supervised alignment to initialize intrinsic reasoning capability. 2) Refinement via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to improve coherence of reasoning through reinforcement learning, incentivizing robust, consistent reasoning traces. 3) Intuitive calibration: train a classifier on intrinsic reasoning traces produced by the refined VLM to achieve stable knowledge transfer without distribution mismatch.
AI.result: Experiments show significant gains for open-instance video classification when moving beyond simple feature imitation to intrinsic reasoning. DeepIntuit outperforms baselines that rely on conventional encoders and standard imitation, highlighting the value of reasoning-based representations for heterogeneous distributions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating intrinsic reasoning into vision-language models via a cold-start alignment, GRPO-based refinement, and intuitive calibration enables better generalization for open-instance video classification, suggesting a viable direction from imitation to intuition in VLM-based video understanding.

title: A Hybrid Knowledge-Grounded Framework for Safety and Traceability in Prescription Verification
comment: 11 pages, 7 figures.Framework for safe prescription auditing and hybrid knowledge-grounded reasoning
summary: Medication errors pose a significant threat to patient safety, making pharmacist verification (PV) a critical, yet heavily burdened, final safeguard. The direct application of Large Language Models (LLMs) to this zero-tolerance domain is untenable due to their inherent factual unreliability, lack of traceability, and weakness in complex reasoning. To address these challenges, we introduce PharmGraph-Auditor, a novel system designed for safe and evidence-grounded prescription auditing. The core of our system is a trustworthy Hybrid Pharmaceutical Knowledge Base (HPKB), implemented under the Virtual Knowledge Graph (VKG) paradigm. This architecture strategically unifies a relational component for set constraint satisfaction and a graph component for topological reasoning via a rigorous mapping layer. To construct this HPKB, we propose the Iterative Schema Refinement (ISR) algorithm, a framework that enables the co-evolution of both graph and relational schemas from medical texts. For auditing, we introduce the KB-grounded Chain of Verification (CoV), a new reasoning paradigm that transforms the LLM from an unreliable generator into a transparent reasoning engine. CoV decomposes the audit task into a sequence of verifiable queries against the HPKB, generating hybrid query plans to retrieve evidence from the most appropriate data store. Experimental results demonstrate robust knowledge extraction capabilities and show promises of using PharmGraph-Auditor to enable pharmacists to achieve safer and faster prescription verification.

AI.tldr: PharmGraph-Auditor introduces a safe, evidence-grounded prescription-auditing workflow using a Hybrid Pharmaceutical Knowledge Base (HPKB) within a Virtual Knowledge Graph (VKG), refined via Iterative Schema Refinement (ISR) and guided by a KB-grounded Chain of Verification (CoV) to convert LLMs into a transparent reasoning engine.
AI.motivation: To overcome pharmacist verification bottlenecks caused by unreliable factual content, lack of traceability, and weak reasoning in direct LLM usage, by building a trustworthy, explainable auditing system.
AI.method: Develop HPKB using a relational layer for constraint satisfaction and a graph layer for topological reasoning within a VKG framework, with a mapping layer. Use Iterative Schema Refinement (ISR) to co-evolve graph and relational schemas from medical texts. Implement KB-grounded Chain of Verification (CoV) that decomposes auditing into verifiable queries against the HPKB, generating hybrid query plans to fetch evidence from appropriate data stores.
AI.result: Experimental results show robust knowledge extraction abilities and indicate strong potential for enabling pharmacists to perform safer and faster prescription verification.
AI.conclusion: HPKB + CoV framework converts LLMs from unreliable generators into trustworthy, evidence-grounded reasoning engines for prescription auditing, supporting safer and more efficient pharmacist verification.

title: Update-Free On-Policy Steering via Verifiers
summary: In recent years, Behavior Cloning (BC) has become one of the most prevalent methods for enabling robots to mimic human demonstrations. However, despite their successes, BC policies are often brittle and struggle with precise manipulation. To overcome these issues, we propose UF-OPS, an Update-Free On-Policy Steering method that enables the robot to predict the success likelihood of its actions and adapt its strategy at execution time. We accomplish this by training verifier functions using policy rollout data obtained during an initial evaluation of the policy. These verifiers are subsequently used to steer the base policy toward actions with a higher likelihood of success. Our method improves the performance of black-box diffusion policy, without changing the base parameters, making it light-weight and flexible. We present results from both simulation and real-world data and achieve an average 49% improvement in success rate over the base policy across 5 real tasks.

AI.tldr: UF-OPS offers update-free on-policy steering that uses verifier functions trained on rollout data to steer a base policy toward high-success actions, yielding about 49% average improvement in success rate on 5 real tasks, with no changes to base parameters.
AI.motivation: Behavior cloning policies are brittle for precise manipulation; there is a need for an execution-time mechanism to improve success without retraining or altering the base policy parameters.
AI.method: Train verifier functions using policy rollout data from an initial evaluation of the policy. During execution, use these verifiers to steer the base policy toward actions with higher likelihood of success. The approach is update-free (no changes to base parameters) and is lightweight, applicable to black-box diffusion policies, demonstrated in both simulation and real-world data.
AI.result: Average 49% improvement in success rate over the base policy across 5 real tasks, with successful demonstrations on both simulated and real-world setups.
AI.conclusion: Update-free on-policy steering via verifiers can substantially improve manipulation success without modifying the base policy, offering a flexible and lightweight augmentation for diffusion-based policies.

title: Large Spikes in Stochastic Gradient Descent: A Large-Deviations View
summary: We analyse SGD training of a shallow, fully connected network in the NTK scaling and provide a quantitative theory of the catapult phase. We identify an explicit criterion separating two behaviours: When an explicit function $G$, depending only on the kernel, learning rate $η$ and data, is positive, SGD produces large NTK-flattening spikes with high probability; when $G<0$, their probability decays like $(n/η)^{-\vartheta/2}$, for an explicitly characterised $\vartheta\in (0,\infty)$. This yields a concrete parameter-dependent explanation for why such spikes may still be observed at practical widths.

AI.tldr: NTK regime analysis of SGD on a shallow network identifies a kernel- and rate-dependent catapult criterion G that splits dynamics into spike-prone positive-G regime and polynomial-decay negative-G regime; explains spikes at finite width.
AI.motivation: Bridge theoretical NTK predictions with practical observations of NTK-flattening spikes during SGD, and quantify when spikes occur.
AI.method: Derives explicit criterion G depending on the kernel, learning rate η, and data; uses probabilistic analysis of SGD trajectories; analyzes spike probability and decay exponent θ (vartheta).
AI.result: Spikes occur with high probability when G>0; when G<0, spike probability decays like (n/η)^{-vartheta/2}, with θ explicitly characterized.
AI.conclusion: Provides a parameter-dependent explanation for spikes in the NTK scaling, clarifying why spikes can persist at practical widths and guiding choice of hyperparameters and network width.

title: Fuel Gauge: Estimating Chain-of-Thought Length Ahead of Time in Large Multimodal Models
summary: Reasoning Large Multi-modality Models (LMMs) have become the de facto choice for many applications. However, these models rely on a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) process that is lengthy and unpredictable at runtime, often resulting in inefficient use of computational resources (due to memory fragmentation) and sub-optimal accuracy (due to under- and over-thinking). We observe empirically that the CoT process follows a very simple form, whose behavior is independent of the specific generated samples. This suggests that the CoT length can be estimated ahead of time based on a hidden parameter representing the amount of "fuel" available to support the reasoning process. Based on this insight, we propose Fuel Gauge, the first method which extracts this hidden signal and predicts CoT length ahead of time. We demonstrate the utility on the Fuel Gauge on two downstream tasks: predictive KV cache allocation, which addresses memory fragmentation in LMM serving systems, and CoT length modulation, which mitigates under-thinking and over-thinking. Extensive experiments on LMMs across text-only, image-text, and video-text question answering benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness, generalizability, and practical value of our Fuel Gauge. For example, on the GPQA-Diamond benchmark, our Fuel Gauge achieves less than half the CoT length prediction error compared to the baseline; this translates into a 13.37x reduction in the memory allocation frequency.

AI.tldr: Predicts the chain-of-thought (CoT) length with a hidden 'fuel' signal (Fuel Gauge) to pre-allocate memory and modulate reasoning in large multi-modality models (LMMs); improves predictive KV cache allocation and mitigates under-/over-thinking with strong cross-modal results.
AI.motivation: CoT in LMMs is lengthy and resource-inefficient, causing memory fragmentation and potential accuracy issues. The authors observe that CoT follows a simple, sample-independent form, suggesting a computable pre-allocation signal for reasoning budget.
AI.method: Introduce Fuel Gauge to extract a hidden signal representing the available reasoning 'fuel'; predict CoT length before generation; use this prediction to drive predictive KV cache allocation and CoT length modulation; evaluate across text-only, image-text, and video-text benchmarks, including GPQA-Diamond.
AI.result: CoT length prediction error is reduced by more than 2× (less than half of the baseline error); achieves a 13.37× reduction in memory allocation frequency; demonstrates generalizability and practical value across modalities with strong empirical results on GPQA-Diamond and other benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Fuel Gauge provides a practical, generalizable mechanism to estimate and control CoT length in LMMs, enabling efficient resource management and consistent reasoning depth across tasks.

title: Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) significantly enhances large language models (LLMs) reasoning but severely suffers from calibration degeneration, where models become excessively over-confident in incorrect answers. Previous studies devote to directly incorporating calibration objective into existing optimization target. However, our theoretical analysis demonstrates that there exists a fundamental gradient conflict between the optimization for maximizing policy accuracy and minimizing calibration error. Building on this insight, we propose DCPO, a simple yet effective framework that systematically decouples reasoning and calibration objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DCPO not only preserves accuracy on par with GRPO but also achieves the best calibration performance and substantially mitigates the over-confidence issue. Our study provides valuable insights and practical solution for more reliable LLM deployment.

AI.tldr: DCPO decouples reasoning and calibration in RL-based LLM training to improve calibration without sacrificing accuracy, addressing gradient conflicts between policy optimization and calibration objectives.
AI.motivation: RLVR improves LLM reasoning but suffers from calibration degeneration (over-confidence). There is a fundamental gradient conflict between maximizing policy accuracy and minimizing calibration error, motivating a decoupled training approach.
AI.method: Propose DCPO, a framework that explicitly decouples reasoning and calibration objectives during training, avoiding conflicting gradients and preserving performance. Evaluate against GRPO via extensive experiments.
AI.result: DCPO achieves state-of-the-art calibration while maintaining accuracy comparable to GRPO, and substantially mitigates over-confidence across experiments.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling reasoning and calibration objectives via DCPO provides a practical and reliable solution for RL-based LLM deployment, offering insights into objective alignment and gradient dynamics.

title: SteadyTray: Learning Object Balancing Tasks in Humanoid Tray Transport via Residual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Stabilizing unsecured payloads against the inherent oscillations of dynamic bipedal locomotion remains a critical engineering bottleneck for humanoids in unstructured environments. To solve this, we introduce ReST-RL, a hierarchical reinforcement learning architecture that explicitly decouples locomotion from payload stabilization, evaluated via the SteadyTray benchmark. Rather than relying on monolithic end-to-end learning, our framework integrates a robust base locomotion policy with a dynamic residual module engineered to actively cancel gait-induced perturbations at the end-effector. This architectural separation ensures steady tray transport without degrading the underlying bipedal stability. In simulation, the residual design significantly outperforms end-to-end baselines in gait smoothness and orientation accuracy, achieving a 96.9% success rate in variable velocity tracking and 74.5% robustness against external force disturbances. Successfully deployed on the Unitree G1 humanoid hardware, this modular approach demonstrates highly reliable zero-shot sim-to-real generalization across various objects and external force disturbances.

AI.tldr: Proposes ReST-RL, a hierarchical RL approach that decouples bipedal locomotion from payload stabilization using a dynamic residual module, achieving robust payload transport and high sim-to-real transfer on Unitree G1.
AI.motivation: Stabilizing payloads on dynamically oscillating bipeds is a core challenge in unstructured environments; existing monolithic end-to-end policies often struggle to separate locomotion stability from payload control, hindering reliability and generalization.
AI.method: A hierarchical RL architecture that integrates a robust base locomotion policy with a dynamic residual module to actively cancel gait-induced end-effector perturbations; evaluated on SteadyTray benchmark with zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.
AI.result: In simulation, the residual module outperforms end-to-end baselines in gait smoothness and orientation accuracy; 96.9% success in variable velocity tracking; 74.5% robustness to external disturbances; deployed on Unitree G1 demonstrating strong sim-to-real generalization across objects and disturbances.
AI.conclusion: Modular decoupling of locomotion and payload stabilization via a residual control pathway yields reliable payload transport without sacrificing bipedal stability, with strong sim-to-real transfer on hardware.

title: Digging Deeper: Learning Multi-Level Concept Hierarchies
comment: Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Principled Design for Trustworthy AI
summary: Although concept-based models promise interpretability by explaining predictions with human-understandable concepts, they typically rely on exhaustive annotations and treat concepts as flat and independent. To circumvent this, recent work has introduced Hierarchical Concept Embedding Models (HiCEMs) to explicitly model concept relationships, and Concept Splitting to discover sub-concepts using only coarse annotations. However, both HiCEMs and Concept Splitting are restricted to shallow hierarchies. We overcome this limitation with Multi-Level Concept Splitting (MLCS), which discovers multi-level concept hierarchies from only top-level supervision, and Deep-HiCEMs, an architecture that represents these discovered hierarchies and enables interventions at multiple levels of abstraction. Experiments across multiple datasets show that MLCS discovers human-interpretable concepts absent during training and that Deep-HiCEMs maintain high accuracy while supporting test-time concept interventions that can improve task performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes Multi-Level Concept Splitting (MLCS) and Deep-HiCEMs to learn and utilize multi-level concept hierarchies from coarse supervision, enabling interpretable interventions without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Limitations of existing concept-based models: rely on exhaustive annotations and flat, independent concepts; HiCEMs and Concept Splitting handle shallow hierarchies and require more supervision. There is a need to discover multi-level concepts with only top-level labels to improve interpretability and controllability.
AI.method: Introduce MLCS to induce multi-level concept hierarchies from top-level supervision; present Deep-HiCEMs architecture to model and intervene at multiple abstraction levels; evaluate across datasets showing discovered concepts without training labels and that interventions can improve task performance while maintaining accuracy.
AI.result: MLCS discovers human-interpretable concepts not present in training data; Deep-HiCEMs achieve high accuracy and allow test-time concept interventions that can boost performance.
AI.conclusion: The approach advances interpretability and controllability in concept-based models by enabling hierarchical, multi-level concepts learned with minimal supervision, with practical benefits in accuracy and intervention capabilities.

title: Overcoming Visual Clutter in Vision Language Action Models via Concept-Gated Visual Distillation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models demonstrate impressive zero-shot generalization but frequently suffer from a "Precision-Reasoning Gap" in cluttered environments. This failure is driven by background-induced feature dilution, where high-frequency semantic noise corrupts the geometric grounding required for precise manipulation. To bridge this gap, we propose Concept-Gated Visual Distillation (CGVD), a training-free, model-agnostic inference framework that stabilizes VLA policies. CGVD operates by parsing instructions into safe and distractor sets, utilizing a two-layer target refinement process--combining cross-validation and spatial disambiguation--to explicitly penalize false positives and isolate genuine manipulation targets. We then process the scene via Fourier-based inpainting, generating a clean observation that actively suppresses semantic distractors while preserving critical spatial geometry and visual proprioception. Extensive evaluations in highly cluttered manipulation tasks demonstrate that CGVD prevents performance collapse. In environments with dense semantic distractors, our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 77.5% success rate compared to the baseline's 43.0%. By enforcing strict attribute adherence, CGVD establishes inference-time visual distillation as a critical prerequisite for robust robotic manipulation in the clutter.

AI.tldr: Training-free, model-agnostic inference-time visual distillation for Vision-Language-Action models in cluttered environments. CGVD parses instructions, refines targets, and uses Fourier-based inpainting to suppress distractors; yields robust manipulation with 77.5% success vs. 43.0% baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the Precision-Reasoning Gap in VLA models caused by background-induced feature dilution and semantic noise that disrupts geometric grounding for manipulation in cluttered scenes.
AI.method: Concept-Gated Visual Distillation (CGVD) parses instructions into safe and distractor sets, applies a two-layer target refinement (cross-validation and spatial disambiguation) to penalize false positives, then performs Fourier-based inpainting on the scene to generate a clean observation that suppresses distractors while preserving spatial geometry and proprioception. The approach is training-free and model-agnostic at inference time.
AI.result: In highly cluttered manipulation tasks, CGVD prevents performance collapse and significantly outperforms baselines in dense distractor settings, achieving 77.5% success versus 43.0% for the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time visual distillation via CGVD is a critical prerequisite for robust robotic manipulation in clutter, stabilizing VLA policies without retraining.

title: PC-Diffuser: Path-Consistent Capsule CBF Safety Filtering for Diffusion-Based Trajectory Planner
summary: Autonomous driving in complex traffic requires planners that generalize beyond hand-crafted rules, motivating data-driven approaches that learn behavior from expert demonstrations. Diffusion-based trajectory planners have recently shown strong closed-loop performance by iteratively denoising a full-horizon plan, but they remain difficult to certify and can fail catastrophically in rare or out-of-distribution scenarios. To address this challenge, we present PC-Diffuser, a safety augmentation framework that embeds a certifiable, path-consistent barrier-function structure directly into the denoising loop of diffusion planning. The key idea is to make safety an intrinsic part of trajectory generation rather than a post-hoc fix: we enforce forward invariance along the rollout while preserving the diffusion model's intended path geometry. Specifically, PC-Diffuser (i) evaluates collision risk using a capsule-distance barrier function that better reflects vehicle geometry and reduces unnecessary conservativeness, (ii) converts denoised waypoints into dynamically feasible motion under a kinematic bicycle model, and (iii) applies a path-consistent safety filter that eliminates residual constraint violations without geometric distortion, so the corrected plan remains close to the learned distribution. By injecting these safety-consistent corrections at every denoising step and feeding the refined trajectory back into the diffusion process, PC-Diffuser enables iterative, context-aware safeguarding instead of post-hoc repair...

AI.tldr: Integrates a path-consistent barrier function into diffusion-based trajectory planning to enforce safety intrinsically, using a capsule-distance metric, kinematic feasibility, and a path-consistent safety filter, enabling iterative safeguarding within the denoising loop.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based planners achieve strong closed-loop performance but lack certifiable safety guarantees and can fail in rare or out-of-distribution scenarios. Embedding safety into the generation process aims to provide intrinsic safety without post-hoc repair.
AI.method: Introduce a capsule-distance collision barrier function to reflect vehicle geometry, convert denoised waypoints into dynamically feasible motion via a kinematic bicycle model, and apply a path-consistent safety filter that removes residual violations without distorting the learned trajectory manifold. These corrections are injected at every denoising step and fed back into the diffusion process, enabling iterative, context-aware safeguarding.
AI.result: A conceptual safety augmentation framework (PC-Diffuser) that enforces forward invariance and preserves path geometry within diffusion planning, reducing conservativeness relative to crude post-hoc fixes and enabling safety-conscious trajectory generation throughout the denoising iterations.
AI.conclusion: PC-Diffuser offers intrinsic safety during diffusion-based planning by integrating geometry-aware collision checks, dynamic feasibility, and path-consistent corrections into the planning loop, with potential improvements in reliability. Future work should validate empirically across scenarios and assess trade-offs between safety, realism, and computation.

title: KernelSkill: A Multi-Agent Framework for GPU Kernel Optimization
summary: Improving GPU kernel efficiency is crucial for advancing AI systems. Recent work has explored leveraging large language models (LLMs) for GPU kernel generation and optimization. However, existing LLM-based kernel optimization pipelines typically rely on opaque, implicitly learned heuristics within the LLMs to determine optimization strategies. This leads to inefficient trial-and-error and weakly interpretable optimizations. Our key insight is to replace implicit heuristics with expert optimization skills that are knowledge-driven and aware of task trajectories. Specifically, we present KernelSkill, a multi-agent framework with a dual-level memory architecture. KernelSkill operates by coordinating agents with long-term memory of reusable expert skills and short-term memory to prevent repetitive backtracking. On KernelBench Levels 1-3, KernelSkill achieves a 100% success rate and average speedups of 5.44x, 2.82x, and 1.92x over Torch Eager on Levels 1, 2, and 3, respectively, outperforming prior baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/0satan0/KernelMem/.

AI.tldr: KernelSkill introduces a dual-memory, multi-agent framework that replaces opaque LLM heuristics with reusable expert optimization skills for GPU kernel development, achieving state-level speedups on KernelBench.
AI.motivation: LLM-driven kernel optimization relies on opaque heuristics leading to inefficient search and poor interpretability; need interpretable, knowledge-driven strategies that track task trajectories.
AI.method: A dual-level memory architecture (long-term memory of expert skills; short-term memory to prevent backtracking) guiding coordinated agents to optimize GPU kernels; evaluated on KernelBench Levels 1-3; code available.
AI.result: 100% success across Levels 1-3; average speedups of 5.44x, 2.82x, 1.92x over Torch Eager; outperforms prior baselines.
AI.conclusion: Expert-skill, memory-augmented optimization improves efficiency and interpretability in LLM-assisted GPU kernel optimization; demonstrates strong generalization to multiple benchmark levels and provides open-source implementation.

title: EmoStory: Emotion-Aware Story Generation
summary: Story generation aims to produce image sequences that depict coherent narratives while maintaining subject consistency across frames. Although existing methods have excelled in producing coherent and expressive stories, they remain largely emotion-neutral, focusing on what subject appears in a story while overlooking how emotions shape narrative interpretation and visual presentation. As stories are intended to engage audiences emotionally, we introduce emotion-aware story generation, a new task that aims to generate subject-consistent visual stories with explicit emotional directions. This task is challenging due to the abstract nature of emotions, which must be grounded in concrete visual elements and consistently expressed across a narrative through visual composition. To address these challenges, we propose EmoStory, a two-stage framework that integrates agent-based story planning and region-aware story generation. The planning stage transforms target emotions into coherent story prompts with emotion agent and writer agent, while the generation stage preserves subject consistency and injects emotion-related elements through region-aware composition. We evaluate EmoStory on a newly constructed dataset covering 25 subjects and 600 emotional stories. Extensive quantitative and qualitative results, along with user studies, show that EmoStory outperforms state-of-the-art story generation methods in emotion accuracy, prompt alignment, and subject consistency.

AI.tldr: Introduces EmoStory, a two-stage emotion-aware story generation framework that ensures subject consistency across frames while embedding explicit emotional directions, validated on a new dataset.
AI.motivation: Current story generation methods produce emotion-neutral outputs and struggle to ground abstract emotions in concrete visuals and maintain consistency across a narrative, limiting emotional engagement.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) emotion-driven planning with an emotion agent and a writer agent to convert target emotions into coherent story prompts, and (2) region-aware visual generation that preserves subject consistency while injecting emotion-related elements through region-aware composition.
AI.result: EmoStory outperforms state-of-the-art methods in emotion accuracy, prompt alignment, and subject consistency, as demonstrated on a new dataset of 25 subjects and 600 emotional stories, with extensive quantitative/qualitative results and user studies.
AI.conclusion: Integrating agent-based planning with region-aware generation effectively grounds emotions in visual narratives, enabling emotion-aware, subject-consistent story generation and highlighting the value of planning-driven prompts for emotion expression in visual storytelling.

title: Adaptive Manipulation Potential and Haptic Estimation for Tool-Mediated Interaction
summary: Achieving human-level dexterity in contact-rich, tool-mediated manipulation remains a significant challenge due to visual occlusion and the underdetermined nature of haptic sensing. This paper introduces a parameterized Equilibrium Manifold (EM) as a unified representation for tool-mediated interaction, and develops a closed-loop framework that integrates haptic estimation, online planning, and adaptive stiffness control. We establish a physical-geometric duality using an adaptive manipulation potential incorporating a differentiable contact model, which induces the manifold's geometric structure and ensures that complex physical interactions are encapsulated as continuous operations on the EM. Within this framework, we reformulate haptic estimation as a manifold parameter estimation problem. Specifically, a hybrid inference strategy (haptic SLAM) is employed in which discrete object shapes are classified via particle filtering, while the continuous object pose is estimated using analytical gradients for efficient optimization. By continuously updating the parameters of the manipulation potential, the framework dynamically reshapes the induced EM to guide online trajectory replanning and implement uncertainty-aware impedance control, thereby closing the perception-action loop. The system is validated through simulation and over 260 real-world screw-loosening trials. Experimental results demonstrate robust identification and manipulation success in standard scenarios while maintaining accurate tracking. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm that haptic SLAM and uncertainty-aware stiffness modulation outperform fixed impedance baselines, effectively preventing jamming during tight tolerance interactions.

AI.tldr: Introduces Equilibrium Manifold (EM) as a unified representation for tool-mediated interaction, with a closed-loop framework combining haptic estimation, online planning, and adaptive stiffness control. Uses a physical-geometric duality via a differentiable manipulation potential; employs a hybrid haptic SLAM for discrete shapes (particle filtering) and continuous pose (analytical gradients). Demonstrates robust real-world screw-loosening manipulation and outperforms fixed-impedance baselines, with ablations confirming the benefits of uncertainty-aware stiffness.
AI.motivation: Tackle dexterous, tool-mediated manipulation under visual occlusion and underdetermined haptics by proposing a unified geometric-physical representation and a closed-loop perception-action pipeline.
AI.method: Define a parameterized Equilibrium Manifold (EM) as the interaction substrate; build an adaptive manipulation potential with a differentiable contact model to induce the EM's geometry. Reformulate haptic estimation as manifold parameter estimation. Implement haptic SLAM: discrete object shapes classified via particle filtering; continuous pose estimated with analytical gradients. Continuously update manipulation potential to reshape EM for online trajectory replanning and uncertainty-aware impedance control, closing the loop.
AI.result: Validated in simulation and over 260 real-world screw-loosening trials. Demonstrates robust object identification and manipulation in standard scenarios with accurate tracking. Ablation shows haptic SLAM and uncertainty-aware stiffness modulation outperform fixed impedance baselines and help prevent jamming in tight tolerances.
AI.conclusion: The EM framework provides a unified, differentiable geometric-physical basis for tool-mediated interaction, enabling continuous operations on the manifold and effective perception-action coupling, with demonstrated practical efficacy in complex manipulation tasks.

title: ES-dLLM: Efficient Inference for Diffusion Large Language Models by Early-Skipping
summary: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) are emerging as a promising alternative to autoregressive models (ARMs) due to their ability to capture bidirectional context and the potential for parallel generation. Despite the advantages, dLLM inference remains computationally expensive as the full input context is processed at every iteration. In this work, we analyze the generation dynamics of dLLMs and find that intermediate representations, including key, value, and hidden states, change only subtly across successive iterations. Leveraging this insight, we propose \textbf{ES-dLLM}, a training-free inference acceleration framework for dLLM that reduces computation by skipping tokens in early layers based on the estimated importance. Token importance is computed with intermediate tensor variation and confidence scores of previous iterations. Experiments on LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B demonstrate that ES-dLLM achieves throughput of up to 226.57 and 308.51 tokens per second (TPS), respectively, on an NVIDIA H200 GPU, delivering 5.6$\times$ to 16.8$\times$ speedup over the vanilla implementation and up to 1.85$\times$ over the state-of-the-art caching method, while preserving generation quality.

AI.tldr: ES-dLLM is a training-free inference acceleration method for diffusion LLMs that skips tokens in early layers based on estimated importance from intermediate tensor variation and prior iteration confidence, achieving large throughput gains while preserving generation quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs process full input context at each iteration, making inference computationally expensive; identifying and exploiting stability of intermediate representations across successive iterations can reduce redundant computation.
AI.method: Propose ES-dLLM, a training-free framework that estimates token importance from intermediate tensor variation and confidence scores from previous iterations, then skips tokens in early layers accordingly during inference.
AI.result: On LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, ES-dLLM achieves 226.57 and 308.51 tokens per second on an NVIDIA H200, delivering 5.6x to 16.8x speedups over vanilla diffusion LLMs and up to 1.85x over a state-of-the-art caching method, while preserving generation quality.
AI.conclusion: ES-dLLM demonstrates that diffusion LLM inference can be substantially accelerated without training by leveraging the relatively stable intermediate representations across iterations to skip computations, offering significant practical gains with maintained quality.

title: StyleGallery: Training-free and Semantic-aware Personalized Style Transfer from Arbitrary Image References
comment: 10 pages, 23 figures, Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026
summary: Despite the advancements in diffusion-based image style transfer, existing methods are commonly limited by 1) semantic gap: the style reference could miss proper content semantics, causing uncontrollable stylization; 2) reliance on extra constraints (e.g., semantic masks) restricting applicability; 3) rigid feature associations lacking adaptive global-local alignment, failing to balance fine-grained stylization and global content preservation. These limitations, particularly the inability to flexibly leverage style inputs, fundamentally restrict style transfer in terms of personalization, accuracy, and adaptability. To address these, we propose StyleGallery, a training-free and semantic-aware framework that supports arbitrary reference images as input and enables effective personalized customization. It comprises three core stages: semantic region segmentation (adaptive clustering on latent diffusion features to divide regions without extra inputs); clustered region matching (block filtering on extracted features for precise alignment); and style transfer optimization (energy function-guided diffusion sampling with regional style loss to optimize stylization). Experiments on our introduced benchmark demonstrate that StyleGallery outperforms state-of-the-art methods in content structure preservation, regional stylization, interpretability, and personalized customization, particularly when leveraging multiple style references.

AI.tldr: StyleGallery is a training-free, semantic-aware diffusion-based style transfer framework that accepts arbitrary style references. It uses (1) adaptive latent-diffusion feature clustering for semantic region segmentation, (2) block-filtered regional feature matching for precise alignment, and (3) energy-guided diffusion sampling with regional style loss to optimize stylization, achieving improved content preservation, regional stylization, interpretability, and personalization, especially with multiple references.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based style transfer methods struggle with semantic gaps between content and style, rely on extra constraints like masks, and lack adaptive global-local alignment, limiting personalization and accuracy. A training-free, semantic-aware approach that can leverage arbitrary style inputs aims to broaden applicability and improve controllability.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) semantic region segmentation via adaptive clustering on latent diffusion features to partition content without extra inputs; (2) clustered region matching using block filtering on extracted features for precise region alignment; (3) style transfer optimization through energy-function-guided diffusion sampling incorporating regional style loss to drive stylization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on a newly introduced benchmark shows StyleGallery surpasses state-of-the-art methods in preserving content structure, achieving regional stylization, interpretability, and personalized customization, particularly when using multiple style references.
AI.conclusion: StyleGallery provides a training-free, semantic-aware framework that flexibly handles arbitrary style references, enabling better personalization, accuracy, and adaptability in diffusion-based style transfer; its multi-stage design yields improved content preservation and region-specific stylization compared to existing methods.

title: Few-Shot Adaptation to Non-Stationary Environments via Latent Trend Embedding for Robotics
summary: Robotic systems operating in real-world environments often suffer from concept shift, where the input-output relationship changes due to latent environmental factors that are not directly observable. Conventional adaptation methods update model parameters, which may cause catastrophic forgetting and incur high computational cost. This paper proposes a latent Trend ID-based framework for few-shot adaptation in non-stationary environments. Instead of modifying model weights, a low-dimensional environmental state, referred to as the Trend ID, is estimated via backpropagation while the model parameters remain fixed. To prevent overfitting caused by per-sample latent variables, we introduce temporal regularization and a state transition model that enforces smooth evolution of the latent space. Experiments on a quantitative food grasping task demonstrate that the learned Trend IDs are distributed across distinct regions of the latent space with temporally consistent trajectories, and that few-shot adaptation to unseen environments is achieved without modifying model parameters. The proposed framework provides a scalable and interpretable solution for robotics applications operating across diverse and evolving environments.

AI.tldr: A lightweight latent-state adaptation framework that uses Trend IDs to adapt in non-stationary environments without updating model weights, enabling few-shot adaptation.
AI.motivation: Robotics face concept shift and catastrophic forgetting; updating weights is costly. A low-dimensional, interpretable latent representation can adapt to environment changes with reduced computation.
AI.method: Keep network weights fixed and learn a low-dimensional Trend ID via backpropagation for each input; apply temporal regularization and a state-transition model to ensure smooth latent evolution across time.
AI.result: Trend IDs cluster in distinct latent regions with temporally coherent trajectories; enabling rapid adaptation to unseen environments with minimal data and no weight updates; demonstrated on a quantitative food grasping task.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a scalable, interpretable framework for robust robotics adaptation across evolving environments by leveraging latent trend states instead of weight updates.

title: A Survey of Weight Space Learning: Understanding, Representation, and Generation
summary: Neural network weights are typically viewed as the end product of training, while most deep learning research focuses on data, features, and architectures. However, recent advances show that the set of all possible weight values (weight space) itself contains rich structure: pretrained models form organized distributions, exhibit symmetries, and can be embedded, compared, or even generated. Understanding such structures has tremendous impact on how neural networks are analyzed and compared, and on how knowledge is transferred across models, beyond individual training instances. This emerging research direction, which we refer to as Weight Space Learning (WSL), treats neural weights as a meaningful domain for analysis and modeling. This survey provides the first unified taxonomy of WSL. We categorize existing methods into three core dimensions: Weight Space Understanding (WSU), which studies the geometry and symmetries of weights; Weight Space Representation (WSR), which learns embeddings over model weights; and Weight Space Generation (WSG), which synthesizes new weights through hypernetworks or generative models. We further show how these developments enable practical applications, including model retrieval, continual and federated learning, neural architecture search, and data-free reconstruction. By consolidating fragmented progress under a coherent framework, this survey highlights weight space as a learnable, structured domain with growing impact across model analysis, transferring, and weight generation. We release an accompanying resource at https://github.com/Zehong-Wang/Awesome-Weight-Space-Learning.

AI.tldr: A survey introducing Weight Space Learning (WSL), a unified taxonomy for analyzing neural network weights as a structured domain, with three pillars: Weight Space Understanding (WSU), Weight Space Representation (WSR), and Weight Space Generation (WSG). It surveys methods, discusses applications (model retrieval, continual/federated learning, NAS, data-free reconstruction), and provides a public resource, arguing weight space insights can enhance model analysis, transfer, and generation.
AI.motivation: Neural network weights, often treated as byproducts of training, exhibit rich structure in the weight space itself. Understanding this structure can enable new analysis, comparison, transfer, and generation across models, beyond individual training runs.
AI.method: A comprehensive survey that categorizes existing work into three core dimensions (WSU, WSR, WSG), synthesizes a unified framework, and discusses practical applications. It also provides a public resource repository for weight-space learning research.
AI.result: A unified taxonomy and framework for Weight Space Learning, consolidating fragmented work into a coherent perspective and highlighting actionable applications across model analysis, transfer, and weight generation.
AI.conclusion: Weight space is a learnable, structured domain with growing impact; the survey outlines a roadmap for further research in analyzing, representing, and generating neural network weights and transferring knowledge across models.

title: One Token, Two Fates: A Unified Framework via Vision Token Manipulation Against MLLMs Hallucination
summary: Current training-free methods tackle MLLM hallucination with separate strategies: either enhancing visual signals or suppressing text inertia. However, these separate methods are insufficient due to critical trade-offs: simply enhancing vision often fails against strong language prior, while suppressing language can introduce extra image-irrelevant noise. Moreover, we find their naive combination is also ineffective, necessitating a unified framework. We propose such a framework by focusing on the core asset: the vision token. Our design leverages two key insights: (1) augmented images offer complementary visual semantics, and (2) removing vision tokens (information-gap) isolates hallucination tendencies more precisely than distorting images (modality-gap). Based on these, our framework uses vision tokens in two distinct ways, both operating on latent representations: our Synergistic Visual Calibration (SVC) module incorporates augmented tokens to strengthen visual representations, while our Causal Representation Calibration (CRC) module uses pruned tokens to create latent-space negative samples for correcting internal model biases. By harmonizing these two roles, our framework effectively restores the vision-language balance, significantly reducing object hallucinations, improving POPE accuracy by an average of 2% absolute on LLaVA-1.5 across multiple benchmarks with only a 1.06x inference latency overhead.

AI.tldr: A training-free, unified vision-token framework (SVC and CRC) to mitigate MLLM hallucination; improves POPE by ~2% absolute on LLaVA-1.5 with 1.06x latency.
AI.motivation: MLLMs exhibit object hallucination driven by strong language priors and weak visual signals. Existing training-free approaches either enhance vision or suppress text inertia, but each option incurs trade-offs and naive combinations fail. A unified framework centered on vision tokens is proposed to balance signals and biases.
AI.method: Introduce two latent-representation modules: (1) Synergistic Visual Calibration (SVC) that augments vision tokens to enrich visual semantics, and (2) Causal Representation Calibration (CRC) that prunes vision tokens to form latent negative samples, enabling bias correction. The approach leverages augmented tokens and information-gap via token-level manipulation rather than image distortion, yielding two complementary pathways to reduce hallucination.
AI.result: Significant reduction in object hallucinations and an average POPE accuracy improvement of 2% absolute on LLaVA-1.5 across multiple benchmarks, with only a 1.06x increase in inference latency.
AI.conclusion: A token-centric, two-module framework harmonizes visual and language signals in MLLMs, providing an effective, training-free solution to object hallucination and improving evaluation metrics with modest latency overhead.

title: ScanDP: Generalizable 3D Scanning with Diffusion Policy
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures, 5 tables. Project Page: https://treeitsuki.github.io/ScanDP/
summary: Learning-based 3D Scanning plays a crucial role in enabling efficient and accurate scanning of target objects. However, recent reinforcement learning-based methods often require large-scale training data and still struggle to generalize to unseen object categories.In this work, we propose a data-efficient 3D scanning framework that uses Diffusion Policy to imitate human-like scanning strategies. To enhance robustness and generalization, we adopt the Occupancy Grid Mapping instead of direct point cloud processing, offering improved noise resilience and handling of diverse object geometries. We also introduce a hybrid approach combining a sphere-based space representation with a path optimization procedure that ensures path safety and scanning efficiency. This approach addresses limitations in conventional imitation learning, such as redundant or unpredictable behavior. We evaluate our method on diverse unseen objects in both shape and scale. Ours achieves higher coverage and shorter paths than baselines, while remaining robust to sensor noise. We further confirm practical feasibility and stable operation in real-world execution.

AI.tldr: Proposes a data-efficient 3D scanning framework that imitates human-like scanning via diffusion policy, uses occupancy grid mapping, and a sphere-based space representation with path optimization to improve coverage, efficiency, and robustness on unseen objects.
AI.motivation: Existing reinforcement learning-based 3D scanning approaches require large training datasets and struggle to generalize to unseen object categories; there is a need for data-efficient, robust scanning strategies.
AI.method: Employ a diffusion policy to imitate human-like scanning; adopt occupancy grid mapping for noise-robust 3D representation; integrate a hybrid sphere-based space representation with a path optimization procedure to ensure safe and efficient scanning.
AI.result: The method achieves higher coverage and shorter scanning paths than baselines on unseen objects with varying shapes and scales; demonstrates robustness to sensor noise and practical feasibility in real-world execution.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers data-efficient, robust 3D scanning with potential to generalize to unseen object classes and operate effectively in real-world conditions.

title: Equivariant Asynchronous Diffusion: An Adaptive Denoising Schedule for Accelerated Molecular Conformation Generation
summary: Recent 3D molecular generation methods primarily use asynchronous auto-regressive or synchronous diffusion models. While auto-regressive models build molecules sequentially, they're limited by a short horizon and a discrepancy between training and inference. Conversely, synchronous diffusion models denoise all atoms at once, offering a molecule-level horizon but failing to capture the causal relationships inherent in hierarchical molecular structures. We introduce Equivariant Asynchronous Diffusion (EAD) to overcome these limitations. EAD is a novel diffusion model that combines the strengths of both approaches: it uses an asynchronous denoising schedule to better capture molecular hierarchy while maintaining a molecule-level horizon. Since these relationships are often complex, we propose a dynamic scheduling mechanism to adaptively determine the denoising timestep. Experimental results show that EAD achieves state-of-the-art performance in 3D molecular generation.

AI.tldr: Introduces Equivariant Asynchronous Diffusion (EAD), a diffusion model with asynchronous denoising and dynamic scheduling for 3D molecular generation, claiming state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of purely autoregressive (limited horizon, training/inference mismatch) and purely synchronous diffusion (loss of causal hierarchical structure) methods by leveraging hierarchy-aware asynchronous diffusion with molecule-level horizon.
AI.method: Develop an equivariant diffusion framework with asynchronous denoising steps and a dynamic scheduler that adaptively selects denoising timesteps to reflect molecular hierarchy; maintain 3D geometric equivariance (SE(3)/E(3)); integrate hierarchical causal relationships into the denoising process.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on 3D molecular generation tasks; improved modeling of hierarchical dependencies; empirical evidence from experiments supporting dynamic scheduling effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: EAD effectively combines autoregressive and diffusion strengths for 3D molecular generation and introduces a dynamic denoising schedule that adapts to hierarchy, achieving strong empirical performance.

title: Geometric Autoencoder for Diffusion Models
comment: Code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/freezing-index/Geometric-Autoencoder-for-Diffusion-Models
summary: Latent diffusion models have established a new state-of-the-art in high-resolution visual generation. Integrating Vision Foundation Model priors improves generative efficiency, yet existing latent designs remain largely heuristic. These approaches often struggle to unify semantic discriminability, reconstruction fidelity, and latent compactness. In this paper, we propose Geometric Autoencoder (GAE), a principled framework that systematically addresses these challenges. By analyzing various alignment paradigms, GAE constructs an optimized low-dimensional semantic supervision target from VFMs to provide guidance for the autoencoder. Furthermore, we leverage latent normalization that replaces the restrictive KL-divergence of standard VAEs, enabling a more stable latent manifold specifically optimized for diffusion learning. To ensure robust reconstruction under high-intensity noise, GAE incorporates a dynamic noise sampling mechanism. Empirically, GAE achieves compelling performance on the ImageNet-1K $256 \times 256$ benchmark, reaching a gFID of 1.82 at only 80 epochs and 1.31 at 800 epochs without Classifier-Free Guidance, significantly surpassing existing state-of-the-art methods. Beyond generative quality, GAE establishes a superior equilibrium between compression, semantic depth and robust reconstruction stability. These results validate our design considerations, offering a promising paradigm for latent diffusion modeling. Code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/freezing-index/Geometric-Autoencoder-for-Diffusion-Models.

AI.tldr: GAE (Geometric Autoencoder) introduces a principled latent diffusion design using Vision Foundation Model priors for semantic supervision, replaces KL with latent normalization, and employs dynamic noise sampling. It achieves state-of-the-art gFID on ImageNet-1K 256×256 (1.82 @ 80e, 1.31 @ 800e) without CFG, suggesting improved tradeoffs among semantic depth, reconstruction fidelity, and latent compactness.
AI.motivation: Address the triad of semantic discriminability, reconstruction fidelity, and latent compactness in latent diffusion models by integrating principled supervision from Vision Foundation Models and a stable latent space optimization.
AI.method: Construct an optimized low-dimensional semantic supervision target from VFMs to guide the autoencoder; replace KL-divergence in VAEs with latent normalization to shape a diffusion-friendly latent manifold; implement dynamic noise sampling to improve robustness under high-intensity noise.
AI.result: GAE achieves gFID of 1.82 at 80 epochs and 1.31 at 800 epochs on ImageNet-1K 256×256 without Classifier-Free Guidance, outperforming prior SOTA. Also reports balanced tradeoffs among compression, semantic depth, and reconstruction stability; code and models released.
AI.conclusion: GAE provides a principled, robust latent diffusion framework that leverages Vision Foundation Model priors, a latent-normalization-based latent space, and dynamic noise sampling to improve generative quality and stability, signaling a promising direction for high-resolution latent diffusion modeling.

title: Safe Probabilistic Planning for Human-Robot Interaction using Conformal Risk Control
summary: In this paper, we present a novel probabilistic safe control framework for human-robot interaction that combines control barrier functions (CBFs) with conformal risk control to provide formal safety guarantees while considering complex human behavior. The approach uses conformal risk control to quantify and control the prediction errors in CBF safety values and establishes formal guarantees on the probability of constraint satisfaction during interaction. We introduce an algorithm that dynamically adjusts the safety margins produced by conformal risk control based on the current interaction context. Through experiments on human-robot navigation scenarios, we demonstrate that our approach significantly reduces collision rates and safety violations as compared to baseline methods while maintaining high success rates in goal-reaching tasks and efficient control. The code, simulations, and other supplementary material can be found on the project website: https://jakeagonzales.github.io/crc-cbf-website/.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic safe control framework for human-robot interaction that fuses control barrier functions with conformal risk control to provide formal safety guarantees under complex human behavior, with adaptive safety margins and improved collision avoidance demonstrated in navigation experiments.
AI.motivation: Ensure formal safety guarantees in HRI despite prediction errors and uncertain human actions; traditional CBFs struggle with uncertain human behavior and risk of constraint violations.
AI.method: Integrate control barrier functions (CBFs) with conformal risk control to quantify and bound CBF safety-value prediction errors; develop an algorithm that adaptively adjusts safety margins produced by conformal risk control based on the interaction context; provide probabilistic guarantees on constraint satisfaction during human-robot interaction.
AI.result: Significant reduction in collision rates and safety violations compared with baselines; high success rates in goal-reaching tasks; efficient control maintained; formal probabilistic guarantees on constraint satisfaction demonstrated; code and materials available online.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a formally grounded, context-adaptive Safety mechanism for HRI by coupling CBFs with conformal risk control, with demonstrated empirical benefits and accessible resources for replication and deployment.

title: Rethinking Adam for Time Series Forecasting: A Simple Heuristic to Improve Optimization under Distribution Shifts
summary: Time-series forecasting often faces challenges from non-stationarity, particularly distributional drift, where the data distribution evolves over time. This dynamic behavior can undermine the effectiveness of adaptive optimizers, such as Adam, which are typically designed for stationary objectives. In this paper, we revisit Adam in the context of non-stationary forecasting and identify that its second-order bias correction limits responsiveness to shifting loss landscapes. To address this, we propose TS_Adam, a lightweight variant that removes the second-order correction from the learning rate computation. This simple modification improves adaptability to distributional drift while preserving the optimizer core structure and requiring no additional hyperparameters. TS_Adam integrates easily into existing models and consistently improves performance across long- and short-term forecasting tasks. On the ETT datasets with the MICN model, it achieves an average reduction of 12.8% in MSE and 5.7% in MAE compared to Adam. These results underscore the practicality and versatility of TS_Adam as an effective optimization strategy for real-world forecasting scenarios involving non-stationary data. Code is available at: https://github.com/DD-459-1/TS_Adam.

AI.tldr: TS_Adam removes Adam's second-order bias correction to improve adaptability to non-stationary drift in time-series forecasting, yielding consistent gains without extra hyperparameters; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Non-stationarity (distributional drift) challenges adaptive optimizers; second-order bias correction can hinder responsiveness to shifting loss landscapes; need a simple, plug-in solution.
AI.method: Introduce TS_Adam by eliminating Adam's second-order learning-rate correction; preserve core optimizer structure; no extra hyperparameters; easy integration.
AI.result: On ETT datasets with MICN, TS_Adam achieves ~12.8% MSE and ~5.7% MAE improvements over Adam; gains observed across long- and short-term forecasting; code available.
AI.conclusion: TS_Adam offers a practical, lightweight enhancement for non-stationary forecasting; maintains simplicity while improving adaptation to drift; suitable for real-world non-stationary data.

title: GeoSense: Internalizing Geometric Necessity Perception for Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Advancing towards artificial superintelligence requires rich and intelligent perceptual capabilities. A critical frontier in this pursuit is overcoming the limited spatial understanding of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), where geometry information is essential. Existing methods often address this by rigidly injecting geometric signals into every input, while ignoring their necessity and adding computation overhead. Contrary to this paradigm, our framework endows the model with an awareness of perceptual insufficiency, empowering it to autonomously engage geometric features in reasoning when 2D cues are deemed insufficient. To achieve this, we first introduce an independent geometry input channel to the model architecture and conduct alignment training, enabling the effective utilization of geometric features. Subsequently, to endow the model with perceptual awareness, we curate a dedicated spatial-aware supervised fine-tuning dataset. This serves to activate the model's latent internal cues, empowering it to autonomously determine the necessity of geometric information. Experiments across multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks validate this approach, demonstrating significant spatial gains without compromising 2D visual reasoning capabilities, offering a path toward more robust, efficient and self-aware multi-modal intelligence.

AI.tldr: A geometry-aware MLLM with an independent geometry input channel and a spatial-aware fine-tuning dataset, enabling selective use of geometric cues for enhanced spatial reasoning with minimal impact on 2D capabilities.
AI.motivation: MLLMs lack robust spatial understanding; indiscriminate geometric augmentation adds overhead and may be unnecessary. Enable perceptual insufficiency awareness so the model autonomously uses geometry only when 2D cues are insufficient.
AI.method: Add an independent geometry input channel to the model; perform alignment training to leverage geometric features. Curate a spatial-aware supervised fine-tuning dataset to activate latent internal cues so the model can decide when geometric information is needed.
AI.result: Significant gains in spatial reasoning across various benchmarks without sacrificing 2D visual reasoning performance; approach offers robustness and efficiency with self-aware multi-modal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable path toward robust, efficient, and self-aware MLLMs by decoupling geometry processing and training the model to selectively engage geometric cues based on perceptual sufficiency.

title: Shape Control of a Planar Hyper-Redundant Robot via Hybrid Kinematics-Informed and Learning-based Approach
summary: Hyper-redundant robots offer high dexterity, making them good at operating in confined and unstructured environments. To extend the reachable workspace, we built a multi-segment flexible rack actuated planar robot. However, the compliance of the flexible mechanism introduces instability, rendering it sensitive to external and internal uncertainties. To address these limitations, we propose a hybrid kinematics-informed and learning-based shape control method, named SpatioCoupledNet. The neural network adopts a hierarchical design that explicitly captures bidirectional spatial coupling between segments while modeling local disturbance along the robot body. A confidence-gating mechanism integrates prior kinematic knowledge, allowing the controller to adaptively balance model-based and learned components for improved convergence and fidelity. The framework is validated on a five-segment planar hyper-redundant robot under three representative shape configurations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms both analytical and purely neural controllers. In complex scenarios, it reduces steady-state error by up to 75.5% against the analytical model, and accelerates convergence by up to 20.5% compared to the data-driven baseline. Furthermore, gating analysis reveals a state-dependent authority fusion, shifting toward data-driven predictions in unstable states, while relying on physical priors in the remaining cases. Finally, we demonstrate robust performance in a dynamic task where the robot maintains a fixed end-effector position while avoiding moving obstacles, achieving a precise tip-positioning accuracy with a mean error of 10.47 mm.

AI.tldr: A hybrid kinematics-informed, learning-based shape control (SpatioCoupledNet) for five-segment planar hyper-redundant robots, achieving superior accuracy and convergence by coupling segments and adaptively fusing model-based priors with data-driven predictions.
AI.motivation: To overcome instability and sensitivity due to compliance and uncertainties in flexible hyper-redundant manipulators, and to extend workspace via a robust control strategy.
AI.method: Proposes SpatioCoupledNet, a hierarchical neural network that models bidirectional spatial coupling and local disturbances, with a confidence-gating mechanism to blend kinematic priors and learned components; validated on a 5-segment planar robot across three configurations; comparisons against analytical and pure NN controllers.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines; reduces steady-state error up to 75.5% relative to analytical model; accelerates convergence up to 20.5% relative to data-driven baseline; gating reveals state-dependent shift toward data-driven predictions in unstable states; dynamic task achieves 10.47 mm mean tip error.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a robust, accurate, and adaptable control framework for hyper-redundant robots, combining physics-informed priors with learning to handle uncertainty and disturbance.

title: Denoising the US Census: Succinct Block Hierarchical Regression
summary:
The US Census Bureau Disclosure Avoidance System (DAS) balances confidentiality and utility requirements for the decennial US Census (Abowd et al., 2022). The DAS was used in the 2020 Census to produce demographic datasets critically used for legislative apportionment and redistricting, federal and state funding allocation, municipal and infrastructure planning, and scientific research. At the heart of DAS is TopDown, a heuristic post-processing method that combines billions of private noisy measurements across six geographic levels in order to produce new estimates that are consistent, more accurate, and satisfy certain structural constraints on the data.
  In this work, we introduce BlueDown, a new post-processing method that produces more accurate, consistent estimates while satisfying the same privacy guarantees and structural constraints. We obtain especially large accuracy improvements for aggregates at the county and tract levels on evaluation metrics proposed by the US Census Bureau.
  From a technical perspective, we develop a new algorithm for generalized least-squares regression that leverages the hierarchical structure of the measurements and that is statistically optimal among linear unbiased estimators. This reduces the computational dependence on the number of geographic regions measured from matrix multiplication time, which would be infeasible for census-scale data, to linear time. We incorporate the additional structural constraints by combining this regression algorithm with an optimization routine that extends TDA to support correlated measurements. We further improve the efficiency of our algorithm using succinct linear-algebraic operations that exploit symmetries in the structure of the measurements and constraints. We believe our hierarchical regression and succinct operations to be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: BlueDown improves post-processing for Census DAS by hierarchical generalized least-squares regression, achieving more accurate and consistent estimates with linear-time complexity while preserving privacy and structural constraints.
AI.motivation: Balance confidentiality and data utility in the US Census DAS; produce accurate, consistent estimates across geographic levels under privacy guarantees for legislative, funding, planning, and research needs.
AI.method: Develop BlueDown: a generalized least-squares regression algorithm that exploits the hierarchical, correlated measurement structure; extend TopDown (TDA) to support correlated measurements; incorporate structural constraints via optimization; employ succinct linear-algebra techniques leveraging symmetry to achieve linear-time performance.
AI.result: Significant accuracy gains at county and tract levels on Census Bureau metrics; statistically optimal among linear unbiased estimators; reduced computational complexity from matrix multiplication to linear time; method remains compatible with existing privacy guarantees and data constraints.
AI.conclusion: BlueDown offers a more accurate, consistent, and scalable post-processing approach for the Census DAS, with potential independent interest in hierarchical regression and efficient linear algebra for correlated, structured measurements.

title: Multi-Person Pose Estimation Evaluation Using Optimal Transportation and Improved Pose Matching
summary: In Multi-Person Pose Estimation, many metrics place importance on ranking of pose detection confidence scores. Current metrics tend to disregard false-positive poses with low confidence, focusing primarily on a larger number of high-confidence poses. Consequently, these metrics may yield high scores even when many false-positive poses with low confidence are detected. For fair evaluation taking into account a tradeoff between true-positive and false-positive poses, this paper proposes Optimal Correction Cost for pose (OCpose), which evaluates detected poses against pose annotations as an optimal transportation. For the fair tradeoff between true-positive and false-positive poses, OCpose equally evaluates all the detected poses regardless of their confidence scores. In OCpose, on the other hand, the confidence score of each pose is utilized to improve the reliability of matching scores between the estimated pose and pose annotations. As a result, OCpose provides a different perspective assessment than other confidence ranking-based metrics.

AI.tldr: Summary generation failed
AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable
AI.method: Method extraction failed
AI.result: Result analysis unavailable
AI.conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

title: Rethinking Gaussian Trajectory Predictors: Calibrated Uncertainty for Safe Planning
summary: Accurate trajectory prediction is critical for safe autonomous navigation in crowded environments. While many trajectory predictors output Gaussian distributions to represent the multi-modal distribution over future pedestrian positions, the reliability of their confidence levels often remains unaddressed. This limitation can lead to unsafe or overly conservative motion planning when the predictor is integrated with an uncertainty-aware planner. Existing Gaussian trajectory predictors primarily rely on the Negative Log-Likelihood loss, which is prone to predict over- or under-confident distributions, and may compromise downstream planner safety. This paper introduces a novel loss function for calibrating prediction uncertainty which leverages Kernel Density Estimation to estimate the empirical distribution of confidence levels. The proposed formulation enforces consistency with the properties of a Gaussian assumption by explicitly matching the estimated empirical distribution to the Chi-squared distribution. To ensure accurate mean prediction, a Mean Squared Error term is also incorporated in the final loss formulation. Experimental results on real-world trajectory datasets show that our method significantly improves the reliability of confidence levels predicted by different State-Of-The-Art Gaussian trajectory predictors. We also demonstrate the importance of providing planners with reliable probabilistic insights (i.e. calibrated confidence levels) for collision-free navigation in complex scenarios. For this purpose, we integrate Gaussian trajectory predictors trained with our loss function with an uncertainty-aware Model Predictive Control on scenarios extracted from real-world datasets, achieving improved planning performance through calibrated confidence levels.

AI.tldr: Calibrated Gaussian trajectory prediction loss using KDE-estimated confidence distributions aligned with a Chi-squared reference, plus an MSE term, to improve confidence reliability and downstream MPC planning.
AI.motivation: Gaussian predictors often miscalibrate uncertainty, which can lead to unsafe or overly conservative planning when integrated with uncertainty-aware controllers.
AI.method: Introduce a loss that uses Kernel Density Estimation to estimate the empirical confidence distribution, enforces Gaussian-consistency by matching this empirical distribution to the Chi-squared distribution, and adds a Mean Squared Error term to ensure accurate mean predictions.
AI.result: On real-world trajectory datasets, the method improves the reliability of predicted confidence levels across state-of-the-art Gaussian predictors and enhances planning performance when combined with uncertainty-aware Model Predictive Control.
AI.conclusion: Calibrating prediction uncertainty with the proposed loss yields more reliable probabilistic insights for safe and efficient collision-free navigation in crowded environments.

title: Hardware Efficient Approximate Convolution with Tunable Error Tolerance for CNNs
summary: Modern CNNs' high computational demands hinder edge deployment, as traditional ``hard'' sparsity (skipping mathematical zeros) loses effectiveness in deep layers or with smooth activations like Tanh. We propose a ``soft sparsity'' paradigm using a hardware efficient Most Significant Bit (MSB) proxy to skip negligible non-zero multiplications. Integrated as a custom RISC-V instruction and evaluated on LeNet-5 (MNIST), this method reduces ReLU MACs by 88.42% and Tanh MACs by 74.87% with zero accuracy loss--outperforming zero-skipping by 5x. By clock-gating inactive multipliers, we estimate power savings of 35.2\% for ReLU and 29.96\% for Tanh. While memory access makes power reduction sub-linear to operation savings, this approach significantly optimizes resource-constrained inference.

AI.tldr: Proposes a soft sparsity approach using an MSB proxy to skip negligible non-zero multiplications, implemented as a RISC-V instruction, achieving large MAC reductions and power savings on LeNet-5 with MNIST and no accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of traditional hard sparsity in deep CNNs, especially in deeper layers and with smooth activations, by enabling selective skipping of computations that contribute minimally to the result.
AI.method: Introduce a hardware-friendly MSB-based proxy for identifying and skipping negligible multiplications (soft sparsity). Implement as a custom RISC-V instruction and apply clock-gating to deactivate unused multipliers. Evaluate on LeNet-5 with MNIST (ReLU and Tanh activations).
AI.result: ReLU MACs reduced by 88.42% and Tanh MACs by 74.87% with zero accuracy loss on LeNet-5; zero-skipping outperformed by 5x. Power savings estimated at 35.2% for ReLU and 29.96% for Tanh; memory access causes sub-linear power gains but overall significant benefits for edge inference.
AI.conclusion: Soft sparsity via an MSB proxy and a dedicated hardware instruction offers substantial computational and energy efficiency gains on compact CNNs without accuracy loss, though results are demonstrated on a small network and may depend on activation smoothness and memory behavior.

title: Motion Forcing: A Decoupled Framework for Robust Video Generation in Motion Dynamics
summary: The ultimate goal of video generation is to satisfy a fundamental trilemma: achieving high visual quality, maintaining rigorous physical consistency, and enabling precise controllability. While recent models can maintain this balance in simple, isolated scenarios, we observe that this equilibrium is fragile and often breaks down as scene complexity increases (e.g., involving collisions or dense traffic). To address this, we introduce \textbf{Motion Forcing}, a framework designed to stabilize this trilemma even in complex generative tasks. Our key insight is to explicitly decouple physical reasoning from visual synthesis via a hierarchical \textbf{``Point-Shape-Appearance''} paradigm. This approach decomposes generation into verifiable stages: modeling complex dynamics as sparse geometric anchors (\textbf{Point}), expanding them into dynamic depth maps that explicitly resolve 3D geometry (\textbf{Shape}), and finally rendering high-fidelity textures (\textbf{Appearance}). Furthermore, to foster robust physical understanding, we employ a \textbf{Masked Point Recovery} strategy.
