By randomly masking input anchors during training and enforcing the reconstruction of complete dynamic depth, the model is compelled to move beyond passive pattern matching and learn latent physical laws (e.g., inertia) to infer missing trajectories. Extensive experiments on autonomous driving benchmarks show that Motion Forcing significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, maintaining trilemma stability across complex scenes. Evaluations on physics and robotics further confirm our framework's generality.

AI.tldr: Proposes Motion Forcing with a Point-Shape-Appearance hierarchy and Masked Point Recovery to stabilize high-quality, physically consistent, and controllable video generation in complex scenes; validated on autonomous driving, physics, and robotics benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current video generation models struggle to maintain physical consistency and controllability as scene complexity grows; a decoupled, hierarchical approach can learn latent physical laws and robust dynamics.
AI.method: Hierarchical decomposition: Point (sparse anchors), Shape (dynamic depth maps for 3D geometry), Appearance (textures). Training includes Masked Point Recovery by randomly masking anchors and reconstructing complete dynamic depth to force learning of motion dynamics and inertia. Evaluated on autonomous driving benchmarks and generalized to physics/robotics tasks.
AI.result: Significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines in maintaining the video generation trilemma (quality, physical consistency, controllability) under complex scenes; demonstrations of generality across physics and robotics experiments.
AI.conclusion: Motion Forcing effectively stabilizes the trilemma in complex generative tasks by decoupling physical reasoning from visual synthesis and enforcing latent physical law learning through masked reconstruction.

title: COHORT: Hybrid RL for Collaborative Large DNN Inference on Multi-Robot Systems Under Real-Time Constraints
comment: Recently accepted at 27th IEEE International Symposium on a World of Wireless, Mobile and Multimedia Networks ( IEEE WoWMoM 2026)
summary: Large deep neural networks (DNNs), especially transformer-based and multimodal architectures, are computationally demanding and challenging to deploy on resource-constrained edge platforms like field robots. These challenges intensify in mission-critical scenarios (e.g., disaster response), where robots must collaborate under tight constraints on bandwidth, latency, and battery life, often without infrastructure or server support. To address these limitations, we present COHORT, a collaborative DNN inference and task-execution framework for multi-robot systems built on the Robotic Operating System (ROS). COHORT employs a hybrid offline-online reinforcement learning (RL) strategy to dynamically schedule and distribute DNN module execution across robots. Our key contributions are threefold: (a) Offline RL policy learning combined with Advantage-Weighted Regression (AWR), trained on auction-based task allocation data from heterogeneous DNN workloads across distributed robots, (b) Online policy adaptation via Multi-Agent PPO (MAPPO), initialized from the offline policy and fine-tuned in real time, and (c) comprehensive evaluation of COHORT on vision-language model (VLM) inference tasks such as CLIP and SAM, analyzing scalability with increasing robot/workload and robustness under . We benchmark COHORT against genetic algorithms and multiple RL baselines. Experimental results demonstrate that COHORT reduces battery consumption by 15.4% and increases GPU utilization by 51.67%, while satisfying frame-rate and deadline constraints 2.55 times of the time.

AI.tldr: COHORT is a ROS-based multi-robot DNN inference framework that uses offline AWR policy learning and online MAPPO adaptation to dynamically schedule DNN modules across robots, evaluated on vision-language model tasks (CLIP, SAM). It achieves energy efficiency and better utilization while meeting real-time constraints.
AI.motivation: Edge robots face strict limits on bandwidth, latency, and power for large DNNs; disaster-response and other mission-critical scenarios benefit from coordinated, infrastructure-free collaboration across multiple robots.
AI.method: Hybrid offline-online reinforcement learning: offline policy learning with Advantage-Weighted Regression (AWR) on auction-based task allocation data from heterogeneous DNN workloads; online policy adaptation via Multi-Agent PPO (MAPPO) initialized from the offline policy and fine-tuned in real time; implementation on ROS to schedule and distribute DNN modules across robots; evaluation on VLM tasks (CLIP, SAM); comparison against genetic algorithms and multiple RL baselines.
AI.result: COHORT reduces battery consumption by 15.4% and increases GPU utilization by 51.67%, while satisfying frame-rate and deadline constraints with a 2.55× improvement over baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates effective, scalable, and energy-efficient cooperative DNN inference for multi-robot systems under bandwidth, latency, and power constraints, with robust online adaptation and superior performance compared with baseline methods.

title: CLIPO: Contrastive Learning in Policy Optimization Generalizes RLVR
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced the reasoning capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, RLVR solely relies on final answers as outcome rewards, neglecting the correctness of intermediate reasoning steps. Training on these process-wrong but outcome-correct rollouts can lead to hallucination and answer-copying, severely undermining the model's generalization and robustness. To address this, we incorporate a Contrastive Learning mechanism into the Policy Optimization (CLIPO) to generalize the RLVR process. By optimizing a contrastive loss over successful rollouts, CLIPO steers the LLM to capture the invariant structure shared across correct reasoning paths. This provides a more robust cross-trajectory regularization than the original single-path supervision in RLVR, effectively mitigating step-level reasoning inconsistencies and suppressing hallucinatory artifacts. In experiments, CLIPO consistently improves multiple RLVR baselines across diverse reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating uniform improvements in generalization and robustness for policy optimization of LLMs. Our code and training recipes are available at https://github.com/Qwen-Applications/CLIPO.

AI.tldr: CLIPO introduces a contrastive learning objective over successful RLVR rollouts to regularize intermediate reasoning steps in LLMs, improving generalization and robustness.
AI.motivation: RLVR relies solely on final outcomes and ignores the correctness of intermediate reasoning; this can lead to training on process-wrong but outcome-correct rollouts, causing hallucinations and poor generalization.
AI.method: Incorporate a contrastive loss over successful rollouts to enforce invariant reasoning structures across correct paths, integrating with policy optimization to generalize RLVR beyond single-path supervision.
AI.result: CLIPO consistently improves multiple RLVR baselines across diverse reasoning benchmarks, yielding better generalization and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive cross-trajectory regularization provides more robust guidance than single-path supervision, mitigating step-level inconsistencies and hallucinations; code and training recipes are publicly available.

title: Frames2Residual: Spatiotemporal Decoupling for Self-Supervised Video Denoising
summary: Self-supervised video denoising methods typically extend image-based frameworks into the temporal dimension, yet they often struggle to integrate inter-frame temporal consistency with intra-frame spatial specificity. Existing Video Blind-Spot Networks (BSNs) require noise independence by masking the center pixel, this constraint prevents the use of spatial evidence for texture recovery, thereby severing spatiotemporal correlations and causing texture loss. To address this, we propose Frames2Residual (F2R), a spatiotemporal decoupling framework that explicitly divides self-supervised training into two distinct stages: blind temporal consistency modeling and non-blind spatial texture recovery. In Stage 1, a blind temporal estimator learns inter-frame consistency using a frame-wise blind strategy, producing a temporally consistent anchor. In Stage 2, a non-blind spatial refiner leverages this anchor to safely reintroduce the center frame and recover intra-frame high-frequency spatial residuals while preserving temporal stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our decoupling strategy allows F2R to outperform existing self-supervised methods on both sRGB and raw video benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Two-stage self-supervised video denoising (Frames2Residual, F2R) that decouples temporal consistency from spatial texture recovery to overcome blind-spot limitations in video BSNs, achieving better spatiotemporal coherence and texture preservation.
AI.motivation: Current Video Blind-Spot Networks enforce noise independence by masking the center pixel, which hinders using spatial cues for texture recovery and disrupts spatiotemporal correlations, leading to texture loss. A method that separately models temporal consistency and spatial detail could preserve temporal stability while restoring high-frequency textures.
AI.method: Stage 1: blind temporal estimator using a frame-wise blind strategy to learn inter-frame consistency and produce a temporally consistent anchor. Stage 2: non-blind spatial refiner uses the anchor to reintroduce the center frame and recover intra-frame high-frequency spatial residuals while maintaining temporal stability. This explicit decoupling guides learning into two complementary objectives rather than a single end-to-end blind process.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that the decoupling strategy enables F2R to outperform existing self-supervised video denoising methods on both sRGB and raw video benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling temporal consistency learning from spatial texture recovery yields improved denoising performance by preserving temporal stability and recovering high-frequency spatial details in a self-supervised setting.

title: AsyncMDE: Real-Time Monocular Depth Estimation via Asynchronous Spatial Memory
summary: Foundation-model-based monocular depth estimation offers a viable alternative to active sensors for robot perception, yet its computational cost often prohibits deployment on edge platforms. Existing methods perform independent per-frame inference, wasting the substantial computational redundancy between adjacent viewpoints in continuous robot operation. This paper presents AsyncMDE, an asynchronous depth perception system consisting of a foundation model and a lightweight model that amortizes the foundation model's computational cost over time. The foundation model produces high-quality spatial features in the background, while the lightweight model runs asynchronously in the foreground, fusing cached memory with current observations through complementary fusion, outputting depth estimates, and autoregressively updating the memory. This enables cross-frame feature reuse with bounded accuracy degradation. At a mere 3.83M parameters, it operates at 237 FPS on an RTX 4090, recovering 77% of the accuracy gap to the foundation model while achieving a 25X parameter reduction. Validated across indoor static, dynamic, and synthetic extreme-motion benchmarks, AsyncMDE degrades gracefully between refreshes and achieves 161FPS on a Jetson AGX Orin with TensorRT, clearly demonstrating its feasibility for real-time edge deployment.

AI.tldr: AsyncMDE uses asynchronous fusion of a foundation model and a lightweight foreground model to amortize heavy foundation-model cost over time, enabling real-time monocular depth estimation on edge hardware with strong parameter efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the high computational cost of foundation-model-based monocular depth estimation on edge devices by exploiting temporal redundancy across frames and enabling continuous operation.
AI.method: Two-stage asynchronous pipeline: the foundation model runs in the background to produce high-quality spatial features, while a lightweight foreground model performs complementary fusion with cached memory and current observations, outputting depth estimates and autoregressively updating memory. This cross-frame feature reuse yields bounded accuracy degradation. Parameters: 3.83M; FPS: 237 on RTX 4090; 161 FPS on Jetson AGX Orin with TensorRT.
AI.result: The method recovers 77% of the foundation-model accuracy gap while achieving a 25× parameter reduction. Validated on indoor static/dynamic and synthetic extreme-motion benchmarks; memory memory is updated autoregressively and degradation between refreshes is graceful.
AI.conclusion: AsyncMDE demonstrates feasibility of real-time edge deployment for foundation-model-based monocular depth estimation by leveraging cross-frame feature reuse, delivering high throughput with strong accuracy maintenance and substantial model compression.

title: Lost in the Middle at Birth: An Exact Theory of Transformer Position Bias
summary:
The ``Lost in the Middle'' phenomenon -- a U-shaped performance curve where LLMs retrieve well from the beginning and end of a context but fail in the middle -- is widely attributed to learned Softmax artifacts or the distance-decay of positional encodings like RoPE. This paper makes a single, precise claim: \emph{the U-shape is already present at initialization, before any training or positional encoding takes effect.} It is an inherent geometric property of the causal decoder with residual connections.
  We model multi-layer causal attention as iterated powers of the Cesàro matrix and derive the exact closed-form influence density in the continuous limit. Causal masking forces a logarithmic divergence of gradient influence at the start of the prompt (the Primacy Tail), while residual connections create an isolated $\mathcal{O}(1)$ anchor at the final token (the Recency Delta). Between these extremes lies a factorial dead zone of order $\mathcal{O}(1/(H{-}1)!)$, where $H$ is the network depth, making middle-context retrieval and training structurally hostile. We validate empirically that untrained Qwen2 and GPT-2 architectures exhibit this U-shape at Step~0, and that it is identical with or without RoPE. Comparing initialized and pretrained networks, we show that standard training does not overcome the topological valley, confirming that the U-shape persists as an architectural baseline under standard pretraining objectives.
  We do not claim that this bias is insurmountable, nor that interventions such as RoPE modifications are useless. We establish what the baseline is and where it comes from, so that future efforts to overcome it can be precisely targeted.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the U-shaped 'Lost in the Middle' performance curve is an architectural property present at initialization in causal-decoder transformers with residual connections, independent of training and positional encodings like RoPE. It identifies two extreme regions (Primacy Tail and Recency Delta) and a factorial dead zone, derives a closed-form influence density via Cesàro-matrix analysis, and validates the baseline on untrained models, arguing that training cannot fully overcome this baseline.
AI.motivation: To pinpoint the origin of the 'Lost in the Middle' phenomenon and distinguish architecture-induced biases from training artifacts or positional encoding effects, providing a precise baseline for future interventions.
AI.method: Model multi-layer causal attention as iterated powers of the Cesàro matrix; derive exact closed-form gradient/influence density in the continuous limit; analyze divergences at prompt start (Primacy Tail) and a final-token anchor (Recency Delta) with a factorial dead zone; empirically validate with untrained Qwen2 and GPT-2 architectures at Step 0 and compare initialized vs pretrained networks; test RoPE's effect by comparing with/without RoPE.
AI.result: Identifies a pre-training, initialization-level U-shaped influence profile that matches the phenomenon regardless of RoPE; shows a persistent architectural baseline under standard pretraining; demonstrates an explicit decomposition of influence into primacy tail, recency delta, and a factorial middle dead zone; training does not remove the baseline.
AI.conclusion: The U-shape is an intrinsic architectural property of causal decoders with residuals, existing before training and independent of RoPE. This baseline should guide future efforts to mitigate middle-context retrieval issues, by targeting the identified structural regions rather than assuming training or positional-encoding changes will suffice.

title: TractoRC: A Unified Probabilistic Learning Framework for Joint Tractography Registration and Clustering
summary: Diffusion MRI tractography enables in vivo reconstruction of white matter (WM) pathways. Two key tasks in tractography analysis include: 1) tractogram registration that aligns streamlines across individuals, and 2) streamline clustering that groups streamlines into compact fiber bundles. Although both tasks share the goal of capturing geometrically similar structures to characterize consistent WM organization, they are typically performed independently. In this work, we propose TractoRC, a unified probabilistic framework that jointly performs tractogram registration and streamline clustering within a single optimization scheme, enabling the two tasks to leverage complementary information. TractoRC learns a latent embedding space for streamline points, which serves as a shared representation for both tasks. Within this space, both tasks are formulated as probabilistic inference over structural representations: registration learns the distribution of anatomical landmarks as probabilistic keypoints to align tractograms across subjects, and clustering learns streamline structural prototypes that capture geometric similarity to form coherent streamline clusters. To support effective learning of this shared space, we introduce a transformation-equivariant self-supervised strategy to learn geometry-aware and transformation-invariant embeddings. Experiments demonstrate that jointly optimizing registration and clustering significantly improves performance in both tasks over state-of-the-art methods that treat them independently. Code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/yishengpoxiao/TractoRC .

AI.tldr: Proposes TractoRC, a unified probabilistic framework that jointly performs tractogram registration and streamline clustering via a shared latent embedding, improving both tasks and leveraging info from each task; includes transformation-equivariant self-supervised learning; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Tractography analysis typically treats tractogram registration and streamline clustering separately, missing potential mutual benefits. A joint approach can leverage complementary information to better capture consistent white matter organization.
AI.method: TractoRC learns a latent embedding space for streamline points serving as a shared representation. Registration is framed as probabilistic inference over anatomical landmark keypoints for aligning tractograms; clustering is probabilistic inference of streamline structural prototypes to form coherent bundles. A transformation-equivariant self-supervised strategy learns geometry-aware, transformation-invariant embeddings within a single optimization framework that unifies registration and clustering.
AI.result: Experiments show that jointly optimizing registration and clustering yields significant improvements in both tasks compared with state-of-the-art methods that treat them independently.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing tractogram registration and streamline clustering in a single probabilistic framework improves accuracy and consistency of WM organization characterization, with a public code release to facilitate adoption.

title: KnowDiffuser: A Knowledge-Guided Diffusion Planner with LM Reasoning and Prior-Informed Trajectory Initialization
summary: Recent advancements in Language Models (LMs) have demonstrated strong semantic reasoning capabilities, enabling their application in high-level decision-making for autonomous driving (AD). However, LMs operate over discrete token spaces and lack the ability to generate continuous, physically feasible trajectories required for motion planning. Meanwhile, diffusion models have proven effective at generating reliable and dynamically consistent trajectories, but often lack semantic interpretability and alignment with scene-level understanding. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{KnowDiffuser}, a knowledge-guided motion planning framework that tightly integrates the semantic understanding of language models with the generative power of diffusion models. The framework employs a language model to infer context-aware meta-actions from structured scene representations, which are then mapped to prior trajectories that anchor the subsequent denoising process. A two-stage truncated denoising mechanism refines these trajectories efficiently, preserving both semantic alignment and physical feasibility. Experiments on the nuPlan benchmark demonstrate that KnowDiffuser significantly outperforms existing planners in both open-loop and closed-loop evaluations, establishing a robust and interpretable framework that effectively bridges the semantic-to-physical gap in AD systems.

AI.tldr: KnowDiffuser is a knowledge-guided diffusion-based motion planning framework for autonomous driving that aligns language-model semantics with continuous trajectory generation through meta-actions and a two-stage denoising process, achieving strong open-loop and closed-loop performance on nuPlan.
AI.motivation: Bridging the semantic understanding of language models with the continuous, physically feasible trajectory generation required for motion planning; addressing the semantic-physical gap and lack of interpretability in diffusion-based planners.
AI.method: 1) Use a language model to infer context-aware meta-actions from structured scene representations. 2) Map meta-actions to prior trajectories to anchor the denoising process. 3) Apply a two-stage truncated denoising mechanism to refine trajectories efficiently while preserving semantic alignment and physical feasibility. 4) Evaluate on the nuPlan benchmark in open-loop and closed-loop settings.
AI.result: Experiments on nuPlan show that KnowDiffuser significantly outperforms existing planners in both open-loop and closed-loop evaluations, demonstrating robust performance and improved interpretability through semantic-to-physical alignment.
AI.conclusion: KnowDiffuser provides a robust, interpretable framework that effectively bridges semantic understanding and physical trajectory generation for autonomous driving by integrating language-guided semantics with diffusion-based motion planning.

title: A neural operator for predicting vibration frequency response curves from limited data
summary: In the design of engineered components, rigorous vibration testing is essential for performance validation and identification of resonant frequencies and amplitudes encountered during operation. Performing this evaluation numerically via machine learning has great potential to accelerate design iteration and make testing workflows more efficient. However, dynamical systems are conventionally difficult to solve via machine learning methods without using physics-based regularizing loss functions. To properly perform this forecasting task, a structure that has an inspectable physical obedience can be devised without the use of regularizing terms from first principles. The method employed in this work is a neural operator integrated with an implicit numerical scheme. This architecture enables operators to learn of the underlying state-space dynamics from limited data, allowing generalization to untested driving frequencies and initial conditions. This network can infer the system's global frequency response by training on a small set of input conditions. As a foundational proof of concept, this investigation verifies the machine learning algorithm with a linear, single-degree-of-freedom system, demonstrating implicit obedience of dynamics. This approach demonstrates 99.87% accuracy in predicting the Frequency Response Curve (FRC), forecasting the frequency and amplitude of linear resonance training on 7% of the bandwidth of the solution. By training machine learning models to internalize physics information rather than trajectory, better generalization accuracy can be realized, vastly improving the timeframe for vibration studies on engineered components.

AI.tldr: A physics-obedient neural operator with an implicit scheme learns state-space dynamics from limited data to predict the global frequency response in vibration systems, achieving ~99.87% accuracy on a linear SDOF using only 7% bandwidth, without regularizing loss terms.
AI.motivation: Accelerate vibration testing and design iteration by enabling ML-based forecasting that generalizes across driving frequencies and initial conditions while maintaining physical consistency without first-principles regularizers.
AI.method: Neural operator integrated with an implicit numerical scheme; learns underlying dynamics, learns generalizable operator from limited data; trained to infer global FRC rather than trajectory; validated on linear SDOF.
AI.result: Achieves 99.87% accuracy in predicting FRC; demonstrates forecasting on a small fraction of the bandwidth (7%).
AI.conclusion: Learning physics information rather than trajectories improves generalization and speeds up vibration studies; supports physics-obedient ML for engineering design validation.

title: World2Act: Latent Action Post-Training via Skill-Compositional World Models
summary: World Models (WMs) have emerged as a promising approach for post-training Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies to improve robustness and generalization under environmental changes. However, most WM-based post-training methods rely on pixel-space supervision, making policies sensitive to pixel-level artifacts and hallucination from imperfect WM rollouts. We introduce World2Act, a post-training framework that aligns VLA actions directly with WM video-dynamics latents using a contrastive matching objective, reducing dependence on pixels. Post-training performance is tied to rollout quality, yet current WMs struggle with arbitrary-length video generation as they are mostly trained on fixed-length clips while robotic execution durations vary widely. To address this, we propose an automatic LLM-based skill-decomposition pipeline that segments high-level instructions into low-level prompts. Our pipeline produces RoboCasa-Skill and LIBERO-Skill, supporting skill-compositional WMs that remain temporally consistent across diverse task horizons. Empirically, applying World2Act to VLAs like GR00T-N1.6 and Cosmos Policy achieves state-of-the-art results on RoboCasa and LIBERO, and improves real-world performance by 6.7%, enhancing embodied agent generalization.

AI.tldr: World2Act aligns VLA actions to WM latent video dynamics via contrastive learning, reducing pixel-dependence; adds LLM-based skill decomposition to handle variable-length tasks; achieves state-of-the-art results and real-world gains.
AI.motivation: WM-based post-training policies traditionally rely on pixel-space supervision, making them sensitive to pixel artifacts and hallucinations from imperfect WM rollouts, and struggle with arbitrary video lengths due to fixed-length training clips. There is a need for pixel-robust, temporally consistent policies that generalize across task horizons.
AI.method: World2Act uses a contrastive matching objective to align VLA actions with WM video-dynamics latents instead of pixel data. It also introduces an automatic LLM-based skill-decomposition pipeline that breaks high-level instructions into low-level prompts, producing RoboCasa-Skill and LIBERO-Skill for skill-compositional, temporally consistent WMs across diverse horizons.
AI.result: Empirically achieves state-of-the-art performance on RoboCasa and LIBERO benchmarks when applied to VLAs like GR00T-N1.6 and Cosmos Policy, with a real-world performance improvement of 6.7%, indicating enhanced embodied-agent generalization.
AI.conclusion: World2Act reduces reliance on pixel-space supervision, improves robustness to visual artifacts, and, together with LLM-driven skill decomposition, enables temporally consistent, scalable WM-based VLA policies with strong generalization across task horizons.

title: DiT4DiT: Jointly Modeling Video Dynamics and Actions for Generalizable Robot Control
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for robot learning, but their representations are still largely inherited from static image-text pretraining, leaving physical dynamics to be learned from comparatively limited action data. Generative video models, by contrast, encode rich spatiotemporal structure and implicit physics, making them a compelling foundation for robotic manipulation. But their potentials are not fully explored in the literature. To bridge the gap, we introduce DiT4DiT, an end-to-end Video-Action Model that couples a video Diffusion Transformer with an action Diffusion Transformer in a unified cascaded framework. Instead of relying on reconstructed future frames, DiT4DiT extracts intermediate denoising features from the video generation process and uses them as temporally grounded conditions for action prediction. We further propose a dual flow-matching objective with decoupled timesteps and noise scales for video prediction, hidden-state extraction, and action inference, enabling coherent joint training of both modules. Across simulation and real-world benchmarks, DiT4DiT achieves state-of-the-art results, reaching average success rates of 98.6% on LIBERO and 50.8% on RoboCasa GR1 while using substantially less training data. On the Unitree G1 robot, it also delivers superior real-world performance and strong zero-shot generalization. Importantly, DiT4DiT improves sample efficiency by over 10x and speeds up convergence by up to 7x, demonstrating that video generation can serve as an effective scaling proxy for robot policy learning. We release code and models at https://dit4dit.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Introduces DiT4DiT, a cascaded video-action diffusion model that extracts temporally grounded action cues from intermediate denoising features in a video diffusion process, enabling end-to-end learning of video prediction and robot action inference with superior data efficiency and convergence; achieves SOTA on LIBERO, RoboCasa GR1, and Unitree G1 zero-shot generalization; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between static image-text pretraining for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models and the need to capture physical dynamics in robot manipulation. Leveraging generative video models can encode rich spatiotemporal structure and implicit physics, potentially improving sample efficiency and generalization.
AI.method: Propose DiT4DiT: an end-to-end cascaded framework combining a video Diffusion Transformer (DiT) and an action Diffusion Transformer. Instead of reconstructing future frames, uses intermediate denoising features from the video generation process as temporally grounded conditioning for action prediction. Introduce a dual flow-matching objective with decoupled timesteps and noise scales across video prediction, hidden-state extraction, and action inference, enabling coherent joint training of both modules.
AI.result: Empirically achieves state-of-the-art performance across simulation and real-world benchmarks: LIBERO 98.6% average success; RoboCasa GR1 50.8%; strong real-world performance and zero-shot generalization on Unitree G1. Demonstrates over 10x improvements in sample efficiency and up to 7x faster convergence. Code and models released at the project site.
AI.conclusion: Video generation serves as an effective scaling proxy for robot policy learning, enabling more data-efficient and generalizable policies. The approach shows promise for integrating generative video models with action inference in robotic manipulation.

title: Mashup Learning: Faster Finetuning by Remixing Past Checkpoints
summary: Finetuning on domain-specific data is a well-established method for enhancing LLM performance on downstream tasks. Training on each dataset produces a new set of model weights, resulting in a multitude of checkpoints saved in-house or on open-source platforms. However, these training artifacts are rarely reused for subsequent experiments despite containing improved model abilities for potentially similar tasks. In this paper, we propose Mashup Learning, a simple method to leverage the outputs of prior training runs to enhance model adaptation to new tasks. Our procedure identifies the most relevant historical checkpoints for a target dataset, aggregates them with model merging, and uses the result as an improved initialization for training. Across 8 standard LLM benchmarks, four models, and two collections of source checkpoints, Mashup Learning consistently improves average downstream accuracy by 0.5-5 percentage points over training from scratch. It also accelerates convergence, requiring 41-46% fewer training steps and up to 37% less total wall-clock time to match from-scratch accuracy, including all selection and merging overhead.

AI.tldr: Mashup Learning merges relevant historical checkpoints to initialize training on a new task, yielding 0.5–5 percentage points accuracy gains and 41–46% fewer steps and 37% less wall-clock time on 8 benchmarks across 4 models and 2 checkpoint collections.
AI.motivation: Reuse previously trained artifacts (checkpoints) to improve efficiency and performance when adapting LLMs to new tasks, addressing underutilization of past training outputs.
AI.method: Identify the most relevant past checkpoints for a target dataset, merge them into a single model via a merging operation, and use the merged model as the initialization for subsequent fine-tuning on the target task.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in average downstream accuracy (0.5–5pp) and accelerated convergence (41–46% fewer training steps; up to 37% less wall-clock time) across 8 benchmarks, 4 models, and 2 source checkpoint collections.
AI.conclusion: Mashup Learning provides a simple, practical strategy to leverage existing training artifacts to boost adaptation performance and efficiency, potentially reducing compute while maintaining or improving accuracy.

title: FAR-Dex: Few-shot Data Augmentation and Adaptive Residual Policy Refinement for Dexterous Manipulation
comment: Accepted to IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: Achieving human-like dexterous manipulation through the collaboration of multi-fingered hands with robotic arms remains a longstanding challenge in robotics, primarily due to the scarcity of high-quality demonstrations and the complexity of high-dimensional action spaces. To address these challenges, we propose FAR-Dex, a hierarchical framework that integrates few-shot data augmentation with adaptive residual refinement to enable robust and precise arm-hand coordination in dexterous tasks. First, FAR-DexGen leverages the IsaacLab simulator to generate diverse and physically constrained trajectories from a few demonstrations, providing a data foundation for policy training. Second, FAR-DexRes introduces an adaptive residual module that refines policies by combining multi-step trajectory segments with observation features, thereby enhancing accuracy and robustness in manipulation scenarios. Experiments in both simulation and real-world demonstrate that FAR-Dex improves data quality by 13.4% and task success rates by 7% over state-of-the-art methods. It further achieves over 80% success in real-world tasks, enabling fine-grained dexterous manipulation with strong positional generalization.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical, data-efficient framework (FAR-Dex) for dexterous manipulation combining few-shot data augmentation and adaptive residual refinement to achieve robust arm-hand coordination, with notable improvements in data quality and success rates in both simulation and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of high-quality demonstrations and the high dimensionality of dexterous manipulation, which hinder robust, generalizable policies for arm-hand coordination.
AI.method: FAR-DexGen uses IsaacLab to generate diverse, physically constrained trajectories from few demonstrations to augment training data. FAR-DexRes introduces an adaptive residual module that refines policies by fusing multi-step trajectory segments with observation features, improving accuracy and robustness.
AI.result: Simulation and real-world experiments show 13.4% improvement in data quality and 7% higher task success rate compared with state-of-the-art methods; real-world success exceeds 80%, enabling fine-grained dexterous manipulation with strong positional generalization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical FAR-Dex framework effectively addresses data scarcity and high-dimensional control for dexterous manipulation, delivering robust performance and strong generalization, with potential applicability to broader dexterous tasks.

title: ReMix: Reinforcement routing for mixtures of LoRAs in LLM finetuning
summary: Low-rank adapters (LoRAs) are a parameter-efficient finetuning technique that injects trainable low-rank matrices into pretrained models to adapt them to new tasks. Mixture-of-LoRAs models expand neural networks efficiently by routing each layer input to a small subset of specialized LoRAs of the layer. Existing Mixture-of-LoRAs routers assign a learned routing weight to each LoRA to enable end-to-end training of the router. Despite their empirical promise, we observe that the routing weights are typically extremely imbalanced across LoRAs in practice, where only one or two LoRAs often dominate the routing weights. This essentially limits the number of effective LoRAs and thus severely hinders the expressive power of existing Mixture-of-LoRAs models. In this work, we attribute this weakness to the nature of learnable routing weights and rethink the fundamental design of the router. To address this critical issue, we propose a new router designed that we call Reinforcement Routing for Mixture-of-LoRAs (ReMix). Our key idea is using non-learnable routing weights to ensure all active LoRAs to be equally effective, with no LoRA dominating the routing weights. However, our routers cannot be trained directly via gradient descent due to our non-learnable routing weights. Hence, we further propose an unbiased gradient estimator for the router by employing the reinforce leave-one-out (RLOO) technique, where we regard the supervision loss as the reward and the router as the policy in reinforcement learning. Our gradient estimator also enables to scale up training compute to boost the predictive performance of our ReMix. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed ReMix significantly outperform state-of-the-art parameter-efficient finetuning methods under a comparable number of activated parameters.

AI.tldr: ReMix introduces non-learnable routing weights for Mixture-of-LoRAs, paired with an unbiased reinforcement learning gradient estimator (RLOO), to prevent dominance by a single LoRA and improve expressivity and performance with a fixed number of active parameters.
AI.motivation: Mixture-of-LoRAs often suffer from highly imbalanced routing weights, where only one or two LoRAs dominate, reducing effective model capacity and expressive power. A router design that keeps multiple LoRAs actively contributing is desirable for better adaptation with parameter efficiency.
AI.method: Replace learnable routing weights with non-learnable ones to ensure equal activity among LoRAs. Develop an unbiased gradient estimator based on reinforce leave-one-out (RLOO) that treats the supervision loss as the reward and the router as the policy, enabling gradient-based training despite non-learnable weights.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that ReMix significantly outperforms state-of-the-art parameter-efficient finetuning methods given a comparable number of activated parameters.
AI.conclusion: Non-learnable routing weights combined with RLOO-based training restore and exploit multiple active LoRAs in Mixture-of-LoRAs, yielding superior performance and sustained expressivity over traditional learnable-router MoLoRAs.

title: LCAMV: High-Accuracy 3D Reconstruction of Color-Varying Objects Using LCA Correction and Minimum-Variance Fusion in Structured Light
summary: Accurate 3D reconstruction of colored objects with structured light (SL) is hindered by lateral chromatic aberration (LCA) in optical components and uneven noise characteristics across RGB channels. This paper introduces lateral chromatic aberration correction and minimum-variance fusion (LCAMV), a robust 3D reconstruction method that operates with a single projector-camera pair without additional hardware or acquisition constraints. LCAMV analytically models and pixel-wise compensates LCA in both the projector and camera, then adaptively fuses multi-channel phase data using a Poisson-Gaussian noise model and minimum-variance estimation. Unlike existing methods that require extra hardware or multiple exposures, LCAMV enables fast acquisition. Experiments on planar and non-planar colored surfaces show that LCAMV outperforms grayscale conversion and conventional channel-weighting, reducing depth error by up to 43.6\%. These results establish LCAMV as an effective solution for high-precision 3D reconstruction of nonuniformly colored objects.

AI.tldr: LCAMV: A single-shot structured-light method that corrects lateral chromatic aberration and fuses RGB phase data under a Poisson-Gaussian noise model using minimum-variance estimation for robust 3D reconstruction of colored objects.
AI.motivation: LCA and channel-dependent noise degrade colored SL reconstruction; existing solutions rely on extra hardware or multiple exposures, limiting speed and practicality.
AI.method: Analytically model and pixel-wise compensate LCA in both projector and camera; adaptively fuse multi-channel phase using a Poisson-Gaussian noise model and minimum-variance estimation, enabling single-pair, hardware-light acquisition.
AI.result: Experiments on planar and non-planar colored surfaces show depth error reduction up to 43.6% compared with grayscale conversion and conventional channel weighting; faster acquisition without extra hardware.
AI.conclusion: LCAMV provides high-precision 3D reconstruction of nonuniformly colored objects with a single projector-camera pair, outperforming traditional grayscale and channel-weighted approaches.

title: DT-BEHRT: Disease Trajectory-aware Transformer for Interpretable Patient Representation Learning
summary: The growing adoption of electronic health record (EHR) systems has provided unprecedented opportunities for predictive modeling to guide clinical decision making. Structured EHRs contain longitudinal observations of patients across hospital visits, where each visit is represented by a set of medical codes. While sequence-based, graph-based, and graph-enhanced sequence approaches have been developed to capture rich code interactions over time or within the same visits, they often overlook the inherent heterogeneous roles of medical codes arising from distinct clinical characteristics and contexts. To this end, in this study we propose the Disease Trajectory-aware Transformer for EHR (DT-BEHRT), a graph-enhanced sequential architecture that disentangles disease trajectories by explicitly modeling diagnosis-centric interactions within organ systems and capturing asynchronous progression patterns. To further enhance the representation robustness, we design a tailored pre-training methodology that combines trajectory-level code masking with ontology-informed ancestor prediction, promoting semantic alignment across multiple modeling modules. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that DT-BEHRT achieves strong predictive performance and provides interpretable patient representations that align with clinicians' disease-centered reasoning. The source code is publicly accessible at https://github.com/GatorAIM/DT-BEHRT.git.

AI.tldr: DT-BEHRT is a graph-enhanced transformer for EHR data that disentangles disease trajectories by modeling diagnosis-centric interactions within organ systems and asynchronous progression, aided by trajectory-level masking and ontology-informed pre-training to improve robustness and interpretability.
AI.motivation: To address the heterogeneous roles of medical codes and asynchronous disease progression in structured EHRs, enabling more accurate predictions and clinically meaningful representations.
AI.method: A graph-enhanced sequential transformer that (1) disentangles disease trajectories via diagnosis-centric, organ-system interactions; (2) captures asynchronous progression patterns; (3) employs trajectory-level code masking and ontology-informed ancestor prediction for pre-training; (4) yields robust, interpretable patient representations.
AI.result: DT-BEHRT achieves strong predictive performance on benchmark EHR datasets and provides interpretable representations that align with clinicians' disease-centered reasoning; source code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that incorporating disease-trajectory structure and ontology-guided pre-training improves predictive accuracy and interpretability in EHR modeling, with practical access to code for reproducibility.

title: Learning to Wander: Improving the Global Image Geolocation Ability of LMMs via Actionable Reasoning
summary: Geolocation, the task of identifying the geographic location of an image, requires abundant world knowledge and complex reasoning abilities. Though advanced large multimodal models (LMMs) have shown superior aforementioned capabilities, their performance on the geolocation task remains unexplored. To this end, we introduce \textbf{WanderBench}, the first open access global geolocation benchmark designed for actionable geolocation reasoning in embodied scenarios. WanderBench contains over 32K panoramas across six continents, organized as navigable graphs that enable physical actions such as rotation and movement, transforming geolocation from static recognition into interactive exploration. Building on this foundation, we propose \textbf{GeoAoT} (Action of Thought), a \underline{Geo}location framework with \underline{A}ction of \underline{T}hough, which couples reasoning with embodied actions. Instead of generating textual reasoning chains, GeoAoT produces actionable plans such as, approaching landmarks or adjusting viewpoints, to actively reduce uncertainty. We further establish an evaluation protocol that jointly measures geolocation accuracy and difficulty-aware geolocation questioning ability. Experiments on 19 large multimodal models show that GeoAoT achieves superior fine-grained localization and stronger generalization in dynamic environments. WanderBench and GeoAoT define a new paradigm for actionable, reasoning driven geolocation in embodied visual understanding.

AI.tldr: Open-access benchmark WanderBench and GeoAoT framework enable actionable, embodied geolocation reasoning, improving localization accuracy and generalization across 19 LMMs.
AI.motivation: Geolocation is knowledge- and reasoning-intensive, and current large multimodal models lack interactive, embodied reasoning for this task. A benchmark and a method that integrates action are needed.
AI.method: WanderBench: 32k panoramas on navigable graphs across six continents enabling rotation/movement. GeoAoT: a reasoning framework that outputs actionable plans (e.g., approach landmarks, adjust viewpoints) rather than textual chains. An evaluation protocol jointly assesses accuracy and difficulty-aware questioning. Experiments with 19 LMMs.
AI.result: GeoAoT achieves superior fine-grained localization and stronger generalization in dynamic environments; the pair defines a new paradigm for embodied, actionable geolocation.
AI.conclusion: WanderBench and GeoAoT advance embodied geolocation by combining interactive environments with action-oriented reasoning, fostering more capable multimodal geolocation systems.

title: DepthCache: Depth-Guided Training-Free Visual Token Merging for Vision-Language-Action Model Inference
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable generalist robotic manipulation but suffer from high inference latency. This bottleneck stems from the massive number of visual tokens processed by large language backbones. Existing methods either prune or merge tokens uniformly, degrading the spatial reasoning essential for robotic control. We present DepthCache, a training-free framework that leverages depth as a structural prior for visual token compression. It partitions observations into depth-based regions and applies spatially differentiated merge ratios, preserving the near-field workspace while compressing the distant background. To exploit temporal redundancy, DepthCache distributes the merging process across consecutive frames, ensuring consistent representations while reducing per-step computation. A motion-adaptive pipeline further optimizes auxiliary view compression based on end-effector dynamics. The framework requires no model modification, generalizing across diverse VLA architectures. On the LIBERO benchmark, DepthCache achieves up to 1.28x inference speedup with less than 1% average success rate degradation across three VLA models (pi_0.5, OpenVLA, GR00T), whereas pruning and merging baselines incur 4--24% degradation at comparable compression. Real-world experiments on a physical manipulator demonstrate that DepthCache enables faster task throughput and more responsive closed-loop control in latency-sensitive scenarios.

AI.tldr: DepthCache is a training-free, depth-guided token compression framework for Vision-Language-Action models, achieving up to 1.28x speedup on LIBERO with minimal (<1%) accuracy loss, via depth-based region partitioning, temporal merging, and motion-adaptive compression, without modifying models.
AI.motivation: VLA inference latency stems from processing大量 visual tokens in large backbones; uniform pruning/merging reduces spatial reasoning, harming robotic control.
AI.method: Partition observations by depth into regions; apply spatially differentiated merge ratios preserving near-field; distribute merging across consecutive frames to maintain temporal consistency; introduce a motion-adaptive pipeline for auxiliary view compression based on end-effector dynamics; no model modifications; training-free; generalizes across diverse VLA architectures.
AI.result: LIBERO benchmark shows up to 1.28x speedup with <1% average success-rate degradation across pi_0.5, OpenVLA, GR00T; pruning/merging baselines degrade 4–24% at comparable compression; real-world experiments show faster task throughput and more responsive closed-loop control.
AI.conclusion: DepthCache provides a practical, architecture-agnostic, training-free approach to reduce VLA inference latency with minimal performance loss, validated on both benchmarks and a real robot.

title: Actor-Accelerated Policy Dual Averaging for Reinforcement Learning in Continuous Action Spaces
summary: Policy Dual Averaging (PDA) offers a principled Policy Mirror Descent (PMD) framework that more naturally admits value function approximation than standard PMD, enabling the use of approximate advantage (or Q-) functions while retaining strong convergence guarantees. However, applying PDA in continuous state and action spaces remains computationally challenging, since action selection involves solving an optimization sub-problem at each decision step. In this paper, we propose \textit{actor-accelerated PDA}, which uses a learned policy network to approximate the solution of the optimization sub-problems, yielding faster runtimes while maintaining convergence guarantees. We provide a theoretical analysis that quantifies how actor approximation error impacts the convergence of PDA under suitable assumptions. We then evaluate its performance on several benchmarks in robotics, control, and operations research problems. Actor-accelerated PDA achieves superior performance compared to popular on-policy baselines such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). Overall, our results bridge the gap between the theoretical advantages of PDA and its practical deployment in continuous-action problems with function approximation.

AI.tldr: Actor-accelerated Policy Dual Averaging (PDA) uses a learned policy network to approximate the action-selection subproblem in continuous PDA, achieving faster runtimes while preserving convergence and outperforming PPO on several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: PDA offers convergence-friendly policy optimization with value-function approximation, but solving the per-step optimization subproblem in continuous state-action spaces is computationally heavy. There is a need to make PDA practical by leveraging function approximation without sacrificing convergence.
AI.method: Introduce an actor-accelerated PDA that uses a learned policy network to approximate the optimization sub-problems in PDA/PMD. Provide a theoretical analysis of how actor approximation error affects PDA convergence under suitable assumptions. Empirically evaluate on robotics, control, and operations research benchmarks, comparing against on-policy baselines such as PPO.
AI.result: The approach yields faster runtimes due to the amortized subproblem solution, while maintaining convergence guarantees. It achieves superior performance relative to PPO on the tested benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Actor-accelerated PDA closes the gap between the theoretical advantages of PDA and practical deployment in continuous-action problems with function approximation, enabling efficient and provably convergent policy optimization.

title: UniPINN: A Unified PINN Framework for Multi-task Learning of Diverse Navier-Stokes Equations
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) have shown promise in solving incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, yet existing approaches are predominantly designed for single-flow settings. When extended to multi-flow scenarios, these methods face three key challenges: (1) difficulty in simultaneously capturing both shared physical principles and flow-specific characteristics, (2) susceptibility to inter-task negative transfer that degrades prediction accuracy, and (3) unstable training dynamics caused by disparate loss magnitudes across heterogeneous flow regimes. To address these limitations, we propose UniPINN, a unified multi-flow PINN framework that integrates three complementary components: a shared-specialized architecture that disentangles universal physical laws from flow-specific features, a cross-flow attention mechanism that selectively reinforces relevant patterns while suppressing task-irrelevant interference, and a dynamic weight allocation strategy that adaptively balances loss contributions to stabilize multi-objective optimization. Extensive experiments on three canonical flows demonstrate that UniPINN effectively unifies multi-flow learning, achieving superior prediction accuracy and balanced performance across heterogeneous regimes while successfully mitigating negative transfer. The source code of this paper will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/OpenFusion

AI.tldr: UniPINN proposes a unified multi-flow PINN with shared-specialized architecture, cross-flow attention, and dynamic loss weighting to jointly learn multiple flows, achieving better accuracy and balanced performance while mitigating negative transfer.
AI.motivation: To address three issues in multi-flow PINNs: capturing shared physics and flow-specific traits, preventing negative transfer between tasks, and stabilizing heterogeneous loss magnitudes during training.
AI.method: Three components: (1) shared-specialized architecture that disentangles universal PDE physics from flow-specific features; (2) cross-flow attention to reinforce relevant patterns and suppress interference; (3) dynamic loss weighting to adaptively balance task losses for stable multi-task optimization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three canonical flows shows superior predictive accuracy and balanced performance across regimes, with mitigated negative transfer; code will be released on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: UniPINN enables robust, unified multi-flow learning in PINNs, offering improved generalization across heterogeneous flow regimes and a practical path toward multi-flow physics-informed modeling.

title: BinWalker: Development and Field Evaluation of a Quadruped Manipulator Platform for Sustainable Litter Collection
summary: Litter pollution represents a growing environmental problem affecting natural and urban ecosystems worldwide. Waste discarded in public spaces often accumulates in areas that are difficult to access, such as uneven terrains, coastal environments, parks, and roadside vegetation. Over time, these materials degrade and release harmful substances, including toxic chemicals and microplastics, which can contaminate soil and water and pose serious threats to wildlife and human health. Despite increasing awareness of the problem, litter collection is still largely performed manually by human operators, making large-scale cleanup operations labor-intensive, time-consuming, and costly. Robotic solutions have the potential to support and partially automate environmental cleanup tasks. In this work, we present a quadruped robotic system designed for autonomous litter collection in challenging outdoor scenarios. The robot combines the mobility advantages of legged locomotion with a manipulation system consisting of a robotic arm and an onboard litter container. This configuration enables the robot to detect, grasp, and store litter items while navigating through uneven terrains. The proposed system aims to demonstrate the feasibility of integrating perception, locomotion, and manipulation on a legged robotic platform for environmental cleanup tasks. Experimental evaluations conducted in outdoor scenarios highlight the effectiveness of the approach and its potential for assisting large-scale litter removal operations in environments that are difficult to reach with traditional robotic platforms. The code associated with this work can be found at: https://github.com/iit-DLSLab/trash-collection-isaaclab.

AI.tldr: Quadruped outdoor robot for autonomous litter collection integrating perception, locomotion, and a manipulation system with a bin; demonstrated in uneven terrains; open-source code available.
AI.motivation: Litter pollution is a growing environmental problem that is labor-intensive to clean manually; robots can access difficult terrains and enable scalable cleanup operations.
AI.method: A legged quadruped equipped with a robotic arm and onboard litter container enabling detection, grasping, and storage while navigating uneven outdoor terrain; integration of perception, locomotion, and manipulation; outdoor experiments; code provided at GitHub.
AI.result: Outdoor experiments validate feasibility and potential for aiding large-scale litter removal on terrains inaccessible to traditional robots, demonstrating end-to-end capabilities from sensing to collection.
AI.conclusion: The work supports the feasibility of legged robotic platforms for environmental cleanup and points to potential for scaling cleanup operations, with future work likely focusing on robustness, efficiency, and deployment at scale.

title: Rethinking the Harmonic Loss via Non-Euclidean Distance Layers
summary: Cross-entropy loss has long been the standard choice for training deep neural networks, yet it suffers from interpretability limitations, unbounded weight growth, and inefficiencies that can contribute to costly training dynamics. The harmonic loss is a distance-based alternative grounded in Euclidean geometry that improves interpretability and mitigates phenomena such as grokking, or delayed generalization on the test set. However, the study of harmonic loss remains narrow: only Euclidean distance is explored, and no systematic evaluation of computational efficiency or sustainability was conducted. We extend harmonic loss by systematically investigating a broad spectrum of distance metrics as replacements for the Euclidean distance. We comprehensively evaluate distance-tailored harmonic losses on both vision backbones and large language models. Our analysis is framed around a three-way evaluation of model performance, interpretability, and sustainability. On vision tasks, cosine distances provide the most favorable trade-off, consistently improving accuracy while lowering carbon emissions, whereas Bray-Curtis and Mahalanobis further enhance interpretability at varying efficiency costs. On language models, cosine-based harmonic losses improve gradient and learning stability, strengthen representation structure, and reduce emissions relative to cross-entropy and Euclidean heads. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/rethinking-harmonic-loss-5BAB/.

AI.tldr: Cosine-distance harmonic loss offers favorable trade-offs across vision and language tasks, improving accuracy and sustainability relative to standard cross-entropy and Euclidean harmonic loss; systematic evaluation across multiple distances.
AI.motivation: Address interpretability and training inefficiencies of cross-entropy; extend harmonic loss beyond Euclidean distance to study impact of various distance metrics on model performance, interpretability, and sustainability.
AI.method: Systematically replace Euclidean distance in harmonic loss with a broad spectrum of distance metrics (e.g., cosine, Bray-Curtis, Mahalanobis) and evaluate on vision backbones and large language models; perform a three-way evaluation on performance, interpretability, and sustainability (carbon emissions).
AI.result: Cosine-based harmonic losses yield the best overall trade-off for vision tasks (higher accuracy, lower emissions); Bray-Curtis and Mahalanobis enhance interpretability with some efficiency costs. For language models, cosine-based harmonic losses improve gradient/learning stability, strengthen representation structure, and reduce emissions relative to cross-entropy and Euclidean heads.
AI.conclusion: Distance-tailored harmonic losses, especially using cosine distance, provide improved performance, stability, and sustainability across vision and language models; the approach offers a more interpretable and efficient alternative to cross-entropy.

title: Fighting Hallucinations with Counterfactuals: Diffusion-Guided Perturbations for LVLM Hallucination Suppression
summary: While large vision-language models (LVLMs) achieve strong performance on multimodal tasks, they frequently generate hallucinations -- unfaithful outputs misaligned with the visual input. To address this issue, we introduce CIPHER (Counterfactual Image Perturbations for Hallucination Extraction and Removal), a training-free method that suppresses vision-induced hallucinations via lightweight feature-level correction. Unlike prior training-free approaches that primarily focus on text-induced hallucinations, CIPHER explicitly targets hallucinations arising from the visual modality. CIPHER operates in two phases. In the offline phase, we construct OHC-25K (Object-Hallucinated Counterfactuals, 25,000 samples), a counterfactual dataset consisting of diffusion-edited images that intentionally contradict the original ground-truth captions. We pair these edited images with the unchanged ground-truth captions and process them through an LVLM to extract hallucination-related representations. Contrasting these representations with those from authentic (image, caption) pairs reveals structured, systematic shifts spanning a low-rank subspace characterizing vision-induced hallucination. In the inference phase, CIPHER suppresses hallucinations by projecting intermediate hidden states away from this subspace. Experiments across multiple benchmarks show that CIPHER significantly reduces hallucination rates while preserving task performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of counterfactual visual perturbations for improving LVLM faithfulness. Code and additional materials are available at https://hamidreza-dastmalchi.github.io/cipher-cvpr2026/.

AI.tldr: CIPHER is a training-free method that mitigates vision-induced hallucinations in LVLMs by counterfactual image perturbations and a subspace projection to suppress hallucination while preserving task performance.
AI.motivation: Vision-driven hallucinations degrade fidelity of LVLM outputs; training-free approaches focusing on the visual modality are underexplored.
AI.method: Offline: build OHC-25K dataset of diffusion-edited counterfactual images paired with true captions; extract hallucination-related representations from LVLM; identify a low-rank subspace of vision-induced hallucinations by comparing with authentic pairs. Inference: project hidden states away from this subspace to suppress hallucinations.
AI.result: Substantial reduction in hallucination rates across benchmarks with preserved task performance.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual perturbations reveal a low-rank structure of vision-induced hallucinations; a lightweight, training-free subspace projection approach effectively improves LVLM faithfulness.

title: TacLoc: Global Tactile Localization on Objects from a Registration Perspective
summary: Pose estimation is essential for robotic manipulation, particularly when visual perception is occluded during gripper-object interactions. Existing tactile-based methods generally rely on tactile simulation or pre-trained models, which limits their generalizability and efficiency. In this study, we propose TacLoc, a novel tactile localization framework that formulates the problem as a one-shot point cloud registration task. TacLoc introduces a graph-theoretic partial-to-full registration method, leveraging dense point clouds and surface normals from tactile sensing for efficient and accurate pose estimation. Without requiring rendered data or pre-trained models, TacLoc achieves improved performance through normal-guided graph pruning and a hypothesis-and-verification pipeline. TacLoc is evaluated extensively on the YCB dataset. We further demonstrate TacLoc on real-world objects across two different visual-tactile sensors.

AI.tldr: TacLoc reframes tactile-based pose estimation as one-shot 3D point cloud registration, using dense tactile point clouds and normals with a graph-theoretic partial-to-full registration approach for efficient, pre-trained-model-free localization.
AI.motivation: Visual occlusion during gripper-object interaction limits perception; existing tactile methods rely on simulation or pre-trained models, reducing generalizability and efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes TacLoc, a one-shot point cloud registration framework that employs a graph-theoretic partial-to-full registration, dense tactile point clouds, and surface normals. It uses normal-guided graph pruning and a hypothesis-and-verification pipeline, without rendering or pre-trained models.
AI.result: Achieves improved pose estimation performance on the YCB dataset and real-world objects across two different tactile sensors.
AI.conclusion: TacLoc provides accurate, generalizable tactile localization without rendered data or pre-trained models, leveraging normal information and graph-based pruning for efficient one-shot registration.

title: SiMPO: Measure Matching for Online Diffusion Reinforcement Learning
summary: A commonly used family of RL algorithms for diffusion policies conducts softmax reweighting over the behavior policy, which usually induces an over-greedy policy and fails to leverage feedback from negative samples. In this work, we introduce Signed Measure Policy Optimization (SiMPO), a simple and unified framework that generalizes reweighting scheme in diffusion RL with general monotonic functions. SiMPO revisits diffusion RL via a two-stage measure matching lens. First, we construct a virtual target policy by $f$-divergence regularized policy optimization, where we can relax the non-negativity constraint to allow for a signed target measure. Second, we use this signed measure to guide diffusion or flow models through reweighted matching. This formulation offers two key advantages: a) it generalizes to arbitrary monotonically increasing weighting functions; and b) it provides a principled justification and practical guidance for negative reweighting. Furthermore, we provide geometric interpretations to illustrate how negative reweighting actively repels the policy from suboptimal actions. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that SiMPO achieves superior performance by leveraging these flexible weighting schemes, and we provide practical guidelines for selecting reweighting methods tailored to the reward landscape.

AI.tldr: SiMPO introduces Signed Measure Policy Optimization, a diffusion RL framework that uses f-divergence regularization to produce a signed (possibly negative) target measure and reweights diffusion/flow models for policy optimization. It unifies varying monotone weighting schemes, offers a principled rationale for negative reweighting, and provides geometric insights into repulsion from suboptimal actions; empirical results show improved performance with guidance for choosing reweighting methods based on reward landscapes.
AI.motivation: Address over-greedy diffusion-policy RL and underutilization of negative samples by enabling flexible reweighting beyond nonnegative importance weights; provide a unified, principled framework that can incorporate negative reweighting and arbitrary monotone weight functions.
AI.method: Two-stage measure matching: (1) construct a virtual target policy via f-divergence regularized policy optimization with relaxed non-negativity, yielding a signed target measure; (2) use this signed measure to guide diffusion/flow models through reweighted matching with arbitrary monotonically increasing weights; provide geometric interpretations of negative reweighting as repulsion.
AI.result: Extensive empirical evaluations show SiMPO achieves superior performance by leveraging flexible weighting schemes; the authors also provide practical guidelines for selecting reweighting methods aligned with the reward landscape.
AI.conclusion: SiMPO generalizes diffusion-policy reweighting to arbitrary monotone functions and enables principled negative reweighting, offering theoretical justification and practical guidance, along with competitive empirical gains and geometric insight into policy repulsion from suboptimal actions.

title: StructDamage:A Large Scale Unified Crack and Surface Defect Dataset for Robust Structural Damage Detection
summary: Automated detection and classification of structural cracks and surface defects is a critical challenge in civil engineering, infrastructure maintenance, and heritage preservation. Recent advances in Computer Vision (CV) and Deep Learning (DL) have significantly improved automatic crack detection. However, these methods rely heavily on large, diverse, and carefully curated datasets that include various crack types across different surface materials. Many existing public crack datasets lack geographic diversity, surface types, scale, and labeling consistency, making it challenging for trained algorithms to generalize effectively in real world conditions. We provide a novel dataset, StructDamage, a curated collection of approximately 78,093 images spanning nine surface types: walls, tile, stone, road, pavement, deck, concrete, and brick. The dataset was constructed by systematically aggregating, harmonizing, and reannotating images from 32 publicly available datasets covering concrete structures, asphalt pavements, masonry walls, bridges, and historic buildings. All images are organized in a folder level classification hierarchy suitable for training Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers. To highlight the practical value of the dataset, we present baseline classification results using fifteen DL architectures from six model families, with twelve achieving macro F1-scores over 0.96. The best performing model DenseNet201 achieves 98.62% accuracy. The proposed dataset provides a comprehensive and versatile resource suitable for classification tasks. With thorough documentation and a standard structure, it is designed to promote reproducible research and support the development and fair evaluation of robust crack damage detection approaches.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive, standardized dataset (StructDamage) with ~78k images across 9 surface types for crack and surface-defect classification, harmonized from 32 public datasets; strong baseline results (12/15 models with macro F1 > 0.96; DenseNet201 at 98.62% accuracy); designed to enable reproducible research and robust evaluation in CV/DL crack detection.
AI.motivation: Public crack datasets lack geographic, material, scale, and labeling diversity, hindering model generalization to real-world conditions; a diversified, well-documented dataset is needed.
AI.method: Aggregate and harmonize images from 32 public datasets covering concrete structures, asphalt pavements, masonry walls, bridges, and historic buildings; annotate and organize by folder-level classes suitable for CNNs and Vision Transformers; benchmark with 15 DL architectures from six model families.
AI.result: Macro F1-scores above 0.96 for 12 of 15 models; DenseNet201 achieves 98.62% accuracy; dataset supports classification tasks and robust evaluation.
AI.conclusion: StructDamage offers a comprehensive, standardized dataset with thorough documentation, enabling fair comparisons, reproducibility, and improved generalization in crack damage detection across diverse surface types.

title: Safety-critical Control Under Partial Observability: Reach-Avoid POMDP meets Belief Space Control
summary: Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) provide a principled framework for robot decision-making under uncertainty. Solving reach-avoid POMDPs, however, requires coordinating three distinct behaviors: goal reaching, safety, and active information gathering to reduce uncertainty. Existing online POMDP solvers attempt to address all three within a single belief tree search, but this unified approach struggles with the conflicting time scales inherent to these objectives. We propose a layered, certificate-based control architecture that operates directly in belief space, decoupling goal reaching, information gathering, and safety into modular components. We introduce Belief Control Lyapunov Functions (BCLFs) that formalize information gathering as a Lyapunov convergence problem in belief space, and show how they can be learned via reinforcement learning. For safety, we develop Belief Control Barrier Functions (BCBFs) that leverage conformal prediction to provide probabilistic safety guarantees over finite horizons. The resulting control synthesis reduces to lightweight quadratic programs solvable in real time, even for non-Gaussian belief representations with dimension $>10^4$. Experiments in simulation and on a space-robotics platform demonstrate real-time performance and improved safety and task success compared to state-of-the-art constrained POMDP solvers.

AI.tldr: Layered belief-space controller for reach-avoid POMDPs using BCLFs and BCBFs, enabling real-time QP-based control with probabilistic safety and learning-enabled information gathering; validated on simulations and space robotics.
AI.motivation: Need to coordinate goal attainment, safety, and information gathering under uncertainty; single-tree POMDP solvers struggle due to conflicting time scales; scalable, modular, real-time approach desirable.
AI.method: Decoupled, certificate-based architecture operating in belief space; Belief Control Lyapunov Functions (BCLFs) for information gathering as Lyapunov convergence; learned via reinforcement learning. Belief Control Barrier Functions (BCBFs) with conformal prediction for probabilistic safety over finite horizons. Controller reduces to lightweight QPs.
AI.result: Real-time performance; improved safety and task success versus state-of-the-art constrained POMDP solvers; handles non-Gaussian beliefs with >10^4 dimensions; validated in simulation and on space-robotics platform.
AI.conclusion: Certificate-based, modular belief-space control yields scalable reach-avoid solutions with provable safety and learning-enabled information gathering; suitable for high-dimensional, non-Gaussian beliefs and real-time deployment.

title: Improving TabPFN's Synthetic Data Generation by Integrating Causal Structure
comment: 8 pages main text, 30 pages total (including supplementary material), 27 figures. Code: https://github.com/DavideTugnoli/tabpfn-causal-synthetic
summary: Synthetic tabular data generation addresses data scarcity and privacy constraints in a variety of domains. Tabular Prior-Data Fitted Network (TabPFN), a recent foundation model for tabular data, has been shown capable of generating high-quality synthetic tabular data. However, TabPFN is autoregressive: features are generated sequentially by conditioning on the previous ones, depending on the order in which they appear in the input data. We demonstrate that when the feature order conflicts with causal structure, the model produces spurious correlations that impair its ability to generate synthetic data and preserve causal effects. We address this limitation by integrating causal structure into TabPFN's generation process through two complementary approaches: Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)-aware conditioning, which samples each variable given its causal parents, and a Completed Partially Directed Acyclic Graph (CPDAG)-based strategy for scenarios with partial causal knowledge. We evaluate these approaches on controlled benchmarks and six CSuite datasets, assessing structural fidelity, distributional alignment, privacy preservation, and Average Treatment Effect (ATE) preservation. Across most settings, DAG-aware conditioning improves the quality and stability of synthetic data relative to vanilla TabPFN. The CPDAG-based strategy shows moderate improvements, with effectiveness depending on the number of oriented edges. These results indicate that injecting causal structure into autoregressive generation enhances the reliability of synthetic tabular data.

AI.tldr: Incorporating causal structure into TabPFN via DAG-aware conditioning (and CPDAG when only partial knowledge is available) improves the quality, stability, and causal fidelity of synthetic tabular data compared with the baseline autoregressive TabPFN.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive tabular data generation can create spurious correlations when the feature order conflicts with the underlying causal structure, undermining causal effect preservation and data utility.
