AI.method: Two complementary strategies are proposed: (1) DAG-aware conditioning that samples each variable conditioned on its causal parents, and (2) CPDAG-based conditioning for scenarios with partial causal knowledge. These are integrated into the TabPFN generation process and evaluated on controlled benchmarks and six CSuite datasets.
AI.result: DAG-aware conditioning improves data quality and stability relative to vanilla TabPFN across most settings. The CPDAG-based strategy yields moderate improvements, with effectiveness depending on the number of oriented edges.
AI.conclusion: Injecting causal structure into autoregressive generation enhances the reliability, structural fidelity, and causal preservation of synthetic tabular data.

title: Spatial self-supervised Peak Learning and correlation-based Evaluation of peak picking in Mass Spectrometry Imaging
summary: Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) enables label-free visualization of molecular distributions across tissue samples but generates large and complex datasets that require effective peak picking to reduce data size while preserving meaningful biological information. Existing peak picking approaches perform inconsistently across heterogeneous datasets, and their evaluation is often limited to synthetic data or manually selected ion images that do not fully represent real-world challenges in MSI. To address these limitations, we propose an autoencoder-based spatial self-supervised peak learning neural network that selects spatially structured peaks by learning an attention mask leveraging both spatial and spectral information. We further introduce an evaluation procedure based on expert-annotated segmentation masks, allowing a more representative and spatially grounded assessment of peak picking performance. We evaluate our approach on four diverse public MSI datasets using our proposed evaluation procedure. Our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art peak picking methods by selecting spatially structured peaks, thus demonstrating its efficacy. These results highlight the value of our spatial self-supervised network in comparison to contemporary state-of-the-art methods. The evaluation procedure can be readily applied to new MSI datasets, thereby providing a consistent and robust framework for the comparison of spatially structured peak picking methods across different datasets.

AI.tldr: A new autoencoder-based spatial self-supervised peak learning network for MSI peak picking uses a spectral-spatial attention mask to select spatially structured peaks, with a novel expert-annotated segmentation-based evaluation across four public datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods and providing a transferable evaluation framework.
AI.motivation: MSI data are large, heterogeneous, and noisy; existing peak picking methods show inconsistent performance and evaluations often rely on synthetic data or hand-picked ions, failing to reflect real-world challenges in MSI.
AI.method: An autoencoder-based spatial self-supervised neural network learns a spectral-spatial attention mask to select peaks with spatial structure. Training uses self-supervision leveraging both spatial and spectral information. Evaluation uses expert-annotated segmentation masks and tests on four diverse public MSI datasets, comparing against state-of-the-art peak-picking methods.
AI.result: The proposed approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art peak picking methods in selecting spatially structured peaks and provides a robust, ground-truth-aligned evaluation framework applicable to new MSI datasets.
AI.conclusion: Spatial self-supervised peak learning effectively enhances peak picking in MSI, and the expert-annotated segmentation-based evaluation framework offers a robust, generalizable means to compare methods across diverse datasets.

title: Recover to Predict: Progressive Retrospective Learning for Variable-Length Trajectory Prediction
summary: Trajectory prediction is critical for autonomous driving, enabling safe and efficient planning in dense, dynamic traffic. Most existing methods optimize prediction accuracy under fixed-length observations. However, real-world driving often yields variable-length, incomplete observations, posing a challenge to these methods. A common strategy is to directly map features from incomplete observations to those from complete ones. This one-shot mapping, however, struggles to learn accurate representations for short trajectories due to significant information gaps. To address this issue, we propose a Progressive Retrospective Framework (PRF), which gradually aligns features from incomplete observations with those from complete ones via a cascade of retrospective units. Each unit consists of a Retrospective Distillation Module (RDM) and a Retrospective Prediction Module (RPM), where RDM distills features and RPM recovers previous timesteps using the distilled features. Moreover, we propose a Rolling-Start Training Strategy (RSTS) that enhances data efficiency during PRF training. PRF is plug-and-play with existing methods. Extensive experiments on datasets Argoverse 2 and Argoverse 1 demonstrate the effectiveness of PRF. Code is available at https://github.com/zhouhao94/PRF.

AI.tldr: A progressive retrospective framework (PRF) enhances trajectory prediction under variable-length, incomplete observations by progressively aligning incomplete features with complete ones via cascading Retrospective Distillation (RDM) and Retrospective Prediction (RPM) units, aided by Rolling-Start Training Strategy (RSTS); it is plug-and-play with existing methods and validated on Argoverse 1/2 with code available.
AI.motivation: Current trajectory prediction methods assume fixed-length observations and struggle with incomplete, short trajectories that create information gaps. A direct one-shot mapping from incomplete to complete features is insufficient for accurate representation learning.
AI.method: PRF introduces a cascade of retrospective units. Each unit contains a Retrospective Distillation Module (RDM) that distills features and a Retrospective Prediction Module (RPM) that recovers previous timesteps using the distilled features. The framework progressively aligns incomplete observations to complete ones across units. A Rolling-Start Training Strategy (RSTS) improves data efficiency during training. PRF is designed to be plug-and-play with existing trajectory prediction models.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Argoverse 2 and Argoverse 1 show the effectiveness of PRF in improving trajectory prediction with incomplete observations. The authors provide code online, indicating practical applicability and reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: PRF offers a practical, modular approach to handling variable-length, incomplete observations in trajectory prediction by progressively aligning features through RDM/RPM units and employing RSTS for efficient training; it can augment existing methods without modification.

title: Discovery of a Hematopoietic Manifold in scGPT Yields a Method for Extracting Performant Algorithms from Biological Foundation Model Internals
summary: We report the discovery and extraction of a compact hematopoietic algorithm from the single-cell foundation model scGPT, to our knowledge the first biologically useful, competitive algorithm extracted from a foundation model via mechanistic interpretability. We show that scGPT internally encodes a compact hematopoietic manifold with significant developmental branch structure, validated on a strict non-overlap Tabula Sapiens external panel and confirmed via frozen-head zero-shot transfer to an independent multi-donor immune panel. To isolate this geometry, we introduce a general three-stage extraction method consisting of direct operator export from frozen attention weights, a lightweight learned adaptor, and a task-specific readout, producing a standalone algorithm without target-dataset retraining. In 88-split donor-holdout benchmarks against scVI, Palantir, DPT, CellTypist, PCA, and raw-expression baselines, the extracted algorithm achieves the strongest pseudotime-depth ordering and leads on key subtype endpoints (CD4/CD8 AUROC 0.867, mono/macro AUROC 0.951). Compared to standard probing of frozen scGPT embeddings with a 3-layer MLP, the extracted head is BH-significantly better on 6/8 classification endpoints while completing a full 12-split evaluation campaign 34.5x faster with approximately 1000x fewer trainable parameters. The exported operator compresses from three pooled attention heads to a single head without statistically significant loss, and further to a rank-64 surrogate. Mechanistic interpretability of the compact operator reveals a concentrated four-factor core explaining 66.2% of ablation impact, with factors resolving into explicit T/lymphoid, B/plasma, granulocytic, and monocyte/macrophage gene programs. A supplementary second-manifold validation (intercellular communication geometry) confirms that the extraction method generalizes beyond hematopoiesis.

AI.tldr: A compact, mechanistically interpretable hematopoietic algorithm is extracted from scGPT via a three-stage, no-target-retraining method, yielding a standalone, highly efficient operator that preserves biological structure and generalizes beyond hematopoiesis.
AI.motivation: Demonstrate that foundation-model mechanistic interpretability can yield compact, biologically meaningful algorithms that perform well on real single-cell analyses and generalize to external datasets, with substantial efficiency gains.
AI.method: Three-stage extraction: (1) direct operator export from frozen attention weights, (2) a lightweight learned adaptor, (3) a task-specific readout; compresses from three attention heads to one, then to a rank-64 surrogate; evaluated on 88-split donor-holdout benchmarks against multiple baselines, with zero-shot transfer to external panels; no target-dataset retraining.
AI.result: The extracted head achieves strong pseudotime-depth ordering and endpoint performance (CD4/CD8 AUROC 0.867; mono/macro AUROC 0.951); BH-significant improvements over frozen-scGPT embeddings vs 3-layer MLP on 6/8 endpoints; 34.5x faster with ~1000x fewer trainable parameters; operator compression preserves performance; a four-factor core explains 66.2% of ablation impact aligning with T/lymphoid, B/plasma, granulocytic, and monocyte/macrophage programs; validated on an external Tabula Sapiens panel and via intercellular communication geometry on a second manifold.
AI.conclusion: A practical, interpretable algorithm can be extracted directly from a foundation model, delivering competitive performance with dramatic efficiency gains and generalizability beyond hematopoiesis, illustrating the value of mechanistic interpretability for biological discovery.

title: IMTBench: A Multi-Scenario Cross-Modal Collaborative Evaluation Benchmark for In-Image Machine Translation
summary: End-to-end In-Image Machine Translation (IIMT) aims to convert text embedded within an image into a target language while preserving the original visual context, layout, and rendering style. However, existing IIMT benchmarks are largely synthetic and thus fail to reflect real-world complexity, while current evaluation protocols focus on single-modality metrics and overlook cross-modal faithfulness between rendered text and model outputs. To address these shortcomings, we present In-image Machine Translation Benchmark (IMTBench), a new benchmark of 2,500 image translation samples covering four practical scenarios and nine languages. IMTBench supports multi-aspect evaluation, including translation quality, background preservation, overall image quality, and a cross-modal alignment score that measures consistency between the translated text produced by the model and the text rendered in the translated image. We benchmark strong commercial cascade systems, and both closed- and open-source unified multi-modal models, and observe large performance gaps across scenarios and languages, especially on natural scenes and resource-limited languages, highlighting substantial headroom for end-to-end image text translation. We hope IMTBench establishes a standardized benchmark to accelerate progress in this emerging task.

AI.tldr: Proposes IMTBench, a 2,500-sample in-image MT benchmark across 4 scenarios and 9 languages, introducing multi-aspect evaluation including cross-modal alignment; reveals large gaps among models, highlighting headroom for end-to-end IIMT and standardization of benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Current IIMT benchmarks are largely synthetic and single-modality, failing to reflect real-world complexity and cross-modal fidelity between rendered text and model outputs.
AI.method: Constructed IMTBench with 2,500 image translation samples spanning four practical scenarios and nine languages; implements multi-aspect evaluation (translation quality, background preservation, overall image quality, cross-modal alignment); evaluated commercial cascade systems and unified multi-modal models (closed- and open-source).
AI.result: Found large performance gaps across scenarios and languages, particularly for natural scenes and resource-limited languages; notable headroom for end-to-end image text translation; cross-modal alignment revealed misalignment between translated text and rendered image.
AI.conclusion: IMTBench provides a standardized benchmark framework to accelerate progress in end-to-end in-image MT and cross-modal evaluation, guiding future research and model development.

title: Learning Bimanual Cloth Manipulation with Vision-based Tactile Sensing via Single Robotic Arm
summary: Robotic cloth manipulation remains challenging due to the high-dimensional state space of fabrics, their deformable nature, and frequent occlusions that limit vision-based sensing. Although dual-arm systems can mitigate some of these issues, they increase hardware and control complexity. This paper presents Touch G.O.G., a compact vision-based tactile gripper and perception/control framework for single-arm bimanual cloth manipulation. The proposed framework combines three key components: (1) a novel gripper design and control strategy for in-gripper cloth sliding with a single robot arm, (2) a Vision Foundation Model-backboned Vision Transformer pipeline for cloth part classification (PC-Net) and edge pose estimation (PE-Net) using real and synthetic tactile images, and (3) an encoder-decoder synthetic data generator (SD-Net) that reduces manual annotation by producing high-fidelity tactile images. Experiments show 96% accuracy in distinguishing edges, corners, interior regions, and grasp failures, together with sub-millimeter edge localization and 4.5° orientation error. Real-world results demonstrate reliable cloth unfolding, even for crumpled fabrics, using only a single robotic arm. These results highlight Touch G.O.G. as a compact and cost-effective solution for deformable object manipulation.

AI.tldr: A compact vision-based tactile gripper enables single-arm bimanual cloth manipulation using perception pipelines and synthetic tactile data generation, achieving high edge detection accuracy and precise edge localization.
AI.motivation: Tackle deformable, occluded cloth manipulation with high-dimensional state space and reduce hardware/control complexity by enabling effective single-arm manipulation with tactile-vision cues.
AI.method: 1) Novel in-gripper cloth sliding gripper and control for single-arm manipulation. 2) PC-Net and PE-Net: Vision Transformer pipelines for cloth part classification and edge pose estimation using real and synthetic tactile images (Vision Foundation Model backbone). 3) SD-Net: encoder-decoder network generating high-fidelity synthetic tactile images to reduce manual annotation.
AI.result: 96% accuracy in classifying edges, corners, interior regions, and grasp failures; sub-millimeter edge localization; 4.5-degree orientation error. Real-world cloth unfolding demonstrated with a single arm, including crumpled fabrics.
AI.conclusion: Touch G.O.G. offers a compact, cost-effective solution for deformable object manipulation, enabling reliable single-arm cloth manipulation via tactile-vision perception and synthetic data augmentation.

title: Estimating condition number with Graph Neural Networks
summary: In this paper, we propose a fast method for estimating the condition number of sparse matrices using graph neural networks (GNNs). To enable efficient training and inference of GNNs, our proposed feature engineering for GNNs achieves $\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{nnz} + n)$, where $\mathrm{nnz}$ is the number of non-zero elements in the matrix and $n$ denotes the matrix dimension. We propose two prediction schemes for estimating the matrix condition number using GNNs. The extensive experiments for the two schemes are conducted for 1-norm and 2-norm condition number estimation, which show that our method achieves a significant speedup over the Hager-Higham and Lanczos methods.

AI.tldr: GNN-based fast condition-number estimation for sparse matrices with O(nnz + n) feature computation; two schemes; reports speedups over Hager-Higham and Lanczos for 1- and 2-norm cond numbers.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational cost of estimating matrix condition numbers for large sparse systems; enable faster decisions in numerical linear algebra pipelines by leveraging graph structure via GNNs.
AI.method: Graph neural networks with engineered features achieving linear time in graph size (O(nnz + n)); two prediction schemes to estimate the matrix condition number; experiments cover 1-norm and 2-norm conditional estimation.
AI.result: Significant speedups reported over Hager-Higham and Lanczos methods; validated via extensive experiments on 1-norm and 2-norm cond number estimation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of learning-based approaches for sparse-matrix condition number estimation; offers scalable alternatives to traditional iterative estimators; invites further work on generalization, error bounds, and integration with solvers.

title: UHD Image Deblurring via Autoregressive Flow with Ill-conditioned Constraints
summary: Ultra-high-definition (UHD) image deblurring poses significant challenges for UHD restoration methods, which must balance fine-grained detail recovery and practical inference efficiency. Although prominent discriminative and generative methods have achieved remarkable results, a trade-off persists between computational cost and the ability to generate fine-grained detail for UHD image deblurring tasks. To further alleviate these issues, we propose a novel autoregressive flow method for UHD image deblurring with an ill-conditioned constraint. Our core idea is to decompose UHD restoration into a progressive, coarse-to-fine process: at each scale, the sharp estimate is formed by upsampling the previous-scale result and adding a current-scale residual, enabling stable, stage-wise refinement from low to high resolution. We further introduce Flow Matching to model residual generation as a conditional vector field and perform few-step ODE sampling with efficient Euler/Heun solvers, enriching details while keeping inference affordable. Since multi-step generation at UHD can be numerically unstable, we propose an ill-conditioning suppression scheme by imposing condition-number regularization on a feature-induced attention matrix, improving convergence and cross-scale consistency. Our method demonstrates promising performance on blurred images at 4K (3840$\times$2160) or higher resolutions.

AI.tldr: Proposes an autoregressive flow framework with a coarse-to-fine UHD deblurring strategy, Flow Matching for residuals, and an ill-conditioning suppression scheme, achieving stable, efficient 4K restoration with few-step ODE sampling.
AI.motivation: UHD deblurring needs fine-grained detail recovery without prohibitive computation; existing discriminative/generative methods struggle to balance performance and efficiency at 4K.
AI.method: Progressive multi-scale restoration: upsample + add residual; Flow Matching models residuals as a conditional vector field; few-step ODE sampling with Euler/Heun solvers; ill-conditioning suppression via condition-number regularization on a feature-induced attention matrix.
AI.result: Demonstrates promising performance on 4K (3840x2160) or higher; stability and cross-scale consistency improved; efficient inference relative to fully autoregressive models.
AI.conclusion: Autoregressive flow with ill-conditioning control enables stable UHD deblurring with detail preservation and practical efficiency; potential applicability to other UHD restoration tasks.

title: AdaClearGrasp: Learning Adaptive Clearing for Zero-Shot Robust Dexterous Grasping in Densely Cluttered Environments
summary: In densely cluttered environments, physical interference, visual occlusions, and unstable contacts often cause direct dexterous grasping to fail, while aggressive singulation strategies may compromise safety. Enabling robots to adaptively decide whether to clear surrounding objects or directly grasp the target is therefore crucial for robust manipulation. We propose AdaClearGrasp, a closed-loop decision-execution framework for adaptive clearing and zero-shot dexterous grasping in densely cluttered environments. The framework formulates manipulation as a controllable high-level decision process that determines whether to directly grasp the target or first clear surrounding objects. A pretrained vision-language model (VLM) interprets visual observations and language task descriptions to reason about grasp interference and generate a high-level planning skeleton, which invokes structured atomic skills through a unified action interface. For dexterous grasping, we train a reinforcement learning policy with a relative hand-object distance representation, enabling zero-shot generalization across diverse object geometries and physical properties. During execution, visual feedback monitors outcomes and triggers replanning upon failures, forming a closed-loop correction mechanism. To evaluate language-conditioned dexterous grasping in clutter, we introduce Clutter-Bench, the first simulation benchmark with graded clutter complexity. It includes seven target objects across three clutter levels, yielding 210 task scenarios. We further perform sim-to-real experiments on three objects under three clutter levels (18 scenarios). Results demonstrate that AdaClearGrasp significantly improves grasp success rates in densely cluttered environments. For more videos and code, please visit our project website: https://chenzixuan99.github.io/adaclear-grasp.github.io/.

AI.tldr: AdaClearGrasp introduces a closed-loop, language-conditioned framework that decides between clearing clutter or directly grasping a target in dense clutter, powered by a vision–language model and a zero-shot dexterous grasping RL policy, and it provides Clutter-Bench for evaluation with sim-to-real experiments.
AI.motivation: Dense clutter creates grasp interference, occlusions, and unstable contacts; direct grasping can fail and aggressive clearing raises safety concerns. A framework that adaptively decides when to clear or grasp is needed for robust manipulation.
AI.method: A high-level decision process uses a pretrained vision–language model to interpret observations and task descriptions, generating a planning skeleton that invokes structured atomic skills. For dexterous grasping, a reinforcement learning policy with a relative hand–object distance representation enables zero-shot generalization across object geometries and properties. Execution is closed-loop with visual feedback triggering replanning upon failures. Clutter-Bench provides a graded clutter benchmark (210 tasks across 7 targets and 3 levels); sim-to-real experiments cover 18 scenarios.
AI.result: AdaClearGrasp substantially improves grasp success rates in densely cluttered environments. Clutter-Bench encompasses 210 tasks; sim-to-real experiments cover 18 scenarios (3 objects × 3 clutter levels).
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively enables adaptive clutter clearing and zero-shot dexterous grasping, supported by a new benchmark and demonstrated sim-to-real transfer.

title: Robust Post-Training for Generative Recommenders: Why Exponential Reward-Weighted SFT Outperforms RLHF
summary: Aligning generative recommender systems to user preferences via post-training is critical for closing the gap between next-item prediction and actual recommendation quality. Existing post-training methods are ill-suited for production-scale systems: RLHF methods reward hack due to noisy user feedback and unreliable reward models, offline RL alternatives require propensity scores that are unavailable, and online interaction is infeasible. We identify exponential reward-weighted SFT with weights $w = \exp(r/λ)$ as uniquely suited to this setting, and provide the theoretical and empirical foundations that explain why. By optimizing directly on observed rewards without querying a learned reward model, the method is immune to reward hacking, requires no propensity scores, and is fully offline. We prove the first policy improvement guarantees for this setting under noisy rewards, showing that the gap scales only logarithmically with catalog size and remains informative even for large item catalogs. Crucially, we show that temperature $λ$ explicitly and quantifiably controls the robustness-improvement tradeoff, providing practitioners with a single interpretable regularization hyperparameter with theoretical grounding. Experiments on three open-source and one proprietary dataset against four baselines confirm that exponential reward weighting is simple, scalable, and consistently outperforms RLHF-based alternatives.

AI.tldr: Exponential reward weighting in post-training for generative recommender systems yields a simple, offline, robust, reward-only optimization with theoretical guarantees and empirical gains over RLHF baselines.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between next-item prediction and actual recommendation quality in production-scale systems by enabling effective post-training without reliance on reward models, propensity scores, or online interaction.
AI.method: Post-training fine-tuning using weights w = exp(r/λ) on observed rewards, optimizing directly on rewards without learned reward models; provides policy-improvement guarantees under noisy rewards; analyzes robustness-improvement trade-off via temperature λ; fully offline and scalable.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three open-source and one proprietary dataset shows consistent outperformance of RLHF-based baselines; theoretical results show log-scale gap with catalog size; λ provides interpretable control over robustness versus improvement.
AI.conclusion: Exponential reward weighting is a simple, scalable, and effective offline post-training method for generative recommenders, with solid theoretical guarantees and practical performance advantages over RLHF approaches.

title: Visually-Guided Controllable Medical Image Generation via Fine-Grained Semantic Disentanglement
summary: Medical image synthesis is crucial for alleviating data scarcity and privacy constraints. However, fine-tuning general text-to-image (T2I) models remains challenging, mainly due to the significant modality gap between complex visual details and abstract clinical text. In addition, semantic entanglement persists, where coarse-grained text embeddings blur the boundary between anatomical structures and imaging styles, thus weakening controllability during generation. To address this, we propose a Visually-Guided Text Disentanglement framework. We introduce a cross-modal latent alignment mechanism that leverages visual priors to explicitly disentangle unstructured text into independent semantic representations. Subsequently, a Hybrid Feature Fusion Module (HFFM) injects these features into a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) through separated channels, enabling fine-grained structural control. Experimental results in three datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches in terms of generation quality and significantly improves performance on downstream classification tasks. The source code is available at https://github.com/hx111/VG-MedGen.

AI.tldr: Visually-Guided Text Disentanglement (VG-MedGen) disentangles clinical text into independent semantic representations using cross-modal latent alignment with visual priors, and injects these via a Hybrid Feature Fusion Module into a Diffusion Transformer for fine-grained control in medical image synthesis; shows improved generation quality and downstream classification performance; code available.
AI.motivation: Medical image synthesis faces a large modality gap between complex visual detail and abstract clinical text, and semantic entanglement between structure and style reduces controllability. There is a need for fine-grained, controllable generation specifically for medical data while preserving privacy and data scarcity constraints.
AI.method: Introduce a cross-modal latent alignment mechanism that leverages visual priors to disentangle unstructured text into independent semantic representations. Use a Hybrid Feature Fusion Module (HFFM) to inject these features into a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) through separated channels, enabling fine-grained structural control.
AI.result: Experimental results on three datasets show that VG-MedGen outperforms existing approaches in generation quality and significantly improves downstream classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Visually-Guided Text Disentanglement framework effectively reduces modality gaps and semantic entanglement in medical image synthesis, enabling precise structural control and boosting task performance; source code is available.

title: Interleaving Scheduling and Motion Planning with Incremental Learning of Symbolic Space-Time Motion Abstractions
summary: Task and Motion Planning combines high-level task sequencing (what to do) with low-level motion planning (how to do it) to generate feasible, collision-free execution plans. However, in many real-world domains, such as automated warehouses, tasks are predefined, shifting the challenge to if, when, and how to execute them safely and efficiently under resource, time and motion constraints. In this paper, we formalize this as the Scheduling and Motion Planning problem for multi-object navigation in shared workspaces. We propose a novel solution framework that interleaves off-the-shelf schedulers and motion planners in an incremental learning loop. The scheduler generates candidate plans, while the motion planner checks feasibility and returns symbolic feedback, i.e., spatial conflicts and timing adjustments, to guide the scheduler towards motion-feasible solutions. We validate our proposal on logistics and job-shop scheduling benchmarks augmented with motion tasks, using state-of-the-art schedulers and sampling-based motion planners. Our results show the effectiveness of our framework in generating valid plans under complex temporal and spatial constraints, where synchronized motion is critical.

AI.tldr: A framework that interleaves schedulers and motion planners to produce motion-feasible, synchronized plans for multi-object navigation in shared spaces, validated on logistics and job-shop benchmarks with motion tasks.
AI.motivation: In practice, predefined tasks in shared workspaces require plans that are not only temporally correct but also spatially feasible under motion constraints; decoupled task scheduling and motion planning often fail to meet timing and collision constraints.
AI.method: An incremental learning loop that alternates between off-the-shelf schedulers (to propose candidate plans) and sampling-based motion planners (to verify feasibility). The motion planner returns symbolic feedback (spatial conflicts and timing adjustments) that guides the scheduler toward feasible solutions.
AI.result: Demonstrates the ability to generate valid, synchronized plans under complex temporal and spatial constraints using existing schedulers and planners, evaluated on augmented logistics and job-shop benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively couples scheduling and motion planning to solve tightly integrated temporal-spatial planning problems; scalability and handling of uncertainty are potential avenues for future work.

title: Taming Score-Based Denoisers in ADMM: A Convergent Plug-and-Play Framework
summary: While score-based generative models have emerged as powerful priors for solving inverse problems, directly integrating them into optimization algorithms such as ADMM remains nontrivial. Two central challenges arise: i) the mismatch between the noisy data manifolds used to train the score functions and the geometry of ADMM iterates, especially due to the influence of dual variables, and ii) the lack of convergence understanding when ADMM is equipped with score-based denoisers. To address the manifold mismatch issue, we propose ADMM plug-and-play (ADMM-PnP) with the AC-DC denoiser, a new framework that embeds a three-stage denoiser into ADMM: (1) auto-correction (AC) via additive Gaussian noise, (2) directional correction (DC) using conditional Langevin dynamics, and (3) score-based denoising. In terms of convergence, we establish two results: first, under proper denoiser parameters, each ADMM iteration is a weakly nonexpansive operator, ensuring high-probability fixed-point $\textit{ball convergence}$ using a constant step size; second, under more relaxed conditions, the AC-DC denoiser is a bounded denoiser, which leads to convergence under an adaptive step size schedule. Experiments on a range of inverse problems demonstrate that our method consistently improves solution quality over a variety of baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduces ADMM-PnP with AC-DC denoiser for score-based priors in inverse problems; provides convergence guarantees and empirical gains over baselines.
AI.motivation: Score-based priors are powerful but do not align with ADMM iterates; lack of convergence guarantees when integrating score-based denoisers.
AI.method: Embed a three-stage denoiser into ADMM: auto-correction via Gaussian noise, directional correction via conditional Langevin dynamics, followed by score-based denoising; analyze convergence under two regimes: weakly nonexpansive fixed-point ball convergence with constant step size; bounded denoiser with adaptive step size for convergence.
AI.result: Empirical results across inverse problems show consistent improvements over baselines.
AI.conclusion: AC-DC ADMM-PnP offers improved solution quality with convergence guarantees for score-based priors; suggests broader applicability and potential for further enhancements.

title: Sparse Task Vector Mixup with Hypernetworks for Efficient Knowledge Transfer in Whole-Slide Image Prognosis
summary: Whole-Slide Images (WSIs) are widely used for estimating the prognosis of cancer patients. Current studies generally follow a cancer-specific learning paradigm. However, the available training samples for one cancer type are usually scarce in pathology. Consequently, the model often struggles to learn generalizable knowledge, thus performing worse on the tumor samples with inherent high heterogeneity. Although multi-cancer joint learning and knowledge transfer approaches have been explored recently to address it, they either rely on large-scale joint training or extensive inference across multiple models, posing new challenges in computational efficiency. To this end, this paper proposes a new scheme, Sparse Task Vector Mixup with Hypernetworks (STEPH). Unlike previous ones, it efficiently absorbs generalizable knowledge from other cancers for the target via model merging: i) applying task vector mixup to each source-target pair and then ii) sparsely aggregating task vector mixtures to obtain an improved target model, driven by hypernetworks. Extensive experiments on 13 cancer datasets show that STEPH improves over cancer-specific learning and an existing knowledge transfer baseline by 5.14% and 2.01%, respectively. Moreover, it is a more efficient solution for learning prognostic knowledge from other cancers, without requiring large-scale joint training or extensive multi-model inference. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/liupei101/STEPH.

AI.tldr: STEPH enables efficient cross-cancer knowledge transfer for prognostic WSI prediction via sparse task-vector mixing and hypernetworks, outperforming cancer-specific and standard transfer baselines across 13 datasets.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and high tumor heterogeneity in cancer prognosis by leveraging knowledge from other cancers without the computational burden of large-scale joint training or multi-model inference.
AI.method: For each source-target cancer pair, apply task-vector mixup to combine learned representations; then sparsely aggregate these task-vector mixtures using hypernetworks to produce an improved target model. This model-merging approach avoids extensive joint training and reduces inference overhead.
AI.result: On 13 cancer datasets, STEPH improved prognosis accuracy by 5.14% over a cancer-specific baseline and 2.01% over a known knowledge-transfer baseline, while being more computationally efficient than existing joint-training/inference schemes.
AI.conclusion: STEPH provides an effective and efficient framework for cross-cancer prognostic learning from WSIs, enabling generalizable knowledge transfer without large-scale joint training; the authors also provide public code.

title: STM32-Based Smart Waste Bin for Hygienic Disposal Using Embedded Sensing and Automated Control
comment: This paper consists of 6 pages, with 3 figures, 3 tables, and 1 algorithm
summary: The increasing demand for hygienic and contactless solutions in public and private environments has encouraged the development of automated systems for everyday applications. This paper presents the design and implementation of a motion- sensing automatic waste bin using an STM32 microcontroller, ultrasonic sensors, and a servo motor. The system detects user presence through ultrasonic sensing and automatically opens the bin lid using a servo motor controlled by the microcontroller. An additional ultrasonic sensor is used to monitor the internal waste level of the bin, while an OLED display provides real- time feedback regarding system status. The proposed system offers a low-cost, reliable, and easily deployable solution for touch-free waste disposal. Experimental evaluation demonstrates fast response time, stable sensing performance, and smooth mechanical operation. The system can be effectively deployed in homes, educational institutions, hospitals, and public facilities to improve hygiene and user convenience.

AI.tldr: Low-cost motion-sensing automated waste bin using STM32, ultrasonic sensors, servo, and OLED display; fast, reliable, touch-free disposal with internal waste level monitoring.
AI.motivation: Enhance hygiene and convenience in public and private spaces by enabling contactless waste disposal.
AI.method: Hardware design with STM32 MCU, two ultrasonic sensors (one for presence, one for internal level), servo-driven lid, OLED feedback; software for sensing, actuation, and status display; experimental validation of response time, sensing stability, and mechanical smoothness.
AI.result: Demonstrates fast response, stable sensing, smooth mechanical operation; reported deployment-ready with low cost.
AI.conclusion: The system offers a practical, deployable solution for touch-free waste disposal in various environments; potential for broader adoption and further refinements.

title: GSVD for Geometry-Grounded Dataset Comparison: An Alignment Angle Is All You Need
summary: Geometry-grounded learning asks models to respect structure in the problem domain rather than treating observations as arbitrary vectors. Motivated by this view, we revisit a classical but underused primitive for comparing datasets: linear relations between two data matrices, expressed via the co-span constraint $Ax = By = z$ in a shared ambient space. To operationalize this comparison, we use the generalized singular value decomposition (GSVD) as a joint coordinate system for two subspaces. In particular, we exploit the GSVD form $A = HCU$, $B = HSV$ with $C^{\top}C + S^{\top}S = I$, which separates shared versus dataset-specific directions through the diagonal structure of $(C, S)$. From these factors we derive an interpretable *angle score* $θ(z) \in [0, π/2]$ for a sample $z$, quantifying whether z is explained relatively more by $A$, more by $B$, or comparably by both. The primary role of $θ(z)$ is as a *per-sample geometric diagnostic*. We illustrate the behavior of the score on MNIST through angle distributions and representative GSVD directions. A binary classifier derived from $θ(z)$ is presented as an illustrative application of the score as an interpretable diagnostic tool.

AI.tldr: Proposes a geometry-aware dataset comparison using GSVD to obtain a per-sample angle score that measures whether a sample is better explained by one data matrix or the other, with MNIST as a case study and a simple classifier derived from the score.
AI.motivation: To respect intrinsic structure in data when comparing datasets, moving beyond treating observations as arbitrary vectors. The co-span constraint enables a joint geometric view of two datasets, and the GSVD provides a natural shared coordinate system to separate shared vs dataset-specific directions, yielding interpretable diagnostics.
AI.method: Model two data matrices A and B as A = HCU and B = HSV with C^T C + S^T S = I, using the generalized SVD to obtain a joint coordinate system. From the diagonal structure (C, S), derive an angle score θ(z) ∈ [0, π/2] for each sample z, indicating whether z is explained more by A, more by B, or similarly by both. Evaluate θ on MNIST and illustrate with angle distributions and GSVD directions; construct a binary classifier from θ as a diagnostic tool.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that per-sample angular diagnostics can reveal how data are explained by each dataset in a shared space. It provides visualizations on MNIST and shows that θ can support a straightforward classification task, illustrating the practical utility of the geometric diagnostic.
AI.conclusion: The GSVD-based angle score offers an interpretable, per-sample diagnostic for comparing datasets under structure-preserving assumptions. It clarifies the balance between shared and dataset-specific information and suggests a useful tool for dataset analysis and model evaluation, with potential extensions to nonlinear settings and scalability.

title: DSFlash: Comprehensive Panoptic Scene Graph Generation in Realtime
summary: Scene Graph Generation (SGG) aims to extract a detailed graph structure from an image, a representation that holds significant promise as a robust intermediate step for complex downstream tasks like reasoning for embodied agents. However, practical deployment in real-world applications - especially on resource constrained edge devices - requires speed and resource efficiency, challenges that have received limited attention in existing research. To bridge this gap, we introduce DSFlash, a low-latency model for panoptic scene graph generation designed to overcome these limitations. DSFlash can process a video stream at 56 frames per second on a standard RTX 3090 GPU, without compromising performance against existing state-of-the-art methods. Crucially, unlike prior approaches that often restrict themselves to salient relationships, DSFlash computes comprehensive scene graphs, offering richer contextual information while maintaining its superior latency. Furthermore, DSFlash is light on resources, requiring less than 24 hours to train on a single, nine-year-old GTX 1080 GPU. This accessibility makes DSFlash particularly well-suited for researchers and practitioners operating with limited computational resources, empowering them to adapt and fine-tune SGG models for specialized applications.

AI.tldr: DSFlash is a real-time, resource-efficient panoptic scene graph generation model that yields comprehensive graphs at high frame rates and trains on modest hardware.
AI.motivation: To enable practical, real-world deployment of scene graph generation on resource-constrained edge devices by addressing speed, memory, and training-time limitations, while producing full (panoptic) scene graphs rather than only salient relations.
AI.method: DSFlash is a low-latency panoptic SGG model designed for video streams. It achieves 56 frames per second on a standard RTX 3090 GPU, outputs comprehensive scene graphs, and requires less than 24 hours of training on a GTX 1080 GPU, making it accessible for researchers with limited computational resources.
AI.result: The model maintains strong performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods while delivering significantly reduced latency and resource requirements. It provides complete scene graphs (panoptic) rather than focusing solely on salient relations and demonstrates real-time processing on consumer-grade hardware.
AI.conclusion: DSFlash enables real-time, panoptic scene graph generation on resource-constrained devices, broadening practical applications in embodied reasoning and other edge deployments, and lowering the barrier to entry for researchers with limited compute.

title: Dynamic Modeling and Attitude Control of a Reaction-Wheel-Based Low-Gravity Bipedal Hopper
summary: Planetary bodies characterized by low gravitational acceleration, such as the Moon and near-Earth asteroids, impose unique locomotion constraints due to diminished contact forces and extended airborne intervals. Among traversal strategies, hopping locomotion offers high energy efficiency but is prone to mid-flight attitude instability caused by asymmetric thrust generation and uneven terrain interactions. This paper presents an underactuated bipedal hopping robot that employs an internal reaction wheel to regulate body posture during the ballistic flight phase. The system is modeled as a gyrostat, enabling analysis of the dynamic coupling between torso rotation and reaction wheel momentum. The locomotion cycle comprises three phases: a leg-driven propulsive jump, mid-air attitude stabilization via an active momentum exchange controller, and a shock-absorbing landing. A reduced-order model is developed to capture the critical coupling between torso rotation and reaction wheel dynamics. The proposed framework is evaluated in MuJoCo-based simulations under lunar gravity conditions (g = 1.625 m/s^2). Results demonstrate that activation of the reaction wheel controller reduces peak mid-air angular deviation by more than 65% and constrains landing attitude error to within 3.5 degrees at touchdown. Additionally, actuator saturation per hop cycle is reduced, ensuring sufficient control authority. Overall, the approach significantly mitigates in-flight attitude excursions and enables consistent upright landings, providing a practical and control-efficient solution for locomotion on irregular extraterrestrial terrains.

AI.tldr: An underactuated bipedal hopping robot uses an internal reaction wheel (gyrostat) to actively regulate attitude during ballistic flight, improving control authority and landing stability on low-gravity bodies.
AI.motivation: On bodies with very low gravity (e.g., Moon, asteroids), hopping is energetically efficient but prone to mid-flight attitude instability due to asymmetric thrust and terrain interactions. An internal momentum management strategy can maintain upright flight and reliable touchdowns.
AI.method: Model the robot as a gyrostat with an internal reaction wheel. Implement a three-phase locomotion cycle: leg-driven propulsion, mid-air attitude stabilization via an active momentum-exchange controller, and shock-absorbing landing. Develop a reduced-order model capturing the coupling between torso rotation and reaction-wheel dynamics. Evaluate in MuJoCo under lunar gravity (g = 1.625 m/s^2).
AI.result: Reaction-wheel control reduces peak mid-air angular deviation by >65% and constrains touchdown attitude error to within 3.5 degrees. Actuator saturation per hop is reduced, providing sufficient control authority and enabling consistent upright landings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed gyrostat-based controller effectively mitigates in-flight attitude excursions and supports reliable hopping on irregular extraterrestrial terrains, offering a practical and energy-efficient locomotion solution for low-gravity environments.

title: Copula-ResLogit: A Deep-Copula Framework for Unobserved Confounding Effects
summary: A key challenge in travel demand analysis is the presence of unobserved factors that may generate non-causal dependencies, obscuring the true causal effects. To address the issue, the study introduces a novel deep learning based fully interpretable joint modelling framework, Copula-ResLogit, which integrates the flexibility of Residual Neural Network (ResNet) architectures with the dependence capturing capabilities of copula models. This hybrid structure enables us to first detect unobserved confounding through traditional copula function based joint modelling and then mitigate these hidden associations by incorporating deep learning components. The study applies this framework to two case studies, including the relationship between stress levels and wait time of pedestrians when crossing mid block in VR and the dependencies between travel mode choice and travel distance in London travel behaviour data. Results show that Copula-ResLogit substantially reduces or eliminates the dependencies, demonstrating the ability of residual layers to account for hidden confounding effects.

AI.tldr: Introduces Copula-ResLogit, a deep-learning augmented, fully interpretable joint model that detects and mitigates unobserved confounding in travel behavior using copula-based dependencies and residual networks; validated on VR pedestrian stress/wait time and London mode-distance data, showing substantial reduction of dependencies.
AI.motivation: Unobserved factors induce non-causal associations in travel demand analysis, obscuring true causal effects. There is a need for interpretable joint models that can detect and adjust for latent dependencies using flexible deep learning components.
AI.method: Propose Copula-ResLogit, a two-stage framework that combines traditional copula-based joint modelling to detect dependence due to unobserved confounding with residual-network components to absorb hidden associations. Applied to two case studies (VR crossing mid-block stress vs wait time; mode choice vs travel distance in London). The approach maintains interpretability while leveraging deep learning to account for non-linear confounding effects.
AI.result: Copula-ResLogit substantially reduces or eliminates the detected dependencies, indicating effective mitigation of hidden confounding; residual layers account for hidden confounding effects and enhance interpretability of the joint model outputs.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a novel, interpretable approach to detect and mitigate unobserved confounding in travel demand analysis, combining copula-based dependence detection with deep learning to improve causal interpretation; validated across two empirical case studies.

title: Prompting with the human-touch: evaluating model-sensitivity of foundation models for musculoskeletal CT segmentation
summary: Promptable Foundation Models (FMs), initially introduced for natural image segmentation, have also revolutionized medical image segmentation. The increasing number of models, along with evaluations varying in datasets, metrics, and compared models, makes direct performance comparison between models difficult and complicates the selection of the most suitable model for specific clinical tasks. In our study, 11 promptable FMs are tested using non-iterative 2D and 3D prompting strategies on a private and public dataset focusing on bone and implant segmentation in four anatomical regions (wrist, shoulder, hip and lower leg). The Pareto-optimal models are identified and further analyzed using human prompts collected through a dedicated observer study. Our findings are: 1) The segmentation performance varies a lot between FMs and prompting strategies; 2) The Pareto-optimal models in 2D are SAM and SAM2.1, in 3D nnInteractive and Med-SAM2; 3) Localization accuracy and rater consistency vary with anatomical structures, with higher consistency for simple structures (wrist bones) and lower consistency for complex structures (pelvis, tibia, implants); 4) The segmentation performance drops using human prompts, suggesting that performance reported on "ideal" prompts extracted from reference labels might overestimate the performance in a human-driven setting; 5) All models were sensitive to prompt variations. While two models demonstrated intra-rater robustness, it did not scale to inter-rater settings. We conclude that the selection of the most optimal FM for a human-driven setting remains challenging, with even high-performing FMs being sensitive to variations in human input prompts. Our code base for prompt extraction and model inference is available: https://github.com/CarolineMagg/segmentation-FM-benchmark/

AI.tldr: A benchmark of 11 promptable foundation models (FMs) for 2D/3D medical image segmentation across four anatomical regions, revealing Pareto-optimal models (2D: SAM, SAM2.1; 3D: nnInteractive, Med-SAM2) and significant sensitivity to prompting strategies, including human prompts which degrade performance and reduce inter-rater reliability; highlights challenges in selecting FMs for human-driven clinical tasks; provides a public codebase for prompt extraction and inference.
AI.motivation: Medical image segmentation with promptable foundation models suffers from inconsistent evaluations due to varying datasets, metrics, and prompting schemes. A unified benchmarking framework and identification of robust Pareto-optimal models are needed to guide clinical deployment.
AI.method: Evaluate 11 promptable FMs using non-iterative 2D and 3D prompting on a private and a public dataset for bone and implant segmentation in four regions (wrist, shoulder, hip, lower leg). Identify Pareto-optimal models. Conduct observer study with human prompts to assess localization accuracy, rater consistency, and sensitivity to prompt variations. Analyze intra- vs inter-rater robustness. Provide code for prompt extraction and inference.
AI.result: 2D Pareto-optimal: SAM and SAM2.1; 3D Pareto-optimal: nnInteractive and Med-SAM2. Segmentation performance varies widely across models and prompting strategies. Localization accuracy and rater consistency depend on anatomy, higher for simple structures (wrist) and lower for complex ones (pelvis, tibia, implants). Performance drops with human prompts, suggesting ideal-prompt benchmarks may overestimate real-world performance. All models are sensitive to prompt variations; intra-rater robustness observed in two models but not scalable to inter-rater settings. Codebase available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Selecting the best FM for human-driven clinical segmentation remains challenging due to sensitivity to prompting and anatomical variability. Standardization of prompts and robust evaluation under human input are essential for reliable deployment. The work contributes a comprehensive benchmarking baseline and transparency through code release.

title: GaLoRA: Parameter-Efficient Graph-Aware LLMs for Node Classification
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, 11 tables, 39th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2025) Workshop
summary: The rapid rise of large language models (LLMs) and their ability to capture semantic relationships has led to their adoption in a wide range of applications. Text-attributed graphs (TAGs) are a notable example where LLMs can be combined with Graph Neural Networks to improve the performance of node classification. In TAGs, each node is associated with textual content and such graphs are commonly seen in various domains such as social networks, citation graphs, recommendation systems, etc. Effectively learning from TAGs would enable better representations of both structural and textual representations of the graph and improve decision-making in relevant domains. We present GaLoRA, a parameter-efficient framework that integrates structural information into LLMs. GaLoRA demonstrates competitive performance on node classification tasks with TAGs, performing on par with state-of-the-art models with just 0.24% of the parameter count required by full LLM fine-tuning. We experiment with three real-world datasets to showcase GaLoRA's effectiveness in combining structural and semantical information on TAGs.

AI.tldr: GaLoRA is a parameter-efficient framework that injects graph structural information into large language models (LLMs) for text-attributed graphs (TAGs) to improve node classification, achieving competitive results with only 0.24% of full LLM fine-tuning parameters across three real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Leverage both textual content and graph structure in TAGs for node classification while drastically reducing the number of finetuned parameters.
AI.method: GaLoRA adopts a LoRA-like, parameter-efficient adaptation to incorporate structural signals into LLMs for TAGs, and evaluates its performance on three real-world TAG datasets, comparing against full LLM fine-tuning and baseline TAG approaches.
AI.result: The method achieves competitive node classification performance, on par with state-of-the-art models, while using only 0.24% of the parameters required for full LLM fine-tuning, demonstrating effective fusion of structural and textual information from TAGs.
AI.conclusion: GaLoRA provides a parameter-efficient, scalable approach to learning from TAGs by integrating graph structure into LLMs, with empirical validation on real-world datasets and potential applicability to broader multimodal graph learning tasks.

title: Towards Cognitive Defect Analysis in Active Infrared Thermography with Vision-Text Cues
summary: Active infrared thermography (AIRT) is currently witnessing a surge of artificial intelligence (AI) methodologies being deployed for automated subsurface defect analysis of high performance carbon fiber-reinforced polymers (CFRP). Deploying AI-based AIRT methodologies for inspecting CFRPs requires the creation of time consuming and expensive datasets of CFRP inspection sequences to train neural networks. To address this challenge, this work introduces a novel language-guided framework for cognitive defect analysis in CFRPs using AIRT and vision-language models (VLMs). Unlike conventional learning-based approaches, the proposed framework does not require developing training datasets for extensive training of defect detectors, instead it relies solely on pretrained multimodal VLM encoders coupled with a lightweight adapter to enable generative zero-shot understanding and localization of subsurface defects. By leveraging pretrained multimodal encoders, the proposed system enables generative zero-shot understanding of thermographic patterns and automatic detection of subsurface defects. Given the domain gap between thermographic data and natural images used to train VLMs, an AIRT-VLM Adapter is proposed to enhance the visibility of defects while aligning the thermographic domain with the learned representations of VLMs. The proposed framework is validated using three representative VLMs; specifically, GroundingDINO, Qwen-VL-Chat, and CogVLM. Validation is performed on 25 CFRP inspection sequences with impacts introduced at different energy levels, reflecting realistic defects encountered in industrial scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that the AIRT-VLM adapter achieves signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) gains exceeding 10 dB compared with conventional thermographic dimensionality-reduction methods, while enabling zero-shot defect detection with intersection-over-union values reaching 70%.

AI.tldr: A zero-shot, language-guided framework (AIRT-VLM Adapter) leveraging pretrained vision-language models to detect and localize subsurface defects in CFRP using active infrared thermography, achieving 10+ dB SNR gains and IoU ~0.70 without training data.
AI.motivation: To circumvent the need for large labeled CFRP thermography datasets for AI defect detectors, addressing domain gap between thermography and natural images, enabling zero-shot defect analysis.
AI.method: Introduce AIRT-VLM Adapter to align thermographic domain with VLMs; employ pretrained multimodal encoders (GroundingDINO, Qwen-VL-Chat, CogVLM) for zero-shot defect detection and localization; validate on 25 CFRP sequences with varying energy levels; compare against dimensionality-reduction baselines.
AI.result: SNR gains > 10 dB over conventional methods; zero-shot defect detection with IoU up to 70%; successful defect localization across energy levels.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of zero-shot CFRP defect analysis via VLMs with a light adaptor, reducing data collection burden and offering robust defect localization in industrial AIRT applications.

title: OnFly: Onboard Zero-Shot Aerial Vision-Language Navigation toward Safety and Efficiency
summary: Aerial vision-language navigation (AVLN) enables UAVs to follow natural-language instructions in complex 3D environments. However, existing zero-shot AVLN methods often suffer from unstable single-stream Vision-Language Model decision-making, unreliable long-horizon progress monitoring, and a trade-off between safety and efficiency. We propose OnFly, a fully onboard, real-time framework for zero-shot AVLN. OnFly adopts a shared-perception dual-agent architecture that decouples high-frequency target generation from low-frequency progress monitoring, thereby stabilizing decision-making. It further employs a hybrid keyframe-recent-frame memory to preserve global trajectory context while maintaining KV-cache prefix stability, enabling reliable long-horizon monitoring with termination and recovery signals. In addition, a semantic-geometric verifier refines VLM-predicted targets for instruction consistency and geometric safety using VLM features and depth cues, while a receding-horizon planner generates optimized collision-free trajectories under geometric safety constraints, improving both safety and efficiency. In simulation, OnFly improves task success from 26.4% to 67.8%, compared with the strongest state-of-the-art baseline, while fully onboard real-world flights validate its feasibility for real-time deployment. The code will be released at https://github.com/Robotics-STAR-Lab/OnFly

AI.tldr: OnFly is a fully onboard zero-shot AVLN framework that decouples perception and monitoring via a dual-agent architecture, uses a memory mechanism to preserve trajectory context, applies a semantic-geometric verifier, and employs a receding-horizon planner, yielding substantial simulation gains and real-world feasibility.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot AVLN approaches struggle with unstable decision-making in single-stream perception, unreliable long-horizon progress tracking, and a trade-off between safety and efficiency. A real-time onboard solution is needed for robust navigation in complex 3D environments.
AI.method: 1) Shared-perception dual-agent design: high-frequency target generation with low-frequency progress monitoring to stabilize decisions. 2) Hybrid keyframe-recent-frame memory for global trajectory context and KV-cache prefix stability. 3) Semantic-geometric verifier combines VLM features and depth cues to refine targets for instruction consistency and safety. 4) Receding-horizon planner computes collision-free trajectories under geometric safety constraints.
AI.result: In simulation, task success rises from 26.4% to 67.8+% over a strong baseline. Real-world onboard flights demonstrate practical feasibility for real-time deployment. Code will be released at the project repository.
AI.conclusion: OnFly demonstrates that onboard, zero-shot AVLN can achieve substantial improvements by decoupling perception and monitoring, maintaining memory for long-horizon context, verifying VLM-predictions with geometric cues, and planning with safety constraints. This yields higher task success and readiness for real-world use.

title: Regime-aware financial volatility forecasting via in-context learning
comment: 11 pages, 1 figure, Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026 Workshop on Advances in Financial AI
summary: This work introduces a regime-aware in-context learning framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) for financial volatility forecasting under nonstationary market conditions. The proposed approach deploys pretrained LLMs to reason over historical volatility patterns and adjust their predictions without parameter fine-tuning. We develop an oracle-guided refinement procedure that constructs regime-aware demonstrations from training data. An LLM is then deployed as an in-context learner that predicts the next-step volatility from the input sequence using demonstrations sampled conditional to the estimated market label. This conditional sampling strategy enables the LLM to adapt its predictions to regime-dependent volatility dynamics through contextual reasoning alone. Experiments with multiple financial datasets show that the proposed regime-aware in-context learning framework outperforms both classical volatility forecasting approaches and direct one-shot learning, especially during high-volatility periods.

AI.tldr: A regime-aware in-context learning framework using LLMs for volatility forecasting under nonstationarity, leveraging oracle-guided regime demonstrations and conditional sampling to adapt predictions without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Tackle nonstationary market conditions in financial volatility forecasting by enabling LLMs to adapt to regime dynamics through context and demonstrations, avoiding parameter updates.
AI.method: Develop an oracle-guided refinement procedure to create regime-aware demonstrations from training data. Use an LLM as an in-context learner, predicting next-step volatility from input sequences using demonstrations sampled conditionally on the estimated market regime, enabling regime-dependent dynamics to be captured through contextual reasoning only.
AI.result: Empirical results show the regime-aware in-context learning framework outperforms classical volatility forecasting methods and one-shot learning, with notable gains during high-volatility periods across multiple financial datasets.
AI.conclusion: Regime-aware in-context learning with conditional demonstrations enables regime-adaptive volatility forecasting under nonstationarity without fine-tuning, demonstrating efficacy and potential for LLM-driven financial forecasting.

title: P-GSVC: Layered Progressive 2D Gaussian Splatting for Scalable Image and Video
summary: Gaussian splatting has emerged as a competitive explicit representation for image and video reconstruction. In this work, we present P-GSVC, the first layered progressive 2D Gaussian splatting framework that provides a unified solution for scalable Gaussian representation in both images and videos. P-GSVC organizes 2D Gaussian splats into a base layer and successive enhancement layers, enabling coarse-to-fine reconstructions. To effectively optimize this layered representation, we propose a joint training strategy that simultaneously updates Gaussians across layers, aligning their optimization trajectories to ensure inter-layer compatibility and a stable progressive reconstruction. P-GSVC supports scalability in terms of both quality and resolution. Our experiments show that the joint training strategy can gain up to 1.9 dB improvement in PSNR for video and 2.6 dB improvement in PSNR for image when compared to methods that perform sequential layer-wise training. Project page: https://longanwang-cs.github.io/PGSVC-webpage/

AI.tldr: First layered progressive 2D Gaussian splatting framework for images and videos that uses base and enhancement layers with joint training, achieving notable PSNR gains over sequential training.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable, high-quality Gaussian splatting representations across resolutions and content types (images and videos) via a unified progressive framework and stable cross-layer optimization.
AI.method: Introduce a base layer plus successive enhancement layers of 2D Gaussians, and a joint training strategy that updates Gaussians across layers to align optimization trajectories and ensure inter-layer compatibility for coarse-to-fine reconstructions.
AI.result: PSNR improvements of up to 1.9 dB for video and 2.6 dB for image over methods that train layers sequentially; demonstrates scalability in quality and resolution.
AI.conclusion: Joint-layer optimization enables stable, scalable progressive reconstruction with a unified framework (P-GSVC) for both images and videos; supported by a project page for further details.

title: MapGCLR: Geospatial Contrastive Learning of Representations for Online Vectorized HD Map Construction
summary: Autonomous vehicles rely on map information to understand the world around them. However, the creation and maintenance of offline high-definition (HD) maps remains costly. A more scalable alternative lies in online HD map construction, which only requires map annotations at training time. To further reduce the need for annotating vast training labels, self-supervised training provides an alternative. This work focuses on improving the latent birds-eye-view (BEV) feature grid representation within a vectorized online HD map construction model by enforcing geospatial consistency between overlapping BEV feature grids as part of a contrastive loss function. To ensure geospatial overlap for contrastive pairs, we introduce an approach to analyze the overlap between traversals within a given dataset and generate subsidiary dataset splits following adjustable multi-traversal requirements. We train the same model supervised using a reduced set of single-traversal labeled data and self-supervised on a broader unlabeled set of data following our multi-traversal requirements, effectively implementing a semi-supervised approach. Our approach outperforms the supervised baseline across the board, both quantitatively in terms of the downstream tasks vectorized map perception performance and qualitatively in terms of segmentation in the principal component analysis (PCA) visualization of the BEV feature space.

AI.tldr: Semi-supervised online HD map construction using a geospatial-consistency contrastive loss on latent BEV grids with multi-traversal data splits; outperforms the supervised baseline.
AI.motivation: HD maps are costly to create and maintain offline. Online HD map construction with minimal labeling and self-supervision aims to scale map-perception capabilities by leveraging geospatial overlap across traversals.
AI.method: Enforce geospatial consistency between overlapping BEV feature grids via a contrastive loss. Analyze dataset traversals to generate multi-traversal subsidiary splits with adjustable requirements. Train the model with a reduced single-traversal labeled set while self-supervising on a broader unlabeled set following multi-traversal rules, i.e., a semi-supervised approach.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms the supervised baseline across both quantitative vectorized map-perception metrics and qualitative PCA-based BEV feature-space visualization.
AI.conclusion: Geospatial-consistency contrastive learning combined with multi-traversal data splits improves semi-supervised online HD map construction compared to a purely supervised approach.

title: What do near-optimal learning rate schedules look like?
summary: A basic unanswered question in neural network training is: what is the best learning rate schedule shape for a given workload? The choice of learning rate schedule is a key factor in the success or failure of the training process, but beyond having some kind of warmup and decay, there is no consensus on what makes a good schedule shape. To answer this question, we designed a search procedure to find the best shapes within a parameterized schedule family. Our approach factors out the schedule shape from the base learning rate, which otherwise would dominate cross-schedule comparisons. We applied our search procedure to a variety of schedule families on three workloads: linear regression, image classification on CIFAR-10, and small-scale language modeling on Wikitext103. We showed that our search procedure indeed generally found near-optimal schedules. We found that warmup and decay are robust features of good schedules, and that commonly used schedule families are not optimal on these workloads. Finally, we explored how the outputs of our shape search depend on other optimization hyperparameters, and found that weight decay can have a strong effect on the optimal schedule shape. To the best of our knowledge, our results represent the most comprehensive results on near-optimal schedule shapes for deep neural network training, to date.

AI.tldr: A method to systematically search for near-optimal learning-rate schedule shapes, independent of base rate, showing warmup/decay are robust and common schedules may be suboptimal; shape interacts with hyperparameters like weight decay.
AI.motivation: Identify the optimal learning-rate schedule shape for a given workload, since shape (beyond warmup/decay) is often under-optimized. Factor out the base learning rate to enable fair cross-schedule comparisons and perform a comprehensive search across multiple workloads.
AI.method: Introduce a procedure to search over a parameterized schedule family, separate the schedule shape from the base learning rate, apply the search to various schedule families, and evaluate on linear regression, CIFAR-10, and Wikitext-103 to identify near-optimal shapes and their dependence on optimization hyperparameters.
AI.result: The search yields near-optimal shapes across workloads; warmup and decay are robust features of good schedules; many standard schedule families may be suboptimal; weight decay significantly influences the optimal schedule shape; provides the most comprehensive empirical analysis of near-optimal shapes to date.
AI.conclusion: A systematic shape-search approach is valuable for discovering effective learning-rate schedules; schedule shape materially affects training outcomes and interacts with other hyperparameters, advocating for data-driven exploration beyond conventional schedules.

title: PET-F2I: A Comprehensive Benchmark and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of LLMs for PET/CT Report Impression Generation
summary: PET/CT imaging is pivotal in oncology and nuclear medicine, yet summarizing complex findings into precise diagnostic impressions is labor-intensive. While LLMs have shown promise in medical text generation, their capability in the highly specialized domain of PET/CT remains underexplored. We introduce PET-F2I-41K (PET Findings-to-Impression Benchmark), a large-scale benchmark for PET/CT impression generation using LLMs, constructed from over 41k real-world reports. Using PET-F2I-41K, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of 27 models across proprietary frontier LLMs, open-source generalist models, and medical-domain LLMs, and we develop a domain-adapted 7B model (PET-F2I-7B) fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct via LoRA. Beyond standard NLG metrics (e.g., BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, BERTScore), we propose three clinically grounded metrics - Entity Coverage Rate (ECR), Uncovered Entity Rate (UER), and Factual Consistency Rate (FCR) - to assess diagnostic completeness and factual reliability. Experiments reveal that neither frontier nor medical-domain LLMs perform adequately in zero-shot settings. In contrast, PET-F2I-7B achieves substantial gains (e.g., 0.708 BLEU-4) and a 3.0x improvement in entity coverage over the strongest baseline, while offering advantages in cost, latency, and privacy. Beyond this modeling contribution, PET-F2I-41K establishes a standardized evaluation framework to accelerate the development of reliable and clinically deployable reporting systems for PET/CT.

AI.tldr: A large-scale PET/CT impression benchmark (PET-F2I-41K) assesses LLMs' ability to generate clinically complete impressions; a domain-adapted 7B model (PET-F2I-7B) via LoRA outperforms baselines on a 41k report dataset and on new clinical metrics.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap in PET/CT report generation by establishing a standardized benchmark for evaluating LLMs in generating accurate, complete impressions while ensuring factual consistency and clinical utility.
AI.method: Assemble 41k real-world PET/CT reports to form PET-F2I-41K; evaluate 27 models spanning frontier LLMs, open-source generalists, and medical-domain LLMs; fine-tune a 7B model (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) with LoRA to create PET-F2I-7B; introduce three clinically grounded metrics (ECR, UER, FCR) alongside standard NLG metrics.
AI.result: Frontier and medical-domain LLMs underperform in zero-shot; PET-F2I-7B achieves notable gains (e.g., BLEU-4 0.708) and 3x higher entity coverage than strongest baseline; PET-F2I-41K provides cost, latency, and privacy advantages and frames a standardized evaluation framework.
AI.conclusion: PET-F2I-41K enables rigorous, clinically relevant evaluation of PET/CT impression generation and paves the way for deployable, reliable reporting systems; future work includes improving factual consistency and extending to broader imaging domains.

title: Federated Active Learning Under Extreme Non-IID and Global Class Imbalance
summary: Federated active learning (FAL) seeks to reduce annotation cost under privacy constraints, yet its effectiveness degrades in realistic settings with severe global class imbalance and highly heterogeneous clients. We conduct a systematic study of query-model selection in FAL and uncover a central insight: the model that achieves more class-balanced sampling, especially for minority classes, consistently leads to better final performance. Moreover, global-model querying is beneficial only when the global distribution is highly imbalanced and client data are relatively homogeneous; otherwise, the local model is preferable. Based on these findings, we propose FairFAL, an adaptive class-fair FAL framework. FairFAL (1) infers global imbalance and local-global divergence via lightweight prediction discrepancy, enabling adaptive selection between global and local query models; (2) performs prototype-guided pseudo-labeling using global features to promote class-aware querying; and (3) applies a two-stage uncertainty-diversity balanced sampling strategy with k-center refinement. Experiments on five benchmarks show that FairFAL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches under challenging long-tailed and non-IID settings. The code is available at https://github.com/chenchenzong/FairFAL.

AI.tldr: Adaptive class-fair Federated Active Learning (FairFAL) balances sampling across classes and chooses between global and local query models to mitigate long-tailed, non-IID data in FL. It uses lightweight prediction discrepancy for model selection, prototype-guided pseudo-labeling with global features, and a two-stage uncertainty-diversity sampling with k-center refinement, achieving consistent gains on five benchmarks.
