AI.motivation: In federated active learning, severe global class imbalance and client heterogeneity impede performance. There is a need for an adaptive query strategy that promotes minority-class coverage while leveraging both global and local information.
AI.method: FairFAL infers global imbalance and local-global divergence via lightweight prediction discrepancy to adaptively switch between global and local query models; it employs prototype-guided pseudo-labeling using global features to promote class-aware querying; and it implements a two-stage uncertainty-diversity balanced sampling with k-center refinement.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five benchmarks shows FairFAL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches under challenging long-tailed and non-IID settings; code is available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: FairFAL offers a robust, adaptive framework for FAL under realistic data distributions, delivering improved class balance and performance by integrating adaptive model querying, prototype-guided labeling, and balanced sampling.

title: Parallel-in-Time Nonlinear Optimal Control via GPU-native Sequential Convex Programming
summary: Real-time trajectory optimization for nonlinear constrained autonomous systems is critical and typically performed by CPU-based sequential solvers. Specifically, reliance on global sparse linear algebra or the serial nature of dynamic programming algorithms restricts the utilization of massively parallel computing architectures like GPUs. To bridge this gap, we introduce a fully GPU-native trajectory optimization framework that combines sequential convex programming with a consensus-based alternating direction method of multipliers. By applying a temporal splitting strategy, our algorithm decouples the optimization horizon into independent, per-node subproblems that execute massively in parallel. The entire process runs fully on the GPU, eliminating costly memory transfers and large-scale sparse factorizations. This architecture naturally scales to multi-trajectory optimization. We validate the solver on a quadrotor agile flight task and a Mars powered descent problem using an on-board edge computing platform. Benchmarks reveal a sustained 4x throughput speedup and a 51% reduction in energy consumption over a heavily optimized 12-core CPU baseline. Crucially, the framework saturates the hardware, maintaining over 96% active GPU utilization to achieve planning rates exceeding 100 Hz. Furthermore, we demonstrate the solver's extensibility to robust Model Predictive Control by jointly optimizing dynamically coupled scenarios under stochastic disturbances, enabling scalable and safe autonomy.

AI.tldr: GPU-native real-time trajectory optimization that decouples the horizon via temporal splitting and solves with a consensus-based ADMM within SCP, enabling massively parallel per-node subproblems on a GPU; achieves >100 Hz planning with high GPU utilization and energy savings, scalable to multi-trajectory and robust MPC.
AI.motivation: CPU-based sequential solvers and global sparse linear algebra/serial DP limit real-time performance on GPUs for nonlinear constrained autonomous systems; need for parallelizable, GPU-friendly trajectory optimization.
AI.method: Combine sequential convex programming with a consensus-based ADMM; apply temporal splitting to decouple the planning horizon into independent per-node subproblems solvable in parallel; fully GPU-resident workflow with no memory transfers or large sparse factorizations; scales to multi-trajectory optimization; extendable to robust Model Predictive Control under disturbances.
AI.result: Validated on quadrotor agile flight and Mars powered descent on-board; benchmarks show 4x throughput speedup and 51% energy reduction versus a heavily optimized 12-core CPU baseline; GPU utilization exceeds 96% and planning rates surpass 100 Hz.
AI.conclusion: A fully GPU-native trajectory optimization framework can overcome CPU-bound bottlenecks for nonlinear constrained autonomous systems, enabling scalable real-time planning and extending naturally to robust MPC.

title: How to make the most of your masked language model for protein engineering
comment: Accepted into the GEM Workshop, ICLR 2026
summary: A plethora of protein language models have been released in recent years. Yet comparatively little work has addressed how to best sample from them to optimize desired biological properties. We fill this gap by proposing a flexible, effective sampling method for masked language models (MLMs), and by systematically evaluating models and methods both in silico and in vitro on actual antibody therapeutics campaigns. Firstly, we propose sampling with stochastic beam search, exploiting the fact that MLMs are remarkably efficient at evaluating the pseudo-perplexity of the entire 1-edit neighborhood of a sequence. Reframing generation in terms of entire-sequence evaluation enables flexible guidance with multiple optimization objectives. Secondly, we report results from our extensive in vitro head-to-head evaluation for the antibody engineering setting. This reveals that choice of sampling method is at least as impactful as the model used, motivating future research into this under-explored area.

AI.tldr: Proposes stochastic beam search for sampling from protein language models (MLMs) to optimize antibody-related properties, and demonstrates that sampling strategy can be as influential as the model itself through in silico and in vitro evaluation.
AI.motivation: Address the under-explored role of sampling strategies in protein/antibody design with MLMs, aiming to connect in silico generation with practical antibody engineering outcomes and to improve therapeutic campaigns.
AI.method: Develops sampling with stochastic beam search, leveraging MLMs' ability to rapidly evaluate pseudo-perplexity across the 1-edit neighborhood. Reframes generation as complete-sequence evaluation to enable multi-objective guidance.
AI.result: In silico analyses show efficient evaluation and guidance; in vitro head-to-head experiments in antibody engineering reveal that the sampling method materially impacts outcomes, at least as much as the choice of model.
AI.conclusion: Sampling methodology is a critical and currently underutilized lever in protein sequence design, warranting further research into robust, multi-objective sampling strategies alongside model development.

title: UniStitch: Unifying Semantic and Geometric Features for Image Stitching
summary: Traditional image stitching methods estimate warps from hand-crafted geometric features, whereas recent learning-based solutions leverage semantic features from neural networks instead. These two lines of research have largely diverged along separate evolution, with virtually no meaningful convergence to date. In this paper, we take a pioneering step to bridge this gap by unifying semantic and geometric features with UniStitch, a unified image stitching framework from multimodal features. To align discrete geometric features (i.e., keypoint) with continuous semantic feature maps, we present a Neural Point Transformer (NPT) module, which transforms unordered, sparse 1D geometric keypoints into ordered, dense 2D semantic maps. Then, to integrate the advantages of both representations, an Adaptive Mixture of Experts (AMoE) module is designed to fuse geometric and semantic representations. It dynamically shifts focus toward more reliable features during the fusion process, allowing the model to handle complex scenes, especially when either modality might be compromised. The fused representation can be adopted into common deep stitching pipelines, delivering significant performance gains over any single feature. Experiments show that UniStitch outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods with a large margin, paving the way for a unified paradigm between traditional and learning-based image stitching.

AI.tldr: UniStitch unifies geometric keypoints and semantic feature maps for image stitching by introducing Neural Point Transformer (NPT) to convert keypoints into dense 2D feature maps and an Adaptive Mixture of Experts (AMoE) to fuse modalities, achieving state-of-the-art performance and robustness.
AI.motivation: Traditional stitching relies on hand-crafted geometric features while learning-based methods rely on semantic features; there is a gap between these approaches. Bridging them aims to leverage complementary strengths and improve robustness in complex scenes or when one modality is degraded.
AI.method: 1) Neural Point Transformer (NPT) converts unordered sparse keypoints (1D) into ordered dense semantic maps (2D). 2) Adaptive Mixture of Experts (AMoE) dynamically fuses geometric and semantic features, focusing on the more reliable modality during fusion. 3) The fused representation can be integrated into existing deep stitching pipelines.
AI.result: Experiments show UniStitch achieves large improvements over state-of-the-art methods, suggesting the approach effectively leverages complementary information from both feature types and improves robustness.
AI.conclusion: A pioneering unification of traditional geometric and learning-based semantic features for image stitching. The framework demonstrates strong empirical gains and establishes a new direction for multimodal feature fusion in stitching.

title: FutureVLA: Joint Visuomotor Prediction for Vision-Language-Action Model
summary: Predictive foresight is important to intelligent embodied agents. Since the motor execution of a robot is intrinsically constrained by its visual perception of environmental geometry, effectively anticipating the future requires capturing this tightly coupled visuomotor interplay. While recent vision-language-action models attempt to incorporate future guidance, they struggle with this joint modeling. Existing explicit methods divert capacity to task-irrelevant visual details, whereas implicit methods relying on sparse frame pairs disrupt temporal continuity. By heavily relying on visual reconstruction, these methods become visually dominated, entangling static scene context with dynamic action intent. We argue that effective joint visuomotor predictive modeling requires both temporal continuity and visually-conditioned supervision decoupling. To this end, we propose FutureVLA, featuring a novel Joint Visuomotor Predictive Architecture. FutureVLA is designed to extract joint visuomotor embeddings by first decoupling visual and motor information, and then jointly encoding generalized physical priors. Specifically, in the pretraining stage, we leverage heterogeneous manipulation datasets and introduce a Joint Visuomotor Gating mechanism to structurally separate visual state preservation from temporal action modeling. It allows the motor stream to focus on continuous physical dynamics while explicitly querying visual tokens for environmental constraints, yielding highly generalizable joint visuomotor embeddings. Subsequently, in the post-training stage, we employ a latent embeddings alignment strategy, enabling diverse downstream VLA models to internalize these temporal priors without modifying their inference architectures. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FutureVLA consistently improves VLA frameworks.

AI.tldr: FutureVLA introduces a Joint Visuomotor Predictive Architecture that decouples visual and motor streams and uses gating plus latent alignment to learn generalizable joint visuomotor embeddings, improving downstream vision-language-action models.
AI.motivation: Existing visuomotor predictive approaches struggle to jointly model perception and action: explicit methods waste capacity on nonessential visual details, while implicit methods harm temporal continuity. There is a need for temporally coherent, visually conditioned supervision that decouples the two streams.
AI.method: Pretrain with heterogeneous manipulation datasets using a Joint Visuomotor Gating mechanism to structurally separate visual state preservation from temporal action modeling; decouple visual and motor information and encode generalized physical priors. In post-training, apply latent embeddings alignment to allow diverse downstream VLA models to incorporate temporal priors without changing inference architectures.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show FutureVLA consistently improves VLA frameworks across tasks/datasets, producing more generalizable joint visuomotor embeddings and better temporal priors without altering downstream model architectures.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling, gating, and latent alignment enable robust joint visuomotor representations, improving predictive foresight in embodied agents and generalizing across VLA systems.

title: Data-Driven Integration Kernels for Interpretable Nonlocal Operator Learning
summary: Machine learning models can represent climate processes that are nonlocal in horizontal space, height, and time, often by combining information across these dimensions in highly nonlinear ways. While this can improve predictive skill, it makes learned relationships difficult to interpret and prone to overfitting as the extent of nonlocal information grows. We address this challenge by introducing data-driven integration kernels, a framework that adds structure to nonlocal operator learning by explicitly separating nonlocal information aggregation from local nonlinear prediction. Each spatiotemporal predictor field is first integrated using learnable kernels (defined as continuous weighting functions over horizontal space, height, and/or time), after which a local nonlinear mapping is applied only to the resulting kernel-integrated features and any optional local inputs. This design confines nonlinear interactions to a small set of integrated features and makes each kernel directly interpretable as a weighting pattern that reveals which horizontal locations, vertical levels, and past timesteps contribute most to the prediction. We demonstrate the framework for South Asian monsoon precipitation using a hierarchy of neural network models with increasing structure, including baseline, nonparametric kernel, and parametric kernel models. Across this hierarchy, kernel-based models achieve near-baseline performance with far fewer trainable parameters, showing that much of the relevant nonlocal information can be captured through a small set of interpretable integrations when appropriate structural constraints are imposed.

AI.tldr: A kernel-based framework for structured nonlocal learning that decouples nonlocal aggregation from local prediction using learnable integration kernels; yields interpretable weighting patterns and parameter efficiency with near-baseline accuracy on monsoon precipitation.
AI.motivation: Nonlocal spatiotemporal dependencies can boost predictive skill but make learned relations opaque and prone to overfitting when information from many locations/times is used. A framework is needed that adds structure for interpretability and data efficiency without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Introduce data-driven integration kernels: learnable continuous weighting functions over horizontal space, height, and/or time. Each spatiotemporal predictor field is integrated via these kernels, then a local nonlinear mapping is applied to the kernel-integrated features (and optional local inputs). This confines nonlinear interactions to a small set of integrated features and yields kernels that directly reveal contributing locations, levels, and past times. A hierarchical model suite (baseline, nonparametric kernel, parametric kernel) is tested on South Asian monsoon precipitation.
AI.result: Kernel-based models achieve near-baseline predictive performance with substantially fewer trainable parameters across the hierarchy, indicating that most nonlocal information can be captured through a small set of interpretable kernel integrations under the imposed structure.
AI.conclusion: Imposing structured, learnable integration kernels to handle nonlocal information yields interpretable weighting patterns and improved parameter efficiency, while maintaining predictive accuracy for climate-related tasks like monsoon precipitation.

title: R4-CGQA: Retrieval-based Vision Language Models for Computer Graphics Image Quality Assessment
summary: Immersive Computer Graphics (CGs) rendering has become ubiquitous in modern daily life. However, comprehensively evaluating CG quality remains challenging for two reasons: First, existing CG datasets lack systematic descriptions of rendering quality; and second existing CG quality assessment methods cannot provide reasonable text-based explanations. To address these issues, we first identify six key perceptual dimensions of CG quality from the user perspective and construct a dataset of 3500 CG images with corresponding quality descriptions. Each description covers CG style, content, and perceived quality along the selected dimensions. Furthermore, we use a subset of the dataset to build several question-answer benchmarks based on the descriptions in order to evaluate the responses of existing Vision Language Models (VLMs). We find that current VLMs are not sufficiently accurate in judging fine-grained CG quality, but that descriptions of visually similar images can significantly improve a VLM's understanding of a given CG image. Motivated by this observation, we adopt retrieval-augmented generation and propose a two-stream retrieval framework that effectively enhances the CG quality assessment capabilities of VLMs. Experiments on several representative VLMs demonstrate that our method substantially improves their performance on CG quality assessment.

AI.tldr: A dataset and QA benchmarks for CG quality assessment; introduces six perceptual dimensions, a 3500-image dataset with quality descriptions, QA benchmarks for VLMs, and a retrieval-augmented two-stream framework that improves CG quality assessment.
AI.motivation: CG rendering quality evaluation is challenging due to lack of descriptive datasets and explainable VLM reasoning; the work aims to enable text-based explanations and robust evaluation of CG quality.
AI.method: Identify six perceptual CG quality dimensions; build a dataset of 3500 CG images with descriptions spanning style, content, and perceived quality; create QA benchmarks from these descriptions to test VLMs; propose a two-stream retrieval-augmented framework to enhance CG quality assessment; validate across several representative VLMs.
AI.result: Current VLMs struggle with fine-grained CG quality judgments; using descriptions of visually similar images via retrieval-augmented generation significantly improves CG quality assessment performance.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented two-stream generation improves VLM-based CG quality assessment; the dataset and QA benchmarks provide a resource for future research and evaluation in CG quality.

title: Causal Concept Graphs in LLM Latent Space for Stepwise Reasoning
summary: Sparse autoencoders can localize where concepts live in language models, but not how they interact during multi-step reasoning. We propose Causal Concept Graphs (CCG): a directed acyclic graph over sparse, interpretable latent features, where edges capture learned causal dependencies between concepts. We combine task-conditioned sparse autoencoders for concept discovery with DAGMA-style differentiable structure learning for graph recovery and introduce the Causal Fidelity Score (CFS) to evaluate whether graph-guided interventions induce larger downstream effects than random ones. On ARC-Challenge, StrategyQA, and LogiQA with GPT-2 Medium, across five seeds ($n{=}15$ paired runs), CCG achieves $\CFS=5.654\pm0.625$, outperforming ROME-style tracing ($3.382\pm0.233$), SAE-only ranking ($2.479\pm0.196$), and a random baseline ($1.032\pm0.034$), with $p<0.0001$ after Bonferroni correction. Learned graphs are sparse (5-6\% edge density), domain-specific, and stable across seeds.

AI.tldr: CCG introduces a causal graph over sparse latent concepts learned per task; shows improved causal fidelity score on reasoning benchmarks vs baselines; graphs are sparse and stable.
AI.motivation: To move beyond locating concepts in language models to understanding how they causally interact during multi-step reasoning, enabling interpretable intervention and reasoning analysis.
AI.method: Task-conditioned sparse autoencoders for concept discovery; DAGMA-style differentiable structure learning to recover a directed acyclic graph; Causal Fidelity Score (CFS) to evaluate graph-guided interventions versus random interventions; evaluated on ARC-Challenge, StrategyQA, and LogiQA with GPT-2 Medium across five seeds (n=15 paired runs).
AI.result: CCG achieves CFS=5.654±0.625, outperforming ROME-style tracing (3.382±0.233), SAE-only ranking (2.479±0.196), and random baseline (1.032±0.034) with p<0.0001 after Bonferroni correction; learned graphs are sparse (5–6% edge density), domain-specific, and stable across seeds.
AI.conclusion: CCG provides interpretable, causal graphs of concept interactions that are sparse, stable, and effective in guiding downstream effects through targeted interventions, indicating a viable direction for analyzing multi-step reasoning in language models.

title: MAVEN: A Meta-Reinforcement Learning Framework for Varying-Dynamics Expertise in Agile Quadrotor Maneuvers
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for achieving online agile navigation with quadrotors. Despite this success, policies trained via standard RL typically fail to generalize across significant dynamic variations, exhibiting a critical lack of adaptability. This work introduces MAVEN, a meta-RL framework that enables a single policy to achieve robust end-to-end navigation across a wide range of quadrotor dynamics. Our approach features a novel predictive context encoder, which learns to infer a latent representation of the system dynamics from interaction history. We demonstrate our method in agile waypoint traversal tasks under two challenging scenarios: large variations in quadrotor mass and severe single-rotor thrust loss. We leverage a GPU-vectorized simulator to distribute tasks across thousands of parallel environments, overcoming the long training times of meta-RL to converge in less than an hour. Through extensive experiments in both simulation and the real world, we validate that MAVEN achieves superior adaptation and agility. The policy successfully executes zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, demonstrating robust online adaptation by performing high-speed maneuvers despite mass variations of up to 66.7% and single-rotor thrust losses as severe as 70%.

AI.tldr: MAVEN enables a single meta-RL policy to navigate across wide quadrotor dynamics via a predictive context encoder, achieving fast training and zero-shot sim-to-real transfer under mass and thrust variations.
AI.motivation: Standard RL policies generalize poorly to dynamic variations in quadrotor dynamics; need adaptable, sample-efficient methods to maintain agility across mass changes and actuator faults.
AI.method: Meta-RL framework MAVEN with a predictive context encoder that infers a latent dynamics representation from interaction history; GPU-vectorized simulator enabling thousands of parallel environments; end-to-end waypoint navigation; tested under large mass variations and single-rotor thrust loss; real-world transfer via zero-shot sim-to-real.
AI.result: Policy exhibits superior adaptation and agility; achieves zero-shot sim-to-real transfer; robust high-speed maneuvers despite mass up to 66.7% and thrust loss up to 70%.
AI.conclusion: A predictive-context meta-RL approach can generalize across broad quadrotor dynamics, enabling robust, online adaptation in agile navigation; large-scale parallelized training drastically reduces meta-RL training time.

title: Attribution as Retrieval: Model-Agnostic AI-Generated Image Attribution
comment: To appear in CVPR 2026, Code is at https://github.com/hongsong-wang/LIDA
summary: With the rapid advancement of AIGC technologies, image forensics will encounter unprecedented challenges. Traditional methods are incapable of dealing with increasingly realistic images generated by rapidly evolving image generation techniques. To facilitate the identification of AI-generated images and the attribution of their source models, generative image watermarking and AI-generated image attribution have emerged as key research focuses in recent years. However, existing methods are model-dependent, requiring access to the generative models and lacking generality and scalability to new and unseen generators. To address these limitations, this work presents a new paradigm for AI-generated image attribution by formulating it as an instance retrieval problem instead of a conventional image classification problem. We propose an efficient model-agnostic framework, called Low-bIt-plane-based Deepfake Attribution (LIDA). The input to LIDA is produced by Low-Bit Fingerprint Generation module, while the training involves Unsupervised Pre-Training followed by subsequent Few-Shot Attribution Adaptation. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that LIDA achieves state-of-the-art performance for both Deepfake detection and image attribution under zero- and few-shot settings. The code is at https://github.com/hongsong-wang/LIDA

AI.tldr: Proposes LIDA, a model-agnostic, instance-retrieval framework for AI-generated image attribution and deepfake detection using low-bit fingerprints and unsupervised pre-training with few-shot adaptation; achieves state-of-the-art results in zero- and few-shot settings.
AI.motivation: Traditional image-forensics methods struggle to generalize to unseen, realistic AI-generated images and require access to generator models; there is a need for a model-agnostic, scalable attribution/detection framework.
AI.method: Low-Bit-plane-based Deepfake Attribution (LIDA) framework. It uses a Low-Bit Fingerprint Generation module to produce inputs, treats attribution as instance retrieval rather than classification, and trains via unsupervised pre-training followed by few-shot attribution adaptation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance for both deepfake detection and image attribution under zero- and few-shot settings.
AI.conclusion: LIDA provides a generalizable, scalable approach to AI-generated image attribution and detection, addressing model-dependency limitations; code is available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Optimal Expert-Attention Allocation in Mixture-of-Experts: A Scalable Law for Dynamic Model Design
summary: This paper presents a novel extension of neural scaling laws to Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, focusing on the optimal allocation of compute between expert and attention sub-layers. As MoE architectures have emerged as an efficient method for scaling model capacity without proportionally increasing computation, determining the optimal expert-attention compute ratio becomes critical. We define the ratio $r$ as the fraction of total FLOPs per token dedicated to the expert layers versus the attention layers, and explore how this ratio interacts with the overall compute budget and model sparsity. Through extensive experiments with GPT-style MoE Transformers, we empirically find that the optimal ratio $r^*$ follows a power-law relationship with total compute and varies with sparsity. Our analysis leads to an explicit formula for $r^*$, enabling precise control over the expert-attention compute allocation. We generalize the Chinchilla scaling law by incorporating this architectural parameter, providing a new framework for tuning MoE models beyond size and data. Our findings offer practical guidelines for designing efficient MoE models, optimizing performance while respecting fixed compute budgets.

AI.tldr: Extends neural scaling laws to Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), deriving an optimal expert-attention compute ratio r* as a power-law function of total compute and sparsity, enabling MoE-aware generalized scaling (Chinchilla-like) for fixed budgets.
AI.motivation: Determine how to allocate compute between expert and attention sub-layers in MoE models under a fixed overall compute budget to maximize performance.
AI.method: Empirical study on GPT-style MoE Transformers. Define r as the fraction of FLOPs per token spent on expert layers versus attention layers. Systematically vary total compute and sparsity, fit a power-law form for r*(C, S), and derive an explicit formula. Generalize the Chinchilla scaling law by including r as an architectural parameter and validate with experiments.
AI.result: Identified that the optimal ratio r* follows a power-law with respect to total compute and is modulated by sparsity. Provided an explicit formula for r*(C, S) and demonstrated that incorporating r into scaling improves efficiency under fixed compute budgets for MoE models.
AI.conclusion: A new MoE-aware scaling framework that extends Chinchilla by incorporating the expert-attention compute ratio. Enables precise control of compute allocation in MoE architectures, guiding design and tuning under fixed budgets.

title: ASTER: Attitude-aware Suspended-payload Quadrotor Traversal via Efficient Reinforcement Learning
summary: Agile maneuvering of the quadrotor cable-suspended system is significantly hindered by its non-smooth hybrid dynamics. While model-free Reinforcement Learning (RL) circumvents explicit differentiation of complex models, achieving attitude-constrained or inverted flight remains an open challenge due to the extreme reward sparsity under strict orientation requirements. This paper presents ASTER, a robust RL framework that achieves, to our knowledge, the first successful autonomous inverted flight for the cable-suspended system. We propose hybrid-dynamics-informed state seeding (HDSS), an initialization strategy that back-propagates target configurations through physics-consistent kinematic inversions across both taut and slack cable phases. HDSS enables the policy to discover aggressive maneuvers that are unreachable via standard exploration. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate remarkable agility, precise attitude alignment, and robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer across complex trajectories.

AI.tldr: ASTER enables autonomous inverted flight of a cable-suspended quadrotor via hybrid-dynamics-informed state seeding, achieving aggressive maneuvers with zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: Non-smooth hybrid dynamics of cable-suspended quadrotors hinder agile maneuvering; model-free RL avoids differential modeling but suffers from extreme reward sparsity under strict orientation constraints, making attitude-constrained or inverted flight challenging.
AI.method: Introduce ASTER, an RL framework; propose hybrid-dynamics-informed state seeding (HDSS) that back-propagates target configurations through physics-consistent kinematic inversions across taut and slack cable phases; uses HDSS to guide exploration and enable discovery of aggressive maneuvers; validate via extensive simulations and real-world experiments with zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.
AI.result: First successful autonomous inverted flight for the cable-suspended system; demonstrations show remarkable agility, precise attitude alignment, and robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer across complex trajectories.
AI.conclusion: HDSS-augmented RL enables inversion and agile maneuvers for cable-suspended quadrotors, addressing reward sparsity and exploration challenges; results motivate broader applicability to non-smooth hybrid dynamics and future work on robustness and scaling.

title: Need for Speed: Zero-Shot Depth Completion with Single-Step Diffusion
summary: We introduce Marigold-SSD, a single-step, late-fusion depth completion framework that leverages strong diffusion priors while eliminating the costly test-time optimization typically associated with diffusion-based methods. By shifting computational burden from inference to finetuning, our approach enables efficient and robust 3D perception under real-world latency constraints. Marigold-SSD achieves significantly faster inference with a training cost of only 4.5 GPU days. We evaluate our method across four indoor and two outdoor benchmarks, demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization and zero-shot performance compared to existing depth completion approaches. Our approach significantly narrows the efficiency gap between diffusion-based and discriminative models. Finally, we challenge common evaluation protocols by analyzing performance under varying input sparsity levels. Page: https://dtu-pas.github.io/marigold-ssd/

AI.tldr: Marigold-SSD is a single-step, late-fusion depth completion method that uses diffusion priors and shifts computation from inference to training, delivering fast inference (training cost ~4.5 GPU-days) and strong cross-domain/zero-shot performance across indoor/outdoor benchmarks, narrowing the gap to discriminative models and enabling analysis under varying sparsity.
AI.motivation: Address latency-constrained depth completion and the heavy test-time optimization required by diffusion-based methods; aim for robust cross-domain generalization with a practical training budget.
AI.method: Proposes a single-step, late-fusion depth completion framework (Marigold-SSD) that leverages strong diffusion priors while moving computational burden from inference to finetuning; eliminates test-time diffusion optimization; reports a training cost of 4.5 GPU days; evaluated on four indoor and two outdoor benchmarks.
AI.result: Achieves significantly faster inference with modest training cost; demonstrates strong cross-domain generalization and zero-shot performance compared to existing depth completion methods; narrows the efficiency gap between diffusion-based and discriminative models; provides analysis of performance under varying input sparsity levels.
AI.conclusion: Marigold-SSD shows that diffusion priors can be exploited efficiently via training-time optimization to enable practical, robust depth completion under latency constraints, and invites reevaluation of standard evaluation protocols in sparsity-aware contexts.

title: Semantic Landmark Particle Filter for Robot Localisation in Vineyards
summary:
Reliable localisation in vineyards is hindered by row-level perceptual aliasing: parallel crop rows produce nearly identical LiDAR observations, causing geometry-only and vision-based SLAM systems to converge towards incorrect corridors, particularly during headland transitions. We present a Semantic Landmark Particle Filter (SLPF) that integrates trunk and pole landmark detections with 2D LiDAR within a probabilistic localisation framework. Detected trunks are converted into semantic walls, forming structural row boundaries embedded in the measurement model to improve discrimination between adjacent rows. GNSS is incorporated as a lightweight prior that stabilises localisation when semantic observations are sparse.
  Field experiments in a 10-row vineyard demonstrate consistent improvements over geometry-only (AMCL), vision-based (RTAB-Map), and GNSS baselines. Compared to AMCL, SLPF reduces Absolute Pose Error by 22% and 65% across two traversal directions; relative to a NoisyGNSS baseline, APE decreases by 65% and 61%. Row correctness improves from 0.67 to 0.73, while mean cross-track error decreases from 1.40 m to 1.26 m. These results show that embedding row-level structural semantics within the measurement model enables robust localisation in highly repetitive outdoor agricultural environments.

AI.tldr: SLPF uses semantic trunk/pole landmarks as walls within a 2D LiDAR-based localization framework to resolve row-level perceptual aliasing in vineyards; GNSS as a lightweight prior; shows substantial improvements over geometry-only and vision-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Perceptual aliasing due to repetitive vineyard rows causes SLAM to converge to incorrect corridors, especially during headland transitions; need semantic cues to disambiguate rows and robustly localize.
AI.method: Semantic Landmark Particle Filter integrating trunk/pole detections converted into semantic walls; measurement model augmented with structural row boundaries; GNSS priors; evaluation in a 10-row vineyard; comparisons to AMCL, RTAB-Map, NoisyGNSS.
AI.result: Compared to AMCL: APE reduced by 22% and 65% in two traversal directions; compared to NoisyGNSS: APE reduced by 65% and 61%; row correctness improved 0.67→0.73; mean cross-track error reduced 1.40 m→1.26 m.
AI.conclusion: Embedding row-level structural semantics in the measurement model yields robust localization in highly repetitive outdoor agricultural environments; SLPF mitigates perceptual aliasing and improves reliability.

title: Layer Consistency Matters: Elegant Latent Transition Discrepancy for Generalizable Synthetic Image Detection
summary: Recent rapid advancement of generative models has significantly improved the fidelity and accessibility of AI-generated synthetic images. While enabling various innovative applications, the unprecedented realism of these synthetics makes them increasingly indistinguishable from authentic photographs, posing serious security risks, such as media credibility and content manipulation. Although extensive efforts have been dedicated to detecting synthetic images, most existing approaches suffer from poor generalization to unseen data due to their reliance on model-specific artifacts or low-level statistical cues. In this work, we identify a previously unexplored distinction that real images maintain consistent semantic attention and structural coherence in their latent representations, exhibiting more stable feature transitions across network layers, whereas synthetic ones present discernible distinct patterns. Therefore, we propose a novel approach termed latent transition discrepancy (LTD), which captures the inter-layer consistency differences of real and synthetic images. LTD adaptively identifies the most discriminative layers and assesses the transition discrepancies across layers. Benefiting from the proposed inter-layer discriminative modeling, our approach exceeds the base model by 14.35\% in mean Acc across three datasets containing diverse GANs and DMs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LTD outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior detection accuracy, generalizability, and robustness. The code is available at https://github.com/yywencs/LTD

AI.tldr: Introduce latent transition discrepancy (LTD) to detect synthetic images by measuring inter-layer latent transition consistency; adaptively selects discriminative layers; yields ~14.35% gain in mean accuracy across three datasets and outperforms SOTA; code released.
AI.motivation: Generative models produce highly realistic images, making detection challenging. Existing detectors often rely on model-specific artifacts or low-level cues, leading to poor generalization. A robust detector should capture semantic-latent structure consistency across layers that distinguishes real from synthetic representations.
AI.method: Propose LTD, an inter-layer discriminative model that quantifies transition discrepancies between latent representations across network layers. It adaptively identifies the most discriminative layers and computes inter-layer consistency gaps between real and synthetic images to form a robust detector.
AI.result: LTD achieves a 14.35% improvement in mean accuracy over a base model across three datasets containing diverse GANs and diffusion models, surpassing recent state-of-the-art methods in detection accuracy, generalization, and robustness.
AI.conclusion: LTD provides a generalizable and robust detection signal by exploiting latent space dynamics across layers rather than relying on artifact cues, illustrating strong cross-domain effectiveness for synthetic image detection; code is publicly available.

title: On the Learning Dynamics of Two-layer Linear Networks with Label Noise SGD
summary: One crucial factor behind the success of deep learning lies in the implicit bias induced by noise inherent in gradient-based training algorithms. Motivated by empirical observations that training with noisy labels improves model generalization, we delve into the underlying mechanisms behind stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with label noise. Focusing on a two-layer over-parameterized linear network, we analyze the learning dynamics of label noise SGD, unveiling a two-phase learning behavior. In \emph{Phase I}, the magnitudes of model weights progressively diminish, and the model escapes the lazy regime; enters the rich regime. In \emph{Phase II}, the alignment between model weights and the ground-truth interpolator increases, and the model eventually converges. Our analysis highlights the critical role of label noise in driving the transition from the lazy to the rich regime and minimally explains its empirical success. Furthermore, we extend these insights to Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), showing that the principles governing label noise SGD also apply to broader optimization algorithms. Extensive experiments, conducted under both synthetic and real-world setups, strongly support our theory. Our code is released at https://github.com/a-usually/Label-Noise-SGD.

AI.tldr: Label-noise SGD on a two-layer over-parameterized linear network exhibits a two-phase learning dynamics: Phase I shrinks weights and exits the lazy regime; Phase II increases alignment with the ground-truth interpolator and converges. Label noise is key to driving the lazy-to-rich regime transition, explaining improved generalization, and these insights extend to Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM). Empirical results on synthetic and real data support the theory; code is released.
AI.motivation: To understand why training with noisy labels via SGD improves generalization and to uncover the implicit bias and dynamics that enable this effect, particularly the transition from lazy (NTK-like) to rich (feature-learning) regimes.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of the learning dynamics of SGD with label noise on a two-layer over-parameterized linear network, identifying a two-phase process. Phase I reduces weight magnitudes and escapes the lazy regime; Phase II increases alignment with the ground-truth interpolator leading to convergence. Extensions to SAM are discussed, and experiments on synthetic and real datasets validate the theory.
AI.result: Demonstrates a two-phase learning behavior: Phase I shrinks weights and escapes the lazy regime; Phase II enhances alignment with the true interpolator and convergence occurs. Label noise is essential for triggering the lazy-to-rich transition. The framework generalizes to SAM, with empirical results supporting the theory.
AI.conclusion: Label noise in SGD induces a transition from lazy to rich representation learning, underpinning improved generalization. These principles apply beyond label-noise SGD, extending to broader optimization strategies like SAM. The findings are corroborated by extensive experiments, and the authors provide code for replication.

title: GRACE: A Unified 2D Multi-Robot Path Planning Simulator & Benchmark for Grid, Roadmap, And Continuous Environments
comment: ICRA 2026, code will be released soon
summary: Advancing Multi-Agent Pathfinding (MAPF) and Multi-Robot Motion Planning (MRMP) requires platforms that enable transparent, reproducible comparisons across modeling choices. Existing tools either scale under simplifying assumptions (grids, homogeneous agents) or offer higher fidelity with less comparable instrumentation. We present GRACE, a unified 2D simulator+benchmark that instantiates the same task at multiple abstraction levels (grid, roadmap, continuous) via explicit, reproducible operators and a common evaluation protocol. Our empirical results on public maps and representative planners enable commensurate comparisons on a shared instance set. Furthermore, we quantify the expected representation-fidelity trade-offs (MRMP solves instances at higher fidelity but lower speed, while grid/roadmap planners scale farther). By consolidating representation, execution, and evaluation, GRACE thereby aims to make cross-representation studies more comparable and provides a means to advance multi-robot planning research and its translation to practice.

AI.tldr: GRACE is a unified 2D simulator/benchmark for MAPF and MRMP that enables same-task evaluation across grid, roadmap, and continuous representations using reproducible operators and a shared protocol, enabling commensurate cross-representation comparisons and elucidating fidelity–speed trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Existing MAPF/MRMP tools either scale with simplified representations (grids, homogeneous agents) but lack comparability, or offer high fidelity with less transparent instrumentation; there is a need for a unified, reproducible platform to compare modeling choices.
AI.method: Introduce GRACE, a unified 2D simulator+benchmark that can instantiate the same task at multiple abstraction levels (grid, roadmap, continuous) via explicit, reproducible operators and a common evaluation protocol; evaluate on public maps with representative planners; provide a shared instance set and quantify representation-fidelity trade-offs.
AI.result: GRACE enables commensurate comparisons on a shared set of instances; it quantifies fidelity trade-offs: MRMP achieves higher representation fidelity but lower speed, while grid/roadmap approaches scale farther; consolidates representation, execution, and evaluation to improve cross-representation studies.
AI.conclusion: GRACE provides a common platform to compare representations in MAPF/MRMP, facilitating progress in multi-robot planning research and smoother translation to practice.

title: Variance-Aware Adaptive Weighting for Diffusion Model Training
summary: Diffusion models have recently achieved remarkable success in generative modeling, yet their training dynamics across different noise levels remain highly imbalanced, which can lead to inefficient optimization and unstable learning behavior. In this work, we investigate this imbalance from the perspective of loss variance across log-SNR levels and propose a variance-aware adaptive weighting strategy to address it. The proposed approach dynamically adjusts training weights based on the observed variance distribution, encouraging a more balanced optimization process across noise levels. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 demonstrate that the proposed method consistently improves generative performance over standard training schemes, achieving lower Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) while also reducing performance variance across random seeds. Additional analysis, including loss-log-SNR visualization, variance heatmaps, and ablation studies, further reveal that the adaptive weighting effectively stabilizes training dynamics. These results highlight the potential of variance-aware training strategies for improving diffusion model optimization.

AI.tldr: Proposes a variance-aware adaptive weighting scheme for diffusion-model training to balance loss variance across log-SNR noise levels; shows improved FID and reduced seed variance on CIFAR-10/100; supported by visualizations and ablation analyses.
AI.motivation: Training dynamics in diffusion models are uneven across noise levels due to varying loss variance, leading to inefficient optimization and unstable learning.
AI.method: Introduce a dynamic, variance-aware weighting strategy that modulates per-noise-level training weights based on observed loss-variance across log-SNR levels, promoting balanced optimization.
AI.result: Empirically improves generative performance (lower FID) and reduces performance variance across seeds on CIFAR-10/100; training dynamics appear more stable, as shown by loss-log-SNR plots and variance heatmaps; ablation studies corroborate the contribution.
AI.conclusion: Variance-aware training offers a practical and effective approach to stabilize diffusion-model optimization and improve robustness; further validation on broader datasets and architectures could establish general applicability.

title: HyPER-GAN: Hybrid Patch-Based Image-to-Image Translation for Real-Time Photorealism Enhancement
summary: Generative models are widely employed to enhance the photorealism of synthetic data for training computer vision algorithms. However, they often introduce visual artifacts that degrade the accuracy of these algorithms and require high computational resources, limiting their applicability in real-time training or evaluation scenarios. In this paper, we propose Hybrid Patch Enhanced Realism Generative Adversarial Network (HyPER-GAN), a lightweight image-to-image translation method based on a U-Net-style generator designed for real-time inference. The model is trained using paired synthetic and photorealism-enhanced images, complemented by a hybrid training strategy that incorporates matched patches from real-world data to improve visual realism and semantic consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that HyPER-GAN outperforms state-of-the-art paired image-to-image translation methods in terms of inference latency, visual realism, and semantic robustness. Moreover, it is illustrated that the proposed hybrid training strategy indeed improves visual quality and semantic consistency compared to training the model solely with paired synthetic and photorealism-enhanced images. Code and pretrained models are publicly available for download at: https://github.com/stefanos50/HyPER-GAN

AI.tldr: A lightweight paired image-to-image translation (HyPER-GAN) that uses a U-Net generator and a hybrid training strategy with matched real patches to boost realism and semantic consistency, achieving lower latency and improved qualitative/semantic performance over current methods; code available.
AI.motivation: Generative models boost photorealism for synthetic data but commonly introduce artifacts and require heavy computation, hindering real-time training/evaluation. There is a need for fast, realistic, semantically robust image-to-image translation.
AI.method: HyPER-GAN uses a compact U-Net-style generator for real-time inference and trains on paired synthetic and photorealism-enhanced images. The key novelty is a hybrid training strategy that incorporates matched patches from real-world data to enhance realism and maintain semantic consistency.
AI.result: Reportedly outperforms state-of-the-art paired image-to-image translation methods in inference latency, visual realism, and semantic robustness. The hybrid training strategy improves visual quality and semantic consistency compared to training with only paired synthetic and photorealism-enhanced images.
AI.conclusion: HyPER-GAN offers real-time, realistic image-to-image translation for synthetic data with improved semantic reliability, supported by publicly available code and pretrained models.

title: Designing Service Systems from Textual Evidence
summary: Designing service systems requires selecting among alternative configurations -- choosing the best chatbot variant, the optimal routing policy, or the most effective quality control procedure. In many service systems, the primary evidence of performance quality is textual -- customer support transcripts, complaint narratives, compliance review reports -- rather than the scalar measurements assumed by classical optimization methods. Large language models (LLMs) can read such textual evidence and produce standardized quality scores, but these automated judges exhibit systematic biases that vary across alternatives and evaluation instances. Human expert review remains accurate but costly. We study how to identify the best service configuration with high confidence while minimizing expensive human audits, given that automated evaluation is cheap but biased. We formalize this as a sequential decision problem where a biased proxy score is observed for every evaluation, and a verified outcome can be acquired selectively at additional cost. We prove that LLM-only selection fails under arm-dependent bias, and that naive selective-audit estimators can be asymptotically biased. We develop an estimator combining proxy scores with inverse-propensity-weighted residuals and construct anytime-valid confidence sequences. Our algorithm, PP-LUCB, jointly decides which alternatives to evaluate and whether to request human audits, concentrating reviews where the LLM judge is least reliable. We prove correctness and establish instance-dependent cost bounds showing near-optimal efficiency. On a customer support ticket classification task, our algorithm correctly identifies the best model in 40/40 trials while achieving 90\% audit cost reduction.

AI.tldr: PP-LUCB enables best-service-configuration selection under biased, arm-dependent automated evaluations by combining cheap proxy scores with selective human audits; uses inverse-propensity-weighted residuals and anytime-valid confidence sequences; achieves near-optimal efficiency. In empirical validation, exactly identified the best model in 40/40 trials with 90% audit-cost reduction.
AI.motivation: In service systems, performance evidence is often textual and evaluated via LLM-based proxies. These proxies are cheap but biased and vary across alternatives and evaluation instances, complicating reliable selection. A principled sequential decision framework is needed to minimize audit costs while maintaining high confidence in the chosen configuration.
AI.method: Formulate as a sequential decision problem with biased proxy scores observed for each evaluation and optional verified outcomes (audits) at additional cost. Develop an estimator using inverse-propensity-weighted residuals to correct for bias, and construct anytime-valid confidence sequences. Propose PP-LUCB, an algorithm that jointly decides which alternatives to evaluate and when to request human audits, targeting evaluations where the LLM judge is least reliable.
AI.result: Theoretical results show that LLM-only selection fails under arm-dependent bias and naive selective-audit estimators can be asymptotically biased. PP-LUCB is correct and achieves instance-dependent cost bounds with near-optimal efficiency. Empirically, on a customer support ticket classification task, the method identified the best model in all 40 trials and achieved a 90% reduction in audit cost.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates a principled, cost-efficient framework for reliable best-arm identification under biased automated evaluation, integrating cheap but biased proxies with selective audits. The approach generalizes to other domains where textual evidence dominates performance signals and auditing is expensive.

title: FG-CLTP: Fine-Grained Contrastive Language Tactile Pretraining for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Recent advancements in integrating tactile sensing into vision-language-action (VLA) models have demonstrated transformative potential for robotic perception. However, existing tactile representations predominantly rely on qualitative descriptors (e.g., texture), neglecting quantitative contact states such as force magnitude, contact geometry, and principal axis orientation, which are indispensable for fine-grained manipulation. To bridge this gap, we propose FG-CLTP, a fine-grained contrastive language tactile pretraining framework. We first introduce a novel dataset comprising over 100k tactile 3D point cloud-language pairs that explicitly capture multidimensional contact states from the sensor's perspective. We then implement a discretized numerical tokenization mechanism to achieve quantitative-semantic alignment, effectively injecting explicit physical metrics into the multimodal feature space. The proposed FG-CLTP model yields a 95.9% classification accuracy and reduces the regression error (MAE) by 52.6% compared to state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, the integration of 3D point cloud representations establishes a sensor-agnostic foundation with a minimal sim-to-real gap of 3.5%. Building upon this fine-grained representation, we develop a 3D tactile-language-action (3D-TLA) architecture driven by a flow matching policy to enable multimodal reasoning and control. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms strong baselines in contact-rich manipulation tasks, providing a robust and generalizable foundation for tactile-language-action models.

AI.tldr: FG-CLTP introduces a fine-grained tactile pretraining framework that uses 3D tactile point clouds and language with discretized numerical tokens to encode quantitative contact states, enabling quantitative-semantic alignment; it yields high classification accuracy, reduced MAE, and a small sim-to-real gap, and supports a 3D-TLA architecture for multimodal reasoning and control that surpasses baselines in contact-rich manipulation.
AI.motivation: Tactile representations in VLA models are largely qualitative (e.g., texture) and miss quantitative contact states (force, geometry, principal axis orientation), which are essential for precise manipulation. A large-scale dataset capturing multidimensional contact states from the sensor perspective and a tokenization scheme for quantitative metrics can improve multimodal alignment and generalization.
AI.method: 1) Build a dataset with over 100k tactile 3D point cloud–language pairs capturing multidimensional contact states. 2) Implement discretized numerical tokenization to align quantitative metrics with language, enabling quantitative-semantic fusion in the multimodal space. 3) Pretrain with FG-CLTP (fine-grained contrastive language tactile pretraining). 4) Develop a 3D tactile-language-action (3D-TLA) architecture driven by a flow matching policy for multimodal reasoning and control.
AI.result: Classification accuracy: 95.9%. MAE regression error reduced by 52.6% relative to state-of-the-art methods. Demonstrates a sensor-agnostic foundation with a sim-to-real gap of 3.5%. Extensive experiments show superior performance over strong baselines in contact-rich manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained tactile-language representations with quantitative tokens can substantially improve multimodal perception and control in tactile-rich tasks. The combination of a large, multidimensional tactile dataset, quantitative tokenization, and a flow-matching 3D-TLA architecture yields robust, generalizable models that bridge tactile sensing, language, and action.

title: Graph-GRPO: Training Graph Flow Models with Reinforcement Learning
summary: Graph generation is a fundamental task with broad applications, such as drug discovery. Recently, discrete flow matching-based graph generation, \aka, graph flow model (GFM), has emerged due to its superior performance and flexible sampling. However, effectively aligning GFMs with complex human preferences or task-specific objectives remains a significant challenge. In this paper, we propose Graph-GRPO, an online reinforcement learning (RL) framework for training GFMs under verifiable rewards. Our method makes two key contributions: (1) We derive an analytical expression for the transition probability of GFMs, replacing the Monte Carlo sampling and enabling fully differentiable rollouts for RL training; (2) We propose a refinement strategy that randomly perturbs specific nodes and edges in a graph, and regenerates them, allowing for localized exploration and self-improvement of generation quality. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of Graph-GRPO. With only 50 denoising steps, our method achieves 95.0\% and 97.5\% Valid-Unique-Novelty scores on the planar and tree datasets, respectively. Moreover, Graph-GRPO achieves state-of-the-art performance on the molecular optimization tasks, outperforming graph-based and fragment-based RL methods as well as classic genetic algorithms.

AI.tldr: Graph-GRPO is an online RL framework for training discrete-flow graph generators (GFMs) with verifiable rewards, introducing an analytic transition probability and a node/edge refinement strategy to enable differentiable rollouts and localized exploration; it achieves strong results on synthetic/real graphs and state-of-the-art performance in molecular optimization.
AI.motivation: GFMs struggle to align with complex human preferences or task-specific objectives due to reliance on Monte Carlo sampling and non-differentiable rollouts. An analytically tractable, differentiable RL framework with verifiable rewards can improve sample efficiency and generation quality.
AI.method: (1) Derive an analytical expression for the GFM transition probability to replace Monte Carlo sampling, enabling fully differentiable rollouts for RL training; (2) Introduce a refinement strategy that randomly perturbs selected nodes and edges and regenerates them, enabling localized exploration and self-improvement of generation quality.
AI.result: Experiments show strong performance on synthetic and real datasets: with 50 denoising steps, achieve 95.0% and 97.5% Valid-Unique-Novelty on planar and tree datasets; state-of-the-art performance on molecular optimization, outperforming graph-based, fragment-based RL methods and classic genetic algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Graph-GRPO effectively aligns GFMs with verifiable rewards, enabling efficient online RL training and improved generation quality across tasks, as evidenced by strong dataset-wide results and molecular optimization benchmarks.

title: Splat2Real: Novel-view Scaling for Physical AI with 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Physical AI faces viewpoint shift between training and deployment, and novel-view robustness is essential for monocular RGB-to-3D perception. We cast Real2Render2Real monocular depth pretraining as imitation-learning-style supervision from a digital twin oracle: a student depth network imitates expert metric depth/visibility rendered from a scene mesh, while 3DGS supplies scalable novel-view observations. We present Splat2Real, centered on novel-view scaling: performance depends more on which views are added than on raw view count. We introduce CN-Coverage, a coverage+novelty curriculum that greedily selects views by geometry gain and an extrapolation penalty, plus a quality-aware guardrail fallback for low-reliability teachers. Across 20 TUM RGB-D sequences with step-matched budgets (N=0 to 2000 additional rendered views, with N unique <= 500 and resampling for larger budgets), naive scaling is unstable; CN-Coverage mitigates worst-case regressions relative to Robot/Coverage policies, and GOL-Gated CN-Coverage provides the strongest medium-high-budget stability with the lowest high-novelty tail error. Downstream control-proxy results versus N provides embodied-relevance evidence by shifting safety/progress trade-offs under viewpoint shift.

AI.tldr: Imitates depth from a digital-twin expert using a novel-view curriculum (CN-Coverage) within Splat2Real, achieving robust monocular 3D perception under viewpoint shifts and varying render budgets; GOL-Gated CN-Coverage delivers strongest stability.
AI.motivation: Physical AI deployment suffers from viewpoint shift; monocular depth must generalize to unseen views. Existing naive data scaling is unstable under diverse view distributions. The work seeks robust, scalable pretraining via imitation from a digital-twin oracle and a principled view-selection curriculum.
AI.method: Real2Render2Real monocular depth pretraining: a student network imitates expert metric depth/visibility rendered from a scene mesh; 3DGS provides scalable novel-view observations. Splat2Real centers on novel-view scaling. CN-Coverage greedily selects views by geometry gain and an extrapolation penalty with a quality-aware guardrail for unreliable teachers. Variants include GOL-Gated CN-Coverage. Experiments on 20 TUM RGB-D sequences with budgets N=0–2000 added rendered views (N unique ≤500, with resampling for larger budgets).
AI.result: CN-Coverage reduces worst-case regressions relative to naive-robot/coverage baselines across budgets; GOL-Gated CN-Coverage achieves strongest medium-high-budget stability with the lowest high-novelty tail error. Downstream control-proxy results under viewpoint shift show embodied relevance by shifting safety/progress trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: A view-selection curriculum grounded in geometry gain and extrapolation penalties yields robust, scalable monocular depth pretraining under viewpoint shifts. Incorporating a quality-aware fallback for unreliable teachers further stabilizes learning, with GOL-Gated CN-Coverage offering the best performance at higher budgets.

title: Effective Dataset Distillation for Spatio-Temporal Forecasting with Bi-dimensional Compression
comment: to be published in the 42nd IEEE International Conference on Data Engineering (ICDE '26)
summary: Spatio-temporal time series are widely used in real-world applications, including traffic prediction and weather forecasting. They are sequences of observations over extensive periods and multiple locations, naturally represented as multidimensional data. Forecasting is a central task in spatio-temporal analysis, and numerous deep learning methods have been developed to address it. However, as dataset sizes and model complexities continue to grow in practice, training deep learning models has become increasingly time- and resource-intensive. A promising solution to this challenge is dataset distillation, which synthesizes compact datasets that can effectively replace the original data for model training. Although successful in various domains, including time series analysis, existing dataset distillation methods compress only one dimension, making them less suitable for spatio-temporal datasets, where both spatial and temporal dimensions jointly contribute to the large data volume. To address this limitation, we propose STemDist, the first dataset distillation method specialized for spatio-temporal time series forecasting. A key idea of our solution is to compress both temporal and spatial dimensions in a balanced manner, reducing training time and memory. We further reduce the distillation cost by performing distillation at the cluster level rather than the individual location level, and we complement this coarse-grained approach with a subset-based granular distillation technique that enhances forecasting performance. On five real-world datasets, we show empirically that, compared to both general and time-series dataset distillation methods, datasets distilled by our STemDist method enable model training (1) faster (up to 6X) (2) more memory-efficient (up to 8X), and (3) more effective (with up to 12% lower prediction error).

AI.tldr: A spatio-temporal dataset distillation method (STemDist) compresses both spatial and temporal dimensions for forecasting, using cluster-level distillation plus subset-based granular refinement to achieve faster, more memory-efficient, and more accurate models on real-world data.
AI.motivation: To address the growing size and complexity of spatio-temporal datasets where existing distillation methods only compress a single dimension, leading to limited efficiency gains for spatial-temporal forecasting.
AI.method: STemDist performs balanced compression across spatial and temporal dimensions. It accelerates distillation by operating at the cluster level (grouping locations) and augments this with a subset-based granular distillation to preserve per-location forecasting performance. This combination reduces training cost while maintaining or improving accuracy.
AI.result: On five real-world datasets, STemDist yields up to 6x faster training, up to 8x memory savings, and up to 12% lower prediction error compared with baselines that include general and time-series distillation methods.
AI.conclusion: STemDist is the first specialized dataset distillation method for spatio-temporal forecasting, enabling substantial efficiency gains without sacrificing accuracy. The cluster-level approach paired with granular refinement balances scalability and performance across space and time.

title: RL-Augmented MPC for Non-Gaited Legged and Hybrid Locomotion
summary: We propose a contact-explicit hierarchical architecture coupling Reinforcement Learning (RL) and Model Predictive Control (MPC), where a high-level RL agent provides gait and navigation commands to a low-level locomotion MPC. This offloads the combinatorial burden of contact timing from the MPC by learning acyclic gaits through trial and error in simulation. We show that only a minimal set of rewards and limited tuning are required to obtain effective policies. We validate the architecture in simulation across robotic platforms spanning 50 kg to 120 kg and different MPC implementations, observing the emergence of acyclic gaits and timing adaptations in flat-terrain legged and hybrid locomotion, and further demonstrating extensibility to non-flat terrains. Across all platforms, we achieve zero-shot sim-to-sim transfer without domain randomization, and we further demonstrate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer without domain randomization on Centauro, our 120 kg wheeled-legged humanoid robot. We make our software framework and evaluation results publicly available at https://github.com/AndrePatri/AugMPC.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical RL+MPC framework where a high-level RL policy provides gait and navigation commands to a low-level MPC controller for locomotion, enabling acyclic gaits and reducing the MPC's contact-timing burden. Demonstrates zero-shot sim-to-sim and sim-to-real transfer without domain randomization across multiple platforms (50–120 kg) and terrains; code release provided.
AI.motivation: Reduce the combinatorial complexity of contact timing in legged locomotion MPC and achieve broad transfer across platforms and terrains by learning gaits, enabling zero-shot sim-to-real deployment without heavy domain randomization.
AI.method: A contact-explicit hierarchical architecture: a high-level RL agent outputs gait and navigation commands to a low-level locomotion MPC. Training in simulation with minimal reward design. Validated across platforms spanning 50–120 kg with different MPC implementations, showing emergence of acyclic gaits and timing adaptations on flat-terrain legged and hybrid locomotion, extendable to non-flat terrains. Demonstrates zero-shot sim-to-sim transfer without domain randomization, and zero-shot sim-to-real transfer without domain randomization on Centauro (120 kg). Code/results released publicly.
AI.result: Emergence of acyclic gaits and adaptive timing across platforms; successful zero-shot sim-to-sim transfer without domain randomization; successful zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on Centauro without domain randomization; broad applicability across platforms and terrains; public framework and results.
AI.conclusion: A hierarchical RL+MPC framework with explicit contact modeling reduces MPC burden, yields generalizable, acyclic gaits, and enables robust cross-platform and sim-to-real deployment without heavy domain randomization, with open-source resources for reproducibility.

title: Less is More: Decoder-Free Masked Modeling for Efficient Skeleton Representation Learning
comment: Please visit our project page at https://kaist-viclab.github.io/SLiM_site/
summary: The landscape of skeleton-based action representation learning has evolved from Contrastive Learning (CL) to Masked Auto-Encoder (MAE) architectures. However, each paradigm faces inherent limitations: CL often overlooks fine-grained local details, while MAE is burdened by computationally heavy decoders. Moreover, MAE suffers from severe computational asymmetry -- benefiting from efficient masking during pre-training but requiring exhaustive full-sequence processing for downstream tasks. To resolve these bottlenecks, we propose SLiM (Skeleton Less is More), a novel unified framework that harmonizes masked modeling with contrastive learning via a shared encoder. By eschewing the reconstruction decoder, SLiM not only eliminates computational redundancy but also compels the encoder to capture discriminative features directly. SLiM is the first framework with decoder-free masked modeling of representative learning. Crucially, to prevent trivial reconstruction arising from high skeletal-temporal correlation, we introduce semantic tube masking, alongside skeletal-aware augmentations designed to ensure anatomical consistency across diverse temporal granularities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SLiM consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across all downstream protocols. Notably, our method delivers this superior accuracy with exceptional efficiency, reducing inference computational cost by 7.89x compared to existing MAE methods.

AI.tldr: SLiM: a decoder-free masked modeling framework for skeleton-based action representation that unifies masked modeling with contrastive learning via a shared encoder; introduces semantic tube masking and skeletal-aware augmentations; achieves state-of-the-art performance with ~7.9x inference efficiency gains over MAE.
AI.motivation: Contrastive Learning (CL) often misses fine-grained local details, while Masked Auto-Encoders (MAE) rely on heavy decoders and incur computational asymmetry during pre-training and downstream tasks. A decoder-free approach aims to reduce computation while forcing the encoder to learn discriminative features.
AI.method: Decoder-free masked modeling using a shared encoder to integrate masked modeling and contrastive learning. Introduces semantic tube masking to mitigate trivial reconstruction due to temporal correlations and skeletal-aware augmentations to maintain anatomical consistency across temporal granularities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show SLiM achieves state-of-the-art performance across all downstream protocols and substantially reduces inference cost (7.89x) compared with existing MAE methods.
AI.conclusion: First decoder-free masked modeling framework for skeleton-based representation; demonstrates that decoupling reconstruction and leveraging masked modeling with a shared encoder yields strong performance and efficiency, suggesting a new direction for unified masked and contrastive learning in skeleton action understanding.

title: Domain-Adaptive Health Indicator Learning with Degradation-Stage Synchronized Sampling and Cross-Domain Autoencoder
summary: The construction of high quality health indicators (HIs) is crucial for effective prognostics and health management. Although deep learning has significantly advanced HI modeling, existing approaches often struggle with distribution mismatches resulting from varying operating conditions. Although domain adaptation is typically employed to mitigate these shifts, two critical challenges remain: (1) the misalignment of degradation stages during random mini-batch sampling, resulting in misleading discrepancy losses, and (2) the structural limitations of small-kernel 1D-CNNs in capturing long-range temporal dependencies within complex vibration signals. To address these issues, we propose a domain-adaptive framework comprising degradation stage synchronized batch sampling (DSSBS) and the cross-domain aligned fusion large autoencoder (CAFLAE). DSSBS utilizes kernel change-point detection to segment degradation stages, ensuring that source and target mini-batches are synchronized by their failure phases during alignment. Complementing this, CAFLAE integrates large-kernel temporal feature extraction with cross-attention mechanisms to learn superior domain-invariant representations. The proposed framework was rigorously validated on a Korean defense system dataset and the XJTU-SY bearing dataset, achieving an average performance enhancement of 24.1% over state-of-the-art methods. These results demonstrate that DSSBS improves cross-domain alignment through stage-consistent sampling, whereas CAFLAE offers a high-performance backbone for long-term industrial condition monitoring.

AI.tldr: A domain-adaptive framework with DSSBS and CAFLAE improves cross-domain prognostics for high-quality health indicators via stage-synchronized sampling and long-kernel autoencoding with cross-attention, yielding ~24.1% gains on defense and bearing datasets.
AI.motivation: Address distribution shifts in health indicator modeling caused by varying operating conditions. Two issues: misalignment of degradation stages during random mini-batch sampling, and insufficient long-range temporal capture by small-kernel 1D-CNNs.
AI.method: Introduce DSSBS, which uses kernel change-point detection to segment degradation stages and synchronize source/target mini-batches by failure phase during domain alignment; combine with CAFLAE, a large-kernel temporal feature extractor integrated with cross-attention to learn domain-invariant representations within a cross-domain aligned fusion autoencoder.
AI.result: Empirical validation on a Korean defense system dataset and the XJTU-SY bearing dataset showing an average performance improvement of 24.1% over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: DSSBS improves cross-domain alignment through stage-consistent sampling, while CAFLAE provides a high-performance backbone for long-term industrial condition monitoring; the proposed framework demonstrates robust cross-domain HI modeling under varying operating conditions.

title: A gripper for flap separation and opening of sealed bags
comment: 8 pages, Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics & Automation (ICRA2026)
summary: Separating thin, flexible layers that must be individually grasped is a common but challenging manipulation primitive for most off-the-shelf grippers. A prominent example arises in clinical settings: the opening of sterile flat pouches for the preparation of the operating room, where the first step is to separate and grasp the flaps. We present a novel gripper design and opening strategy that enables reliable flap separation and robust seal opening. This capability addresses a high-volume repetitive hospital procedure in which nurses manually open up to 240 bags per shift, a physically demanding task linked to musculoskeletal injuries. Our design combines an active dented-roller fingertip with compliant fingers that exploit environmental constraints to robustly grasp thin flexible flaps. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed gripper reliably grasps and separates sealed bag flaps and other thin-layered materials from the hospital, the most sensitive variable affecting performance being the normal force applied. When two copies of the gripper grasp both flaps, the system withstands the forces needed to open the seals robustly. To our knowledge, this is one of the first demonstrations of robotic assistance to automate this repetitive, low-value, but critical hospital task.

AI.tldr: Novel active dented-roller fingertip plus compliant fingers enabling reliable separation/opening of thin, flexible hospital bag flaps, enabling robotic assistance for a high-volume, repetitive task.
AI.motivation: Address a physically demanding, high-volume hospital task (opening sterile bags) to reduce nurse injuries and labor by automating flap separation and seal opening.
AI.method: Develop a gripper that combines an active dented roller fingertip with compliant fingers that exploit environmental constraints; validate by grasping/separating sealed bag flaps and other thin-layered materials; test configurations including two grippers grasping both flaps and measure sensitivity to normal force.
