AI.result: Demonstrated reliable grasping and separation of bag flaps; ability to open seals robustly when two grippers grip both flaps; performance mainly sensitive to the applied normal force; demonstrates feasibility of robotic assistance for this repetitive hospital task.
AI.conclusion: One of the first demonstrations of robotic assistance for this repetitive, low-value but critical hospital task; potential to reduce manual effort in high-volume bag opening; applicable to other thin-layered materials.

title: Are Video Reasoning Models Ready to Go Outside?
summary: In real-world deployment, vision-language models often encounter disturbances such as weather, occlusion, and camera motion. Under such conditions, their understanding and reasoning degrade substantially, revealing a gap between clean, controlled (i.e., unperturbed) evaluation settings and real-world robustness. To address this limitation, we propose ROVA, a novel training framework that improves robustness by modeling a robustness-aware consistency reward under spatio-temporal corruptions. ROVA introduces a difficulty-aware online training strategy that prioritizes informative samples based on the model's evolving capability. Specifically, it continuously re-estimates sample difficulty via self-reflective evaluation, enabling adaptive training with a robustness-aware consistency reward. We also introduce PVRBench, a new benchmark that injects real-world perturbations into embodied video datasets to assess both accuracy and reasoning quality under realistic disturbances. We evaluate ROVA and baselines on PVRBench, UrbanVideo, and VisBench, where open-source and proprietary models suffer up to 35% and 28% drops in accuracy and reasoning under realistic perturbations. ROVA effectively mitigates performance degradation, boosting relative accuracy by at least 24% and reasoning by over 9% compared with baseline models (QWen2.5/3-VL, InternVL2.5, Embodied-R). These gains transfer to clean standard benchmarks, yielding consistent improvements.

AI.tldr: ROVA introduces a robustness-aware training framework with a difficulty-aware online strategy to improve vision-language models under spatio-temporal perturbations, plus PVRBench benchmark for evaluating robustness and reasoning under real-world disturbances; results show substantial robustness gains and transfer to clean benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Real-world perturbations (weather, occlusion, motion) cause large drops in vision-language model performance. There is a gap between clean, controlled evaluation and real-world robustness; need methods that jointly improve accuracy and reasoning under disturbances.
AI.method: ROVA trains with a robustness-aware consistency reward and a difficulty-aware online training strategy that re-estimates sample difficulty via self-reflective evaluation, enabling adaptive training under perturbations. PVRBench injects real-world perturbations into embodied video datasets for evaluation of accuracy and reasoning under disturbances.
AI.result: Under realistic perturbations, baselines suffer up to 35% accuracy drop and 28% reasoning drop. ROVA increases relative accuracy by at least 24% and reasoning by over 9% compared with strong baselines (QWen2.5/3-VL, InternVL2.5, Embodied-R); gains also translate to clean benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ROVA effectively mitigates robustness degradation and enhances both accuracy and reasoning under real-world perturbations; PVRBench provides a realistic benchmark to assess robustness, and the proposed framework yields consistent improvements across diverse settings.

title: The Curse and Blessing of Mean Bias in FP4-Quantized LLM Training
summary: Large language models trained on natural language exhibit pronounced anisotropy: a small number of directions concentrate disproportionate energy, while the remaining dimensions form a broad semantic tail. In low-bit training regimes, this geometry becomes numerically unstable. Because blockwise quantization scales are determined by extreme elementwise magnitudes, dominant directions stretch the dynamic range, compressing long-tail semantic variation into narrow numerical bins. We show that this instability is primarily driven by a coherent rank-one mean bias, which constitutes the dominant component of spectral anisotropy in LLM representations. This mean component emerges systematically across layers and training stages and accounts for the majority of extreme activation magnitudes, making it the principal driver of dynamic-range inflation under low precision. Crucially, because the dominant instability is rank-one, it can be eliminated through a simple source-level mean-subtraction operation. This bias-centric conditioning recovers most of the stability benefits of SVD-based spectral methods while requiring only reduction operations and standard quantization kernels. Empirical results on FP4 (W4A4G4) training show that mean removal substantially narrows the loss gap to BF16 and restores downstream performance, providing a hardware-efficient path to stable low-bit LLM training.

AI.tldr: A rank-one mean bias in LLM activations causes spectral anisotropy and instability under low-bit training; removing this mean at the source stabilizes training and closes much of the performance gap to BF16, using a simple, hardware-friendly approach.
AI.motivation: LLMs exhibit highly anisotropic representations. Low-bit quantization amplifies dynamic-range issues, and existing spectral conditioning methods are computationally heavy or intrusive. A lightweight, scalable fix is desired to enable stable low-bit training.
AI.method: Identify the dominant rank-one mean bias across layers and training stages. Apply source-level mean subtraction to activations before quantization. Compare this mean-centric conditioning to SVD-based spectral methods. Validate on FP4 (W4A4G4) training with standard quantization kernels.
AI.result: Mean removal substantially narrows the loss gap to BF16 and restores downstream performance in FP4 training, indicating improved stability with minimal compute overhead.
AI.conclusion: The rank-one mean bias is the principal driver of dynamic-range inflation and spectral instability in LLMs under low precision. A simple mean-subtraction at the source-level effectively stabilizes training and recovers performance with hardware-efficient operations.

title: Contact Coverage-Guided Exploration for General-Purpose Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Deep Reinforcement learning (DRL) has achieved remarkable success in domains with well-defined reward structures, such as Atari games and locomotion. In contrast, dexterous manipulation lacks general-purpose reward formulations and typically depends on task-specific, handcrafted priors to guide hand-object interactions. We propose Contact Coverage-Guided Exploration (CCGE), a general exploration method designed for general-purpose dexterous manipulation tasks. CCGE represents contact state as the intersection between object surface points and predefined hand keypoints, encouraging dexterous hands to discover diverse and novel contact patterns, namely which fingers contact which object regions. It maintains a contact counter conditioned on discretized object states obtained via learned hash codes, capturing how frequently each finger interacts with different object regions. This counter is leveraged in two complementary ways: (1) to assign a count-based contact coverage reward that promotes exploration of novel contact patterns, and (2) an energy-based reaching reward that guides the agent toward under-explored contact regions. We evaluate CCGE on a diverse set of dexterous manipulation tasks, including cluttered object singulation, constrained object retrieval, in-hand reorientation, and bimanual manipulation. Experimental results show that CCGE substantially improves training efficiency and success rates over existing exploration methods, and that the contact patterns learned with CCGE transfer robustly to real-world robotic systems. Project page is https://contact-coverage-guided-exploration.github.io.

AI.tldr: CCGE is a general exploration method for dexterous manipulation using contact-coverage signals derived from hand-object contact intersections; it introduces a contact counter conditioned on discretized object states via learned hashes, and uses both a count-based reward and an energy-based reaching reward to encourage diverse, under-explored contacts; shows improved training efficiency and transfer to real robots across multiple tasks.
AI.motivation: Dexterous manipulation lacks general-purpose reward formulations; exploration biased by handcrafted priors is common but limits scalability and generality. A general mechanism to encourage diverse contact patterns could improve sample efficiency and real-world transfer.
AI.method: Define contact state as the intersection between object surface points and predefined hand keypoints. Maintain a contact counter conditioned on discretized object states obtained via learned hash codes. Use the counter in two ways: (1) a count-based contact coverage reward promoting novel contact patterns, and (2) an energy-based reaching reward guiding the agent toward under-explored contact regions. Evaluate on cluttered object singulation, constrained object retrieval, in-hand reorientation, and bimanual manipulation; report transfer to real-world robots via a project page.
AI.result: CCGE substantially improves training efficiency and success rates over existing exploration methods; the learned contact patterns transfer robustly to real-world robotic systems.
AI.conclusion: CCGE provides a general, transferable exploration signal for dexterous manipulation, enabling broader exploration of contact patterns and improved sample efficiency with demonstrated sim-to-real transfer across diverse tasks.

title: How To Embed Matters: Evaluation of EO Embedding Design Choices
summary: Earth observation (EO) missions produce petabytes of multispectral imagery, increasingly analyzed using large Geospatial Foundation Models (GeoFMs). Alongside end-to-end adaptation, workflows make growing use of intermediate representations as task-agnostic embeddings, enabling models to compute representations once and reuse them across downstream tasks. Consequently, when GeoFMs act as feature extractors, decisions about how representations are obtained, aggregated, and combined affect downstream performance and pipeline scalability. Understanding these trade-offs is essential for scalable embedding-based EO workflows, where compact embeddings can replace raw data while remaining broadly useful. We present a systematic analysis of embedding design in GeoFM-based EO workflows. Leveraging NeuCo-Bench, we study how backbone architecture, pretraining strategy, representation depth, spatial aggregation, and representation combination influence EO task performance. We demonstrate the usability of GeoFM embeddings by aggregating them into fixed-size representations more than 500x smaller than the raw input data. Across models, we find consistent trends: transformer backbones with mean pooling provide strong default embeddings, intermediate ResNet layers can outperform final layers, self-supervised objectives exhibit task-specific strengths, and combining embeddings from different objectives often improves robustness.

AI.tldr: Systematic analysis of embedding design for GeoFM-based EO workflows; compact embeddings enable scalable, effective reuse across tasks, with transformer mean-pooled embeddings performing well and heterogeneous objectives yielding robustness.
AI.motivation: Understand trade-offs in producing and using task-agnostic embeddings in Earth observation pipelines to balance task performance with pipeline scalability and data reduction.
AI.method: Empirical study using NeuCo-Bench to evaluate multiple embedding design facets (backbone architecture: transformer vs ResNet; pretraining strategy: supervised vs self-supervised; representation depth; spatial aggregation via pooling; and representation combination). Embeddings are aggregated into fixed-size representations ~500x smaller than raw imagery and evaluated across EO tasks.
AI.result: Key trends emerged: transformer backbones with mean pooling provide strong default embeddings; intermediate ResNet layers can outperform final layers; self-supervised objectives offer task-specific strengths; combining embeddings from different objectives enhances robustness across tasks.
AI.conclusion: For scalable EO workflows, adopt transformer-based backbones with mean pooling as a strong default; consider mid-layer representations for potential gains; leverage diverse objective embeddings and their combinations to improve robustness; exploit the substantial data compression (≈500x) achievable with GeoFM embeddings.

title: Learning Adaptive Force Control for Contact-Rich Sample Scraping with Heterogeneous Materials
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables; Submitted to IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS) 2026
summary: The increasing demand for accelerated scientific discovery, driven by global challenges, highlights the need for advanced AI-driven robotics. Deploying robotic chemists in human-centric labs is key for the next horizon of autonomous discovery, as complex tasks still demand the dexterity of human scientists. Robotic manipulation in this context is uniquely challenged by handling diverse chemicals (granular, powdery, or viscous liquids), under varying lab conditions. For example, humans use spatulas for scraping materials from vial walls. Automating this process is challenging because it goes beyond simple robotic insertion tasks and traditional lab automation, requiring the execution of fine-granular movements within a constrained environment (the sample vial). Our work proposes an adaptive control framework to address this, relying on a low-level Cartesian impedance controller for stable and compliant physical interaction and a high-level reinforcement learning agent that learns to dynamically adjust interaction forces at the end-effector. The agent is guided by perception feedback, which provides the material's location. We first created a task-representative simulation environment with a Franka Research 3 robot, a scraping tool, and a sample vial containing heterogeneous materials. To facilitate the learning of an adaptive policy and model diverse characteristics, the sample is modelled as a collection of spheres, where each sphere is assigned a unique dislodgement force threshold, which is procedurally generated using Perlin noise. We train an agent to autonomously learn and adapt the optimal contact wrench for a sample scraping task in simulation and then successfully transfer this policy to a real robotic setup. Our method was evaluated across five different material setups, outperforming a fixed-wrench baseline by an average of 10.9%.

AI.tldr: Adaptive impedance control with a perception-guided RL policy enables robust, sim-to-real scraping in vial-like environments; shows ~11% improvement over a fixed-wrench baseline across diverse materials.
AI.motivation: Enable autonomous robotic chemists to perform fine-granular material manipulation in constrained lab environments, addressing the gap between high-level automation and dexterous human tasks.
AI.method: Low-level Cartesian impedance control for stability and compliance; high-level reinforcement learning agent adjusts end-effector contact wrench guided by perception feedback of material location; simulation environment with Franka robot, scraping tool, and a vial containing heterogeneous materials modeled as spheres with Perlin-noise-generated dislodgement thresholds; policy trained in simulation and transferred to real hardware.
AI.result: The agent autonomously learns adaptive contact forces and outperforms a fixed-wrench baseline by an average of 10.9% across five material setups; successful sim-to-real transfer demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that combining adaptive impedance control with perception-informed RL enables robust, dexterous manipulation in constrained lab tasks, bridging sim-to-real transfer for complex, fine-grained manipulation with diverse materials.

title: A$^2$-Edit: Precise Reference-Guided Image Editing of Arbitrary Objects and Ambiguous Masks
summary: We propose \textbf{A$^2$-Edit}, a unified inpainting framework for arbitrary object categories, which allows users to replace any target region with a reference object using only a coarse mask. To address the issues of severe homogenization and limited category coverage in existing datasets, we construct a large-scale, multi-category dataset \textbf{UniEdit-500K}, which includes 8 major categories, 209 fine-grained subcategories, and a total of 500,104 image pairs. Such rich category diversity poses new challenges for the model, requiring it to automatically learn semantic relationships and distinctions across categories. To this end, we introduce the \textbf{Mixture of Transformer} module, which performs differentiated modeling of various object categories through dynamic expert selection, and further enhances cross-category semantic transfer and generalization through collaboration among experts. In addition, we propose a \textbf{Mask Annealing Training Strategy} (MATS) that progressively relaxes mask precision during training, reducing the model's reliance on accurate masks and improving robustness across diverse editing tasks. Extensive experiments on benchmarks such as VITON-HD and AnyInsertion demonstrate that A$^2$-Edit consistently outperforms existing approaches across all metrics, providing a new and efficient solution for arbitrary object editing.

AI.tldr: A2-Edit is a unified inpainting framework for arbitrary object editing using coarse masks, backed by a large multi-category dataset UniEdit-500K, a Mixture of Transformer for cross-category reasoning, and a Mask Annealing Training Strategy (MATS); it achieves state-of-the-art results on VITON-HD and AnyInsertion.
AI.motivation: To overcome severe homogenization and limited category coverage in existing inpainting datasets by providing a large-scale, diverse, multi-category dataset and a model capable of automatic semantic transfer across categories under coarse masks.
AI.method: Proposes A2-Edit with a Mixture of Transformer module that uses dynamic expert selection and collaboration among experts for differentiated modeling across object categories; builds UniEdit-500K (8 major categories, 209 subcategories, ~500k pairs); introduces Mask Annealing Training Strategy (MATS) to progressively relax mask precision during training; evaluates on VITON-HD and AnyInsertion.
AI.result: A2-Edit consistently outperforms existing approaches across all evaluation metrics on the provided benchmarks, demonstrating improved generalization and robustness for arbitrary object editing.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a scalable, efficient solution for arbitrary object editing, leveraging cross-category expert collaboration and training strategies to enhance generalization and robustness, aided by a large, diverse dataset.

title: PPGuide: Steering Diffusion Policies with Performance Predictive Guidance
summary: Diffusion policies have shown to be very efficient at learning complex, multi-modal behaviors for robotic manipulation. However, errors in generated action sequences can compound over time which can potentially lead to failure. Some approaches mitigate this by augmenting datasets with expert demonstrations or learning predictive world models which might be computationally expensive. We introduce Performance Predictive Guidance (PPGuide), a lightweight, classifier-based framework that steers a pre-trained diffusion policy away from failure modes at inference time. PPGuide makes use of a novel self-supervised process: it uses attention-based multiple instance learning to automatically estimate which observation-action chunks from the policy's rollouts are relevant to success or failure. We then train a performance predictor on this self-labeled data. During inference, this predictor provides a real-time gradient to guide the policy toward more robust actions. We validated our proposed PPGuide across a diverse set of tasks from the Robomimic and MimicGen benchmarks, demonstrating consistent improvements in performance.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, classifier-based performance predictor (PPGuide) guides diffusion policies at inference time to avoid failure modes, using self-supervised MIL to label success/failure-relevant behavior; shows improved robustness on Robomimic and MimicGen benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based robotic manipulation can incur compounding errors; traditional remedies (expert demonstrations, world models) are data- or compute-intensive. There is a need for a lightweight, inference-time mitigation to steer policies away from failures.
AI.method: PPGuide introduces a self-supervised pipeline: (1) use attention-based multiple instance learning to identify observation-action chunks from policy rollouts relevant to success or failure; (2) train a performance predictor on this self-labeled data; (3) at inference, the predictor provides a real-time gradient to guide the pre-trained diffusion policy toward robust actions.
AI.result: Empirical validation across Robomimic and MimicGen benchmarks shows consistent improvements in performance, indicating effective mitigation of failure modes with minimal overhead.
AI.conclusion: PPGuide offers a lightweight, inference-time corrective mechanism for diffusion-based robotic policies, leveraging self-supervised MIL-derived labels to train a performance predictor that steers actions toward robustness across diverse tasks.

title: GGMPs: Generalized Gaussian Mixture Processes
summary: Conditional density estimation is complicated by multimodality, heteroscedasticity, and strong non-Gaussianity. Gaussian processes (GPs) provide a principled nonparametric framework with calibrated uncertainty, but standard GP regression is limited by its unimodal Gaussian predictive form. We introduce the Generalized Gaussian Mixture Process (GGMP), a GP-based method for multimodal conditional density estimation in settings where each input may be associated with a complex output distribution rather than a single scalar response. GGMP combines local Gaussian mixture fitting, cross-input component alignment and per-component heteroscedastic GP training to produce a closed-form Gaussian mixture predictive density. The method is tractable, compatible with standard GP solvers and scalable methods, and avoids the exponentially large latent-assignment structure of naive multimodal GP formulations. Empirically, GGMPs improve distributional approximation on synthetic and real-world datasets with pronounced non-Gaussianity and multimodality.

AI.tldr: GGMP extends Gaussian processes to multimodal conditional densities by fitting local Gaussian mixtures, aligning components across inputs, and training per-component heteroscedastic GPs to yield a closed-form Gaussian mixture predictive density; it is scalable and improves non-Gaussian, multimodal distributions in synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Standard GP regression is unimodal and Gaussian; real-world conditional densities can be multimodal, heteroscedastic, and non-Gaussian. A GP-based method that can capture multimodality while preserving calibrated uncertainty is needed.
AI.method: Aggregate local Gaussian mixture fitting with cross-input component alignment, and train per-component heteroscedastic GPs for each mixture component. This yields a closed-form Gaussian mixture predictive density that is tractable, compatible with standard GP solvers, and avoids the exponential latent-assignment complexity of naive multimodal GP formulations.
AI.result: Empirically, GGMP improves distributional approximation on both synthetic and real-world datasets characterized by pronounced non-Gaussianity and multimodality.
AI.conclusion: GGMP provides a tractable, scalable GP-based framework for multimodal conditional density estimation, delivering a closed-form predictive distribution and improved distributional accuracy without incurring prohibitive latent-variable complexity.

title: Bioinspired CNNs for border completion in occluded images
summary: We exploit the mathematical modeling of the border completion problem in the visual cortex to design convolutional neural network (CNN) filters that enhance robustness to image occlusions. We evaluate our CNN architecture, BorderNet, on three occluded datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and EMNIST) under two types of occlusions: stripes and grids. In all cases, BorderNet demonstrates improved performance, with gains varying depending on the severity of the occlusions and the dataset.

AI.tldr: BorderNet applies neuroscience-inspired border-completion modeling to CNN filters to boost occlusion robustness; tested on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and EMNIST with stripes and grids occlusions; shows consistent accuracy improvements with performance varying by dataset and occlusion severity.
AI.motivation: Occlusions degrade image recognition performance. Inspired by the visual cortex border completion mechanism, the work aims to design CNN filters that leverage border information to improve robustness to occlusions.
AI.method: Develop a CNN architecture (BorderNet) whose filters are informed by mathematical modeling of border completion. Evaluate on three occluded datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, EMNIST) using two occlusion patterns (stripes, grids).
AI.result: BorderNet achieves improved recognition performance across all datasets and occlusion types, with gains dependent on occlusion severity and dataset.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating border-completion principles into CNN filters enhances occlusion robustness, offering a viable strategy for robust recognition under occlusion and potential applicability to other occluded vision tasks.

title: UAV-MARL: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Time-Critical and Dynamic Medical Supply Delivery
summary: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly used to support time-critical medical supply delivery, providing rapid and flexible logistics during emergencies and resource shortages. However, effective deployment of UAV fleets requires coordination mechanisms capable of prioritizing medical requests, allocating limited aerial resources, and adapting delivery schedules under uncertain operational conditions. This paper presents a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework for coordinating UAV fleets in stochastic medical delivery scenarios where requests vary in urgency, location, and delivery deadlines. The problem is formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) in which UAV agents maintain awareness of medical delivery demands while having limited visibility of other agents due to communication and localization constraints. The proposed framework employs Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) as the primary learning algorithm and evaluates several variants, including asynchronous extensions, classical actor--critic methods, and architectural modifications to analyze scalability and performance trade-offs. The model is evaluated using real-world geographic data from selected clinics and hospitals extracted from the OpenStreetMap dataset. The framework provides a decision-support layer that prioritizes medical tasks, reallocates UAV resources in real time, and assists healthcare personnel in managing urgent logistics. Experimental results show that classical PPO achieves superior coordination performance compared to asynchronous and sequential learning strategies, highlighting the potential of reinforcement learning for adaptive and scalable UAV-assisted healthcare logistics.

AI.tldr: A MARL framework coordinates UAV fleets for stochastic medical deliveries under partial observability, using PPO with variants; evaluated on real OpenStreetMap data; finds PPO outperforms asynchronous/sequential methods and offers a scalable decision-support tool for urgent healthcare logistics.
AI.motivation: Address the need for efficient, real-time coordination of limited UAV resources to fulfill urgent medical requests under uncertainty and communication constraints in healthcare logistics.
AI.method: Formulates UAV coordination as a POMDP. Employs Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) as the main learning algorithm, with variants including asynchronous extensions, classical actor–critic methods, and architectural modifications to assess scalability. Evaluation uses real geographic data from clinics/hospitals in OpenStreetMap. Outputs a decision-support layer for task prioritization and resource reallocation.
AI.result: Classical PPO achieves superior coordination performance compared to asynchronous and sequential learning approaches, indicating effective adaptive coordination and scalability for UAV-assisted medical logistics.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement learning, particularly PPO, can provide effective, scalable coordination for UAV fleets in uncertain medical delivery scenarios, with practical utility as a decision-support system in healthcare logistics.

title: RandMark: On Random Watermarking of Visual Foundation Models
summary: Being trained on large and diverse datasets, visual foundation models (VFMs) can be fine-tuned to achieve remarkable performance and efficiency in various downstream computer vision tasks. The high computational cost of data collection and training makes these models valuable assets, which motivates some VFM owners to distribute them alongside a license to protect their intellectual property rights. In this paper, we propose an approach to ownership verification of visual foundation models that leverages a small encoder-decoder network to embed digital watermarks into an internal representation of a hold-out set of input images. The method is based on random watermark embedding, which makes the watermark statistics detectable in functional copies of the watermarked model. Both theoretically and experimentally, we demonstrate that the proposed method yields a low probability of false detection for non-watermarked models and a low probability of false misdetection for watermarked models.

AI.tldr: A watermarking approach for visual foundation models using a small encoder-decoder to embed a random watermark into internal representations of a hold-out image set, enabling reliable ownership verification with low false positive/negative rates.
AI.motivation: Protect intellectual property of visual foundation models distributed with licenses; high training/collection costs incentivize watermark-based ownership verification.
AI.method: Train a compact encoder–decoder that embeds a random watermark into internal representations of a held-out input set; watermark statistics are detectable in functional copies of the watermarked model; theoretical and empirical analysis of detection performance.
AI.result: The method achieves a low probability of false detection for non-watermarked models and a low probability of false misdetection for watermarked models, supported by both theory and experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed watermarking framework provides a practical, statistically grounded approach for ownership verification of visual foundation models.

title: Unlearning the Unpromptable: Prompt-free Instance Unlearning in Diffusion Models
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove specific outputs from trained models, often at the concept level, such as forgetting all occurrences of a particular celebrity or filtering content via text prompts. However, many undesired outputs, such as an individual's face or generations culturally or factually misinterpreted, cannot often be specified by text prompts. We address this underexplored setting of instance unlearning for outputs that are undesired but unpromptable, where the goal is to forget target outputs selectively while preserving the rest. To this end, we introduce an effective surrogate-based unlearning method that leverages image editing, timestep-aware weighting, and gradient surgery to guide trained diffusion models toward forgetting specific outputs. Experiments on conditional (Stable Diffusion 3) and unconditional (DDPM-CelebA) diffusion models demonstrate that our prompt-free method uniquely unlearns unpromptable outputs, such as faces and culturally inaccurate depictions, with preserved integrity, unlike prompt-based and prompt-free baselines. Our proposed method would serve as a practical hotfix for diffusion model providers to ensure privacy protection and ethical compliance.

AI.tldr: Surrogate-based unlearning for diffusion models to forget unpromptable outputs (e.g., faces, culturally inaccurate depictions) without prompts, using image editing, timestep-aware weighting, and gradient surgery; demonstrated on conditional Stable Diffusion 3 and unconditional DDPM-CelebA; preserves model integrity better than prompt-based/baseline methods.
AI.motivation: Some undesired outputs cannot be specified by text prompts (e.g., a person's face or culturally misrepresented depictions). There is a need for instance-level, prompt-free unlearning to selectively forget such outputs while preserving other capabilities of generative models.
AI.method: A surrogate-based unlearning framework that incorporates image editing signals, timestep-aware weighting, and gradient surgery to steer diffusion models toward forgetting targeted outputs without needing prompts.
AI.result: Empirical results on conditional (Stable Diffusion 3) and unconditional (DDPM-CelebA) diffusion models show the method uniquely unlearns unpromptable outputs (e.g., faces, culturally inaccurate depictions) while preserving overall image quality and diversity, outperforming prompt-based and prompt-free baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach offers a practical hotfix for diffusion-model providers to enforce privacy and ethical compliance by enabling targeted forgetting of outputs that cannot be prompted, while maintaining model utility.

title: UniCom: Unified Multimodal Modeling via Compressed Continuous Semantic Representations
summary: Current unified multimodal models typically rely on discrete visual tokenizers to bridge the modality gap. However, discretization inevitably discards fine-grained semantic information, leading to suboptimal performance in visual understanding tasks. Conversely, directly modeling continuous semantic representations (e.g., CLIP, SigLIP) poses significant challenges in high-dimensional generative modeling, resulting in slow convergence and training instability. To resolve this dilemma, we introduce UniCom, a unified framework that harmonizes multimodal understanding and generation via compressed continuous representation. We empirically demonstrate that reducing channel dimension is significantly more effective than spatial downsampling for both reconstruction and generation. Accordingly, we design an attention-based semantic compressor to distill dense features into a compact unified representation. Furthermore, we validate that the transfusion architecture surpasses query-based designs in convergence and consistency. Experiments demonstrate that UniCom achieves state-of-the-art generation performance among unified models. Notably, by preserving rich semantic priors, it delivers exceptional controllability in image editing and maintains image consistency even without relying on VAE.

AI.tldr: UniCom presents a unified multimodal framework that compresses continuous semantic representations for improved understanding and generation, surpassing discrete tokenizers and query-based designs.
AI.motivation: Discretizing visual information with tokenizers loses fine-grained semantics, while direct continuous modeling (e.g., CLIP, SigLIP) challenges high-dimensional generation, causing slow convergence and instability. A middle ground is needed to preserve semantics and enable efficient generation.
AI.method: Introduce an attention-based semantic compressor that reduces dense visual features to a compact unified representation; show that channel dimension reduction beats spatial downsampling for reconstruction and generation; adopt a transfusion architecture over query-based designs for better convergence and consistency; demonstrate state-of-the-art generation among unified models and strong editing controllability without relying on VAE.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate that reducing channel dimension yields greater performance gains than spatial downsampling for both reconstruction and generation; the transfusion architecture improves convergence and consistency; UniCom achieves state-of-the-art generation within unified multimodal models and enables rich editing control while preserving image consistency without VAE.
AI.conclusion: A practical path to unified multimodal modeling lies in compressed continuous representations; the key innovations—semantic compression and transfusion-based design—offer improved understanding, generation, and controllability, with robust editing capabilities and stability.

title: SCORE: Replacing Layer Stacking with Contractive Recurrent Depth
summary: Residual connections are central to modern deep neural networks, enabling stable optimization and efficient information flow across depth. In this work, we propose SCORE (Skip-Connection ODE Recurrent Embedding), a discrete recurrent alternative to classical layer stacking. Instead of composing multiple independent layers, SCORE iteratively applies a single shared neural block using an ODE (Ordinary Differential Equation)-inspired contractive update: ht+1 = (1 - dt) * ht + dt * F(ht) This formulation can be interpreted as a depth-by-iteration refinement process, where the step size dt explicitly controls stability and update magnitude. Unlike continuous Neural ODE approaches, SCORE uses a fixed number of discrete iterations and standard backpropagation without requiring ODE solvers or adjoint methods. We evaluate SCORE across graph neural networks (ESOL molecular solubility), multilayer perceptrons, and Transformer-based language models (nanoGPT). Across architectures, SCORE generally improves convergence speed and often accelerates training. SCORE is reducing parameter count through shared weights. In practice, simple Euler integration provides the best trade-off between computational cost and performance, while higher-order integrators yield marginal gains at increased compute. These results suggest that controlled recurrent depth with contractive residual updates offers a lightweight and effective alternative to classical stacking in deep neural networks.

AI.tldr: SCORE introduces a discrete, shared-parameter recurrent update ht+1 = (1 - dt) ht + dt F(ht) as an alternative to deep layer stacking. It uses a fixed number of iterations, avoids ODE solvers/adjoint methods, and shows faster convergence and reduced parameter count across GNNs, MLPs, and transformers, with Euler integration offering the best cost-performance trade-off.
AI.motivation: Address optimization and parameter inefficiency in very deep networks by replacing dense layer stacking with a contractive, recurrent-like update that preserves stability and enables efficient training without ODE solvers.
AI.method: A shared neural block F is applied iteratively with a contractive update ht+1 = (1 - dt) ht + dt F(ht). This yields a depth-by-iteration interpretation, controlled by step size dt. The model uses a fixed number of discrete iterations and standard backpropagation, avoiding ODE solvers/adjoint methods. Evaluations span graph neural networks (ESOL), multilayer perceptrons, and Transformer-based models (nanoGPT). Comparisons include different numerical integrators, with Euler typically preferred.
AI.result: Across architectures, SCORE improves convergence speed and often accelerates training. Parameter count is reduced via weight sharing. Simple Euler integration provides the best trade-off between compute and performance; higher-order integrators yield marginal gains at higher cost.
AI.conclusion: Controlled recurrent depth with contractive residual updates offers a lightweight, effective alternative to classical stacking in deep networks. SCORE demonstrates cross-architecture benefits and practical efficiency, suggesting potential for broader adoption and further investigation into step-size and iteration-count trade-offs.

title: Muscle Synergy Priors Enhance Biomechanical Fidelity in Predictive Musculoskeletal Locomotion Simulation
summary: Human locomotion emerges from high-dimensional neuromuscular control, making predictive musculoskeletal simulation challenging. We present a physiology-informed reinforcement-learning framework that constrains control using muscle synergies. We extracted a low-dimensional synergy basis from inverse musculoskeletal analyses of a small set of overground walking trials and used it as the action space for a muscle-driven three-dimensional model trained across variable speeds, slopes and uneven terrain. The resulting controller generated stable gait from 0.7-1.8 m/s and on $\pm$ 6$^{\circ}$ grades and reproduced condition-dependent modulation of joint angles, joint moments and ground reaction forces. Compared with an unconstrained controller, synergy-constrained control reduced non-physiological knee kinematics and kept knee moment profiles within the experimental envelope. Across conditions, simulated vertical ground reaction forces correlated strongly with human measurements, and muscle-activation timing largely fell within inter-subject variability. These results show that embedding neurophysiological structure into reinforcement learning can improve biomechanical fidelity and generalization in predictive human locomotion simulation with limited experimental data.

AI.tldr: Physiology-informed RL uses a low-dimensional muscle synergy action space to constrain control in a 3D musculoskeletal model, achieving stable, physiologically plausible gait across speeds, slopes, and uneven terrain, with improved fidelity vs an unconstrained controller and good alignment to human data.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of high-dimensional neuromuscular control in predictive locomotion by injecting neurophysiological structure (muscle synergies) into reinforcement learning to improve data efficiency, generalization, and biomechanical realism.
AI.method: Extract a low-dimensional synergy basis from inverse analyses of a small set of overground walking trials. Use this basis as the action space for a muscle-driven 3D model trained across variable speeds, slopes (±6°), and uneven terrain. Compare synergy-constrained RL to an unconstrained controller on gait stability, joint kinematics, joint moments, ground reaction forces, and muscle activation timing.
AI.result: Synergy-constrained controller produced stable gait from 0.7–1.8 m/s and ±6° grades, reproduced condition-dependent joint modulation, reduced non-physiological knee kinematics, kept knee moments within experimental envelopes, vertical ground reaction forces strongly correlated with human data, and muscle-activation timing within inter-subject variability.
AI.conclusion: Embedding neurophysiological structure via muscle synergies into reinforcement learning enhances biomechanical fidelity and generalization of predictive locomotion simulations, even with limited experimental data.

title: Spatio-Temporal Forecasting of Retaining Wall Deformation: Mitigating Error Accumulation via Multi-Resolution ConvLSTM Stacking Ensemble
summary: This study proposes a multi-resolution Convolutional Long Short-Term Memory (ConvLSTM) ensemble framework that leverages diverse temporal input resolutions to mitigate error accumulation and improve long-horizon forecasting of retaining-structure behavior during staged excavation. An extensive database of lateral wall displacement responses was generated through PLAXIS2D simulations incorporating five-layered soil stratigraphy, two excavation depths (14 and 20 m), and stochastically varied geotechnical and structural parameters, yielding 2,000 time-series deflection profiles. Three ConvLSTM models trained at different input resolutions were integrated using a fully connected neural network meta-learner to construct the ensemble model. Validation using both numerical results and field measurements demonstrated that the ensemble approach consistently outperformed the standalone ConvLSTM models, particularly in long-term multi-step prediction, exhibiting reduced error propagation and improved generalization. These findings underscore the potential of multi-resolution ensemble strategies that jointly exploit diverse temporal input scales to enhance predictive stability and accuracy in AI-driven geotechnical forecasting.

AI.tldr: A multi-resolution ConvLSTM ensemble improves long-horizon displacement forecasting in staged excavation by integrating three input resolutions via a meta-learner; validated with PLAXIS2D-simulated and field data, showing reduced error propagation and better generalization.
AI.motivation: To address error accumulation in long-horizon geotechnical forecasting of retaining-structure displacement during staged excavation and to enhance predictive stability by exploiting information at multiple temporal scales.
AI.method: Generate 2,000 time-series of lateral wall displacement using PLAXIS2D with five-layer soil stratigraphy and two depths (14, 20 m), with stochastic parameter variation. Train three ConvLSTM models at different input resolutions. Combine predictions with a fully connected meta-learner to form an ensemble. Validate against simulations and field measurements.
AI.result: The ensemble consistently outperforms individual ConvLSTM models, especially in long-term multi-step forecasts, reducing error propagation and improving generalization to unseen conditions.
AI.conclusion: Multi-resolution ensemble strategies that exploit diverse temporal input scales are effective for AI-driven geotechnical forecasting, enhancing predictive stability and accuracy for retaining-structure response.

title: WalkGPT: Grounded Vision-Language Conversation with Depth-Aware Segmentation for Pedestrian Navigation
summary: Ensuring accessible pedestrian navigation requires reasoning about both semantic and spatial aspects of complex urban scenes, a challenge that existing Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) struggle to meet. Although these models can describe visual content, their lack of explicit grounding leads to object hallucinations and unreliable depth reasoning, limiting their usefulness for accessibility guidance. We introduce WalkGPT, a pixel-grounded LVLM for the new task of Grounded Navigation Guide, unifying language reasoning and segmentation within a single architecture for depth-aware accessibility guidance. Given a pedestrian-view image and a navigation query, WalkGPT generates a conversational response with segmentation masks that delineate accessible and harmful features, along with relative depth estimation. The model incorporates a Multi-Scale Query Projector (MSQP) that shapes the final image tokens by aggregating them along text tokens across spatial hierarchies, and a Calibrated Text Projector (CTP), guided by a proposed Region Alignment Loss, that maps language embeddings into segmentation-aware representations. These components enable fine-grained grounding and depth inference without user-provided cues or anchor points, allowing the model to generate complete and realistic navigation guidance. We also introduce PAVE, a large-scale benchmark of 41k pedestrian-view images paired with accessibility-aware questions and depth-grounded answers. Experiments show that WalkGPT achieves strong grounded reasoning and segmentation performance. The source code and dataset are available on the \href{https://sites.google.com/view/walkgpt-26/home}{project website}.

AI.tldr: WalkGPT is a pixel-grounded LVLM designed for grounded navigation guidance in urban scenes, introducing MSQP and CTP modules and a new PAVE benchmark to enable depth-aware, segmentation-grounded responses without user cues.
AI.motivation: To address object hallucinations and unreliable depth reasoning in existing LVLMs when providing accessibility-guided navigation for pedestrians, requiring both semantic understanding and spatial grounding.
AI.method: Proposes WalkGPT, combining language reasoning with segmentation to produce a conversation and segmentation masks, along with relative depth. Key components: Multi-Scale Query Projector (MSQP) aggregating image tokens across spatial hierarchies guided by text tokens; Calibrated Text Projector (CTP) guided by Region Alignment Loss to map language embeddings into segmentation-aware representations. Operates without extra cues or anchors to yield complete, realistic navigation guidance. Introduces PAVE, a 41k-pedestrian-view image benchmark with accessibility questions and depth-grounded answers.
AI.result: Empirical results show WalkGPT achieves strong grounded reasoning and segmentation performance on the proposed tasks and benchmark; code and dataset are released on the project website.
AI.conclusion: WalkGPT advances grounded navigation for accessibility by tightly integrating segmentation and depth estimation within a single LVLM, reducing hallucinations and enabling reliable, context-aware navigation guidance; PAVE provides a large-scale evaluation resource for this problem.

title: UAV traffic scene understanding: A cross-spectral guided approach and a unified benchmark
summary: Traffic scene understanding from unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) platforms is crucial for intelligent transportation systems due to its flexible deployment and wide-area monitoring capabilities. However, existing methods face significant challenges in real-world surveillance, as their heavy reliance on optical imagery leads to severe performance degradation under adverse illumination conditions like nighttime and fog. Furthermore, current Visual Question Answering (VQA) models are restricted to elementary perception tasks, lacking the domain-specific regulatory knowledge required to assess complex traffic behaviors. To address these limitations, we propose a novel Cross-spectral Traffic Cognition Network (CTCNet) for robust UAV traffic scene understanding. Specifically, we design a Prototype-Guided Knowledge Embedding (PGKE) module that leverages high-level semantic prototypes from an external Traffic Regulation Memory (TRM) to anchor domain-specific knowledge into visual representations, enabling the model to comprehend complex behaviors and distinguish fine-grained traffic violations. Moreover, we develop a Quality-Aware Spectral Compensation (QASC) module that exploits the complementary characteristics of optical and thermal modalities to perform bidirectional context exchange, effectively compensating for degraded features to ensure robust representation in complex environments. In addition, we construct Traffic-VQA, the first large-scale optical-thermal infrared benchmark for cognitive UAV traffic understanding, comprising 8,180 aligned image pairs and 1.3 million question-answer pairs across 31 diverse types. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CTCNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both cognition and perception scenarios. The dataset is available at https://github.com/YuZhang-2004/UAV-traffic-scene-understanding.

AI.tldr: CTCNet: a cross-spectral UAV traffic cognition network that uses Prototype-Guided Knowledge Embedding (PGKE) and Quality-Aware Spectral Compensation (QASC) to fuse optical-thermal data and incorporate Traffic Regulation Memory (TRM) for robust traffic understanding; introduces Traffic-VQA, a large optical-thermal VQA benchmark with 8,180 image pairs and 1.3M QA pairs across 31 types.
AI.motivation: To overcome severe performance degradation of optical-only methods under night/fog and to equip VQA with domain-specific regulatory knowledge for complex traffic behaviors; leverage cross-spectral data and external memory to improve recognition and reasoning.
AI.method: Prototype-Guided Knowledge Embedding (PGKE) anchors domain knowledge from Traffic Regulation Memory (TRM) into visual representations via semantic prototypes; Quality-Aware Spectral Compensation (QASC) enables bidirectional exchange between optical and thermal modalities to compensate degraded features; proposes Traffic-VQA dataset for cognitive UAV traffic understanding.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CTCNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both recognition/prediction tasks (cognition) and visual question answering (perception) under challenging conditions.
AI.conclusion: CTCNet and the Traffic-VQA benchmark advance robust, domain-aware cross-spectral UAV traffic understanding, addressing illumination-related challenges and enabling more reliable traffic cognition and reasoning.

title: Gradient Flow Drifting: Generative Modeling via Wasserstein Gradient Flows of KDE-Approximated Divergences
summary: We reveal a precise mathematical framework about a new family of generative models which we call Gradient Flow Drifting. With this framework, we prove an equivalence between the recently proposed Drifting Model and the Wasserstein gradient flow of the forward KL divergence under kernel density estimation (KDE) approximation. Specifically, we prove that the drifting field of drifting model (arXiv:2602.04770) equals, up to a bandwidth-squared scaling factor, the difference of KDE log-density gradients $\nabla \log p_{\mathrm{kde}} - \nabla \log q_{\mathrm{kde}}$, which is exactly the particle velocity field of the Wasserstein-2 gradient flow of $KL(q\|p)$ with KDE-approximated densities. Besides that, this broad family of generative models can also include MMD-based generators, which arises as special cases of Wasserstein gradient flows of different divergences under KDE approximation. We provide a concise identifiability proof, and a theoretically grounded mixed-divergence strategy. We combine reverse KL and $χ^2$ divergence gradient flows to simultaneously avoid mode collapse and mode blurring, and extend this method onto Riemannian manifold which loosens the constraints on the kernel function, and makes this method more suitable for the semantic space. Preliminary experiments on synthetic benchmarks validate the framework.

AI.tldr: A unified gradient-flow framework for generative models via KDE-approximated Wasserstein flows, tying the Drifting Model to exact gradient-flow dynamics, generalizing to MMD-based generators, and enabling mixed-divergence strategies and Riemannian extensions, with preliminary empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Provide a principled, unifying theory for gradient-flow-based generative models; enable identifiability, flexible divergences, and extension to non-Euclidean spaces to improve training stability and sample quality.
AI.method: Show the drifting field equals KDE log-density gradient difference (up to bandwidth^2), matching the Wasserstein-2 gradient flow for KL(q||p) under KDE; include MMD as a special case of these gradient flows; give an identifiability proof; propose a mixed-divergence framework combining reverse KL and chi-square gradient flows; extend framework to Riemannian manifolds to relax kernel constraints; validate with synthetic experiments.
AI.result: Theoretical equivalence between drifting model and KDE-based Wasserstein flow; identifiability result; mixed-divergence strategy; manifold extension; preliminary empirical validation on synthetic benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A cohesive theoretical lens for gradient-flow generative models that unifies existing approaches, suggests practical training strategies to mitigate mode issues, and broadens applicability;需 further large-scale empirical validation.

title: Lifelong Imitation Learning with Multimodal Latent Replay and Incremental Adjustment
summary: We introduce a lifelong imitation learning framework that enables continual policy refinement across sequential tasks under realistic memory and data constraints. Our approach departs from conventional experience replay by operating entirely in a multimodal latent space, where compact representations of visual, linguistic, and robot's state information are stored and reused to support future learning. To further stabilize adaptation, we introduce an incremental feature adjustment mechanism that regularizes the evolution of task embeddings through an angular margin constraint, preserving inter-task distinctiveness. Our method establishes a new state of the art in the LIBERO benchmarks, achieving 10-17 point gains in AUC and up to 65% less forgetting compared to previous leading methods. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of each component, showing consistent gains over alternative strategies. The code is available at: https://github.com/yfqi/lifelong_mlr_ifa.

AI.tldr: A lifelong imitation learning framework uses a multimodal latent space and angular-margin regularization to enable continual policy refinement under memory constraints, achieving SOTA on LIBERO with reduced forgetting; code available.
AI.motivation: Address continual imitation learning across sequential tasks under realistic memory and data limitations, where conventional replay methods struggle to retain task-specific knowledge and prevent catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: Operates entirely in a compact multimodal latent space that encodes visual, linguistic, and robot state information. Stores and reuses these latent representations to support future learning. Introduces an incremental feature adjustment mechanism with an angular margin constraint to stabilize adaptation by regularizing the evolution of task embeddings and preserving inter-task distinctiveness.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on LIBERO benchmarks with 10-17 point AUC gains and up to 65% reduction in forgetting compared to prior methods. Ablation studies validate each component's contribution and show consistent gains over alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective lifelong imitation learning under memory/data constraints, with a memory-efficient, modular approach that stabilizes continual adaptation and reduces forgetting; code is released for reproducibility.

title: A Universal Nearest-Neighbor Estimator for Intrinsic Dimensionality
summary: Estimating the intrinsic dimensionality (ID) of data is a fundamental problem in machine learning and computer vision, providing insight into the true degrees of freedom underlying high-dimensional observations. Existing methods often rely on geometric or distributional assumptions and can significantly fail when these assumptions are violated. In this paper, we introduce a novel ID estimator based on nearest-neighbor distance ratios that involves simple calculations and achieves state-of-the-art results. Most importantly, we provide a theoretical analysis proving that our estimator is \emph{universal}, namely, it converges to the true ID independently of the distribution generating the data. We present experimental results on benchmark manifolds and real-world datasets to demonstrate the performance of our estimator.

AI.tldr: A universal, nearest-neighbor distance ratio–based intrinsic dimensionality estimator with state-of-the-art performance and a theoretical convergence guarantee that holds regardless of the data-generating distribution.
AI.motivation: Estimating intrinsic dimensionality (ID) is fundamental but challenging when geometric or distributional assumptions fail; existing estimators can be unreliable under violations, motivating a robust, distribution-free approach.
AI.method: Introduces a simple NN distance ratio estimator and provides a theoretical analysis proving universality—the estimator converges to the true ID for any data-generating distribution. Empirical validation on benchmark manifolds and real-world datasets.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on benchmark manifolds and real-world datasets, validating both accuracy and robustness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: The proposed estimator is universal (distribution-independent convergence to true ID), practical (simple computations), and empirically competitive across diverse datasets.

title: eLasmobranc Dataset: An Image Dataset for Elasmobranch Species Recognition and Biodiversity Monitoring
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 5 tables. A future extended version of this work will be submitted to Scientific Data
summary:
Elasmobranch populations are experiencing significant global declines, and several species are currently classified as threatened. Reliable monitoring and species-level identification are essential to support conservation and spatial planning initiatives such as Important Shark and Ray Areas (ISRAs). However, existing visual datasets are predominantly detection-oriented, underwater-acquired, or limited to coarse-grained categories, restricting their applicability to fine-grained morphological classification.
  We present the eLasmobranc Dataset, a curated and publicly available image collection from seven ecologically relevant elasmobranch species inhabiting the eastern Spanish Mediterranean coast, a region where two ISRAs have been identified. Images were obtained through dedicated data collection, including field campaigns and collaborations with local fish markets and projects, as well as from open-access public sources. The dataset was constructed predominantly from images acquired outside the aquatic environment under standardized protocols to ensure clear visualization of diagnostic morphological traits. It integrates expert-validated species annotations, structured spatial and temporal metadata, and complementary species-level information.
  The eLasmobranc Dataset is specifically designed to support supervised species-level classification, population studies, and the development of artificial intelligence systems for biodiversity monitoring. By combining morphological clarity, taxonomic reliability, and public accessibility, the dataset addresses a critical gap in fine-grained elasmobranch identification and promotes reproducible research in conservation-oriented computer vision. The dataset is publicly available at https://zenodo.org/records/18549737.

AI.tldr: A publicly available, expert-annotated image dataset (eLasmobranc) of seven elasmobranch species from the eastern Spanish Mediterranean, designed for fine-grained species-level classification and biodiversity monitoring using AI, with standardized, morphology-focused imagery and rich metadata.
AI.motivation: To enable reliable species-level identification and monitoring essential for conservation and spatial planning (e.g., ISRAs). Current datasets are predominantly coarse, detection-oriented, or aquatic, limiting fine-grained classification; this region hosts ISRAs and benefits from accessible, morphology-clear imagery.
AI.method: Data collected from field campaigns, fish markets, projects, and open sources; images largely captured outside water under standardized protocols to highlight diagnostic morphological traits. Expert-validated species annotations, structured spatial/temporal metadata, and supplementary species information accompany the images. Public release via Zenodo.
AI.result: A curated seven-species image dataset with high morphological clarity and expert annotations, enabling supervised species-level classification and population studies. Public availability and standardized metadata support reproducible, AI-driven biodiversity monitoring in the region.
AI.conclusion: The dataset fills a gap in fine-grained elasmobranch identification, supporting conservation-oriented computer vision and regional monitoring efforts (e.g., ISRAs) by providing morphology-focused, accessible data with comprehensive metadata.

title: DynVLA: Learning World Dynamics for Action Reasoning in Autonomous Driving
summary: We propose DynVLA, a driving VLA model that introduces a new CoT paradigm termed Dynamics CoT. DynVLA forecasts compact world dynamics before action generation, enabling more informed and physically grounded decision-making. To obtain compact dynamics representations, DynVLA introduces a Dynamics Tokenizer that compresses future evolution into a small set of dynamics tokens. Considering the rich environment dynamics in interaction-intensive driving scenarios, DynVLA decouples ego-centric and environment-centric dynamics, yielding more accurate world dynamics modeling. We then train DynVLA to generate dynamics tokens before actions through SFT and RFT, improving decision quality while maintaining latency-efficient inference. Compared to Textual CoT, which lacks fine-grained spatiotemporal understanding, and Visual CoT, which introduces substantial redundancy due to dense image prediction, Dynamics CoT captures the evolution of the world in a compact, interpretable, and efficient form. Extensive experiments on NAVSIM, Bench2Drive, and a large-scale in-house dataset demonstrate that DynVLA consistently outperforms Textual CoT and Visual CoT methods, validating the effectiveness and practical value of Dynamics CoT.

AI.tldr: Proposes DynVLA with Dynamics CoT: a driving model that predicts compact world dynamics via a Dynamics Tokenizer to guide action generation, decoupling ego-centric and environment-centric dynamics, trained with SFT and RFT for latency-efficient, informed decisions; outperforms Textual/Visual CoT on driving benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Enhance driving decision-making by incorporating fine-grained spatiotemporal world dynamics in a compact, efficient representation, avoiding redundancy from dense image predictions and richer than textual CoT.
AI.method: Introduce Dynamics Tokenizer to compress future world evolution into a small set of dynamics tokens; decouple ego-centric and environment-centric dynamics; train DynVLA to generate dynamics tokens before actions using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT); ensure latency-efficient inference.
AI.result: DynVLA consistently outperforms Textual CoT and Visual CoT on NAVSIM, Bench2Drive, and a large-scale in-house dataset, validating Dynamics CoT’s effectiveness and practical value.
AI.conclusion: Dynamics CoT provides a compact, interpretable, and efficient representation of world dynamics that improves decision quality in driving and outperforms existing CoT variants.

title: World Model for Battery Degradation Prediction Under Non-Stationary Aging
summary: Degradation prognosis for lithium-ion cells requires forecasting the state-of-health (SOH) trajectory over future cycles. Existing data-driven approaches can produce trajectory outputs through direct regression, but lack a mechanism to propagate degradation dynamics forward in time. This paper formulates battery degradation prognosis as a world model problem, encoding raw voltage, current, and temperature time-series from each cycle into a latent state and propagating it forward via a learned dynamics transition to produce a future trajectory spanning 80 cycles. To investigate whether electrochemical knowledge improves the learned dynamics, a Single Particle Model (SPM) constraint is incorporated into the training loss. Three configurations are evaluated on the Severson LiFePO4 (LFP) dataset of 138 cells. Iterative rollout halves the trajectory forecast error compared to direct regression from the same encoder. The SPM constraint improves prediction at the degradation knee where the resistance to SOH relationship is most applicable, without changing aggregate accuracy.

AI.tldr: A world-model approach for degradation prognosis of Li-ion cells that encodes cycle-level sensor time-series (voltage, current, temperature) into a latent state and propagates it with learned dynamics to forecast an 80-cycle SOH trajectory; a physics-informed Single Particle Model (SPM) constraint is evaluated; results show iterative rollout halves forecast error versus direct regression, with the SPM constraint improving predictions at the degradation knee but not altering aggregate accuracy.
AI.motivation: Address the need to forecast future degradation trajectories (SOH) for lithium-ion cells and overcome the limitations of direct regression that cannot propagate dynamics forward in time; incorporate electrochemical knowledge to improve dynamic realism.
AI.method: Encode voltage, current, and temperature time-series from each cycle into a latent state; learn a forward dynamics model to roll the latent state across future cycles (80-cycle horizon); evaluate three configurations on the Severson LiFePO4 (LFP) dataset (138 cells); incorporate a Single Particle Model (SPM) constraint into the training loss to inject electrochemical knowledge; compare iterative rollout against direct regression.
AI.result: Iterative rollout reduces trajectory forecast error by about 50% compared with direct regression for the same encoder. The SPM constraint improves predictions at the degradation knee where the resistance-SOH relationship is most relevant, without changing aggregate accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The world-model–based degradation prognosis with a physics-informed loss can better capture forward degradation dynamics, particularly near the degradation knee, and is promising on Severson LFP data; further work should test generalization to other chemistries and longer horizons and examine data requirements.

title: Just-in-Time: Training-Free Spatial Acceleration for Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion Transformers have established a new state-of-the-art in image synthesis, but the high computational cost of iterative sampling severely hampers their practical deployment. While existing acceleration methods often focus on the temporal domain, they overlook the substantial spatial redundancy inherent in the generative process, where global structures emerge long before fine-grained details are formed. The uniform computational treatment of all spatial regions represents a critical inefficiency. In this paper, we introduce Just-in-Time (JiT), a novel training-free framework that addresses this challenge by acceleration in the spatial domain. JiT formulates a spatially approximated generative ordinary differential equation (ODE) that drives the full latent state evolution based on computations from a dynamically selected, sparse subset of anchor tokens. To ensure seamless transitions as new tokens are incorporated to expand the dimensions of the latent state, we propose a deterministic micro-flow, a simple and effective finite-time ODE that maintains both structural coherence and statistical correctness. Extensive experiments on the state-of-the-art FLUX.1-dev model demonstrate that JiT achieves up to a 7x speedup with nearly lossless performance, significantly outperforming existing acceleration methods and establishing a new and superior trade-off between inference speed and generation fidelity.

AI.tldr: JiT accelerates diffusion transformer sampling by exploiting spatial redundancy via a training-free, spatially adaptive ODE called JiT, using sparse anchor tokens and a deterministic micro-flow; achieves up to 7x speedup with near-lossless fidelity on FLUX.1-dev.
AI.motivation: High compute cost of iterative sampling; spatial redundancy means not all regions require equal compute; uniform treatment wastes resources.
AI.method: Spatially approximated generative ODE driven by a dynamic subset of anchor tokens; deterministic finite-time micro-flow to handle token expansion and maintain coherence; training-free framework; experiments on FLUX.1-dev.
AI.result: Up to 7x speedup with nearly lossless generation quality; outperforms existing acceleration methods; improves speed-fidelity trade-off.
AI.conclusion: JiT provides a principled, training-free spatial-domain acceleration for diffusion transformers, reducing computational burden while maintaining fidelity; establishes a new efficiency paradigm.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Conditional Expectation Reward
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has proven effective in enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models, particularly in domains such as mathematics where reliable rule-based verifiers can be constructed. However, the reliance on handcrafted, domain-specific verification rules substantially limits the applicability of RLVR to general reasoning domains with free-form answers, where valid answers often exhibit significant variability, making it difficult to establish complete and accurate rules. To address this limitation, we propose Conditional Expectation Reward (CER), which leverages the large language model itself as an implicit verifier, and is therefore applicable to general domains and eliminates the need for external verifiers or auxiliary models. CER is defined as the expected likelihood of generating the reference answer conditioned on the generated answer. In contrast to rule-based verifiers that yield binary feedback, CER provides a soft, graded reward signal that reflects varying degrees of correctness, making it better suited to tasks where answers vary in correctness. Experimental results demonstrate that CER is effective across a wide range of reasoning tasks, spanning both mathematical and general domains, indicating that CER serves as a flexible and general verification mechanism. The code is available at https://github.com/changyi7231/CER.

AI.tldr: Conditional Expectation Reward (CER) uses the LLM itself as a verifier by computing the expected likelihood of the reference answer conditioned on the generated answer, providing a soft, graded reward for general-domain reasoning.
AI.motivation: RLVR relies on handcrafted, domain-specific verifiers, limiting applicability to free-form, general reasoning; CER aims to provide a general, model-based verification signal without external modules.
AI.method: CER defines reward as the conditional probability P(reference|generated) under the LLM, i.e., the expected likelihood of the reference answer given the model's generated answer; uses this as a soft reward; avoids external verifiers; applicable to math and general tasks.
AI.result: Empirical results show CER improves reasoning across both mathematical and general domains; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: CER offers a flexible, general verification mechanism for LLMs, enabling verifiable rewards in wide-ranging reasoning tasks without handcrafted rules; potential for broader applicability and more nuanced feedback.

title: Event-based Photometric Stereo via Rotating Illumination and Per-Pixel Learning
summary: Photometric stereo is a technique for estimating surface normals using images captured under varying illumination. However, conventional frame-based photometric stereo methods are limited in real-world applications due to their reliance on controlled lighting, and susceptibility to ambient illumination. To address these limitations, we propose an event-based photometric stereo system that leverages an event camera, which is effective in scenarios with continuously varying scene radiance and high dynamic range conditions. Our setup employs a single light source moving along a predefined circular trajectory, eliminating the need for multiple synchronized light sources and enabling a more compact and scalable design. We further introduce a lightweight per-pixel multi-layer neural network that directly predicts surface normals from event signals generated by intensity changes as the light source rotates, without system calibration. Experimental results on benchmark datasets and real-world data collected with our data acquisition system demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, achieving a 7.12\% reduction in mean angular error compared to existing event-based photometric stereo methods. In addition, our method demonstrates robustness in regions with sparse event activity, strong ambient illumination, and scenes affected by specularities.

AI.tldr: An event-based photometric stereo method using a single rotating light and a per-pixel lightweight neural network maps event streams to surface normals, achieving 7.12% lower mean angular error than prior event-based PS methods and robust to sparse events, strong ambient illumination, and specularities without calibration.
AI.motivation: Frame-based photometric stereo struggles with ambient illumination and the need for controlled lighting; there is a demand for robust, calibration-free PS under continuously varying radiance and high dynamic range settings.
AI.method: Use an event camera with a single light source moving along a circular trajectory (no need for multiple synchronized lights). Introduce a lightweight per-pixel multi-layer neural network that directly predicts surface normals from intensity-change event signals generated as the light rotates, without system calibration.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on benchmark datasets and real-world data shows a 7.12% reduction in mean angular error compared to existing event-based PS methods; the approach is robust in regions with sparse events, under strong ambient illumination, and in scenes with specularities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system offers a compact, scalable, calibration-free solution for event-based PS, effectively handling dynamic radiance and HDR-like conditions and improving normal estimation accuracy over prior methods.

title: Tackling Length Inflation Without Trade-offs: Group Relative Reward Rescaling for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning significantly enhances LLM capabilities but suffers from a critical issue: length inflation, where models adopt verbosity or inefficient reasoning to maximize rewards. Prior approaches struggle to address this challenge in a general and lossless manner, primarily because additive penalties introduce a compensatory effect that creates optimization shortcuts, while heuristic gating strategies lack generality beyond binary feedback. To bridge this gap, we present Group Relative Reward Rescaling (GR$^3$), which reframes length control as a multiplicative rescaling paradigm, effectively establishing a generalized, continuous, and reward-dependent gating mechanism. To further ensure lossless optimization, we incorporate group-relative regularization and advantage-aware calibration, which dynamically adapt length budgets to instance difficulty and preserve the advantage signal of high-quality trajectories. Empirically, across both RLHF and RLVR settings, GR$^3$~maintains training dynamics and downstream performance comparable to standard GRPO while significantly mitigating length inflation, outperforming state-of-the-art length-regularized baselines.

AI.tldr: GR^3 introduces a multiplicative length-control framework for RL with LLMs to reduce verbosity without loss in performance, combining group-relative regularization and advantage-aware calibration to adapt to difficulty and preserve high-quality trajectories.
AI.motivation: Length inflation in reinforcement learning for LLMs: models tend to be verbose to maximize rewards. Additive penalties cause shortcuts; gating by heuristics is not general. A general, lossless, continuous length-control mechanism is needed.
AI.method: Propose Group Relative Reward Rescaling (GR^3), a multiplicative rescaling paradigm that yields a continuous, reward-dependent length gate. Complemented by group-relative regularization and advantage-aware calibration to adapt budgets to instance difficulty and preserve advantage signals.
