AI.result: Empirically, GR^3 maintains training dynamics and downstream performance comparable to standard GRPO in RLHF and RLVR, while significantly reducing length inflation and outperforming state-of-the-art length-regularized baselines.
AI.conclusion: GR^3 offers a general, lossless, and scalable approach for length control in RL with LLMs, reducing verbosity while preserving performance and surpassing prior length-regularization methods.

title: CodePercept: Code-Grounded Visual STEM Perception for MLLMs
summary: When MLLMs fail at Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) visual reasoning, a fundamental question arises: is it due to perceptual deficiencies or reasoning limitations? Through systematic scaling analysis that independently scales perception and reasoning components, we uncover a critical insight: scaling perception consistently outperforms scaling reasoning. This reveals perception as the true lever limiting current STEM visual reasoning. Motivated by this insight, our work focuses on systematically enhancing the perception capabilities of MLLMs by establishing code as a powerful perceptual medium--executable code provides precise semantics that naturally align with the structured nature of STEM visuals. Specifically, we construct ICC-1M, a large-scale dataset comprising 1M Image-Caption-Code triplets that materializes this code-as-perception paradigm through two complementary approaches: (1) Code-Grounded Caption Generation treats executable code as ground truth for image captions, eliminating the hallucinations inherent in existing knowledge distillation methods; (2) STEM Image-to-Code Translation prompts models to generate reconstruction code, mitigating the ambiguity of natural language for perception enhancement. To validate this paradigm, we further introduce STEM2Code-Eval, a novel benchmark that directly evaluates visual perception in STEM domains. Unlike existing work relying on problem-solving accuracy as a proxy that only measures problem-relevant understanding, our benchmark requires comprehensive visual comprehension through executable code generation for image reconstruction, providing deterministic and verifiable assessment. Code is available at https://github.com/TongkunGuan/Qwen-CodePercept.

AI.tldr: Scaling perception in MLLMs yields greater gains than scaling reasoning for STEM visual reasoning; proposes code-as-perception via executable code, with ICC-1M (1M image-caption-code) and two methods: Code-Grounded Caption Generation and STEM Image-to-Code Translation; introduces STEM2Code-Eval benchmark to assess perception via executable code; code released at the linked repository.
AI.motivation: Perceptual vs reasoning bottlenecks in STEM visual reasoning. Systematic scaling analysis shows perception as the true limiting factor, prompting a paradigm shift to strengthen perception using executable code as a precise perceptual medium aligned with STEM visuals.
AI.method: Independent scaling of perception and reasoning components; build ICC-1M containing image-caption-code triplets; Code-Grounded Caption Generation treats code as ground truth for captions to reduce hallucinations; STEM Image-to-Code Translation prompts models to produce reconstruction code to mitigate natural-language ambiguity in perception tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates that scaling perception outperforms scaling reasoning; introduces the code-as-perception paradigm; provides ICC-1M dataset and STEM2Code-Eval benchmark; reports reduced hallucinations and improved perception through executable code; codebase released.
AI.conclusion: Perception is the primary bottleneck in STEM visual reasoning; the code-as-perception paradigm offers a promising direction to enhance MLLMs, supported by the ICC-1M dataset and STEM2Code-Eval benchmark; future work can extend perception scaling and code-based perceptual representations.

title: Guiding Diffusion Models with Semantically Degraded Conditions
summary: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) is a cornerstone of modern text-to-image models, yet its reliance on a semantically vacuous null prompt ($\varnothing$) generates a guidance signal prone to geometric entanglement. This is a key factor limiting its precision, leading to well-documented failures in complex compositional tasks. We propose Condition-Degradation Guidance (CDG), a novel paradigm that replaces the null prompt with a strategically degraded condition, $\boldsymbol{c}_{\text{deg}}$. This reframes guidance from a coarse "good vs. null" contrast to a more refined "good vs. almost good" discrimination, thereby compelling the model to capture fine-grained semantic distinctions. We find that tokens in transformer text encoders split into two functional roles: content tokens encoding object semantics, and context-aggregating tokens capturing global context. By selectively degrading only the former, CDG constructs $\boldsymbol{c}_{\text{deg}}$ without external models or training. Validated across diverse architectures including Stable Diffusion 3, FLUX, and Qwen-Image, CDG markedly improves compositional accuracy and text-image alignment. As a lightweight, plug-and-play module, it achieves this with negligible computational overhead. Our work challenges the reliance on static, information-sparse negative samples and establishes a new principle for diffusion guidance: the construction of adaptive, semantically-aware negative samples is critical to achieving precise semantic control. Code is available at https://github.com/Ming-321/Classifier-Degradation-Guidance.

AI.tldr: Replace the null prompt in classifier-free guidance with a degraded content condition (c_deg), enabling a 'good vs almost good' discrimination that improves compositional accuracy and text-image alignment with minimal overhead; no external models required; validated across SD-3, FLUX, and Qwen-Image; provides a path toward adaptive semantic negatives.
AI.motivation: CFG’s semantically vacuous null prompt introduces geometry entanglement and poor performance on complex compositional tasks. By moving to a semantically-aware negative signal, the model is forced to distinguish fine-grained semantics.
AI.method: Identify two token roles in transformer text encoders (content vs context tokens) and selectively degrade only content tokens to form c_deg, without external models or training. Implement as a plug-in module; evaluate across multiple diffusion architectures.
AI.result: Notable improvement in compositional accuracy and text-image alignment with negligible computational overhead; demonstrated across diverse architectures (Stable Diffusion 3, FLUX, Qwen-Image) and released code.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, semantically-aware negative samples are crucial for precise semantic control in diffusion guidance; CDG introduces a lightweight, plug-in paradigm that challenges the reliance on static negative prompts.

title: Contract And Conquer: How to Provably Compute Adversarial Examples for a Black-Box Model?
summary: Black-box adversarial attacks are widely used as tools to test the robustness of deep neural networks against malicious perturbations of input data aimed at a specific change in the output of the model. Such methods, although they remain empirically effective, usually do not guarantee that an adversarial example can be found for a particular model. In this paper, we propose Contract And Conquer (CAC), an approach to provably compute adversarial examples for neural networks in a black-box manner. The method is based on knowledge distillation of a black-box model on an expanding distillation dataset and precise contraction of the adversarial example search space. CAC is supported by the transferability guarantee: we prove that the method yields an adversarial example for the black-box model within a fixed number of algorithm iterations. Experimentally, we demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art black-box attack methods on ImageNet dataset for different target models, including vision transformers.

AI.tldr: CAC provides a provable black-box adversarial attack by distilling the black-box model on an expanding dataset and contracting the search space; it yields adversarial examples within a fixed number of iterations and beats prior black-box attacks on ImageNet, including ViTs.
AI.motivation: To overcome the lack of guarantees in black-box attacks and deliver bounded, provable adversarial example generation with strong empirical performance across architectures.
AI.method: Use knowledge distillation from the black-box model onto an expanding distillation dataset to approximate its decision boundary; apply a precise contraction of the adversarial search space to ensure convergence; provide a transferability proof ensuring the constructed perturbation attacks the real black-box model within a fixed number of iterations.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art black-box attacks on ImageNet for various target models, including vision transformers.
AI.conclusion: CAC delivers a principled, provable framework for black-box adversarial example generation with demonstrated practical effectiveness; its guarantees rely on assumptions about distillation accuracy and dataset expansion; further work could address query efficiency, robustness to defenses, and generalization to other domains.

title: Learning to Score: Tuning Cluster Schedulers through Reinforcement Learning
summary:
Efficiently allocating incoming jobs to nodes in large-scale clusters can lead to substantial improvements in both cluster utilization and job performance. In order to allocate incoming jobs, cluster schedulers usually rely on a set of scoring functions to rank feasible nodes. Results from individual scoring functions are usually weighted equally, which could lead to sub-optimal deployments as the one-size-fits-all solution does not take into account the characteristics of each workload. Tuning the weights of scoring functions, however, requires expert knowledge and is computationally expensive.
  This paper proposes a reinforcement learning approach for learning the weights in scheduler scoring algorithms with the overall objective of improving the end-to-end performance of jobs for a given cluster. Our approach is based on percentage improvement reward, frame-stacking, and limiting domain information. We propose a percentage improvement reward to address the objective of multi-step parameter tuning. The inclusion of frame-stacking allows for carrying information across an optimization experiment. Limiting domain information prevents overfitting and improves performance in unseen clusters and workloads. The policy is trained on different combinations of workloads and cluster setups. We demonstrate the proposed approach improves performance on average by 33\% compared to fixed weights and 12\% compared to the best-performing baseline in a lab-based serverless scenario.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement learning is used to adapt scheduler scoring weights to optimize end-to-end job performance in large-scale clusters.
AI.motivation: Fixed, equal-weighted scoring functions can lead to suboptimal deployments across diverse workloads; manual tuning is costly and not scalable.
AI.method: A reinforcement learning approach learns the weights of scoring functions with a percentage improvement reward, uses frame-stacking to retain information across optimization steps, and limits domain information to enhance generalization; the policy is trained on combinations of different workloads and cluster setups.
AI.result: On average, performance improves by 33% over fixed weights and 12% over the best-performing baseline in a lab-based serverless scenario.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RL-based weighting scheme improves end-to-end job performance and generalizes to unseen clusters/workloads, reducing reliance on expert tuning.

title: Taking Shortcuts for Categorical VQA Using Super Neurons
summary: Sparse Attention Vectors (SAVs) have emerged as an excellent training-free alternative to supervised finetuning or low-rank adaptation to improve the performance of Vision Language Models (VLMs). At their heart, SAVs select a few accurate attention heads for a task of interest and use them as classifiers, rather than relying on the model's prediction. In a similar spirit, we find that directly probing the raw activations of the VLM, in the form of scalar values, is sufficient to yield accurate classifiers on diverse visually grounded downstream tasks. Shifting focus from attention vectors to scalar activations dramatically increases the search space for accurate parameters, allowing us to find more discriminative neurons immediately from the first generated token. We call such activations Super Neurons (SNs). In this probing setting, we discover that enough SNs appear in the shallower layers of the large language model to allow for extreme early exiting from the first layer of the model at the first generated token. Compared to the original network, SNs robustly improve the classification performance while achieving a speedup of up to 5.10x.

AI.tldr: Proposes Super Neurons (SNs): using scalar activations from VLMs as classifiers for fast, accurate downstream tasks, enabling early exiting and up to 5.1x speedup.
AI.motivation: To explore whether discriminative information resides in raw scalar activations of VLMs, offering an alternative to attention-based SAVs and enabling faster inference without supervised fine-tuning.
AI.method: Probe raw activations (scalars) from the VLM, identify discriminative neurons (SNs) from early layers/first token, and enable extreme early exiting from the first layer. Compare SNs against Sparse Attention Vectors (SAVs).
AI.result: SNs yield accurate classifiers across diverse tasks, with early exiting from the first generated token from shallow layers. They provide robust classification improvements over the original network and achieve up to 5.1x speedup.
AI.conclusion: Scalar activations are a powerful source of discriminative information in VLMs; SNs enable fast, accurate inference, offering a compelling alternative to attention-based methods for efficient downstream classification.

title: A Bipartite Graph Approach to U.S.-China Cross-Market Return Forecasting
summary: This paper studies cross-market return predictability through a machine learning framework that preserves economic structure. Exploiting the non-overlapping trading hours of the U.S. and Chinese equity markets, we construct a directed bipartite graph that captures time-ordered predictive linkages between stocks across markets. Edges are selected via rolling-window hypothesis testing, and the resulting graph serves as a sparse, economically interpretable feature-selection layer for downstream machine learning models. We apply a range of regularized and ensemble methods to forecast open-to-close returns using lagged foreign-market information. Our results reveal a pronounced directional asymmetry: U.S. previous-close-to-close returns contain substantial predictive information for Chinese intraday returns, whereas the reverse effect is limited. This informational asymmetry translates into economically meaningful performance differences and highlights how structured machine learning frameworks can uncover cross-market dependencies while maintaining interpretability.

AI.tldr: Cross-market return predictability between U.S. and China using a time-ordered, graph-based ML framework with edge selection via rolling tests; U.S. past close predicts Chinese intraday returns, while the reverse is weak, enabling interpretable feature selection and improved forecasting.
AI.motivation: To uncover cross-market predictive linkages under economic structure preservation by exploiting non-overlapping trading hours and using a graph-based feature-selection layer for downstream ML models.
AI.method: Construct a directed bipartite graph of stocks across U.S. and Chinese markets with time-ordered edges representing predictive linkages. Select edges via rolling-window hypothesis tests; treat the resulting graph as a sparse feature-selection layer. Use regularized and ensemble ML models to forecast open-to-close returns using lagged foreign-market information.
AI.result: A pronounced directional asymmetry is found: U.S. previous close-to-close returns contain substantial predictive power for Chinese intraday returns, while the reverse effect is limited. This leads to economically meaningful performance differences and demonstrates that structured ML can reveal cross-market dependencies while preserving interpretability.
AI.conclusion: A graph-based, economically structured ML framework reveals cross-market dependencies and an asymmetry in information flow, yielding interpretable predictors and potential performance gains.

title: Phase-Interface Instance Segmentation as a Visual Sensor for Laboratory Process Monitoring
summary: Reliable visual monitoring of chemical experiments remains challenging in transparent glassware, where weak phase boundaries and optical artifacts degrade conventional segmentation. We formulate laboratory phenomena as the time evolution of phase interfaces and introduce the Chemical Transparent Glasses dataset 2.0 (CTG 2.0), a vessel-aware benchmark with 3,668 images, 23 glassware categories, and five multiphase interface types for phase-interface instance segmentation. Building on YOLO11m-seg, we propose LGA-RCM-YOLO, which combines Local-Global Attention (LGA) for robust semantic representation and a Rectangular Self-Calibration Module (RCM) for boundary refinement of thin, elongated interfaces. On CTG 2.0, the proposed model achieves 84.4% AP@0.5 and 58.43% AP@0.5-0.95, improving over the YOLO11m baseline by 6.42 and 8.75 AP points, respectively, while maintaining near real-time inference (13.67 FPS, RTX 3060). An auxiliary color-attribute head further labels liquid instances as colored or colorless with 98.71% precision and 98.32% recall. Finally, we demonstrate continuous process monitoring in separatory-funnel phase separation and crystallization, showing that phase-interface instance segmentation can serve as a practical visual sensor for laboratory automation.

AI.tldr: Proposes CTG 2.0 dataset and LGA-RCM-YOLO for phase-interface instance segmentation in transparent glassware; achieves state-of-the-art AP with near real-time speed and adds a color-attribute head; demonstrates practical lab-automation monitoring.
AI.motivation: Reliable, automated visual monitoring of chemical experiments in transparent glassware is challenged by weak phase boundaries and optical artifacts; a phase-interface time-evolution framing and vessel-aware dataset/benchmark are needed to advance segmentation performance.
AI.method: Introduce CTG 2.0 dataset (3,668 images, 23 glassware categories, five multiphase interface types). Build on YOLO11m-seg; propose LGA-RCM-YOLO with Local-Global Attention for robust semantic representation and a Rectangular Self-Calibration Module for boundary refinement of thin interfaces; add an auxiliary color-attribute head to classify liquid instances as colored or colorless.
AI.result: On CTG 2.0, achieve 84.4% AP@0.5 and 58.43% AP@0.5-0.95; improvements over YOLO11m baseline by 6.42 and 8.75 AP points; 13.67 FPS on RTX 3060 (near real-time). Color-head yields 98.71% precision and 98.32% recall. Demonstrated continuous process monitoring in separatory-funnel phase separation and crystallization.
AI.conclusion: Phase-interface instance segmentation can serve as a practical visual sensor for laboratory automation; CTG 2.0 provides a vessel-aware benchmark; LGA-RCM-YOLO advances segmentation in challenging transparent-glassware scenes and sets a path for real-time monitoring and automation.

title: Riemannian Geometry-Preserving Variational Autoencoder for MI-BCI Data Augmentation
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of generating synthetic electroencephalogram (EEG) covariance matrices for motor imagery brain-computer interface (MI-BCI) applications. Objective: We aim to develop a generative model capable of producing high-fidelity synthetic covariance matrices while preserving their symmetric positive-definite nature. Approach: We propose a Riemannian geometry-preserving variational autoencoder (RGP-VAE) integrating geometric mappings with a composite loss function combining Riemannian distance, tangent space reconstruction accuracy and generative diversity. Results: The model generates valid, representative EEG covariance matrices, while learning a subject-invariant latent space. Synthetic data proves practically useful for MI-BCI, with its impact depending on the paired classifier. Contribution: This work introduces and validates the RGP-VAE as a geometry-preserving generative model for EEG covariance matrices, highlighting its potential for signal privacy, scalability and data augmentation.

AI.tldr: Proposes a geometry-preserving VAE (RGP-VAE) to generate synthetic, SPD EEG covariance matrices for motor imagery BCI, achieving valid matrices and a subject-invariant latent space to support data augmentation and privacy.
AI.motivation: Need for high-fidelity, geometry-consistent synthetic EEG covariance matrices to augment MI-BCI data while preserving SPD structure and enabling scalable, privacy-preserving data generation.
AI.method: Introduce a Riemannian geometry-preserving variational autoencoder (RGP-VAE) that operates on SPD matrices via geometric mappings. The model optimizes a composite loss combining Riemannian distance on the SPD manifold, tangent-space reconstruction accuracy, and a term promoting generative diversity to ensure varied, realistic samples and a latent space that is largely subject-invariant.
AI.result: The model yields valid, representative EEG covariance matrices and learns a largely subject-invariant latent space. Synthetic data facilitate MI-BCI tasks, with effectiveness depending on the downstream classifier.
AI.conclusion: RGP-VAE constitutes a geometry-preserving generative model for EEG covariance matrices, with promising implications for signal privacy, scalability, and data augmentation in MI-BCI.

title: The Quadratic Geometry of Flow Matching: Semantic Granularity Alignment for Text-to-Image Synthesis
summary: In this work, we analyze the optimization dynamics of generative fine-tuning. We observe that under the Flow Matching framework, the standard MSE objective can be formulated as a Quadratic Form governed by a dynamically evolving Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK). This geometric perspective reveals a latent Data Interaction Matrix, where diagonal terms represent independent sample learning and off-diagonal terms encode residual correlation between heterogeneous features. Although standard training implicitly optimizes these cross-term interferences, it does so without explicit control; moreover, the prevailing data-homogeneity assumption may constrain the model's effective capacity. Motivated by this insight, we propose Semantic Granularity Alignment (SGA), using Text-to-Image synthesis as a testbed. SGA engineers targeted interventions in the vector residual field to mitigate gradient conflicts. Evaluations across DiT and U-Net architectures confirm that SGA advances the efficiency-quality trade-off by accelerating convergence and improving structural integrity.

AI.tldr: Analyzes optimization dynamics of generative fine-tuning under Flow Matching by reframing MSE as a quadratic form steered by a dynamic NTK, unveiling a Data Interaction Matrix; proposes Semantic Granularity Alignment (SGA) to mitigate gradient conflicts; demonstrates improved convergence and structural integrity on DiT and U-Net in Text-to-Image tasks.
AI.motivation: Understand and control training dynamics of generative fine-tuning, particularly cross-term gradient interference arising from heterogeneous features and data-homogeneity assumptions that may limit model capacity.
AI.method: Derive a quadratic-form representation of the MSE objective under Flow Matching with a evolving Neural Tangent Kernel; identify a latent Data Interaction Matrix separating diagonal (independent sample learning) and off-diagonal (residual feature correlations) terms; design Semantic Granularity Alignment (SGA) to intervene in the vector residual field to reduce gradient conflicts; validate on Text-to-Image synthesis using DiT and U-Net architectures.
AI.result: SGA accelerates convergence and improves the quality/structure of generated outputs, enhancing the efficiency-quality trade-off and preserving structural integrity across evaluated models.
AI.conclusion: Semantic Granularity Alignment is an effective, architecture-agnostic intervention to manage gradient interference in generative fine-tuning by leveraging NTK-informed dynamics; insights link data interaction and capacity constraints to training dynamics.

title: CUPID: A Plug-in Framework for Joint Aleatoric and Epistemic Uncertainty Estimation with a Single Model
summary: Accurate estimation of uncertainty in deep learning is critical for deploying models in high-stakes domains such as medical diagnosis and autonomous decision-making, where overconfident predictions can lead to harmful outcomes. In practice, understanding the reason behind a model's uncertainty and the type of uncertainty it represents can support risk-aware decisions, enhance user trust, and guide additional data collection. However, many existing methods only address a single type of uncertainty or require modifications and retraining of the base model, making them difficult to adopt in real-world systems. We introduce CUPID (Comprehensive Uncertainty Plug-in estImation moDel), a general-purpose module that jointly estimates aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty without modifying or retraining the base model. CUPID can be flexibly inserted into any layer of a pretrained network. It models aleatoric uncertainty through a learned Bayesian identity mapping and captures epistemic uncertainty by analyzing the model's internal responses to structured perturbations. We evaluate CUPID across a range of tasks, including classification, regression, and out-of-distribution detection. The results show that it consistently delivers competitive performance while offering layer-wise insights into the origins of uncertainty. By making uncertainty estimation modular, interpretable, and model-agnostic, CUPID supports more transparent and trustworthy AI. Related code and data are available at https://github.com/a-Fomalhaut-a/CUPID.

AI.tldr: CUPID is a modular plug-in that jointly estimates aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in pretrained networks without retraining, via a Bayesian identity mapping for aleatoric uncertainty and structured perturbation analysis for epistemic uncertainty; it plugs into any layer and provides layer-wise interpretability across classification, regression, and OOD tasks; code available.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty estimation is essential for high-stakes applications; existing methods often handle only one type of uncertainty or require modifying/retraining the base model, hindering deployment in real-world systems.
AI.method: A general-purpose module that can be inserted into any layer of a pretrained network. It models aleatoric uncertainty with a learned Bayesian identity mapping and captures epistemic uncertainty by analyzing the model's internal responses to structured perturbations, enabling joint estimation without modifying the base model.
AI.result: CUPID delivers competitive performance across classification, regression, and out-of-distribution detection tasks while providing layer-wise insights into the origins of uncertainty, and it remains model-agnostic and modular.
AI.conclusion: CUPID enables modular, interpretable uncertainty estimation that can be deployed without retraining, supporting transparent and trustworthy AI; the authors provide code and data to facilitate adoption.

title: Implicit Statistical Inference in Transformers: Approximating Likelihood-Ratio Tests In-Context
comment: Accepted at the Latent and Implicit Thinking Workshop (ICLR 2026)
summary: In-context learning (ICL) allows Transformers to adapt to novel tasks without weight updates, yet the underlying algorithms remain poorly understood. We adopt a statistical decision-theoretic perspective by investigating simple binary hypothesis testing, where the optimal policy is determined by the likelihood-ratio test. Notably, this setup provides a mathematically rigorous setting for mechanistic interpretability where the target algorithmic ground truth is known. By training Transformers on tasks requiring distinct geometries (linear shifted means vs. nonlinear variance estimation), we demonstrate that the models approximate the Bayes-optimal sufficient statistics from context up to some monotonic transformation, matching the performance of an ideal oracle estimator in nonlinear regimes. Leveraging this analytical ground truth, mechanistic analysis via logit lens and circuit alignment suggests that the model does not rely on a fixed kernel smoothing heuristic. Instead, it appears to adapt the point at which decisions become linearly decodable: exhibiting patterns consistent with a voting-style ensemble for linear tasks while utilizing a deeper sequential computation for nonlinear tasks. These findings suggest that ICL emerges from the construction of task-adaptive statistical estimators rather than simple similarity matching.

AI.tldr: ICL can be interpreted as adaptive Bayesian-style statistics; Transformers approximate Bayes-optimal sufficient statistics across tasks, with task-dependent computation (voting-like ensemble for linear tasks; deeper sequential processing for nonlinear tasks).
AI.motivation: Provide a principled, mechanistic understanding of in-context learning by aligning it with a known statistical decision problem (binary hypothesis testing), enabling ground-truth algorithmic interpretation.
AI.method: Train Transformers on tasks with distinct geometric cues (linear shifted means vs nonlinear variance estimation). Use a Bayesian decision-theoretic framework (likelihood-ratio test) as the ground truth. Analyze model mechanics via logit lens and circuit alignment to assess whether decisions rely on fixed kernel smoothing or task-adaptive decision points.
AI.result: Models approximate Bayes-optimal sufficient statistics from context up to monotone transformations and match oracle performance in nonlinear regimes. Mechanistic analyses reveal a shift from fixed-heurstic similarity to task-adaptive computation: linear tasks show voting-like ensembles; nonlinear tasks employ deeper sequential processing.
AI.conclusion: ICL emerges from task-adaptive statistical estimators tailored to the geometry of the task, rather than simple similarity matching; provides a principled path to mechanistic interpretability of ICL.

title: PolGS++: Physically-Guided Polarimetric Gaussian Splatting for Fast Reflective Surface Reconstruction
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2509.19726
summary: Accurate reconstruction of reflective surfaces remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision, with broad applications in real-time virtual reality and digital content creation. Although 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) enables efficient novel-view rendering with explicit representations, its performance on reflective surfaces still lags behind implicit neural methods, especially in recovering fine geometry and surface normals. To address this gap, we propose PolGS++, a physically-guided polarimetric Gaussian Splatting framework for fast reflective surface reconstruction. Specifically, we integrate a polarized BRDF (pBRDF) model into 3DGS to explicitly decouple diffuse and specular components, providing physically grounded reflectance modeling and stronger geometric cues for reflective surface recovery. Furthermore, we introduce a depth-guided visibility mask acquisition mechanism that enables angle-of-polarization (AoP)-based tangent-space consistency constraints in Gaussian Splatting without costly ray-tracing intersections. This physically guided design improves reconstruction quality and efficiency, requiring only about 10 minutes of training. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets validate the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: PolGS++ adds a physics-guided polarimetric component to 3D Gaussian Splatting, decoupling diffuse/specular via a pBRDF, and uses a depth-guided visibility mask to enforce AoP-based tangent-space consistency without ray tracing, achieving faster and more accurate reflective-surface reconstruction (~10 min training) validated on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Reflective surfaces pose a major challenge for explicit 3D representations like Gaussian Splatting. While 3DGS enables fast novel-view rendering, its handling of specular highlights and accurate normals lags behind implicit neural methods. Polarization offers physical cues about surface normals and reflectance, suggesting a physics-informed approach to improve geometry and appearance estimation.
AI.method: Integrates a polarized BRDF (pBRDF) model into 3D Gaussian Splatting to explicitly separate diffuse and specular components, providing physically grounded reflectance cues. Introduces a depth-guided visibility mask to enable AoP-based tangent-space consistency constraints in Gaussian Splatting without expensive ray-tracing intersections.
AI.result: Reported improvements in reconstruction quality for reflective surfaces, demonstrated on both synthetic and real-world datasets. Training time is claimed to be around 10 minutes, indicating notable efficiency gains over some prior methods.
AI.conclusion: A physics-guided, polarization-aware augmentation of 3DGS yields better geometry and normals for reflective surfaces while maintaining low training time, suggesting practical utility for real-time VR and digital content pipelines. Further details (metrics, ablations) would clarify the magnitude of gains and robustness across materials.

title: HAPEns: Hardware-Aware Post-Hoc Ensembling for Tabular Data
summary: Ensembling is commonly used in machine learning on tabular data to boost predictive performance and robustness, but larger ensembles often lead to increased hardware demand. We introduce HAPEns, a post-hoc ensembling method that explicitly balances accuracy against hardware efficiency. Inspired by multi-objective and quality diversity optimization, HAPEns constructs a diverse set of ensembles along the Pareto front of predictive performance and resource usage. Existing hardware-aware post-hoc ensembling baselines are not available, highlighting the novelty of our approach. Experiments on 83 tabular classification datasets show that HAPEns significantly outperforms baselines, finding superior trade-offs for ensemble performance and deployment cost. Ablation studies also reveal that memory usage is a particularly effective objective metric. Further, we show that even a greedy ensembling algorithm can be significantly improved in this task with a static multi-objective weighting scheme.

AI.tldr: HAPEns introduces a post-hoc, hardware-aware ensembling method that optimizes accuracy and resource usage by constructing diverse ensembles along the Pareto front.
AI.motivation: to address the trade-off between predictive performance and hardware cost in tabular data; existing post-hoc baselines lack hardware-awareness.
AI.method: uses multi-objective optimization and quality diversity to create ensembles on the Pareto front; evaluates 83 tabular datasets; highlights memory usage as a key objective; demonstrates gains with a greedy scheme and static multi-objective weighting.
AI.result: significantly outperforms baselines across datasets; discovers superior accuracy-versus-resource trade-offs; memory objective is particularly effective; ablations support design choices.
AI.conclusion: offers a practical framework for hardware-aware ensembling in tabular ML; memory usage is an effective objective; even simple greedy ensembling benefits from proper multi-objective weighting; novelty lies in hardware-aware post-hoc approach.

title: Backdoor Directions in Vision Transformers
summary: This paper investigates how Backdoor Attacks are represented within Vision Transformers (ViTs). By assuming knowledge of the trigger, we identify a specific ``trigger direction'' in the model's activations that corresponds to the internal representation of the trigger. We confirm the causal role of this linear direction by showing that interventions in both activation and parameter space consistently modulate the model's backdoor behavior across multiple datasets and attack types. Using this direction as a diagnostic tool, we trace how backdoor features are processed across layers. Our analysis reveals distinct qualitative differences: static-patch triggers follow a different internal logic than stealthy, distributed triggers. We further examine the link between backdoors and adversarial attacks, specifically testing whether PGD-based perturbations (de-)activate the identified trigger mechanism. Finally, we propose a data-free, weight-based detection scheme for stealthy-trigger attacks. Our findings show that mechanistic interpretability offers a robust framework for diagnosing and addressing security vulnerabilities in computer vision.

AI.tldr: Mechanistic interpretability reveals a trigger-direction in ViT activations that encodes backdoor features; causal interventions in activation/parameters modulate backdoor behavior across datasets/attacks, revealing differences between static-patch and distributed triggers, linking backdoors with adversarial perturbations, and enabling a data-free, weight-based detection scheme.
AI.motivation: To understand how backdoor features are represented and processed in Vision Transformers using mechanistic interpretability, enabling robust detection and mitigation of backdoor vulnerabilities in CV systems.
AI.method: Assume trigger knowledge to identify a trigger-direction in model activations; perform activation- and parameter-space interventions to test causal role; trace backdoor feature processing across layers; compare static-patch vs distributed triggers; test PGD perturbations for (de-)activation; propose a data-free, weight-based detection scheme.
AI.result: Identified a specific trigger-direction in ViT activations that encodes the trigger; demonstrated causal influence of this direction via interventions across datasets and attack types; mapped how backdoor features propagate through layers and that trigger logic differs between static-patch and stealthy distributed triggers; showed PGD perturbations can modulate the trigger mechanism; proposed a data-free, weight-based detection method for stealthy triggers.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic interpretability provides a robust framework to diagnose and address CV security vulnerabilities, enabling precise understanding and mitigation of backdoor mechanisms in Vision Transformers.

title: AI-Enhanced Spatial Cellular Traffic Demand Prediction with Contextual Clustering and Error Correction for 5G/6G Planning
comment: 5 pages, 8 figures. Submitted to IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
summary: Accurate spatial prediction of cellular traffic demand is essential for 5G NR capacity planning, network densification, and data-driven 6G planning. Although machine learning can fuse heterogeneous geospatial and socio-economic layers to estimate fine-grained demand maps, spatial autocorrelation can cause neighborhood leakage under naive train/test splits, inflating accuracy and weakening planning reliability. This paper presents an AI-driven framework that reduces leakage and improves spatial generalization via a context-aware two-stage splitting strategy with residual spatial error correction. Experiments using crowdsourced usage indicators across five major Canadian cities show consistent mean absolute error (MAE) reductions relative to location-only clustering, supporting more reliable bandwidth provisioning and evidence-based spectrum planning and sharing assessments.

AI.tldr: An AI-based framework improves spatial prediction of cellular demand by mitigating spatial leakage via a context-aware two-stage train/test split and residual error correction, achieving MAE gains over naive location-based clustering in five Canadian cities.
AI.motivation: Accurately predicting fine-grained cellular demand is crucial for 5G/6G planning, but spatial autocorrelation causes neighbor leakage in naive train/test splits, leading to inflated accuracy and unreliable resource provisioning.
AI.method: Proposes a context-aware two-stage splitting strategy to reduce leakage, combined with residual spatial error correction within an AI-driven framework that fuses heterogeneous geospatial and socio-economic layers.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on crowdsourced usage indicators across five major Canadian cities shows consistent MAE reductions compared with location-only clustering, indicating improved spatial generalization.
AI.conclusion: The framework enhances reliability of bandwidth provisioning and spectrum planning by mitigating leakage and improving spatial generalization in cellular demand modeling.

title: HanMoVLM: Large Vision-Language Models for Professional Artistic Painting Evaluation
summary: While Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate impressive general visual capabilities, they remain artistically blind and unable to offer professional evaluation of artworks within specific artistic domains like human experts. To bridge this gap, we transform VLMs into experts capable of professional-grade painting evaluation in the Chinese Artistic Domain, which is more abstract and demands extensive artistic training for evaluation. We introduce HanMo-Bench, a new dataset that features authentic auction-grade masterpieces and AI-generated works, grounded in real-world market valuations. To realize the rigorous judgment, we propose the HanMoVLM and construct a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) validated by experts. This CoT guides the model to perform expert-level reasoning: from content identification and Region of Interest (RoI) localization to professional evaluation, guided by both theme-specific evaluation and typical three-tier evaluation in Chinese paintings. Furthermore, we design a reward function to refine the reasoning process of the HanMoVLM to improve the accuracy. We demonstrate that HanMoVLM can serve as a critical backbone for Test-time Scaling in image generation. By acting as a high-quality verifier, HanMoVLM enables generative models to select the most artistically superior outputs from multiple candidates. Experimental results and human studies confirm that the proposed HanMoVLM effectively bridges the gap, achieving a high consistency with professional experts and significantly improving the quality of Chinese Painting generation.

AI.tldr: HanMoVLM: a VLM-based expert evaluator for Chinese painting, trained with HanMo-Bench (authentic artworks + AI-generated pieces) and expert-validated Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to perform content/ROI analysis and three-tier evaluation, with a reward function for refining reasoning. It enables test-time scaling of image generation by selecting top-art outputs aligned with professional standards, achieving high agreement with experts and improving generation quality.
AI.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models struggle with domain-specific, professional evaluation in art, especially in the abstract, training-heavy Chinese painting domain. There is a need for a dedicated, expert-level verifier to bridge AI-generated art with professional appraisal standards.
AI.method: 1) Construct HanMo-Bench dataset with authentic auction-grade Chinese artworks and AI-generated pieces tied to market valuations. 2) Develop HanMoVLM and a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) framework validated by domain experts, guiding steps from content identification and RoI localization to professional evaluation. 3) Incorporate theme-specific and three-tier Chinese painting evaluation criteria. 4) Design a reward function to refine the reasoning process. 5) Demonstrate test-time scaling by using HanMoVLM as a verifier to select superior outputs from multiple candidates in image generation.
AI.result: HanMoVLM achieves high consistency with professional experts in evaluation and significantly improves the quality of Chinese painting generation. Experimental results and human studies validate its effectiveness as a back-bone verifier for generation and its role in bridging the gap between LLM-based VLMs and domain-specific professional judgment.
AI.conclusion: HanMoVLM effectively bridges the gap between general VLMs and professional art evaluation in the Chinese Artistic Domain. It serves as a high-quality verifier enabling improved generation quality and reliable expert-aligned judgments, with potential for broader deployment as a test-time scaling backbone for art generation systems.

title: Protein Counterfactuals via Diffusion-Guided Latent Optimization
comment: 16 pages, 7 figures, accepted at the Gen2 Workshop at ICLR 2026
summary: Deep learning models can predict protein properties with unprecedented accuracy but rarely offer mechanistic insight or actionable guidance for engineering improved variants. When a model flags an antibody as unstable, the protein engineer is left without recourse: which mutations would rescue stability while preserving function? We introduce Manifold-Constrained Counterfactual Optimization for Proteins (MCCOP), a framework that computes minimal, biologically plausible sequence edits that flip a model's prediction to a desired target state. MCCOP operates in a continuous joint sequence-structure latent space and employs a pretrained diffusion model as a manifold prior, balancing three objectives: validity (achieving the target property), proximity (minimizing mutations), and plausibility (producing foldable proteins). We evaluate MCCOP on three protein engineering tasks - GFP fluorescence rescue, thermodynamic stability enhancement, and E3 ligase activity recovery - and show that it generates sparser, more plausible counterfactuals than both discrete and continuous baselines. The recovered mutations align with known biophysical mechanisms, including chromophore packing and hydrophobic core consolidation, establishing MCCOP as a tool for both model interpretation and hypothesis-driven protein design. Our code is publicly available at github.com/weroks/mccop.

AI.tldr: MCCOP computes minimal, plausible sequence edits in a joint sequence-structure latent space using a diffusion-prior to flip a protein model's prediction, enabling interpretable counterfactual design; it outperforms baselines and recovers biologically meaningful mutations.
AI.motivation: To extract actionable, mechanistic guidance from deep learning protein predictors by generating minimal, valid counterfactuals that rescue a target property while preserving function.
AI.method: Operate in a continuous joint sequence-structure latent space and use a pretrained diffusion model as a manifold prior. Optimize for three objectives—validity (achieve target property), proximity (minimize mutations), and plausibility (produce foldable proteins)—to generate minimal, biologically plausible edits that flip the predictor's decision.
AI.result: MCCOP yields sparser, more plausible counterfactual mutations than both discrete and continuous baselines. Recovered mutations align with known biophysical mechanisms (e.g., chromophore packing, hydrophobic core consolidation). Demonstrated on GFP fluorescence rescue, thermodynamic stability enhancement, and E3 ligase activity recovery; code available at github.com/weroks/mccop.
AI.conclusion: MCCOP provides a framework for interpretable model-driven protein design and hypothesis testing, with potential to guide experimental engineering via minimal, plausible sequence edits.

title: A dataset of medication images with instance segmentation masks for preventing adverse drug events
comment: 25 pages, 19 figures. Submitted to Scientific Data (Nature Portfolio)
summary: Medication errors and adverse drug events (ADEs) pose significant risks to patient safety, often arising from difficulties in reliably identifying pharmaceuticals in real-world settings. AI-based pill recognition models offer a promising solution, but the lack of comprehensive datasets hinders their development. Existing pill image datasets rarely capture real-world complexities such as overlapping pills, varied lighting, and occlusions. MEDISEG addresses this gap by providing instance segmentation annotations for 32 distinct pill types across 8262 images, encompassing diverse conditions from individual pill images to cluttered dosette boxes. We trained YOLOv8 and YOLOv9 on MEDISEG to demonstrate their usability, achieving mean average precision at IoU 0.5 of 99.5 percent on the 3-Pills subset and 80.1 percent on the 32-Pills subset. We further evaluate MEDISEG under a few-shot detection protocol, demonstrating that base training on MEDISEG significantly improves recognition of unseen pill classes in occluded multi-pill scenarios compared to existing datasets. These results highlight the dataset's ability not only to support robust supervised training but also to promote transferable representations under limited supervision, making it a valuable resource for developing and benchmarking AI-driven systems for medication safety.

AI.tldr: MEDISEG is a comprehensive instance-segmentation pill dataset enabling robust AI-based pill recognition, trained with YOLO models, showing strong in-domain and few-shot generalization performance.
AI.motivation: Fill the real-world data gap in pill recognition by providing diverse, annotated images that capture overlap, occlusion, and lighting variations to improve patient safety.
AI.method: Create MEDISEG with 32 pill types across 8262 images with instance segmentation annotations; train YOLOv8/YOLOv9; evaluate on 3-Pills and 32-Pills subsets; assess few-shot transfer to unseen classes in occluded multi-pill scenes.
AI.result: YOLOv8/YOLOv9 achieve mAP@IoU=0.5 of 99.5% on 3-Pills and 80.1% on 32-Pills; base training on MEDISEG improves recognition of unseen pill classes in occlusions under few-shot conditions versus existing datasets.
AI.conclusion: MEDISEG effectively supports supervised training and transfer learning for medication safety AI, offering a valuable benchmark for robust pill recognition in realistic settings.

title: BALD-SAM: Disagreement-based Active Prompting in Interactive Segmentation
summary: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) has revolutionized interactive segmentation through spatial prompting. While existing work primarily focuses on automating prompts in various settings, real-world annotation workflows involve iterative refinement where annotators observe model outputs and strategically place prompts to resolve ambiguities. Current pipelines typically rely on the annotator's visual assessment of the predicted mask quality. We postulate that a principled approach for automated interactive prompting is to use a model-derived criterion to identify the most informative region for the next prompt. In this work, we establish active prompting: a spatial active learning approach where locations within images constitute an unlabeled pool and prompts serve as queries to prioritize information-rich regions, increasing the utility of each interaction. We further present BALD-SAM: a principled framework adapting Bayesian Active Learning by Disagreement (BALD) to spatial prompt selection by quantifying epistemic uncertainty. To do so, we freeze the entire model and apply Bayesian uncertainty modeling only to a small learned prediction head, making intractable uncertainty estimation practical for large multi-million parameter foundation models. Across 16 datasets spanning natural, medical, underwater, and seismic domains, BALD-SAM demonstrates strong cross-domain performance, ranking first or second on 14 of 16 benchmarks. We validate these gains through a comprehensive ablation suite covering 3 SAM backbones and 35 Laplace posterior configurations, amounting to 38 distinct ablation settings. Beyond strong average performance, BALD-SAM surpasses human prompting and, in several categories, even oracle prompting, while consistently outperforming one-shot baselines in final segmentation quality, particularly on thin and structurally complex objects.

AI.tldr: BALD-SAM: an active learning framework for interactive image segmentation that uses BALD-based uncertainty to select the most informative region to prompt, applied to a frozen SAM with a small learned head; achieves strong cross-domain gains, ranking top-2 on most benchmarks and outperforming humans/oracles in several cases.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap in real-world interactive annotation where prompts are chosen heuristically. Propose a principled, model-driven criterion to maximize information gain per interaction using epistemic uncertainty.
AI.method: Treat image locations as an unlabeled pool; prompts are queries. Apply Bayesian Active Learning by Disagreement (BALD) to select prompts. Freeze the SAM backbone; train a small head with Bayesian uncertainty (Laplace approximation) to estimate epistemic uncertainty. Evaluate across 16 datasets (natural, medical, underwater, seismic), 3 SAM backbones, and 35 posterior configurations (38 ablations).
AI.result: BALD-SAM ranks first or second on 14 of 16 benchmarks; surpasses human prompting and, in several categories, even oracle prompting; consistently outperforms one-shot baselines in final segmentation quality, especially for thin or structurally complex objects.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that uncertainty-guided spatial prompting is effective for interactive segmentation on large foundation models. By confining Bayesian modeling to a small head, practical epistemic uncertainty estimation is achieved. The approach delivers strong cross-domain performance and suggests broader applicability of active prompting to interactive AI tasks.

title: Spatio-Temporal Attention Graph Neural Network: Explaining Causalities With Attention
summary: Industrial Control Systems (ICS) underpin critical infrastructure and face growing cyber-physical threats due to the convergence of operational technology and networked environments. While machine learning-based anomaly detection approaches in ICS shows strong theoretical performance, deployment is often limited by poor explainability, high false-positive rates, and sensitivity to evolving system behavior, i.e., baseline drifting. We propose a Spatio-Temporal Attention Graph Neural Network (STA-GNN) for unsupervised and explainable anomaly detection in ICS that models both temporal dynamics and relational structure of the system. Sensors, controllers, and network entities are represented as nodes in a dynamically learned graph, enabling the model to capture inter-dependencies across physical processes and communication patterns. Attention mechanisms provide influential relationships, supporting inspection of correlations and potential causal pathways behind detected events. The approach supports multiple data modalities, including SCADA point measurements, network flow features, and payload features, and thus enables unified cyber-physical analysis. To address operational requirements, we incorporate a conformal prediction strategy to control false alarm rates and monitor performance degradation under drifting of the environment. Our findings highlight the possibilities and limitations of model evaluation and common pitfalls in anomaly detection in ICS. Our findings emphasise the importance of explainable, drift-aware evaluation for reliable deployment of learning-based security monitoring systems.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised, explainable anomaly detection in ICS using a Spatio-Temporal Attention Graph Neural Network (STA-GNN) with conformal prediction for drift-aware false-alarm control and multi-modal data fusion.
AI.motivation: Industrial Control Systems (ICS) are critical infrastructure; ML-based anomaly detection often lacks explainability, is prone to high false positives, and is sensitive to baseline drift. A model that captures spatio-temporal dependencies and supports multiple data modalities with interpretable outputs is needed.
AI.method: Construct a dynamic graph where nodes are sensors, controllers, and network entities. Apply a Spatio-Temporal Attention Graph Neural Network to learn temporal dynamics and relational structure; use attention to reveal influential relationships for explainability. Fuse multiple modalities (SCADA point measurements, network flow features, payload features). Employ conformal prediction to calibrate false alarm rates and monitor performance under concept drift.
AI.result: The abstract presents qualitative findings emphasizing the potential and limitations of the approach. It highlights the importance of explainable, drift-aware evaluation for reliable deployment and notes common pitfalls in model evaluation for anomaly detection in ICS.
AI.conclusion: Explainable, drift-aware evaluation and conformal calibration are essential for dependable deployment of learning-based ICS security monitors. STA-GNN enables unified cyber-physical analysis across modalities and provides insights into correlations and potential causal pathways behind detected anomalies.

title: On the Reliability of Cue Conflict and Beyond
summary: Understanding how neural networks rely on visual cues offers a human-interpretable view of their internal decision processes. The cue-conflict benchmark has been influential in probing shape-texture preference and in motivating the insight that stronger, human-like shape bias is often associated with improved in-domain performance. However, we find that the current stylization-based instantiation can yield unstable and ambiguous bias estimates. Specifically, stylization may not reliably instantiate perceptually valid and separable cues nor control their relative informativeness, ratio-based bias can obscure absolute cue sensitivity, and restricting evaluation to preselected classes can distort model predictions by ignoring the full decision space. Together, these factors can confound preference with cue validity, cue balance, and recognizability artifacts. We introduce REFINED-BIAS, an integrated dataset and evaluation framework for reliable and interpretable shape-texture bias diagnosis. REFINED-BIAS constructs balanced, human- and model- recognizable cue pairs using explicit definitions of shape and texture, and measures cue-specific sensitivity over the full label space via a ranking-based metric, enabling fairer cross-model comparisons. Across diverse training regimes and architectures, REFINED-BIAS enables fairer cross-model comparison, more faithful diagnosis of shape and texture biases, and clearer empirical conclusions, resolving inconsistencies that prior cue-conflict evaluations could not reliably disambiguate.

AI.tldr: REFINED-BIAS offers a robust dataset and evaluation framework to diagnose shape-texture bias in neural networks, addressing instability and interpretability issues of prior cue-conflict methods by explicit cue definitions and a full-label-space, ranking-based sensitivity measure.
AI.motivation: Existing cue-conflict approaches (stylization-based) yield unstable, ambiguous bias estimates due to uncontrolled cue validity, balance, and evaluation scope; a need for reliable, interpretable bias diagnosis across models.
AI.method: Construct balanced, human- and model-recognizable shape/texture cue pairs with explicit definitions; evaluate cue-specific sensitivity across the full label space using a ranking-based metric, enabling fair cross-model comparisons.
AI.result: Demonstrates across diverse training regimes and architectures that REFINED-BIAS yields fairer comparisons and clearer bias diagnoses, resolving inconsistencies of prior cue-conflict evaluations.
AI.conclusion: REFINED-BIAS provides a more faithful and interpretable framework for shape-texture bias analysis and should be adopted for robust cross-model benchmarking.

title: Surrogate models for nuclear fusion with parametric Shallow Recurrent Decoder Networks: applications to magnetohydrodynamics
summary: Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) effects play a key role in the design and operation of nuclear fusion systems, where electrically conducting fluids (such as liquid metals or molten salts in reactor blankets) interact with magnetic fields of varying intensity and orientation, which affect the resulting flow. The numerical resolution of MHD models involves highly nonlinear multiphysics systems of equations and can become computationally expensive, particularly in multi-query, parametric, or real-time contexts. This work investigates a fully data-driven framework for MHD state reconstruction that combines dimensionality reduction via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) with the SHallow REcurrent Decoder (SHRED), a neural network architecture designed to recover the full spatio-temporal state from sparse time-series measurements of a limited number of observables. The methodology is applied to a parametric MHD test case involving compressible lead-lithium flow in a stepped channel subjected to thermal gradients and magnetic fields spanning a broad range of intensities. To improve efficiency, the full-order dataset is first compressed using SVD, yielding a reduced representation used as reference truth for training. Only temperature measurements from three sensors are provided as input, while the network reconstructs the full fields of velocity, pressure, and temperature. To assess robustness with respect to sensor placement, thirty randomly generated sensor configurations are tested in ensemble mode. Results show that SHRED accurately reconstructs the full MHD state even for magnetic field intensities not included in the training set. These findings demonstrate the potential of SHRED as a computationally efficient surrogate modeling strategy for fusion-relevant multiphysics problems, enabling low-cost state estimation with possible applications in real-time monitoring and control.

AI.tldr: Data-driven MHD state reconstruction using SVD for dimensionality reduction and SHRED (SHallow REcurrent Decoder) to recover full 3D, time-dependent fields from sparse temperature sensors; robust across 30 random sensor placements and extrapolates to unseen magnetic field strengths.
AI.motivation: Full MHD simulations are computationally expensive, especially for parametric, multi-query, or real-time scenarios in fusion-relevant multiphysics. A fast surrogate that can reconstruct complete state from limited sensors is highly desirable for monitoring and control.
AI.method: Compress the full-order MHD data with SVD to obtain a reduced representation. Train SHRED to map sparse time-series inputs (temperature from three sensors) to the full spatio-temporal state (velocity, pressure, temperature) using the reduced-order alias. Evaluate robustness by testing 30 randomly generated sensor configurations in ensemble mode across a parametric range of magnetic field intensities.
AI.result: SHRED accurately reconstructs the full MHD state from sparse measurements, including parameters (magnetic field intensities) outside the training set. The approach is robust to sensor placement and offers a computationally efficient surrogate, suitable for real-time state estimation in fusion-relevant multiphysics.
AI.conclusion: Combining SVD with SHRED yields an effective, real-time-capable surrogate modeling framework for complex MHD flows, enabling low-cost reconstruction of velocity, pressure, and temperature from limited observations and supporting monitoring/control in fusion systems.

title: Evaluating Few-Shot Pill Recognition Under Visual Domain Shift
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures. Submitted to IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Conference (EMBC) 2026
summary: Adverse drug events are a significant source of preventable harm, which has led to the development of automated pill recognition systems to enhance medication safety. Real-world deployment of these systems is hindered by visually complex conditions, including cluttered scenes, overlapping pills, reflections, and diverse acquisition environments. This study investigates few-shot pill recognition from a deployment-oriented perspective, prioritizing generalization under realistic cross-dataset domain shifts over architectural innovation. A two-stage object detection framework is employed, involving base training followed by few-shot fine-tuning. Models are adapted to novel pill classes using one, five, or ten labeled examples per class and are evaluated on a separate deployment dataset featuring multi-object, cluttered scenes. The evaluation focuses on classification-centric and error-based metrics to address heterogeneous annotation strategies. Findings indicate that semantic pill recognition adapts rapidly with few-shot supervision, with classification performance reaching saturation even with a single labeled example. However, stress testing under overlapping and occluded conditions demonstrates a marked decline in localization and recall, despite robust semantic classification. Models trained on visually realistic, multi-pill data consistently exhibit greater robustness in low-shot scenarios, underscoring the importance of training data realism and the diagnostic utility of few-shot fine-tuning for deployment readiness.

AI.tldr: Few-shot, two-stage pill recognition achieves rapid semantic classification with minimal labeled data but suffers localization under clutter/occlusion; training realism improves robustness, underscoring deployment-focused data and evaluation needs.
AI.motivation: Adverse drug events drive demand for automated pill recognition; real-world deployment is hindered by visually complex scenes and cross-dataset domain shifts, necessitating deployment-oriented evaluation.
AI.method: A two-stage object detection framework with base training followed by few-shot fine-tuning. Novel pill classes are learned with 1, 5, or 10 labeled examples per class and evaluated on a separate deployment dataset featuring multi-object clutter. Evaluation emphasizes classification-centric and error-based metrics to accommodate heterogeneous annotations.
AI.result: Semantic pill recognition adapts rapidly under few-shot supervision, with classification performance saturating even from a single example. However, localization and recall degrade substantially under overlapping and occluded conditions. Models trained on visually realistic, multi-pill data demonstrate greater robustness in low-shot scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Training data realism and the utility of few-shot fine-tuning are critical for deployment readiness. While few-shot learning boosts semantic classification quickly, robust localization under clutter/occlusion remains challenging, highlighting the need for realistic deployment data and cross-dataset generalization.

title: Towards Cold-Start Drafting and Continual Refining: A Value-Driven Memory Approach with Application to NPU Kernel Synthesis
summary: Deploying Large Language Models to data-scarce programming domains poses significant challenges, particularly for kernel synthesis on emerging Domain-Specific Architectures where a "Data Wall" limits available training data. While models excel on data-rich platforms like CUDA, they suffer catastrophic performance drops on data-scarce ecosystems such as NPU programming. To overcome this cold-start barrier without expensive fine-tuning, we introduce EvoKernel, a self-evolving agentic framework that automates the lifecycle of kernel synthesis from initial drafting to continual refining. EvoKernel addresses this by formulating the synthesis process as a memory-based reinforcement learning task. Through a novel value-driven retrieval mechanism, it learns stage-specific Q-values that prioritize experiences based on their contribution to the current objective, whether bootstrapping a feasible draft or iteratively refining latency. Furthermore, by enabling cross-task memory sharing, the agent generalizes insights from simple to complex operators. By building an NPU variant of KernelBench and evaluating on it, EvoKernel improves frontier models' correctness from 11.0% to 83.0% and achieves a median speedup of 3.60x over initial drafts through iterative refinement. This demonstrates that value-guided experience accumulation allows general-purpose models to master the kernel synthesis task on niche hardware ecosystems. Our official page is available at https://evokernel.zhuo.li.

AI.tldr: EvoKernel uses a memory-based reinforcement learning agent with value-guided retrieval to automate kernel synthesis on data-scarce NPUs, enabling cross-task knowledge transfer and delivering large gains in correctness and latency reduction without expensive fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity in niche hardware domains (e.g., NPUs) creating a “Data Wall” that limits training data and hinders performance; aims to bootstrap from drafts to refined kernels without costly fine-tuning.
AI.method: Formulate kernel synthesis as a memory-based RL task. Introduce a value-driven retrieval mechanism that learns stage-specific Q-values to prioritize experiences by their contribution to the current objective, and enable cross-task memory sharing. Build an NPU-specific KernelBench variant and evaluate the framework's ability to bootstrap and refine kernels.
AI.result: Correctness on frontier models improves from 11.0% to 83.0%; median speedup over initial drafts is 3.60x through iterative refinement.
AI.conclusion: Value-guided experience accumulation allows general-purpose models to master kernel synthesis in data-scarce, niche hardware ecosystems and generalize insights across tasks; demonstrated via an NPU-focused KernelBench evaluation.

title: Riemannian MeanFlow for One-Step Generation on Manifolds
summary: Flow Matching enables simulation-free training of generative models on Riemannian manifolds, yet sampling typically still relies on numerically integrating a probability-flow ODE. We propose Riemannian MeanFlow (RMF), extending MeanFlow to manifold-valued generation where velocities lie in location-dependent tangent spaces. RMF defines an average-velocity field via parallel transport and derives a Riemannian MeanFlow identity that links average and instantaneous velocities for intrinsic supervision. We make this identity practical in a log-map tangent representation, avoiding trajectory simulation and heavy geometric computations. For stable optimization, we decompose the RMF objective into two terms and apply conflict-aware multi-task learning to mitigate gradient interference. RMF also supports conditional generation via classifier-free guidance. Experiments on spheres, tori, and SO(3) demonstrate competitive one-step sampling with improved quality-efficiency trade-offs and substantially reduced sampling cost.

AI.tldr: RMF extends MeanFlow to manifold-valued data by transporting velocities along tangent spaces to define an intrinsic mean-velocity field, enabling one-step sampling without trajectory integration; uses a log-map tangent representation and multi-task learning for stable optimization; supports conditional generation via classifier-free guidance; shows competitive quality and notably reduced sampling cost on spheres, tori, and SO(3).
AI.motivation: Flow Matching enables simulation-free training on Riemannian manifolds, but sampling often requires numerical integration of a probability-flow ODE. There is a need for intrinsic, efficient, and geometry-consistent generation on manifolds.
AI.method: Define an average-velocity field via parallel transport of instantaneous velocities in location-dependent tangent spaces; derive a Riemannian MeanFlow identity linking average and instantaneous velocities for intrinsic supervision; implement in a log-map tangent representation to avoid trajectory simulation and heavy geometry; decompose the objective into two terms for stable optimization; apply conflict-aware multi-task learning to mitigate gradient interference; support conditional generation via classifier-free guidance.
AI.result: Experiments on spheres, tori, and SO(3) show competitive one-step sampling with improved quality–efficiency trade-offs and substantially reduced sampling cost.
AI.conclusion: RMF delivers efficient, intrinsic, one-step manifold-valued generation with stable optimization and flexible conditioning, achieving notable reductions in sampling cost across tested manifolds.

title: UltrasoundAgents: Hierarchical Multi-Agent Evidence-Chain Reasoning for Breast Ultrasound Diagnosis
summary: Breast ultrasound diagnosis typically proceeds from global lesion localization to local sign assessment and then evidence integration to assign a BI-RADS category and determine benignity or malignancy. Many existing methods rely on end-to-end prediction or provide only weakly grounded evidence, which can miss fine-grained lesion cues and limit auditability and clinical review. To align with the clinical workflow and improve evidence traceability, we propose a hierarchical multi-agent framework, termed UltrasoundAgents. A main agent localizes the lesion in the full image and triggers a crop-and-zoom operation. A sub-agent analyzes the local view and predicts four clinically relevant attributes, namely echogenicity pattern, calcification, boundary type, and edge (margin) morphology. The main agent then integrates these structured attributes to perform evidence-based reasoning and output the BI-RADS category and the malignancy prediction, while producing reviewable intermediate evidence. Furthermore, hierarchical multi-agent training often suffers from error propagation, difficult credit assignment, and sparse rewards. To alleviate this and improve training stability, we introduce a decoupled progressive training strategy. We first train the attribute agent, then train the main agent with oracle attributes to learn robust attribute-based reasoning, and finally apply corrective trajectory self-distillation with spatial supervision to build high-quality trajectories for supervised fine-tuning, yielding a deployable end-to-end policy. Experiments show consistent gains over strong vision-language baselines in diagnostic accuracy and attribute agreement, together with structured evidence and traceable reasoning.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical multi-agent framework, UltrasoundAgents, for breast ultrasound diagnosis that localizes lesions, crops zoomed views, and predicts four attributes (echogenicity, calcification, boundary type, edge morphology) to enable evidence-based BI-RADS and malignancy predictions, with a decoupled progressive training strategy to improve stability and traceable reasoning.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between end-to-end predictions and clinically actionable, auditable evidence in breast ultrasound; improve interpretation, lesion cue capture, and decision traceability to align with clinical workflow.
AI.method: A two-tier system: a main agent localizes the lesion and triggers crop-and-zoom; a sub-agent analyzes local view and outputs four clinically relevant attributes. The main agent integrates these attributes to produce BI-RADS category and malignancy, with intermediate, reviewable evidence. Training uses a decoupled progressive strategy: (1) train attribute agent, (2) train main agent with oracle attributes, (3) corrective trajectory self-distillation with spatial supervision for end-to-end fine-tuning.
AI.result: Consistent gains over strong vision-language baselines in diagnostic accuracy and attribute agreement, with structured evidence and traceable reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that hierarchical multi-agent reasoning with staged training yields accurate, interpretable BI-RADS predictions and deployable end-to-end policy, improving evidence traceability and training stability.

title: $V_{0.5}$: Generalist Value Model as a Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts
summary: In Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), constructing a robust advantage baseline is critical for policy gradients, effectively guiding the policy model to reinforce desired behaviors. Recent research has introduced Generalist Value Models (such as $V_0$), which achieve pre-trained value estimation by explicitly encoding model capabilities in-context, eliminating the need to synchronously update the value model alongside the policy model. In this paper, we propose $V_{0.5}$, which adaptively fuses the baseline predicted by such value model (acting as a prior) with the empirical mean derived from sparse rollouts. This constructs a robust baseline that balances computational efficiency with extremely low variance. Specifically, we introduce a real-time statistical testing and dynamic budget allocation. This balances the high variance caused by sparse sampling against the systematic bias (or hallucinations) inherent in the value model's prior. By constructing a hypothesis test to evaluate the prior's reliability in real-time, the system dynamically allocates additional rollout budget on demand. This mechanism minimizes the baseline estimator's Mean Squared Error (MSE), guaranteeing stable policy gradients, even under extreme sparsity with a group size of 4. Extensive evaluations across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that $V_{0.5}$ significantly outperforms GRPO and DAPO, achieving faster convergence and over some 10% performance improvement.

AI.tldr: Adaptive baseline V_{0.5} fuses a prior value model with empirical rollouts using real-time hypothesis testing and dynamic rollout budgeting to minimize MSE in policy-gradient RL under sparse data; yields faster convergence and ~10% gains over GRPO and DAPO on six math-reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To reduce baseline estimation variance and bias in policy gradients when using priors from Generalist Value Models, especially under sparse sampling where hallucinations and miscalibration can harm learning.
AI.method: Introduce V_{0.5}, an adaptive fusion of prior value estimates (V_0) with the empirical mean from rollouts. Implement real-time statistical testing to assess prior reliability and dynamically allocate additional rollout budget as needed, minimizing baseline MSE. Evaluate on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks and compare to GRPO and DAPO, including a group size of 4.
AI.result: V_{0.5} achieves faster convergence and around 10% performance improvement over GRPO and DAPO. Demonstrates robust policy gradients under extreme sparsity by reducing baseline estimator MSE through adaptive budgeting guided by real-time hypothesis testing.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive baseline fusion with real-time reliability testing and dynamic budgeting improves stability and efficiency of policy-gradient learning in RL with priors, particularly under sparse sampling.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Reliability and Uncertainty Estimation in Convolutional Neural Networks
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have become integral to a wide range of scientific and practical applications due to their flexibility and strong predictive performance. Despite their accuracy, however, DNNs frequently exhibit poor calibration, often assigning overly confident probabilities to incorrect predictions. This limitation underscores the growing need for integrated mechanisms that provide reliable uncertainty estimation.
