In this article, we compare two prominent approaches for uncertainty quantification: a Bayesian approximation via Monte Carlo Dropout and the nonparametric Conformal Prediction framework. Both methods are assessed using two convolutional neural network architectures; H-CNN VGG16 and GoogLeNet, trained on the Fashion-MNIST dataset. The empirical results show that although H-CNN VGG16 attains higher predictive accuracy, it tends to exhibit pronounced overconfidence, whereas GoogLeNet yields better-calibrated uncertainty estimates. Conformal Prediction additionally demonstrates consistent validity by producing statistically guaranteed prediction sets, highlighting its practical value in high-stakes decision-making contexts. Overall, the findings emphasize the importance of evaluating model performance beyond accuracy alone and contribute to the development of more reliable and trustworthy deep learning systems.

AI.tldr: The study contrasts Monte Carlo Dropout (Bayesian) with Conformal Prediction for uncertainty quantification in CNNs (H-CNN VGG16 and GoogLeNet) trained on Fashion-MNIST. VGG16 achieves higher accuracy but shows overconfidence; GoogLeNet yields better calibration. Conformal Prediction provides valid, guaranteed prediction sets, underscoring calibration importance beyond accuracy.
AI.motivation: DNNs often produce overconfident predictions despite high accuracy, undermining reliability in high-stakes tasks. The work evaluates two prominent UQ approaches—Bayesian approximation via MC Dropout and nonparametric Conformal Prediction—to assess calibration, validity, and practical usefulness on standard CNN architectures.
AI.method: Train two CNN architectures (H-CNN VGG16 and GoogLeNet) on Fashion-MNIST. Apply Monte Carlo Dropout to obtain predictive distributions for Bayesian uncertainty, and apply Conformal Prediction to generate statistically valid prediction sets. Compare accuracy, calibration (e.g., ECE), and the practicality of uncertainty estimates across methods.
AI.result: VGG16/H-CNN delivers higher predictive accuracy but exhibits pronounced overconfidence. GoogLeNet provides better-calibrated uncertainty estimates. Conformal Prediction yields prediction sets with statistical validity (guaranteed coverage), demonstrating practical value for high-stakes decisions.
AI.conclusion: Performance evaluation should extend beyond accuracy to include calibration and validity. Conformal Prediction offers reliable uncertainty quantification with guaranteed coverage, while Bayesian MC Dropout can underperform in calibration on some architectures. The results advocate integrating calibration-focused evaluation for trustworthy deep learning systems.

title: Beyond Sequential Distance: Inter-Modal Distance Invariant Position Encoding
summary: Despite the remarkable capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), they still suffer from visual fading in long-context scenarios. Specifically, the attention to visual tokens diminishes as the text sequence lengthens, leading to text generation detached from visual constraints. We attribute this degradation to the inherent inductive bias of Multimodal RoPE, which penalizes inter-modal attention as the distance between visual and text tokens increases. To address this, we propose inter-modal Distance Invariant Position Encoding (DIPE), a simple but effective mechanism that disentangles position encoding based on modality interactions. DIPE retains the natural relative positioning for intra-modal interactions to preserve local structure, while enforcing an anchored perceptual proximity for inter-modal interactions. This strategy effectively mitigates the inter-modal distance-based penalty, ensuring that visual signals remain perceptually consistent regardless of the context length. Experimental results demonstrate that by integrating DIPE with Multimodal RoPE, the model maintains stable visual grounding in long-context scenarios, significantly alleviating visual fading while preserving performance on standard short-context benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/lchen1019/DIPE.

AI.tldr: DIPE introduces inter-modal Distance Invariant Position Encoding to address visual fading in long-context Multimodal LLMs by decoupling intra- and inter-modal positional encodings, preserving local structure while anchoring inter-modal proximity.
AI.motivation: Visual fading arises because Multimodal RoPE penalizes inter-modal attention with increasing token distance, causing visual tokens to fade as text length grows. The goal is to maintain visual grounding regardless of context length.
AI.method: DIPE disentangles position encoding by modality interactions: keep natural relative pos for intra-modal (preserving local structure) and apply an anchored perceptual proximity for inter-modal interactions to counteract distance-based penalties.
AI.result: When combined with Multimodal RoPE, DIPE preserves visual grounding in long-context scenarios and reduces visual fading, while preserving performance on standard short-context benchmarks; code available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: DIPE effectively mitigates inter-modal distance penalties in Multimodal RoPE configurations, enabling stable visual grounding in long-context generation without sacrificing short-context performance; potential applicability to broader MLLM architectures.

title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows
comment: 37 pages, 1 figure, 15 tables. Three implementations: Python (PyPI: mlw), R (CRAN: ml), Julia. Code: github.com/epagogy/ml
summary: Data leakage affected 294 published papers across 17 scientific fields (Kapoor & Narayanan, 2023). The dominant response has been documentation: checklists, linters, best-practice guides. Documentation does not prevent these failures. This paper proposes a structural remedy: a grammar that decomposes the supervised learning lifecycle into 7 kernel primitives connected by a typed directed acyclic graph (DAG), with four hard constraints that reject the two most damaging leakage classes at call time. The grammar's core contribution is the terminal assess constraint: a runtime-enforced evaluate/assess boundary where repeated test-set assessment is rejected by a guard on a nominally distinct Evidence type. A companion study across 2,047 experimental instances quantifies why this matters: selection leakage inflates performance by d_z = 0.93 and memorization leakage by d_z = 0.53-1.11. Three separate implementations (Python, R, and Julia) confirm the claims. The appendix specification lets anyone build a conforming version.

AI.tldr: Proposes a grammar-based remedy for data leakage in ML by decomposing the lifecycle into 7 kernel primitives connected via a typed DAG, with runtime constraints that reject leakage at call time; empirical evidence shows substantial inflation of performance due to selection and memorization leakage, validated across three language implementations and an open appendix for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Data leakage contaminates published work across sciences; existing responses are documentation-heavy and insufficient to prevent leakage, prompting a structural solution that enforces correct evaluation boundaries at runtime.
AI.method: Introduce a grammar with 7 kernel primitives connected by a typed DAG. Impose four hard constraints at call time, including a terminal assess constraint that enforces a runtime evaluate/assess boundary via a guard on an Evidence type. Conduct a companion study with 2,047 experiments to quantify leakage effects. Validate through three independent implementations (Python, R, Julia) and provide an appendix specification enabling conforming versions.
AI.result: Leakage inflates performance: selection leakage dz=0.93; memorization leakage dz=0.53–1.11. Results replicated across 3 implementations; open appendix enables reproducibility and extension.
AI.conclusion: A grammar-based, runtime-enforced framework directly targets the root causes of data leakage, offering a practical and reproducible remedy beyond traditional documentation and checklists.

title: Bilevel Layer-Positioning LoRA for Real Image Dehazing
summary: Learning-based real image dehazing methods have achieved notable progress, yet they still face adaptation challenges in diverse real haze scenes. These challenges mainly stem from the lack of effective unsupervised mechanisms for unlabeled data and the heavy cost of full model fine-tuning. To address these challenges, we propose the haze-to-clear text-directed loss that leverages CLIP's cross-modal capabilities to reformulate real image dehazing as a semantic alignment problem in latent space, thereby providing explicit unsupervised cross-modal guidance in the absence of reference images. Furthermore, we introduce the Bilevel Layer-positioning LoRA (BiLaLoRA) strategy, which learns both the LoRA parameters and automatically search the injection layers, enabling targeted adaptation of critical network layers. Extensive experiments demonstrate our superiority against state-of-the-art methods on multiple real-world dehazing benchmarks. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/YanZhang-zy/BiLaLoRA.

AI.tldr: Proposes CLIP-guided unsupervised dehazing via haze-to-clear text-directed loss and a bilevel, layer-positioning LoRA (BiLaLoRA) for selective network adaptation, achieving SOTA on real-world hazy images with no labeled references.
AI.motivation: Addresses adaptation challenges in real image dehazing: unlabeled data and high fine-tuning cost; seeks unsupervised guidance and parameter-efficient adaptation.
AI.method: Twofold: (1) haze-to-clear text-directed loss that uses CLIP to align latent representations with semantic text guidance for dehazing without references; (2) BiLaLoRA, a bilevel LoRA approach that jointly learns LoRA parameters and searches injection layers to adapt critical network parts efficiently.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show superiority over state-of-the-art on multiple real-world dehazing benchmarks; code released.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides effective unsupervised cross-modal guidance and targeted, parameter-efficient adaptation, advancing real-world dehazing performance.

title: Continuous Diffusion Transformers for Designing Synthetic Regulatory Elements
summary: We present a parameter-efficient Diffusion Transformer (DiT) for generating 200bp cell-type-specific regulatory DNA sequences. By replacing the U-Net backbone of DNA-Diffusion with a transformer denoiser equipped with a 2D CNN input encoder, our model matches the U-Net's best validation loss in 13 epochs (60$\times$ fewer) and converges 39% lower, while reducing memorization from 5.3% to 1.7% of generated sequences aligning to training data via BLAT. Ablations show the CNN encoder is essential: without it, validation loss increases 70% regardless of positional embedding choice. We further apply DDPO finetuning using Enformer as a reward model, achieving a 38$\times$ improvement in predicted regulatory activity. Cross-validation against DRAKES on an independent prediction task confirms that improvements reflect genuine regulatory signal rather than reward model overfitting.

AI.tldr: A parameter-efficient Diffusion Transformer (DiT) replaces the U-Net in DNA-Diffusion with a transformer denoiser that uses a 2D CNN input encoder to generate 200bp cell-type-specific regulatory DNA sequences. It matches the U-Net's best validation loss in 13 epochs (60x faster) and converges 39% lower, while reducing memorization from 5.3% to 1.7% (via BLAT). Ablations show the CNN encoder is essential. DDPO finetuning with Enformer as reward yields 38x improvement in predicted regulatory activity. Cross-validation against DRAKES shows improvements reflect true regulatory signal rather than reward-model overfitting.
AI.motivation: To create a parameter- and data-efficient diffusion-based generator for regulatory DNA sequences, addressing training efficiency, memorization, and the ability to capture authentic regulatory signals without overfitting to training data.
AI.method: Replace the U-Net backbone of DNA-Diffusion with a transformer-based denoiser, equipped with a 2D CNN input encoder. Train with diffusion objectives; perform ablations removing the CNN encoder. Apply DDPO fine-tuning using Enformer as a reward model. Evaluate memorization with BLAT and validate regulatory activity predictions, including cross-validation with DRAKES on an independent task.
AI.result: The DiT model matches the U-Net best validation loss in 13 epochs (60× faster) and converges 39% lower; memorization of training data reduced from 5.3% to 1.7%. Ablations show the CNN encoder is essential. DDPO fine-tuning with Enformer reward yields a 38× improvement in predicted regulatory activity. Cross-validation with DRAKES suggests improvements reflect genuine regulatory signal rather than reward-model overfitting.
AI.conclusion: A compact, transformer-based denoiser with a 2D CNN encoder yields substantial gains in training efficiency and generalization for diffusion-based regulatory DNA sequence generation, and reward-model-guided fine-tuning further boosts functional predictions while maintaining signal integrity.

title: S2D: Sparse to Dense Lifting for 3D Reconstruction with Minimal Inputs
summary: Explicit 3D representations have already become an essential medium for 3D simulation and understanding. However, the most commonly used point cloud and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) each suffer from non-photorealistic rendering and significant degradation under sparse inputs. In this paper, we introduce Sparse to Dense lifting (S2D), a novel pipeline that bridges the two representations and achieves high-quality 3DGS reconstruction with minimal inputs. Specifically, the S2D lifting is two-fold. We first present an efficient one-step diffusion model that lifts sparse point cloud for high-fidelity image artifact fixing. Meanwhile, to reconstruct 3D consistent scenes, we also design a corresponding reconstruction strategy with random sample drop and weighted gradient for robust model fitting from sparse input views to dense novel views. Extensive experiments show that S2D achieves the best consistency in generating novel view guidance and first-tier sparse view reconstruction quality under different input sparsity. By reconstructing stable scenes with the least possible captures among existing methods, S2D enables minimal input requirements for 3DGS applications.

AI.tldr: S2D introduces Sparse to Dense lifting for 3D Gaussian Splatting, using a single-step diffusion model to lift sparse point clouds and a robust reconstruction strategy with random sample dropping and weighted gradients to achieve high-quality, consistent 3D reconstructions from minimal inputs.
AI.motivation: To overcome non-photorealistic rendering and degradation under sparse inputs in existing explicit 3D representations (point clouds and 3D Gaussian Splatting), by bridging sparse data and dense 3D representations to enable reliable reconstruction from few views.
AI.method: 1) A one-step diffusion model lifts sparse point clouds to suppress artifacts and produce high-fidelity dense representations; 2) A reconstruction strategy uses random sample drop and weighted gradient to robustly fit a 3D consistent scene from sparse input views to dense novel views, ensuring consistency across views.
AI.result: Experiments show S2D achieves the best view-consistency for novel-view guidance and high-quality sparse-view reconstructions across varying input sparsity, enabling stable 3DGS reconstructions with minimal captures.
AI.conclusion: S2D effectively bridges sparse inputs and dense 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling high-quality, consistent 3D reconstructions with minimal input, and sets a new standard for sparse-to-dense 3D reconstruction pipelines.

title: Dynamics-Predictive Sampling for Active RL Finetuning of Large Reasoning Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) finetuning has become a key technique for enhancing the reasoning abilities of large language models (LLMs). However, its effectiveness critically depends on the selection of training data. Recent advances underscore the importance of online prompt selection methods, which typically concentrate training on partially solved or moderately challenging examples under the current policy, thereby yielding more effective model updates. While significantly accelerating RL finetuning in terms of training steps, they also incur substantial computational overhead by requiring extensive LLM rollouts over large candidate batches to identify informative samples, an expense that can outweigh the finetuning process itself. To address this challenge, this work proposes Dynamics-Predictive Sampling (DPS), which online predicts and selects informative prompts by inferring their learning dynamics prior to costly rollouts. Specifically, we introduce a new perspective by modeling each prompt's solving progress during RL finetuning as a dynamical system, where the extent of solving is represented as the state and the transition is characterized by a hidden Markov model. Using historical rollout reward signals, we perform online Bayesian inference to estimate evolving state distributions, and the inference outcome provides a predictive prior for efficient prompt selection without rollout-intensive filtering. Empirical results across diverse reasoning tasks, including mathematics, planning, and visual geometry, demonstrate that DPS substantially reduces redundant rollouts, accelerates the training process, and achieves superior reasoning performance.

AI.tldr: DPS uses online Bayesian inference on a dynamical-system model of prompt-solving progress to select informative prompts, reducing costly rollouts and speeding RL finetuning of LLMs while improving reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: RL finetuning data selection boosts efficiency, but current online prompt selection requires many LLM rollouts over large candidate sets, making training expensive.
AI.method: Model each prompt's progress as a state in a hidden Markov dynamic system; use historical rollout rewards to perform online Bayesian inference of evolving state distributions, yielding a predictive prior to select prompts without expensive rollouts.
AI.result: DPS reduces redundant rollouts, accelerates training, and improves reasoning performance across math, planning, and visual geometry tasks.
AI.conclusion: DPS provides a data-efficient prompt-selection mechanism for RL finetuning, cutting computation while maintaining or enhancing performance across domains.

title: Prioritizing Gradient Sign Over Modulus: An Importance-Aware Framework for Wireless Federated Learning
summary: Wireless federated learning (FL) facilitates collaborative training of artificial intelligence (AI) models to support ubiquitous intelligent applications at the wireless edge. However, the inherent constraints of limited wireless resources inevitably lead to unreliable communication, which poses a significant challenge to wireless FL. To overcome this challenge, we propose Sign-Prioritized FL (SP-FL), a novel framework that improves wireless FL by prioritizing the transmission of important gradient information through uneven resource allocation. Specifically, recognizing the importance of descent direction in model updating, we transmit gradient signs in individual packets and allow their reuse for gradient descent if the remaining gradient modulus cannot be correctly recovered. To further improve the reliability of transmission of important information, we formulate a hierarchical resource allocation problem based on the importance disparity at both the packet and device levels, optimizing bandwidth allocation across multiple devices and power allocation between sign and modulus packets. To make the problem tractable, the one-step convergence behavior of SP-FL, which characterizes data importance at both levels in an explicit form, is analyzed. We then propose an alternating optimization algorithm to solve this problem using the Newton-Raphson method and successive convex approximation (SCA). Simulation results confirm the superiority of SP-FL, especially in resource-constrained scenarios, demonstrating up to 9.96\% higher testing accuracy on the CIFAR-10 dataset compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: Sign-Prioritized FL (SP-FL) transmits gradient signs to improve wireless federated learning under tight resources; uses hierarchical resource allocation across devices and packets; analyzes one-step convergence; demonstrates up to ~10% accuracy gain on CIFAR-10 vs baselines; solved via Newton-Raphson and SCA.
AI.motivation: Wireless FL is hampered by unreliable communication due to limited wireless resources. There is a need to prioritize transmission of critical gradient information to improve learning performance under resource constraints.
AI.method: SP-FL transmits gradient signs in packets and reuses them for descent if gradients' moduli cannot be accurately recovered. It introduces a hierarchical resource allocation problem at packet and device levels, optimizing bandwidth across devices and power allocation between sign and modulus packets. For tractability, it analyzes the one-step convergence behavior and designs an alternating optimization algorithm using Newton-Raphson and successive convex approximation (SCA).
AI.result: SP-FL yields superior performance in resource-constrained scenarios with up to 9.96% higher testing accuracy on CIFAR-10 compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Prioritizing gradient signs and hierarchical resource allocation can substantially improve wireless FL under resource constraints; the one-step convergence analysis supports the design, and the proposed optimization algorithm effectively allocates resources to critical information.

title: Novel Architecture of RPA In Oral Cancer Lesion Detection
summary: Accurate and early detection of oral cancer lesions is crucial for effective diagnosis and treatment. This study evaluates two RPA implementations, OC-RPAv1 and OC-RPAv2, using a test set of 31 images. OC-RPAv1 processes one image per prediction in an average of 0.29 seconds, while OCRPAv2 employs a Singleton design pattern and batch processing, reducing prediction time to just 0.06 seconds per image. This represents a 60-100x efficiency improvement over standard RPA methods, showcasing that design patterns and batch processing can enhance scalability and reduce costs in oral cancer detection

AI.tldr: OC-RPAv1 and OC-RPAv2 for oral cancer detection; v2 uses Singleton and batch processing to achieve 0.06s per image, 60-100x faster than standard RPA, indicating design patterns boost scalability and reduce costs.
AI.motivation: Early and accurate detection of oral cancer requires fast, scalable inference; evaluating design-pattern-based implementations can reveal efficiency gains for practical deployment.
AI.method: Evaluate two RPA implementations on a test set of 31 images. OC-RPAv1 processes one image per prediction (0.29s). OC-RPAv2 adopts a Singleton pattern and batch processing to achieve 0.06s per image.
AI.result: OC-RPAv2 significantly accelerates predictions (0.06s) compared with OC-RPAv1 (0.29s), implying 60-100x efficiency over standard RPA methods.
AI.conclusion: Employing design patterns and batch processing can substantially improve throughput and reduce costs in oral cancer lesion detection, enabling scalable, real-time or high-throughput screening.

title: LookaheadKV: Fast and Accurate KV Cache Eviction by Glimpsing into the Future without Generation
summary: Transformer-based large language models (LLMs) rely on key-value (KV) caching to avoid redundant computation during autoregressive inference. While this mechanism greatly improves efficiency, the cache size grows linearly with the input sequence length, quickly becoming a bottleneck for long-context tasks. Existing solutions mitigate this problem by evicting prompt KV that are deemed unimportant, guided by estimated importance scores. Notably, a recent line of work proposes to improve eviction quality by "glimpsing into the future", in which a draft generator produces a surrogate future response approximating the target model's true response, and this surrogate is subsequently used to estimate the importance of cached KV more accurately. However, these approaches rely on computationally expensive draft generation, which introduces substantial prefilling overhead and limits their practicality in real-world deployment. To address this challenge, we propose LookaheadKV, a lightweight eviction framework that leverages the strength of surrogate future response without requiring explicit draft generation. LookaheadKV augments transformer layers with parameter-efficient modules trained to predict true importance scores with high accuracy. Our design ensures negligible runtime overhead comparable to existing inexpensive heuristics, while achieving accuracy superior to more costly approximation methods. Extensive experiments on long-context understanding benchmarks, across a wide range of models, demonstrate that our method not only outperforms recent competitive baselines in various long-context understanding tasks, but also reduces the eviction cost by up to 14.5x, leading to significantly faster time-to-first-token. Our code is available at https://github.com/SamsungLabs/LookaheadKV.

AI.tldr: LookaheadKV trains lightweight, parameter-efficient modules to predict true KV eviction importance without draft generation, outperforming expensive future-glimpse methods while adding negligible overhead and significantly reducing eviction costs (up to 14.5x).
AI.motivation: KV caching in transformer LLMs grows with input length, creating a bottleneck for long-context tasks. Existing eviction methods either rely on simple heuristics or expensive draft-generation-based “glimpsing into the future,” which incurs substantial prefilling overhead. A practical method needs higher eviction accuracy with minimal runtime impact.
AI.method: Integrate parameter-efficient modules into transformer layers and train them to predict true KV eviction importance scores directly, avoiding explicit draft generation. The approach aims for high prediction accuracy with negligible runtime overhead, enabling more efficient eviction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on long-context understanding benchmarks across diverse models show LookaheadKV outperforms recent baselines in long-context tasks and reduces eviction cost by up to 14.5x, leading to faster time-to-first-token.
AI.conclusion: LookaheadKV provides a practical, accurate eviction solution that matches or surpasses costly “future-aware” methods while maintaining low overhead, enabling faster long-context inference. Code is released at the provided GitHub link.

title: Dynamics-Informed Deep Learning for Predicting Extreme Events
summary: Predicting extreme events in high-dimensional chaotic dynamical systems remains a fundamental challenge, as such events are rare, intermittent, and arise from transient dynamical mechanisms that are difficult to infer from limited observations. Accordingly, real-time forecasting calls for precursors that encode the mechanisms driving extremes, rather than relying solely on statistical associations. We propose a fully data-driven framework for long-lead prediction of extreme events that constructs interpretable, mechanism-aware precursors by explicitly tracking transient instabilities preceding event onset. The approach leverages a reduced-order formulation to compute finite-time Lyapunov exponent (FTLE)-like precursors directly from state snapshots, without requiring knowledge of the governing equations. To avoid the prohibitive computational cost of classical FTLE computation, instability growth is evaluated in an adaptively evolving low-dimensional subspace spanned by Optimal Time-Dependent (OTD) modes, enabling efficient identification of transiently amplifying directions. These precursors are then provided as input to a Transformer-based model, enabling forecast of extreme event observables. We demonstrate the framework on Kolmogorov flow, a canonical model of intermittent turbulence. The results show that explicitly encoding transient instability mechanisms substantially extends practical prediction horizons compared to baseline observable-based approaches.

AI.tldr: A data-driven framework for long-lead extreme-event forecasting using FTLE-like precursors computed in a reduced OTD subspace, fed into a Transformer; demonstrated on Kolmogorov flow with improved prediction horizons.
AI.motivation: Extreme events in high-dimensional chaotic systems are rare, driven by transient instabilities, and hard to predict with purely statistical approaches. Interpretable, mechanism-aware precursors are needed for reliable forecasting.
AI.method: Compute finite-time Lyapunov exponent–like precursors within an adaptively evolving low-dimensional subspace spanned by Optimal Time-Dependent (OTD) modes, avoiding full FTLE costs. Use these instability-based precursors as inputs to a Transformer model to forecast extreme-event observables.
AI.result: Demonstrated on Kolmogorov flow; explicitly encoding transient instability mechanisms substantially extends practical prediction horizons over baseline observable-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Encoding transient instability mechanisms in a reduced-order, data-driven framework enables interpretable, mechanism-aware long-lead forecasts of extreme events in high-dimensional chaotic systems.

title: Bridging the Skill Gap in Clinical CBCT Interpretation with CBCTRepD
summary: Generative AI has advanced rapidly in medical report generation; however, its application to oral and maxillofacial CBCT reporting remains limited, largely because of the scarcity of high-quality paired CBCT-report data and the intrinsic complexity of volumetric CBCT interpretation. To address this, we introduce CBCTRepD, a bilingual oral and maxillofacial CBCT report-generation system designed for integration into routine radiologist-AI co-authoring workflows. We curated a large-scale, high-quality paired CBCT-report dataset comprising approximately 7,408 studies, covering 55 oral disease entities across diverse acquisition settings, and used it to develop the system. We further established a clinically grounded, multi-level evaluation framework that assesses both direct AI-generated drafts and radiologist-edited collaboration reports using automatic metrics together with radiologist- and clinician-centered evaluation. Using this framework, we show that CBCTRepD achieves superior report-generation performance and produces drafts with writing quality and standardization comparable to those of intermediate radiologists. More importantly, in radiologist-AI collaboration, CBCTRepD provides consistent and clinically meaningful benefits across experience levels: it helps novice radiologists improve toward intermediate-level reporting, enables intermediate radiologists to approach senior-level performance, and even assists senior radiologists by reducing omission-related errors, including clinically important missed lesions. By improving report structure, reducing omissions, and promoting attention to co-existing lesions across anatomical regions, CBCTRepD shows strong and reliable potential as a practical assistant for real-world CBCT reporting across multi-level care settings.

AI.tldr: A bilingual CBCT report-generation system (CBCTRepD) with a 7.4k-study paired dataset and multi-level evaluation demonstrates that radiologist-AI collaboration improves report quality and reduces omissions across experience levels.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of high-quality paired CBCT-report data and the complexity of volumetric CBCT interpretation hindering AI-assisted reporting.
AI.method: Curated a large-scale paired CBCT-report dataset (~7,408 studies, 55 diseases), developed a bilingual reporting system CBCTRepD, and established a multi-level evaluation framework combining automatic metrics and radiologist/clinician assessments to evaluate AI drafts and radiologist-AI collaboration.
AI.result: CBCTRepD yields superior report-generation performance; drafts comparable to intermediate radiologists; collaboration benefits observed across novice, intermediate, and senior radiologists; reduction in omission-related errors and missed lesions; improved structure and attention to co-existing lesions.
AI.conclusion: CBCTRepD has strong potential as a practical assistant for real-world CBCT reporting across multi-level care settings, enabling effective radiologist-AI co-authoring workflows.

title: Safe RLHF Beyond Expectation: Stochastic Dominance for Universal Spectral Risk Control
summary: Safe Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) typically enforces safety through expected cost constraints, but the expectation captures only a single statistic of the cost distribution and fails to account for distributional uncertainty, particularly under heavy tails or rare catastrophic events. This limitation is problematic when robustness and risk sensitivity are critical. Stochastic dominance offers a principled alternative by comparing entire cost distributions rather than just their averages, enabling direct control over tail risks and potential out-of-distribution failures that expectation-based constraints may overlook. In this work, we propose Risk-sensitive Alignment via Dominance (RAD), a novel alignment framework that replaces scalar expected cost constraints with First-Order Stochastic Dominance (FSD) constraints. We operationalize this constraint by comparing the target policy's cost distribution to that of a reference policy within an Optimal Transport (OT) framework, using entropic regularization and Sinkhorn iterations to obtain a differentiable and computationally efficient objective for stable end-to-end optimization. Furthermore, we introduce quantile-weighted FSD constraints and show that weighted FSD universally controls a broad class of Spectral Risk Measures (SRMs), so that improvements under weighted dominance imply guaranteed improvements in the corresponding spectral risk. This provides a principled mechanism for tuning a model's risk profile via the quantile weighting function. Empirical results demonstrate that RAD improves harmlessness over baselines while remaining competitive in helpfulness, and exhibits greater robustness on out-of-distribution harmlessness evaluations.

AI.tldr: RAD replaces scalar expected-cost constraints in RLHF with First-Order Stochastic Dominance (FSD) constraints, implemented via an Optimal Transport (OT) framework with entropic regularization (Sinkhorn). It adds quantile-weighted FSD constraints that universally control Spectral Risk Measures (SRMs), enabling tunable risk sensitivity. Empirically, RAD improves harmlessness and OOD robustness while remaining competitive in helpfulness.
AI.motivation: Limitations of expectation-based safety constraints in RLHF: they summarize the cost distribution with a single statistic, failing to capture distributional uncertainty, heavy tails, and rare catastrophic events. There is a need for risk-sensitive control that accounts for the full distribution, particularly for robustness and safety in deployment.
AI.method: Replace scalar expected-cost constraints with First-Order Stochastic Dominance constraints by comparing the target policy's cost distribution to a reference policy within an Optimal Transport framework. Use entropic regularization and Sinkhorn iterations to obtain a differentiable, computationally efficient objective suitable for end-to-end optimization. Introduce quantile-weighted FSD constraints and show that weighted FSD controls a broad class of Spectral Risk Measures, enabling tunable risk profiles via the quantile weighting function.
AI.result: Empirically, RAD improves harmlessness relative to baselines while remaining competitive in helpfulness and exhibits greater robustness on out-of-distribution harmlessness evaluations.
AI.conclusion: A principled mechanism to tune risk via quantile-weighted FSD within an OT framework yields improved safety under distributional uncertainty; FSD-based constraints ensure improvements translate to a broad class of spectral risks, and the framework provides stable, differentiable optimization for RLHF safety.

title: Pointy - A Lightweight Transformer for Point Cloud Foundation Models
comment: To appear in the proceedings of ACIVS 2025. An earlier version was presented at the SCI-FM workshop at ICLR 2025
summary: Foundation models for point cloud data have recently grown in capability, often leveraging extensive representation learning from language or vision. In this work, we take a more controlled approach by introducing a lightweight transformer-based point cloud architecture. In contrast to the heavy reliance on cross-modal supervision, our model is trained only on 39k point clouds - yet it outperforms several larger foundation models trained on over 200k training samples. Interestingly, our method approaches state-of-the-art results from models that have seen over a million point clouds, images, and text samples, demonstrating the value of a carefully curated training setup and architecture. To ensure rigorous evaluation, we conduct a comprehensive replication study that standardizes the training regime and benchmarks across multiple point cloud architectures. This unified experimental framework isolates the impact of architectural choices, allowing for transparent comparisons and highlighting the benefits of our design and other tokenizer-free architectures. Our results show that simple backbones can deliver competitive results to more complex or data-rich strategies. The implementation, including code, pre-trained models, and training protocols, is available at https://github.com/KonradSzafer/Pointy.

AI.tldr: A compact transformer-based point cloud model trained on 39k samples outperforms larger models and nears the top models trained on far more data; a standardized replication study highlights the value of simple backbones and tokenizer-free design.
AI.motivation: Question whether massive data and cross-modal supervision are strictly necessary for strong point cloud foundation models; explore data-efficient, architecture-focused approach.
AI.method: Propose a lightweight transformer-based point cloud architecture trained on 39k point clouds; compare with larger models; conduct a unified replication study across multiple point cloud architectures under standardized training; emphasize tokenizer-free design.
AI.result: Outperforms several larger foundation models trained on >200k samples; approaches state-of-the-art among models trained on >1M samples; demonstrates that carefully designed, data-efficient backbones can be competitive.
AI.conclusion: Supports data-efficiency and architecture choice as key drivers; tokenizer-free backbones deliver competitive performance; contributes to reproducibility with unified evaluation and public code.

title: Evaluating randomized smoothing as a defense against adversarial attacks in trajectory prediction
summary: Accurate and robust trajectory prediction is essential for safe and efficient autonomous driving, yet recent work has shown that even state-of-the-art prediction models are highly vulnerable to inputs being mildly perturbed by adversarial attacks. Although model vulnerabilities to such attacks have been studied, work on effective countermeasures remains limited. In this work, we develop and evaluate a new defense mechanism for trajectory prediction models based on randomized smoothing -- an approach previously applied successfully in other domains. We evaluate its ability to improve model robustness through a series of experiments that test different strategies of randomized smoothing. We show that our approach can consistently improve prediction robustness of multiple base trajectory prediction models in various datasets without compromising accuracy in non-adversarial settings. Our results demonstrate that randomized smoothing offers a simple and computationally inexpensive technique for mitigating adversarial attacks in trajectory prediction.

AI.tldr: Randomized smoothing defense improves robustness of trajectory prediction models to adversarial perturbations across multiple models and datasets, with no loss in clean accuracy; simple and efficient.
AI.motivation: Trajectory prediction models in autonomous driving are vulnerable to mild adversarial perturbations; there is a need for practical defenses that preserve performance in non-adversarial settings.
AI.method: Apply randomized smoothing as a defense for trajectory prediction. Evaluate via experiments with various smoothing strategies on multiple base models and datasets; assess robustness against adversarial inputs and verify clean accuracy remains high.
AI.result: The approach consistently improves robustness across base models and datasets and does not degrade accuracy on non-adversarial data; smoothing is simple and computationally inexpensive.
AI.conclusion: Randomized smoothing is a viable, simple, and efficient defense for mitigating adversarial attacks in trajectory prediction, scalable for practical deployment.

title: Contrastive learning-based video quality assessment-jointed video vision transformer for video recognition
summary: Video quality significantly affects video classification. We found this problem when we classified Mild Cognitive Impairment well from clear videos, but worse from blurred ones. From then, we realized that referring to Video Quality Assessment (VQA) may improve video classification. This paper proposed Self-Supervised Learning-based Video Vision Transformer combined with No-reference VQA for video classification (SSL-V3) to fulfill the goal. SSL-V3 leverages Combined-SSL mechanism to join VQA into video classification and address the label shortage of VQA, which commonly occurs in video datasets, making it impossible to provide an accurate Video Quality Score. In brief, Combined-SSL takes video quality score as a factor to directly tune the feature map of the video classification. Then, the score, as an intersected point, links VQA and classification, using the supervised classification task to tune the parameters of VQA. SSL-V3 achieved robust experimental results on two datasets. For example, it reached an accuracy of 94.87% on some interview videos in the I-CONECT (a facial video-involved healthcare dataset), verifying SSL-V3's effectiveness.

AI.tldr: SSL-V3 combines Self-Supervised Video Vision Transformer with No-reference Video Quality Assessment through a Combined-SSL mechanism that uses video quality scores to modulate feature maps and jointly tunes VQA and classification, achieving high accuracy (94.87%) on I-CONECT interview videos.
AI.motivation: Video quality substantially impacts video classification performance. Observations on Mild Cognitive Impairment classification showed performance drop on blurred videos, motivating integration of Video Quality Assessment (VQA) to boost classification reliability, especially under label scarcity for VQA.
AI.method: A Self-Supervised Learning-based Video Vision Transformer (SSL-V3) is proposed, integrating No-reference VQA via a Combined-SSL strategy. Combined-SSL uses the predicted/estimated video quality score as a factor to directly adjust the feature maps of the video classifier, while the same score serves as a bridge that links VQA and classification, enabling the supervised classification task to fine-tune the VQA parameters.
AI.result: The approach yields robust results on two datasets, including 94.87% accuracy on interview videos in the I-CONECT healthcare dataset, indicating the method’s effectiveness in leveraging VQA for video classification under challenging conditions.
AI.conclusion: Combined-SSL successfully couples VQA with video classification by using video quality as an intermediate factor, addressing label scarcity for VQA and improving robustness to video quality variation, demonstrated by strong performance on healthcare-related video datasets.

title: GroundCount: Grounding Vision-Language Models with Object Detection for Mitigating Counting Hallucinations
summary: Vision Language Models (VLMs) exhibit persistent hallucinations in counting tasks, with accuracy substantially lower than other visual reasoning tasks (excluding sentiment). This phenomenon persists even in state-of-the-art reasoning-capable VLMs. Conversely, CNN-based object detection models (ODMs) such as YOLO excel at spatial localization and instance counting with minimal computational overhead. We propose GroundCount, a framework that augments VLMs with explicit spatial grounding from ODMs to mitigate counting hallucinations. In the best case, our prompt-based augmentation strategy achieves 81.3% counting accuracy on the best-performing model (Ovis2.5-2B) - a 6.6pp improvement - while reducing inference time by 22% through elimination of hallucination-driven reasoning loops for stronger models. We conduct comprehensive ablation studies demonstrating that positional encoding is a critical component, being beneficial for stronger models but detrimental for weaker ones. Confidence scores, by contrast, introduce noise for most architectures and their removal improves performance in four of five evaluated models. We further evaluate feature-level fusion architectures, finding that explicit symbolic grounding via structured prompts outperforms implicit feature fusion despite sophisticated cross-attention mechanisms. Our approach yields consistent improvements across four of five evaluated VLM architectures (6.2--7.5pp), with one architecture exhibiting degraded performance due to incompatibility between its iterative reflection mechanisms and structured prompts. These results suggest that counting failures stem from fundamental spatial-semantic integration limitations rather than architecture-specific deficiencies, while highlighting the importance of architectural compatibility in augmentation strategies.

AI.tldr: GroundCount augments VLMs with explicit spatial grounding from object detectors to reduce counting hallucinations, yielding notable accuracy and speed gains across several architectures; positional encoding is crucial, confidence scores may add noise, and structured prompts outperform implicit fusion.
AI.motivation: Persistent counting hallucinations in VLMs despite advanced reasoning; ODMs excel at spatial localization; integrate to improve counting reliability and efficiency.
AI.method: Prompt-based augmentation with explicit visual grounding from CNN-based ODMs; experiments include positional encoding, confidence scoring, and comparisons with feature-level fusion; ablations across five VLM architectures.
AI.result: Best-case 81.3% counting accuracy on Ovis2.5-2B with 6.6pp improvement; 22% faster inference by bypassing hallucination-driven reasoning loops; four of five architectures show 6.2-7.5pp gains; one architecture degrades due to incompatibility with iterative reflection mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Counting failures arise from spatial-semantic integration limitations rather than model-specific deficits; architectural compatibility is crucial for augmentation strategies; explicit symbolic grounding via structured prompts is effective.

title: Med-DualLoRA: Local Adaptation of Foundation Models for 3D Cardiac MRI
summary: Foundation models (FMs) show great promise for robust downstream performance across medical imaging tasks and modalities, including cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR), following task-specific adaptation. However, adaptation using single-site data may lead to suboptimal performance and increased model bias, while centralized fine-tuning on clinical data is often infeasible due to privacy constraints. Federated fine-tuning offers a privacy-preserving alternative; yet conventional approaches struggle under heterogeneous, non-IID multi-center data and incur substantial communication overhead when adapting large models. In this work, we study federated FM fine-tuning for 3D CMR disease detection and propose Med-DualLoRA, a client-aware parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) federated framework that disentangles globally shared and local low-rank adaptations (LoRA) through additive decomposition. Global and local LoRA modules are trained locally, but only the global component is shared and aggregated across sites, keeping local adapters private. This design improves personalization while significantly reducing communication cost, and experiments show that adapting only two transformer blocks preserves performance while further improving efficiency. We evaluate our method on a multi-center state-of-the-art cine 3D CMR FM fine-tuned for disease detection using ACDC and combined M\&Ms datasets, treating each vendor as a federated client. Med-DualLoRA achieves statistically significant improved performance (balanced accuracy 0.768, specificity 0.612) compared to other federated PEFT baselines, while maintaining communication efficiency. Our approach provides a scalable solution for local federated adaptation of medical FMs under realistic clinical constraints.

AI.tldr: Med-DualLoRA: a federated, parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework for 3D CMR disease detection that decomposes LoRA adapters into global and local components. Only the global LoRA is aggregated across sites to reduce communication, while local adapters are kept private, enabling personalization. Adapting only two transformer blocks yields strong performance with reduced overhead, outperforming other federated PEFT baselines on multi-center datasets (ACDC and M&Ms).
AI.motivation: Federated fine-tuning of large medical foundation models is needed to preserve patient privacy and handle non-IID, multi-center data. Conventional federated fine-tuning suffers from scalability issues (high communication cost) and performance degradation due to data heterogeneity when adapting full models.
AI.method: Introduce a client-aware PEFT approach (Med-DualLoRA) that decouples globally shared and locally adaptive LoRA modules via additive decomposition. Train both global and local LoRA locally; only the global component is shared/aggregated across sites. Limit active adaptation to a small number of transformer blocks (two). Evaluate in a federated setting with vendors as clients on cine 3D CMR data (ACDC and M&Ms), comparing against other federated PEFT baselines.
AI.result: Med-DualLoRA achieves statistically significant improvements in balanced accuracy (0.768) and specificity (0.612) over other federated PEFT baselines, while maintaining lower communication overhead due to sharing only the global LoRA. Adapting only two transformer blocks preserves performance and enhances efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, privacy-preserving federated fine-tuning framework for medical foundation models that balances personalization and communication efficiency. The approach is applicable to multi-center settings with heterogeneous data and could generalize to other medical imaging tasks and modalities.

title: Does AI See like Art Historians? Interpreting How Vision Language Models Recognize Artistic Style
summary: VLMs have become increasingly proficient at a range of computer vision tasks, such as visual question answering and object detection. This includes increasingly strong capabilities in the domain of art, from analyzing artwork to generation of art. In an interdisciplinary collaboration between computer scientists and art historians, we characterize the mechanisms underlying VLMs' ability to predict artistic style and assess the extent to which they align with the criteria art historians use to reason about artistic style. We employ a latent-space decomposition approach to identify concepts that drive art style prediction and conduct quantitative evaluations, causal analysis and assessment by art historians. Our findings indicate that 73% of the extracted concepts are judged by art historians to exhibit a coherent and semantically meaningful visual feature and 90% of concepts used to predict style of a given artwork were judged relevant. In cases where an irrelevant concept was used to successfully predict style, art historians identified possible reasons for its success; for example, the model might "understand" a concept in more formal terms, such as dark/light contrasts.

AI.tldr: VLMs' ability to predict artistic style is analyzed via latent-space decomposition, revealing concepts driving style prediction; high alignment with art-historical criteria, with most concepts deemed coherent and relevant; some predictions arise from more formal or low-level features.
AI.motivation: Understand the mechanisms by which vision-language models predict artistic style and assess whether these mechanisms align with art historians' reasoning, bridging ML interpretability with humanities.
AI.method: Latent-space decomposition to extract concepts that drive style prediction, followed by quantitative evaluation, causal analysis, and assessment by art historians of the concepts.
AI.result: 73% of extracted concepts were judged by art historians to exhibit coherent and semantically meaningful visual features; 90% of concepts used to predict the style of a given artwork were judged relevant. When an irrelevant concept predicted style, reasons included the model leveraging more formal or low-level features (e.g., dark/light contrasts).
AI.conclusion: The approach reveals substantial alignment between VLM-driven style predictions and art-historical reasoning for many concepts, while also highlighting cases where the model relies on interpretable-but-orthogonal cues; underscores potential for improving interpretability and alignment with domain criteria.

title: VCR: Variance-Driven Channel Recalibration for Robust Low-Light Enhancement
summary: Most sRGB-based LLIE methods suffer from entangled luminance and color, while the HSV color space offers insufficient decoupling at the cost of introducing significant red and black noise artifacts. Recently, the HVI color space has been proposed to address these limitations by enhancing color fidelity through chrominance polarization and intensity compression. However, existing methods could suffer from channel-level inconsistency between luminance and chrominance, and misaligned color distribution may lead to unnatural enhancement results. To address these challenges, we propose the Variance-Driven Channel Recalibration for Robust Low-Light Enhancement (VCR), a novel framework for low-light image enhancement. VCR consists of two main components, including the Channel Adaptive Adjustment (CAA) module, which employs variance-guided feature filtering to enhance the model's focus on regions with high intensity and color distribution. And the Color Distribution Alignment (CDA) module, which enforces distribution alignment in the color feature space. These designs enhance perceptual quality under low-light conditions. Experimental results on several benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance compared with existing methods.

AI.tldr: A variance-driven channel recalibration framework (VCR) for robust low-light image enhancement, featuring Channel Adaptive Adjustment (CAA) and Color Distribution Alignment (CDA), achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: LLIE methods based on sRGB suffer entangled luminance/color; HSV/ HVI spaces have artifacts and misalignments. There is a need for channel-consistent enhancement and color distribution alignment to improve perceptual quality under low light.
AI.method: VCR comprises two modules: (1) Channel Adaptive Adjustment (CAA) using variance-guided feature filtering to emphasize regions with high intensity and diverse color distribution; (2) Color Distribution Alignment (CDA) enforcing distribution alignment in the color feature space to reduce channel-level inconsistency.
AI.result: Experimental results on several benchmark datasets show state-of-the-art performance compared with existing LLIE methods.
AI.conclusion: Variance-driven channel recalibration via CAA and CDA improves perceptual quality and robustness in low-light enhancement by mitigating luminance–color decoupling and color-distribution misalignment.

title: 6ABOS: An Open-Source Atmospheric Correction Framework for the EnMAP Hyperspectral Mission Based on 6S
summary: The Environmental Mapping and Analysis Program (EnMAP) mission has opened new frontiers in the monitoring of optically complex environments. However, the accurate retrieval of surface reflectance over water bodies remains a significant challenge, as the water-leaving signal typically accounts for only a small fraction of the total radiance, being easily obscured by atmospheric scattering and surface reflection effects. This paper introduces 6ABOS (6S-based Atmospheric Background Offset Subtraction), a novel open-source Python framework designed to automate the atmospheric correction (AC) of EnMAP hyperspectral imagery. By leveraging the Second Simulation of the Satellite Signal in the Solar Spectrum (6S) radiative transfer model, 6ABOS implements a physically-based inversion scheme that accounts for Rayleigh scattering, aerosol interactions, and gaseous absorption. The framework integrates automated EnMAP metadata parsing with dynamic atmospheric parameter retrieval via the Google Earth Engine (GEE) Application Programming Interface (API). Validation was conducted over two Mediterranean inland water reservoirs with contrasting trophic states: the oligotrophic Benag{'e}ber and the hypertrophic Bell{'u}s. Results demonstrate a high degree of spectral similarity between in situ measurements and EnMAP-derived water-leaving reflectances. The Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM) values remained consistently low (SAM $<$ 10$^\circ$) across both study sites. 6ABOS is distributed via conda-forge, providing the scientific community with a scalable, transparent, and reproducible open-science tool for advancing hyperspectral aquatic research in the cloud-computing era.

AI.tldr: 6ABOS is a physically-based, open-source Python framework for automated atmospheric correction of EnMAP hyperspectral data using 6S, with GEE-based parameter retrieval; validated on two Mediterranean reservoirs, showing strong spectral agreement (SAM < 10°) between in situ and remotely sensed water-leaving reflectance.
AI.motivation: Accurate retrieval of water-leaving reflectance is hampered by atmospheric scattering and surface effects; there is a need for an automated, reproducible AC workflow for EnMAP hyperspectral imagery, especially in cloud-enabled, scalable contexts.
AI.method: 6ABOS implements a 6S-based atmospheric correction with an inversion scheme that models Rayleigh scattering, aerosols, and gas absorption. It automates EnMAP metadata parsing and leverages Google Earth Engine (GEE) API for dynamic atmospheric parameter retrieval, all within an open-source Python framework (conda-forge).
AI.result: Validated on two Mediterranean inland water bodies with contrasting trophic states (oligotrophic Benagéber and hypertrophic Bellús). Water-leaving reflectance retrieved from EnMAP closely matches in situ measurements; SAM values consistently < 10 degrees across sites, indicating high spectral fidelity.
AI.conclusion: 6ABOS provides a scalable, transparent, and reproducible open-science tool for hyperspectral aquatic research in the cloud-era, enabling robust atmospheric correction and improved surface reflectance retrieval for optically complex waters.

title: SNPgen: Phenotype-Supervised Genotype Representation and Synthetic Data Generation via Latent Diffusion
summary: Polygenic risk scores and other genomic analyses require large individual-level genotype datasets, yet strict data access restrictions impede sharing. Synthetic genotype generation offers a privacy-preserving alternative, but most existing methods operate unconditionally, producing samples without phenotype alignment, or rely on unsupervised compression, creating a gap between statistical fidelity and downstream task utility. We present SNPgen, a two-stage conditional latent diffusion framework for generating phenotype-supervised synthetic genotypes. SNPgen combines GWAS-guided variant selection (1,024-2,048 trait-associated SNPs) with a variational autoencoder for genotype compression and a latent diffusion model conditioned on binary disease labels via classifier-free guidance. Evaluated on 458,724 UK Biobank individuals across four complex diseases (coronary artery disease, breast cancer, type 1 and type 2 diabetes), models trained on synthetic data matched real-data predictive performance in a train-on-synthetic, test-on-real protocol, approaching genome-wide PRS methods that use $2$-$6\times$ more variants. Privacy analysis confirmed zero identical matches, near-random membership inference (AUC $\approx 0.50$), preserved linkage disequilibrium structure, and high allele frequency correlation ($r \geq 0.95$) with source data. A controlled simulation with known causal effects verified faithful recovery of the imposed genetic association structure.

AI.tldr: A two-stage conditional latent diffusion framework (SNPgen) generates phenotype-supervised synthetic genotypes by combining GWAS-guided variant selection, VAE compression, and a diffusion model conditioned on disease labels. It achieves train-on-synthetic/test-on-real parity for downstream risk predictions, preserves LD, shows high allele-frequency fidelity, and demonstrates privacy protections against re-identification and leakage; validated on UK Biobank across four diseases and with simulated causal structure recovery.
AI.motivation: Provide a privacy-preserving alternative to sharing large individual-level genotypes for polygenic risk scoring and GWAS, addressing the gap between statistical fidelity and utility when using synthetic data.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) GWAS-guided selection of 1,024–2,048 trait-associated SNPs; (2) VAE compress genotype data and a latent diffusion model generates genotypes conditioned on binary disease labels via classifier-free guidance; evaluated with train-on-synthetic/test-on-real on 458k UKB individuals for four diseases; privacy by design claims including no identical matches, AUC ≈ 0.50 MLI, preserved LD, high allele-frequency correlation; simulation with known causal effects.
AI.result: Synthetic data enables predictive models that match real-data performance in downstream tasks; performance approaches genome-wide PRS methods using many more variants; LD structure preserved; allele frequencies highly correlated with source data; privacy analyses show minimal leakage; causal simulation recovers imposed structure.
AI.conclusion: SNPgen provides a practical, privacy-preserving pathway to generate phenotype-aligned synthetic genotypes at scale, enabling utility in downstream analyses while maintaining strong privacy and alignment with genetic architecture.

title: Too Vivid to Be Real? Benchmarking and Calibrating Generative Color Fidelity
summary: Recent advances in text-to-image (T2I) generation have greatly improved visual quality, yet producing images that appear visually authentic to real-world photography remains challenging. This is partly due to biases in existing evaluation paradigms: human ratings and preference-trained metrics often favor visually vivid images with exaggerated saturation and contrast, which make generations often too vivid to be real even when prompted for realistic-style images. To address this issue, we present Color Fidelity Dataset (CFD) and Color Fidelity Metric (CFM) for objective evaluation of color fidelity in realistic-style generations. CFD contains over 1.3M real and synthetic images with ordered levels of color realism, while CFM employs a multimodal encoder to learn perceptual color fidelity. In addition, we propose a training-free Color Fidelity Refinement (CFR) that adaptively modulates spatial-temporal guidance scale in generation, thereby enhancing color authenticity. Together, CFD supports CFM for assessment, whose learned attention further guides CFR to refine T2I fidelity, forming a progressive framework for assessing and improving color fidelity in realistic-style T2I generation. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/ZhengyaoFang/CFM.

AI.tldr: CFD and CFM for objective color fidelity evaluation in realistic-style T2I; introduces training-free CFR to improve color authenticity; dataset/code provided.
AI.motivation: Human-rated evaluations biased toward visually vivid images; need objective, perceptual color fidelity metrics and a refinement framework.
AI.method: CFD: 1.3M images with color realism levels; CFM: multimodal encoder learning perceptual color fidelity; CFR: training-free, adaptively modulates spatial-temporal guidance scale guided by CFM attention.
AI.result: CFM aligns with perceptual color fidelity for realistic generations; CFR improves color authenticity; forms progressive assessment-improvement framework; dataset/code available.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a scalable framework for measuring and enhancing color fidelity in realistic-style T2I generation; facilitates objective benchmarking and iterative refinement.

title: LiTo: Surface Light Field Tokenization
summary: We propose a 3D latent representation that jointly models object geometry and view-dependent appearance. Most prior works focus on either reconstructing 3D geometry or predicting view-independent diffuse appearance, and thus struggle to capture realistic view-dependent effects. Our approach leverages that RGB-depth images provide samples of a surface light field. By encoding random subsamples of this surface light field into a compact set of latent vectors, our model learns to represent both geometry and appearance within a unified 3D latent space. This representation reproduces view-dependent effects such as specular highlights and Fresnel reflections under complex lighting. We further train a latent flow matching model on this representation to learn its distribution conditioned on a single input image, enabling the generation of 3D objects with appearances consistent with the lighting and materials in the input. Experiments show that our approach achieves higher visual quality and better input fidelity than existing methods.

AI.tldr: A 3D latent representation that jointly encodes geometry and view-dependent appearance from RGB-D to capture surface light field; supports single-image conditioned generation via latent flow, improving view-dependent realism over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Prev work often handles either geometry reconstruction or diffuse appearance, failing to capture rich view-dependent effects under complex lighting. A unified 3D latent space trained on RGB-D surface light-field samples can model both geometry and appearance.
AI.method: Encode random subsamples of the surface light field derived from RGB-D into a compact 3D latent space. Train a latent-flow matching model to map a single input image to the latent distribution, enabling generation of 3D objects with lighting- and material-consistent appearances.
AI.result: Experiments report higher visual quality and better input fidelity than existing methods, with the ability to reproduce view-dependent effects such as specular highlights and Fresnel reflections under complex lighting.
AI.conclusion: A unified 3D latent representation plus latent-flow conditioning improves realism and input-consistency for view-dependent appearance, suggesting scalable generation of 3D objects reflecting lighting and material properties from limited inputs.

title: LAtte: Hyperbolic Lorentz Attention for Cross-Subject EEG Classification
summary: Electroencephalogram (EEG) classification is critical for applications ranging from medical diagnostics to brain-computer interfaces, yet it remains challenging due to the inherently low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and high inter-subject variability. To address these issues, we propose LAtte, a novel framework that integrates a Lorentz Attention Module with an InceptionTime-based encoder to enable robust and generalizable EEG classification. Unlike prior work, which evaluates primarily on single-subject performance, LAtte focuses on cross-subject training. First, we learn a shared baseline signal across all subjects using pretraining tasks to capture common underlying patterns. Then, we utilize novel Lorentz low-rank adapters to learn subject-specific embeddings that model individual differences. This allows us to learn a shared model that performs robustly across subjects, and can be subsequently finetuned for individual subjects or used to generalize to unseen subjects. We evaluate LAtte on three well-established EEG datasets, achieving a substantial improvement in performance over current state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: LAtte is a cross-subject EEG classifier that combines a Lorentz Attention Module with an InceptionTime-based encoder, employing shared pretraining to learn a baseline and Lorentz low-rank adapters to capture subject-specific variability; it achieves strong cross-subject performance across three datasets.
AI.motivation: EEG signals have low SNR and high inter-subject variability, making cross-subject generalization difficult. Most prior work emphasizes single-subject performance, limiting real-world applicability in BCIs and diagnostics.
AI.method: LAtte integrates a Lorentz Attention Module with an InceptionTime-based encoder. It first learns a shared baseline via pretraining tasks across all subjects, then applies Lorentz low-rank adapters to learn subject-specific embeddings. This yields a shared model with personalized adjustments and enables finetuning for individuals or generalization to unseen subjects. Evaluation is conducted on three established EEG datasets with cross-subject training.
AI.result: The approach yields substantial improvement over current state-of-the-art methods in cross-subject EEG classification, demonstrating robust performance across subjects and potential for unseen-subject generalization.
AI.conclusion: LAtte provides a robust, generalizable cross-subject EEG classifier by coupling a shared backbone with efficient subject-specific adapters, achieving superior cross-subject performance across multiple datasets and enabling effective adaptation to new subjects.

title: COMIC: Agentic Sketch Comedy Generation
summary: We propose a fully automated AI system that produces short comedic videos similar to sketch shows such as Saturday Night Live. Starting with character references, the system employs a population of agents loosely based on real production studio roles, structured to optimize the quality and diversity of ideas and outputs through iterative competition, evaluation, and improvement. A key contribution is the introduction of LLM critics aligned with real viewer preferences through the analysis of a corpus of comedy videos on YouTube to automatically evaluate humor. Our experiments show that our framework produces results approaching the quality of professionally produced sketches while demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in video generation.

AI.tldr: A fully automated AI system generates short comedy sketches via a multi-agent production-like pipeline and LLM-based humor critics trained on YouTube data, achieving near-professional quality and state-of-the-art video generation.
AI.motivation: Automate scalable comedic video production and align humor evaluation with viewer preferences to match professional sketch quality while leveraging advances in large language models and video synthesis.
AI.method: Develop a population of agents loosely modeled on production roles; start with character references; iterative competition, evaluation, and improvement; train/align LLM critics using YouTube comedy corpus to evaluate humor; integrate into a pipeline for idea generation and video synthesis.
AI.result: Experiments show the framework approaches the quality of professionally produced sketches and achieves state-of-the-art performance in video generation.
AI.conclusion: The multi-agent production framework with LLM-based humor critics is effective for automated comedic video creation and closely approaches professional quality, with potential for scalable content generation and avenues for further improvement.

title: Ergodicity in reinforcement learning
comment: Accepted article to appear in Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A: Mathematical, Physical and Engineering Sciences
summary: In reinforcement learning, we typically aim to optimize the expected value of the sum of rewards an agent collects over a trajectory. However, if the process generating these rewards is non-ergodic, the expected value, i.e., the average over infinitely many trajectories with a given policy, is uninformative for the average over a single, but infinitely long trajectory. Thus, if we care about how the individual agent performs during deployment, the expected value is not a good optimization objective. In this paper, we discuss the impact of non-ergodic reward processes on reinforcement learning agents through an instructive example, relate the notion of ergodic reward processes to more widely used notions of ergodic Markov chains, and present existing solutions that optimize long-term performance of individual trajectories under non-ergodic reward dynamics.

AI.tldr: Non-ergodic rewards make the standard expected return over infinite trajectories a poor objective for single long trajectories; the work connects ergodicity in rewards to ergodic Markov chains and surveys methods that maximize long-term per-trajectory performance under non-ergodic dynamics.
AI.motivation: Deploy-time performance concerns a single, very long trajectory. When rewards are non-ergodic, the conventional expected return across many trajectories is uninformative for this scenario, motivating revised objectives and analysis.
AI.method: The paper uses an instructive example, builds a bridge between non-ergodic reward processes and ergodic Markov chain concepts, and surveys existing approaches that optimize long-term, per-trajectory performance under non-ergodic reward dynamics.
AI.result: A conceptual synthesis: (i) identification of non-ergodicity as a core issue for typical RL objectives, (ii) a relation to ergodic theory and Markov chains, and (iii) a survey of methods that target long-term per-trajectory performance in non-ergodic settings.
AI.conclusion: Non-ergodic reward dynamics require alternative optimization objectives in RL. Ergodic-theoretic perspectives can align deployment-time performance with training objectives, and several existing solutions address multi-trajectory long-term optimization under non-ergodic dynamics.

title: Agentar-Fin-OCR
summary: In this paper, we propose Agentar-Fin-OCR, a document parsing system tailored to financial-domain documents, transforming ultra-long financial PDFs into semantically consistent, highly accurate, structured outputs with auditing-grade provenance. To address finance-specific challenges such as complex layouts, cross-page structural discontinuities, and cell-level referencing capability, Agentar-Fin-OCR combines (1) a Cross-page Contents Consolidation algorithm to restore continuity across pages and a Document-level Heading Hierarchy Reconstruction (DHR) module to build a globally consistent Table of Contents (TOC) tree for structure-aware retrieval, and (2) a difficulty-adaptive curriculum learning training strategy for table parsing, together with a CellBBoxRegressor module that uses structural anchor tokens to localize table cells from decoder hidden states without external detectors. Experiments demonstrate that our model shows high performance on the table parsing metrics of OmniDocBench. To enable realistic evaluation in the financial vertical, we further introduce FinDocBench, a benchmark that includes six financial document categories with expert-verified annotations and evaluation metrics including Table of Contents edit-distance-based similarity (TocEDS), cross-page concatenated TEDS, and Table Cell Intersection over Union (C-IoU). We evaluate a wide range of state-of-the-art models on FinDocBench to assess their capabilities and remaining limitations on financial documents. Overall, Agentar-Fin-OCR and FinDocBench provide a practical foundation for reliable downstream financial document applications.

AI.tldr: Finance-domain OCR: Agentar-Fin-OCR for ultra-long PDFs with cross-page consolidation, DHR TOC, curriculum table parsing, and CellBBoxRegressor; FinDocBench benchmark with TocEDS, cross-page TEDS, and C-IoU.
AI.motivation: Finance documents have long, complex layouts with cross-page references and dense tables, requiring end-to-end parsing with provenance and structure-aware retrieval; standard OCR models underperform on such tasks.
AI.method: 1) Cross-page Contents Consolidation to restore continuity; 2) Document-level Heading Hierarchy Reconstruction for a global TOC; 3) Difficulty-adaptive curriculum learning for table parsing; 4) CellBBoxRegressor that localizes table cells from decoder states using structural anchors without external detectors; FinDocBench with six financial categories and new metrics (TocEDS, cross-page concatenated TEDS, C-IoU).
AI.result: Shows high performance on table parsing metrics on OmniDocBench; introduces FinDocBench to enable realistic evaluation in finance; broad evaluation on SOTA models to reveal capabilities and remaining limitations on financial documents.
AI.conclusion: Agentar-Fin-OCR and FinDocBench together provide a practical foundation for reliable downstream financial document applications.

title: NCAA Bracket Prediction Using Machine Learning and Combinatorial Fusion Analysis
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, Published in Proceedings of the 2024 IEEE Cyber Science and Technology Congress (CyberSciTech)
summary: Machine learning models have demonstrated remarkable success in sports prediction in the past years, often treating sports prediction as a classification task within the field. This paper introduces new perspectives for analyzing sports data to predict outcomes more accurately. We leverage rankings to generate team rankings for the 2024 dataset using Combinatorial Fusion Analysis (CFA), a new paradigm for combining multiple scoring systems through the rank-score characteristic (RSC) function and cognitive diversity (CD). Our result based on rank combination with respect to team ranking has an accuracy rate of $74.60\%$, which is higher than the best of the ten popular public ranking systems ($73.02\%$). This exhibits the efficacy of CFA in enhancing the precision of sports prediction through different lens.

AI.tldr: CFA-based rank fusion improves sports prediction accuracy to 74.60% on 2024 dataset, outperforming public ranking systems at 73.02%.
AI.motivation: Address heterogeneity in scoring systems by integrating them via ranking fusion to improve outcome prediction.
AI.method: Apply Combinatorial Fusion Analysis (CFA) using rank-score characteristic (RSC) and cognitive diversity (CD) to combine multiple scoring systems into team rankings and predict outcomes.
AI.result: Achieves 74.60% accuracy on 2024 dataset, surpassing the best public ranking (73.02%).
AI.conclusion: CFA adds predictive value by leveraging diverse rankings; promising for broader sports prediction tasks, with potential need for further validation and generalization studies.
